(reversible), JC virus infection, leukopenia, macrocytic anemia, malaise, malignant lymphoma, malignant neoplasm of

Indications

Suspected uncomplicated genital chlamydial infections (e.g., urethritis, cervicitis, trachoma);

low-risk community-acquired pneumonia (CAP-LR) in adults, when Legionella, Chlamydia or Mycoplasma is
suspected, or when there is hypersensitivity to the beta-lactams;

non-severe CAP in child with hypersensitivity to amoxicillin.

Contraindications

With history of cholestatic jaundice or hepatic impairment associated with prior azithromycin use.

Precautions

Cardiac conditions: congenital long QT syndrome and family history of QT prolongation; torsades de pointes,
ventricular arrhythmia and other arrhythmias, congestive heart failure, recent MI;

Proarrhythmic conditions (e.g., hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia);

Renal impairment
Exacerbations of myasthenia gravis

Elderly patients (may be more susceptible to drug-associated QT prolongation);
Breastfeeding (limited drug information available – use only if adequate alternatives are not available).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Abdominal pain, arthralgia, cramping, diarrhea, dizziness, dyspepsia, flatulence, headache, malaise,
nausea, pruritic, rash, vaginitis, vomiting.

Less Common: Abnormal heart rhythm, allergic reaction, anorexia, anxiety, chest pain, conjunctivitis, constipation,
drowsiness, edema, enteritis, fatigue, gastritis, hyperkinesia, hypesthesia, hypotension, insomnia, leukopenia, liver
disorders, myasthenia gravis, neutropenia, palpitations, pancreatitis, paresthesia, photosensitivity, rash, sleep
disturbances, somnolence, thrombocytopenia, tinnitus, urticaria, vertigo.

Rare: Acute renal failure, agitation, angioedema, cholestatic jaundice, convulsions, disturbances in taste and vision,
hemolytic anemia, hepatic necrosis and failure, interstitial nephritis, seizures, syncope.

NOTE: Macrolides inhibit P-glycoprotein, which may lead to drug interactions.

Reduces absorption of Azithromycin:

Reduces therapeutic effect of Contraceptives (Oral)

Increases serum concentration of Digoxin

Amiodarone, Artemether + Lumefantrine

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Refer to adult dosing.

Renal Impairment:

IM, IV: Adults: Following initial dose, maintenance doses should be given as follows:

ClCr 10-30mL/minute: 50% of usual dose at the usual interval.

Indications

Treatment of hospital-acquired and ventilator-associated pneumonia, and pathogen-specific infections

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to aztreonam or any component of the formulation.

Precautions

Rare cross-allergenicity to penicillins and cephalosporins has been reported;
Prolonged use may result in fungal or bacterial superinfection, including C. difficile-associated diarrhea (CDAD) and
pseudomembranous colitis. CDAD has been observed >2 months post-antibiotic treatment;
Use the injectable formulation with caution in patients with renal impairment, dosing adjustment required.

anaphylaxis, anemia, angioedema, breast tenderness, bronchospasm, chest pain, diaphoresis, dizziness, dermatitis,
fever, flushing, pancytopenia, paresthesia, petechiae, pruritus, purpura, sneezing, tinnitus, urticaria, vaginitis,
vertigo, wheezing.

Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
BCG, Typhoid vaccine (Only the live attenuated Ty21a strain is affected.)

Mechanism of Action

They have a narrow and characteristic spectrum of activity which acts by inhibiting bacterial cell wall synthesis due to
its high affinity for penicillin-binding protein 3 (PBP-3) of gram-negative bacteria. It is also active against most
Enterobacteriaceae (including E. coli, Citrobacter, Enterobacter, Klebsiella, Proteus, Providencia, Salmonella,
Serratia, Shigella, Yersinia spp. and Morganella morganii)

text:
﻿April 24, 2026, 10:39:29 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

BALANCED MULTIPLE REPLACEMENT SOLUTION WITH PH 7.4

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

B05BB02

ANATOMICAL

Blood and Blood Forming Organs

THERAPEUTIC

Blood Substitutes and Perfusion Solutions

PHARMACOLOGICAL

I.V. Solutions Affecting the Electrolyte Balance

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IV INFUSION › 500 mL bottle / bag (Rx)

IV INFUSION › 1 L bottle / bag (Rx)

Composition: Na+ 140 mmol/L K+ 5 mmol/L Mg++ 3 mmol/L Cl- 98 mmol/L Acetate 50 mmol/L

Dosage

IV INFUSION › 500 mL bottle / bag (Rx)

Dose is dependent on age, weight, and degree of fluid and electrolyte loss.

Administration

IV INFUSION › 500 mL bottle / bag (Rx)

Administer by IV infusion. Contains no bacteriostat, antimicrobial agent, or added buffer (except for pH
adjustment) and is intended only for use as a single-dose injection. Discard unused portion. Inspect visually for
particulate matter and discoloration prior to administration. Do NOT administer unless solution is clear, and
container is undamaged. Determine the amount to be infused based on replacement of losses of extracellular
fluid volume in the individual patient. Up to 3 times the volume of estimated blood loss during and after surgery
can be given to correct circulatory volume when there is only a moderate loss of blood.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Select doses with caution. Begin at the low end of the dosing range.

Page 1 of 3



April 24, 2026, 10:39:29 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Indications

Replacement of acute losses of extracellular fluid volume in surgery, trauma, burns, or shock; adjunct to restore a
decrease in circulatory volume in patients with moderate blood loss.

NOTE: NOT intended to supplant transfusion of whole blood or packed red cells in the presence of uncontrolled
hemorrhage or severe reductions of red cell volume.

NOT intended to correct acidosis or large deficits of individual electrolytes, nor to replace blood or plasma
expanders when these are indicated.

Contraindications

Hypernatremia or hyperchloremia and related conditions (hypermagnesemia, hyperphosphatemia, edema, certain
cardiac diseases).

Precautions

Can cause fluid and/or solute overloading resulting in dilution of serum electrolyte concentrations, overhydration,
congested states, or pulmonary edema; Clinical evaluation and periodic laboratory determinations are necessary to
monitor changes in fluid balance, electrolyte concentrations and acid-base balance during prolonged parenteral
therapy or whenever the condition of the patient warrants such evaluation; Sodium content (use with caution in CHF,
concomitant corticosteroid use, severe renal insufficiency, and clinical states with edema with sodium retention);
Potassium content (use with caution in hyperkalemia, severe renal failure, and potassium retention); Acetate
content (use with caution in metabolic or respiratory alkalosis and severe hepatic insufficiency); Renal impairment
(monitor renal function during fluid replacement); Elderly (have been shown to secrete higher levels of ADH;
increased risk for developing fluid overload and dilutional hyponatremia).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Febrile response, infection at the site of injection, venous thrombosis or phlebitis extending from the site of
injection, extravasation, hypervolemia

NOTE: May occur because of the solution or the technique during administration.

If an adverse reaction occurs, discontinue the infusion, evaluate the patient, institute appropriate therapeutic
countermeasures, and save the remainder of the fluid for examination, if deemed necessary.

Page 2 of 3



April 24, 2026, 10:39:29 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Increases risk of hypertension and edema of the following drugs due to sodium and water retention:
Corticosteroids e.g. Prednisone

NOTE: Additives may be incompatible. Consult with pharmacist.

Mechanism of Action

A sterile, nonpyrogenic, isotonic solution of balanced electrolytes in water for injection, administered by IV infusion
for parenteral replacement of acute losses of extracellular fluid.

Pregnancy & Remarks

PREGNANCY CATEGORY

BARIUM SULFATE

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

V08BA01; V08BA02

ANATOMICAL

Various

THERAPEUTIC

Contrast Media

PHARMACOLOGICAL

X-Ray Contrast Media, Non-Iodinated

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › USP grade powder suspended in water, 340 g pouch, LDPE bag (Rx)

ORAL › USP grade powder suspended in water, 454 g pouch, LDPE bag (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › USP grade powder suspended in water, 340 g pouch, LDPE bag (Rx)

NOTE: Determine volume to be administered based on degree and extent of contrast required in the area(s) under
examination and on the technique employed. GI tract marking, by mouth, ADULT, 900–1,350 mL of prepared
suspension, 20–30 minutes prior to scheduled procedure; for improved gastric marking, consume the final 200 mL
immediately prior to the scan.

Administration

ORAL › USP grade powder suspended in water, 340 g pouch, LDPE bag (Rx)

Prepare solution to a concentration of 0.1% w/v or 0.1% w/w. To be administered on an empty stomach, free of
fluids as much as possible. Patients should be instructed to neither eat nor drink after the evening meal on the
night preceding the examination. Rehydrate patient as quickly as possible following barium study of the GI tract to
prevent impaction of barium. Cathartics may also be required. NOTE: Saline cathartics are recommended on a
routine basis, especially in patients with a history of constipation, unless clinically contraindicated. If enema tips
are used, use caution during insertion. Too forceful or deep insertion may cause tearing or perforation of the
rectum. If balloon retention tips are used, inflate under fluoroscopic control by qualified personnel. NOTE: Use
caution to avoid over-inflation. Overfilling or asymmetrical filling with tip displacement may occur, which can lead
to rectal perforation or barium granulomas.

Page 1 of 3



April 24, 2026, 10:39:42 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Patients with history of food aspiration:
Administer initially small amounts such as ½ teaspoon and carefully observe swallowing under fluoroscopic control.

Obesity and patients with delayed transit:
Increase total volume up to 1,800 mL.

Indications

Radiographical contrast media for upper and lower gastrointestinal procedures by conventional x-ray or computed
tomography

Contraindications

Proximal to a known obstruction of the colon; known or suspected GI perforation.

Precautions

WARNING:
MRI Studies. Diagnostic procedures involving the use of radiopaque contrast agents should be carried out under the
direction of personnel with the requisite training and a thorough knowledge of the particular procedure to be
performed.

Allergic Reactions. A history of bronchial asthma, atopy, or a previous reaction to a contrast agent, warrant special
attention. Use with caution in severely debilitated patients and in those with marked hypertension or advanced
cardiac disease.

History of food aspiration (barium swallow studies required);
Pregnancy (use radiographic procedures only if deemed essential).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Less Common:
weakness, pallor, tinnitus, diaphoresis, bradycardia

Rare:
Aspiration pneumonitis, barium impaction, granuloma formation, intravasation, embolization, peritonitis, vasovagal
episodes, syncopal episodes, fatalities, generalized pruritus, erythema, urticaria, laryngeal edema, bronchospasm,
hypotension, peripheral vasodilation, hypotension, reflex tachycardia, dyspnea, agitation, confusion, cyanosis,
unconsciousness, severe stomach cramps (transient), diarrhea (transient)

Page 2 of 3



April 24, 2026, 10:39:42 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

No information found
TEST INTERACTION. May appear as a negative GI marker with certain pulse sequences in MR studies

Mechanism of Action

Powder for suspension that acts as a positive radio contrast agent for radiographic studies. Barium sulfate is not
absorbed or metabolized and is eliminate from the GIT unchanged

Pregnancy & Remarks

PREGNANCY CATEGORY

Not classified

BASILIXIMAB

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

L04AC02

ANATOMICAL

Antineoplastic and Immunomodulating Agents

THERAPEUTIC

Immunosuppressants

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Interleukin Inhibitors

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IV BOLUS › IV INFUSION › 20 mg vial (Rx)

Dosage

IV BOLUS / IV INFUSION › 20 mg vial (Rx)

Acute renal transplant rejection prophylaxis, by IV infusion, ADULT and CHILD=35 kg, with cyclosporine and
corticosteroids, 20 mg within 2 hours prior to transplant surgery, followed by a second 20 mg dose 4 days after
transplantation; withhold second dose if complications occur; CHILD<35 kg, with cyclosporine and
corticosteroids, 10 mg within 2 hours prior to transplant surgery, followed by a second 10 mg dose 4 days after
transplantation; the second dose should be withheld if complications occur (including severe hypersensitivity
reactions or graft loss).

Administration

IV BOLUS / IV INFUSION › 20 mg vial (Rx)

For IV administration only. Administer by IV bolus infusion or IV infusion over 20–30 minutes. Administer only after
assurance that patient will receive renal graft and immunosuppression. NOTE: Bolus dosing is associated with
nausea, vomiting, and local pain at the injection site.

Dose Adjustment

No information found

Page 1 of 3



April 24, 2026, 10:39:48 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Indications

Prophylaxis of acute organ rejection in renal transplantation

Contraindications

No information found

Precautions

WARNING: Should only be used by physicians experienced in immunosuppressive therapy and management of
transplant patients. Adequate laboratory and supportive medical resources must be readily available.

Re-exposure to basiliximab with a subsequent course of therapy should only be done with extreme caution.

Anaphylactoid/hypersensitivity reactions Diabetes (new-onset diabetes) Human antimurine antibodies [HAMA]
(lymphoproliferative disorders (may increase incidence)); opportunistic infections (may increase incidence))

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Abdominal pain, abnormal heart sounds, abnormal vision, accidental trauma, acidosis, acne, agitation,
albuminuria, anemia, angina, anxiety, arrhythmia (tachycardia), arthralgia, arthropathy, atrial fibrillation, back pain,
bladder disorder, bronchitis, bronchospasm, cataract, chest pain, conjunctivitis, constipation, cough, cramps, cyst,
cytomegalovirus (CMV) infection, dehydration, depression, diabetes mellitus, diarrhea, fever, dizziness, dyspepsia,
dyspnea, dysuria, fluid overload, enlarged abdomen, esophagitis, fatigue, flatulence, fracture, gastroenteritis,
generalized edema, genital edema (male), GI hemorrhage, gingival hyperplasia, increased glucocorticoids, headache,
heart failure, hematoma, hematuria, hernia, herpes infection (simplex and zoster), hypercholesterolemia,
hyper/hypocalcemia, hyper-/hypokalemia, hyperglycemia, insomnia, hyperlipemia, hypertension, hypertrichosis,
hyperuricemia, impotence, hypoesthesia, hypoglycemia, hypomagnesemia, hyponatremia, leg pain, hypotension,
infection, upper respiratory infection, leukopenia, moniliasis, malaise, melena, myalgia, nausea, neuropathy, oliguria,
pain, paresthesia, peripheral edema, pharyngitis, pneumonia, polycythemia, pruritus, pulmonary edema, purpura,
rash, renal tubular necrosis, rhinitis, rigors, sepsis, sinusitis, skin disorder, skin ulceration, tremor, stomatitis,
thrombocytopenia, thrombosis, ureteral disorder, urinary frequency, urinary retention, urinary tract infection, viral
infection, vomiting, weakness, weight gain, wound complications

Less Common: Anaphylaxis, capillary leak syndrome, cytokine release syndrome, diabetes (new onset),
lymphoproliferative disease, hypersensitivity reaction

Page 2 of 3



April 24, 2026, 10:39:48 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Basiliximab: Trastuzumab (neutropenic effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Basiliximab: Denosumab (serious infections)
Reduces diagnostic effect of Coccidioides immitis Skin Test

Avoid concomitant use with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Basiliximab: Roflumilast (immunosuppressive effect)
Enhances therapeutic effect of Tofacitinib (immunosuppressive effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Basiliximab: Pimecrolimus, Tacrolimus (Topical),
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Belimumab, Leflunomide (hematologic toxicity, such
as pancytopenia, agranulocytosis, and/or thrombocytopenia), Natalizumab (concurrent infection), Live Vaccines
(infection)
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: BCG (Intravesical), Vaccines

Mechanism of Action

Chimeric (murine/human) immunosuppressant monoclonal antibody, which blocks the alpha-chain of the
interleukin2 (IL-2) receptor complex. This receptor is expressed on activated T-lymphocytes and is a critical pathway
for activating cell-mediated allograft rejection.

BCG VACCINE

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

J07AN01

ANATOMICAL

Anti-Infectives for systemic use

THERAPEUTIC

Vaccines

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Bacterial Vaccines

CHEMICAL CLASS

Drugs for Tuberculosis

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

I-DERMAL › Freeze-dried power, 100 micrograms/0.1 mL, 1 mL vial (Rx)

I-DERMAL › Freeze-dried power, 100 micrograms/0.1 mL, 1.5 mL vial (Rx)

I-DERMAL › Freeze-dried power, 100 micrograms/0.1 mL, 2 mL vial (Rx)

I-DERMAL › 500 microgram/mL vial + 1 mL diluent in ampule, 20 doses (Rx)

Dosage

I-DERMAL › Freeze-dried power, 100 micrograms/0.1 mL, 1 mL vial (Rx)

Immunization against tuberculosis, by ID injection, ADULT and CHILD >12 months 0.1 mL; INFANT up to 12 months,
0.05 mL. NOTE: Tuberculin test must be done before vaccination (except in infants <6 months); give vaccine if
induration is <5 mm 48-72 hours after dose of 10 tuberculin units. Vaccine is preferably given at birth, or any time
after birth; may be given at the same time as other live vaccines, if not given simultaneously, should be given 4
weeks apart; when given to infants, no need to delay primary immunizations.

Administration

I-DERMAL › Freeze-dried power, 100 micrograms/0.1 mL, 1 mL vial (Rx)

Give by ID injection. Stretch skin between thumb and finger above the insertion of the deltoid muscle onto the
humerus. Store at 2-8°C. Freezing is the most common cause of vaccine damage. Do NOT use a defrosted vaccine
unless freezing is the recommended storage condition.

Page 1 of 3



April 24, 2026, 10:39:54 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Indications

Active immunization against tuberculosis

Contraindications

Known hypersensitivity to the vaccine or any of its components; previous TB infection, or tuberculin reactions >5
mm; HIV infection; significant fever (give at least 1 month after fever subsides); patients receiving
immunosuppressive therapy; primary and secondary immune deficiency states; generalized septic skin conditions;
burns; pregnancy.

Precautions

Eczema and scabies;
Patients at high risk for HIV infection.
Pregnancy (first trimester: theoretical risk of congenital malformations; but the need for vaccination may,
sometimes, outweigh the possible risk to fetus)

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Enlargement of regional lymph nodes, ulcer at injection site (2–6 weeks after vaccination)
Less Common: Fainting, fever, injection site reactions (pain, redness, itching, small hard lump that may persist for
some weeks)
Rare: Abscess, allergic reactions including anaphylaxis, disseminated infection, keloid formation, lymphadenitis,
osteiti

Page 2 of 3



April 24, 2026, 10:39:54 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Other vaccines and immunoglobulins [administer at least 4 weeks apart]

Avoid concomitant use with:
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Anti-infectives
Immunosuppressive Agents e.g. Corticosteroids Antineoplastic (decreases antibody response)

TEST INTERACTION. May suppress response to tuberculin test if given within 4–6 weeks of vaccination.

Mechanism of Action

A vaccine prepared from bacillus Calmette-Guerin, an attenuated strain of Mycobacterium bovis, which reduces the
incidence of meningeal and military TB in early childhood.

BEDAQUILINE FUMARATE

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

J04AK05

ANATOMICAL

Anti-Infectives for systemic use

THERAPEUTIC

Antimycobacterials

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Drugs for Treatment of Tuberculosis

CHEMICAL CLASS

Drugs for Tuberculosis

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › 100 mg tablet (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › 100 mg tablet (Rx)

Note: Prior to treatment with Bedaquiline, obtain susceptibility information against M. tuberculosis, ECG, serum
electrolyte concentrations and liver function tests. Combination therapy, by mouth, ADULT (≥18 years), 400 mg
once daily for the first 2 weeks, followed by 200 mg orally 3 times per week (at least 48 hours between doses) for
22 weeks. The total duration of therapy is 24 weeks. Combination therapy, by mouth, CHILD (12 - 18 years, ≥30 kg
body weight), 400 mg once daily for the first 2 weeks, followed by 200 mg orally 3 times per week (at least 48 hours
between doses) for 22 weeks. The total duration of therapy is 24 weeks. (off label use but dose recommendation
from the WHO).

Administration

ORAL › 100 mg tablet (Rx)

Take Bedaquiline with food. Administer by directly observed therapy and used only in combination with other anti-
mycobacterial drugs. Emphasize the need for compliance with full course of therapy.

Page 1 of 3



April 24, 2026, 10:39:59 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Lactation: Monitor infants for signs of hepatotoxicity.

Bedaquiline may be present in breast milk.

Pediatric: Safety and effectiveness of Bedaquiline have not been established for those <12 years of age and <30 kg
body weight.

Hepatic: For severe impairment, use with caution and monitor for adverse reactions.

Renal: Use with caution.

Indications

Treatment of pulmonary multi-drug resistant tuberculosis in combination with other drugs.

Contraindications

None.

Precautions

Resistance to Bedaquiline: Use only in combination with other MDR-TB treatment.

WARNING:
An INCREASED RISK OF DEATH was seen with Bedaquiline use among adults. Only use Bedaquiline for those ≥12
years of age when an effective treatment regimen cannot be otherwise provided.

WARNING:
Bedaquiline can PROLONG THE QT INTERVAL.
Avoid use with drugs that may cause additive QT prolongation. Obtain and monitor ECG and serum electrolytes.
Discontinue if significant ventricular arrhythmia or if QTcF interval prolongation of greater than 500 ms develops.
Hepatotoxicity: Monitor symptoms (fatigue, anorexia, nausea, jaundice, dark urine, liver tenderness, hepatomegaly)
and liver function tests; test for viral hepatitis; Discontinue other hepatotoxic drugs.

STORAGE AND STABILITY.
Store at 25°C, excursions permitted between 15°C and 30°C. Keep out of reach of children.

Page 2 of 3



April 24, 2026, 10:39:59 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common:
Incidence ≥10%: ADULT, nausea, arthralgia, headache, hemoptysis and chest pain, CHILD,arthralgia, nausea,
abdominal pain.

Less common:
Incidence <10%: Anorexia, increased transaminases, rash, and increased blood amylase.

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Increases AUC of Bedaquiline: Lopinavir/ritonavir

Avoid concomitant use with:
Reduces the therapeutic effect of Bedaquiline: Strong CYP3A4 Inducers (rifampin, rifapentine, rifabutin), moderate
CYP3A4 Inducers (antiretroviral drugs: efavirenz)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Bedaquiline:
CYP3A4 Inhibitors (ketoconazole, itraconazole), QT Prolonging drugs (ketoconazole, clofazimine)

Mechanism of Action

Bedaquiline fumarate is diarylquinoline antimycobacterial drug that inhibits mycobacterial ATP synthase, by binding
to subunit c of the enzyme, blocking the energy generation of M. tuberculosis.

BENZYL BENZOATE

Drug Classification

dose is instilled over 2–3 seconds followed by at least 30 seconds of manual ventilation or until stable. Each
quarter-dose is administered with the infant in a different position. Slightly downward inclination with head

Indications

Management of drug-induced extrapyramidal symptoms (except tardive dyskinesia) by phenothiazines,
butyrophenones or reserpine; adjunctive treatment of post-encephalitic, idiopathic or arteriosclerotic Parkinson’s
disease.

Contraindications

Angle-closure glaucoma; untreated urinary retention; prostatic hypertrophy; myasthenia gravis; and GI obstruction
or atony; toxic megacolon.

Precautions

Avoid abrupt withdrawal in Parkinson’s disease (to prevent acute exacerbation); tardive dyskinesia; fever or high
ambient temperature (hyperthermia).

Heart diseases e.g., arrhythmias, coronary heart disease, and heart failure (may be exacerbated); hepatic impairment.

Inflammatory bowel disease (paralytic ileus); dementia (anticholinergics are best avoided; may worsen symptoms and
antagonize therapeutic effects of anticholinesterases); GERD (may be aggravated).

Elderly (more sensitive to anticholinergic adverse effects; confusion may be precipitated or worsened); children
(more susceptible to anticholinergic side effects).

Pregnancy; lactation (use with caution).

SKILLED TASKS. May impair ability to perform tasks, which require mentalmachinery. alertness, like driving or
operating

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Blurred vision, confusion, constipation, decreased sweating, disorientation, dizziness, drowsiness,
flushing, lightheadedness, dryness of mouth, mydriasis, nausea, nervousness, reduced bronchial secretions, thirst,
urinary hesitancy and retention, vomiting.

Less Common or Rare: Agitation, angle-closure glaucoma, arrhythmias, delirium, delusions, diplopia, excitement,
hallucinations, epigastric distress, hypotension, memory impairment, muscular cramping and weakness,
palpitations, paralytic ileus, paresthesia, rash, psychiatric disturbances, urticaria, bradycardia followed by
tachycardia.

Administration

For SC injection only. Administer 30–45 minutes before meals. Shake the suspension gently before withdrawing a
dose. Follow manufacturer’s instructions. Inspect visually prior to use. It should not contain particulate matter and
should appear uniformly cloudy after mixing. Do NOT use if particulate matter is seen. Do NOT mix with other
insulins or diluents. WARNING: NEVER administer intramuscularly or intravenously. NOT suitable for the
emergency treatment of diabetic ketoacidosis.

Dose Adjustment

Hepatic Impairment: Insulin requirements may be decreased in patients with hepatic impairment.
Renal Impairment: In mild-to-moderate impairment, reduce dose. In severe impairment, refer to a specialist
CrCl 10-50 mL/min - Administer at 75% of normal dose and monitor glucose closely
CrCl <10 mL/min - Administer at 25% to 50% of normal dose and monitor glucose closely

Indications

Management of diabetes mellitus

Contraindications

Hypoglycemia

Precautions

Acute illness or conditions, e.g., trauma, Myocardial Infarction; Infections; Stroke; Coma; Infections; Diabetic
ketoacidosis; Surgery; Renal impairment; Exercise; Pregnancy

SKILLED TASKS: Driving may be hazardous when hypoglycemic since awareness is impaired. Check blood glucose
concentration before driving, and at intervals of approximately 2 hours when on long journeys.

MONITORING: The facility should have monitoring at the point of care. Blood glucose concentration varies
throughout the day. Diabetes patients should aim to maintain their blood glucose concentration between 4- 9
mmol/L for most of the time [ideally, 80-130 mg/dL (4-7 mmol/L before meals and <180 mg/dL (<9 mmol/L) after
meals]. Prevent blood glucose concentration from falling below 72mg/dL (4 mmol/L) because of the risk of
hypoglycemia. Glycated hemoglobin concentration (HbA1c) should be <7% (53 mmol/L).

STABILITY AND STORAGE: Insulin preparations should be stored in a refrigerator at 2-8oC, protected from light and
not allowed to freeze. Patients should be advised not to expose their vials or cartridges to excessive heat or
sunlight.

Common: Hypoglycemia

Less Common: Edema, lipodystrophy (either as lipoatrophy or lipohypertrophy) at the site of injection, weight gain.

Rare: Localized reactions (e.g., redness, swelling, itching), generalized hypersensitivity reactions (including rash
over the whole body, shortness of breath, wheezing, hypotension, tachycardia, sweating, or very rarely, anaphylactic
reactions).

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Insulin: ACE Inhibitors, e.g., Enalapril, Beta Blockers, e.g., Atenolol, Propranolol
(hypoglycemic effect), Alcohol (inhibits hepatic glucose output; decreases blood glucose concentration)
Increases risk of adverse effects of Insulin: Alcohol (hypoglycemia; masks warning symptoms), Beta Blockers, e.g.,
Atenolol, Propranolol (masks warning signs of hypoglycemia), Drugs increasing blood glucose concentration, e.g.,
Glucocorticoids, Antipsychotics, Calcineurin Inhibitors, High-dose Thiazide Diuretics (alters diabetes control of
insulin) Nifedipine (impairs glucose tolerance), Thiazolidinediones (increases risk of edema and heart failure)
Reduces therapeutic effect of insulin: Drugs increasing blood glucose concentration, e.g., Glucocorticoids,
antipsyhocitcs, Calcineurin inhibitors, High-dose Thiazide Diurectics
Avoid concomitant use with:
Reduces therapeutic effect of Insulin: Contraceptives, Oral, e.g., Levonorgestrel, Medroxyprogesterone
(Corticosteroids, e.g., Dexamethasone, Hydrocortisone, Prednisolone; Diuretics, e.g., Furosemide,
Hydrochlorothiazide)

Mechanism of Action

A fixed ratio premix recombinant human insulin formulation containing a short-acting insulin and an
intermediateacting insulin (70% isophane insulin and 30% soluble insulin). Human insulin is produced by recombinant
DNA technology utilizing a non-pathogenic laboratory strain of Escherichia coli. It is a suspension of crystals
produced from combining human insulin and protamine sulfate under appropriate conditions for crystal formation
and mixing with human insulin injection.

No information found

the large intestine. It also alters water and electrolyte secretion, producing net interstitial fluid accumulation and
laxation.

Indications

Management of coronary heart failure

Page 2 of 6



April 24, 2026, 10:44:09 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Reversible airway diseases; second- or third-degree heart block (except in patients with functioning artificial
pacemaker); shock (cardiogenic and hypovolemic); bradycardia (45 to 50 beats/minute); sick sinus syndrome (except
in the presence of a pacemaker); severe hypotension; uncontrolled heart failure; concomitant IV administration of
calcium channel blockers; pulmonary hypertension

Precautions

WARNING: Do NOT withdraw beta blocker therapy abruptly. Gradually taper over 1–2 weeks to avoid acute tachycardia,
hypertension, and/or ischemia. Abrupt withdrawal may exacerbate angina and, in some cases, cause myocardial
infarction, ventricular arrhythmias, and rebound hypertension. Temporary but prompt resumption of beta-blocker
therapy may be indicated with worsening of angina or acute coronary insufficiency.

Major surgery: Do NOT withdraw chronic beta blocker therapy
prior to major surgery.

Atrial fibrillation; Atrioventricular block (commonly produces mild first-degree heart block; may also produce severe
first- (P-R interval ≥0.26 sec), second-, or third-degree heart block; patients with acute myocardial infarction have a
high risk of developing heart block of varying degrees)
Hypotension (symptomatic hypotension may occur with use)
Anaphylactic reactions (may become more sensitive to repeated challenges; treatment of anaphylaxis, e.g.,
epinephrine, may be ineffective or even promote undesirable effects)
Extravasation (can lead to skin necrosis and sloughing)
Hyperkalemia (associated with elevations in serum potassium and development of hyperkalemia; monitor serum
potassium during therapy); hepatic impairment.
Bronchospastic disease (may be exacerbated)
Conduction abnormality (can cause bradycardia, including sinus pause, heart block, severe bradycardia, and cardiac
arrest)
Diabetes (may potentiate and/or mask signs and symptoms of hypoglycemia)
Heart failure (monitor for worsening of condition).
Myasthenia gravis
Peripheral vascular disease and Raynaud’s disease (can precipitate or aggravate symptoms or arterial insufficiency)
Pheochromocytoma (may aggravate hypertension; requires adequate alpha blockade prior to use of beta-blockers)
Prinzmetal variant angina (requires concomitant alpha1-adrenergic receptor blockage, unopposed alpha1-adrenergic
receptors mediate coronary vasoconstriction and can worsen angina symptoms).
Psoriasis (associated with induction or exacerbation of psoriasis)
Psychiatric disease (may cause or exacerbate CNS depression)
Renal impairment (active metabolite is retained); thyroid disease (may mask signs of hyperthyroidism, e.g.,
tachycardia; abrupt withdrawal may exacerbate symptoms of hyperthyroidism and precipitate thyroid storm; may
alter thyroid function tests).
Elderly (bradycardia may be observed more frequently in patients >65 years).
Pregnancy (crosses the placenta; may cause intrauterine growth restriction, small placenta, fetal or neonatal
bradycardia, hypoglycemia, respiratory depression); lactation (excreted in breast milk and detected in the serum and
urine of nursing infants; use with caution).

No information found

Precautions

WARNING: Should be administered under the supervision of an experienced cancer chemotherapy physician.
Administer in a facility sufficient to appropriately diagnose and manage complications.
Occurrence of pulmonary fibrosis is the most severe toxicity. It commonly presents as pneumonitis, occasionally
progressing to pulmonary fibrosis. Risk is higher in elderly patients, glomerular filtration rate <80 mL/minute
advanced disease, or patients receiving >400 units total lifetime dose or >300 units cumulative dose. Risk is also
increased in surgery patients receiving O2.
A severe idiosyncratic reaction consisting of hypotension, mental confusion, fever, chills, and wheezing has been
reported.

Mild to moderate impairment: No dosage adjustments; initiate therapy conservatively and titrate cautiously.
Severe impairment: Use of bromazepam is contraindicated.
Precautions:
WARNING: Patients must be cautioned about performing tasks which require mental alertness (e.g., operating
machinery or driving).

Concerns related to adverse effects:
Anterograde amnesia; CNS depression; paradoxical reactions (e.g., hyperactive or aggressive behavior); sleep-
related activities.

Use with caution in:
Depression (not recommended in patients with depressive disorders or psychosis);
Drug abuse (dependence and tolerance); Withdrawal (use caution when reducing dose or withdrawing therapy;
decrease slowly and monitor for withdrawal symptoms);
Post-traumatic Stress Disorder; Hepatic and renal impairment; Respiratory depression;

Elderly (impaired cognition, delirium, falls, fractures, and motor vehicle accidents. (avoid use for treatment of
insomnia, agitation, or delirium);
Pregnancy (not recommended as treatment for anxiety during pregnancy, especially during the first trimester; drug
should be tapered if discontinued); Lactation (use while breastfeeding is not recommended).

MONITORING PARAMETERS: Respiratory, cardiovascular, and mental status; periodic CBC and liver function tests.

STORAGE AND STABILITY: Store at room temperature of 15°C to 30°C (59°F to 86°F).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Cardiac arrest, hypotension, palpitations, tachycardia; Ataxia, confusion, depression, dizziness,
drowsiness, emotional lability, euphoria, fatigue, headache, impaired consciousness, myasthenia, seizure, Pruritus,
skin rash, change in libido, Gastrointestinal distress, nausea, vomiting, xerostomia; urinary incontinence, muscle
spasm, Blurred vision, diplopia, respiratory depression.

Page 3 of 4



April 24, 2026, 10:48:16 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Reduces the therapeutic effect of Bromazepam: Theophylline Derivatives e.g., Aminophylline

Enhances the therapeutic effects of CNS depressants e.g.
Alcohol

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
Flumazenil (precipitate seizures)
Pramipexole, Ropinirole, Rotigotine (sedative effect)
SSRIs (psychomotor impairment)

Mechanism of Action

Bromazepam is a benzodiazepine. It has been used in the short-term treatment of anxiety disorders occurring alone
or associated with insomnia.

Nervous System

THERAPEUTIC

Anti-Parkinson Drugs

Dopaminergic Agents

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › 2.5 mg tablet (as mesilate) (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › 2.5 mg tablet (as mesilate) (Rx)

Hyperprolactinemia, by mouth, ADULT, initially 0.5 to 2.5 mg once daily at bedtime to increase tolerance; slowly
increase the daily dose by 1.25 to 2.5 mg every 1–2 weeks up to an average of 20–30 mg once daily (maximum, 100
mg once daily).

Administration

ORAL › 2.5 mg tablet (as mesilate) (Rx)

Administer with food to decrease GI distress.

Dose Adjustment

Hepatic Impairment:
May require dose adjustment. Undergoes extensive hepatic metabolism. Use with caution.

Indications

Management of hyperprolactinemia.

Page 1 of 3



April 24, 2026, 10:48:21 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Uncontrolled hypertension; eclampsia; preeclampsia; pregnancy-induced hypertension; postpartum patients with
coronary artery disease or other severe cardiovascular conditions.

Precautions

Cardiac valvular fibrosis; cardiovascular effects (e.g., symptomatic hypotension; seizures, hypertension, MI, and
stroke).

Visual or auditory hallucinations; impulse control disorders; sedation (e.g., sudden sleep onset and somnolence);
dementia; psychosis.

Melanoma; pleural or retroperitoneal fibrosis (prolonged and high-dose daily use); peptic ulcer disease (severe GI
bleeding; may be fatal).

Acromegaly (digital vasospasm); diabetic ketoacidosis; macroadenomas; pituitary irradiation; prolactinsecreting
adenomas.

Elderly (concomitant diseases or drug therapy may complicate therapy).

Pregnancy (a pregnancy test every 4 weeks is recommended as long as no menses occur); lactation.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Hypotension, Raynaud’s phenomenon, syncope, vasospasm (digital), dizziness, fatigue, headache,
drowsiness, hypoglycemia, nausea, lightheadedness, constipation, anorexia, GI hemorrhage, abdominal cramps,
dyspepsia, diarrhea, vomiting, xerostomia, increased susceptibility to infection weakness, amblyopia, rhinitis, flu-like
symptoms, nasal congestion, sinusitis.

Less Common: Abdominal distress, acquired valvular heart disease, alopecia, anxiety, ataxia, blepharospasm, blurred
vision, bradycardia, cardiac arrhythmia, cerebrovascular accident (postpartum), cold extremities, cold intolerance,
confusion, constrictive pericarditis, delusions, depression, dyskinesia, dysphagia, dyspnea, epileptiform seizures,
ergot alkaloids toxicity, erythromelalgia, GI ulcer, hallucination, heavy headedness, MI (postpartum), hypersensitivity
reaction, hypertension (postpartum), increased cerebrospinal fluid pressure, increased libido, insomnia, lassitude,
lethargy, muscle cramps, narcolepsy, nervousness, nightmares, onoff phenomenon, pallor, paranoia, paresthesia,
pericardial effusion, peripheral edema, pleural effusion, psychomotor agitation, psychosis, pulmonary fibrosis,
retroperitoneal fibrosis, pleurisy, seizure (postpartum), skin mottling, skin rash, status epilepticus (postpartum),
sleep disorder, tachycardia, tingling of the ears, tinnitus, transient blindness, urinary frequency, urinary
incontinence, vasodepressor syncope, urinary retention, ventricular tachycardia, vertigo, visual disturbance.

Mechanism of Action

Bromocriptine is a semisynthetic ergot alkaloid and dopamine agonist with high affinity for the D2 receptor and weak
a-agonist activity. The dopamine receptor agonist activity also causes inhibition of prolactinsecreting cells in the
anterior pituitary for the treatment of hyperprolactinemia and for reducing tumor sizes in prolactin-and growth
hormone-secreting pituitary adenomas.

BUDESONIDE (DECONGESTANTS AND OTHER NASAL
PREPARATIONS FOR TOPICAL USE)

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

R01AD05

ANATOMICAL

Respiratory System

THERAPEUTIC

Nasal Preparations

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Decongestants and Other Nasal Preparations for Topical Use

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

RESPIR › DPI: 100 micrograms/dose x 200 doses with appropriate accompanying dispenser (Rx)

RESPIR › DPI: 200 micrograms/dose x 100 doses and 200 doses with appropriate accompanying dispenser (Rx)

RESPIR › MDI: 200 micrograms/dose x 120 actuations (Rx)

RESPIR › Respiratory solution for nebulization: 250 micrograms/mL respiratory solution (for nebulization), 2 mL
(unite dose) (Rx)

Dosage

RESPIR › DPI: 100 micrograms/dose x 200 doses with appropriate accompanying dispenser (Rx)

NOTE: Dose should be individualized and tapered to the smallest dose maintaining symptom control. Rhinitis, into
the nostril, ADULT and CHILD ≥6 years, initially 256 micrograms daily once daily in the morning or in 2 divided doses
(morning and evening). Nasal polyps, into the nostril, ADULT, 64–100 micrograms into each nostril twice daily for up
to 3 months.

Administration

RESPIR › DPI: 100 micrograms/dose x 200 doses with appropriate accompanying dispenser (Rx)

Shake well before use. If using for the first time, remove the protective cap from the tip and prime metered pump
by depressing the pump firmly several times. To spray, hold bottle with thumb at base and nozzle between first and
second fingers. Without tilting your head, insert the nozzle into nostril. Fully depress rim with a firm, even stroke
and sniff deeply. Wipe nozzle clean after use.

Page 1 of 4



April 24, 2026, 10:48:43 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Administer the smallest possible effective dose for the shortest duration.

Pediatric:
Titrate to lowest effective dose to minimize systemic effects.

Indications

For seasonal allergic and non-allergic perennial rhinitis; treatment of nasal polyps; prevention of nasal polyps after
polypectomy

Contraindications

Primary treatment of status asthmaticus, acute episodes of asthma

NOTE: NOT for the relief of acute bronchospasm

Precautions

WARNING: Discontinuation of Therapy. Withdraw systemic therapy with gradual tapering of dose. There have been
reports of systemic corticosteroid withdrawal symptoms (e.g., joint or muscle pain, lassitude, depression) when
withdrawing oral inhalation therapy.

Adrenal suppression; Bronchospasm (if this occurs, stop steroid and treat with a fast-acting bronchodilator); Asthma
(not to be used in status asthmaticus or for the
relief of acute bronchospasm); Immunosuppression (use is restricted in active TB); Local oral infections; Kaposi's
sarcoma (if noted, discontinue therapy); myopathy
(monitor creatine kinase; recovery may be delayed); myasthenia gravis; Psychiatric disturbances; seizure disorders;
Cardiovascular disease (myocardial infarction); Diabetes; gastrointestinal disease; Hepatic impairment (long-term
use has been associated with fluid retention); renal impairment (fluid retention may occur); Thyroid disease (changes
in thyroid status may necessitate dose adjustments); Ocular disease; Osteoporosis; Elderly; children (routinely
monitor growth); lactation (excreted in breast milk).

Page 2 of 4



April 24, 2026, 10:48:43 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Syncope, headache, pain, hypertonia, insomnia, voice disorder, weight gain, emotional lability, fatigue, skin
rash, contact dermatitis, eczema, pruritus, pustular rash, dyspepsia, nausea, gastroenteritis, diarrhea, vomiting,
abdominal pain, dysgeusia, xerostomia, anorexia, cervical purpura, bruise, lymphadenopathy, hypersensitivity
reaction, candidiasis, viral infection, herpes simplex infection, infection, bone fracture, hyperkinesia, myalgia,
arthralgia, weakness, back pain, conjunctivitis, eye infection, otitis media, otic infection, otalgia, otitis externa,
cough, epistaxis, flu-like symptoms, stridor, nasopharyngitis, respiratory infection, rhinitis, chest pain, sinusitis,
nasal congestion, pharyngitis, fever

Less Common: Adrenocortical insufficiency, aggressive behavior, anxiety, avascular necrosis of femoral head,
bronchitis, bruise, cataract, depression, glaucoma, growth suppression, headache, hypercorticolism,
hypersensitivity (including angioedema, bronchospasm, urticaria), increased intraocular pressure, irritability,
nervousness, osteoporosis, pain, pharyngitis, psychosis, restlessness, skin irritation (facial), throat irritation,
wheezing (patients with severe milk allergy)

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with: Enhances therapeutic effect of Budesonide: Trastuzumab (neutropenic effect)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Budesonide: Denosumab (serious infections)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Amphotericin B (hypokalemic effect), Deferasirox (GI

Enhances therapeutic effect of Tofacitinib
(immunosuppressive effect)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Budesonide:
Pimecrolimus, Tacrolimus (Topical)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following
drugs:
Leflunomide (hematologic toxicity such as pancytopenia,
agranulocytosis, and/or thrombocytopenia), Loxapine,
Natalizumab (concurrent infection)

Conivaptan (CYP3A4 Inhibitors), Fusidic Acid (Systemic),
Grapefruit Juice, Mifepristone, Stiripentol, Telaprevir

Aldesleukin (antineoplastic effect), BCG (Intravesical),
Hyaluronidase, Vaccines (Inactivated) [complete all age_x0002_appropriate vaccinations at least 2 weeks prior to
starting budesonide; if vaccinated during budesonide
therapy, revaccinate at least 3 months after
discontinuation]

Mechanism of Action

A corticosteroid with potent glucocorticoid activity and weak mineralocorticoid activity. It controls the rate of
protein synthesis, depresses the migration of polymorphonuclear leukocytes, fibroblasts, and reverses capillary
permeability and lysosomal stabilization at the cellular level to prevent or control inflammation.

BUDESONIDE (OTHER DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY
DISEASES, INHALANTS)

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

R03BA02

ANATOMICAL

Respiratory System

THERAPEUTIC

Drugs for Obstructive Airway Disease

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Other Drugs for Obstructive Airway Diseases, Inhalants

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

RESPIR › Inhalation: DPI: 100 micrograms/dose x 200 doses with appropriate accompanying dispenser (Rx)

RESPIR › Inhalation: DPI: 200 micrograms/dose x 100 doses with appropriate accompanying dispenser (Rx)

RESPIR › Inhalation:DPI: 200 micrograms/dose x 200 doses with appropriate accompanying dispenser (Rx)

RESPIR › Respiratory solution for nebulization: 250 micrograms/mL, 2 mL (unit dose) (Rx)

Dosage

RESPIR › Inhalation: DPI: 100 micrograms/dose x 200 doses with appropriate accompanying dispenser (Rx)

NOTE: A low-dose inhaled corticosteroid (in addition to an as-needed short acting beta 2agonist) is the initial
preferred long-term control medication for children, adolescents, and adult patients with persistent asthma who
are candidates for treatment according to a step_x0002_wise treatment approach Asthma, by inhalation, ADULT,
initially 360 micrograms twice daily, some patients may be initiated at 180 micrograms twice daily (maximum, 720
micrograms twice daily), may increase dose after 1–2 weeks of therapy in patients who are not adequately
controlled; CHILD ≥6 years, initially 180 micrograms twice daily, some patients may be initiated at 360 micrograms
twice daily (maximum: 360 micrograms twice daily), may increase dose after 1–2 weeks of therapy in patients who
are not adequately controlled; by nebulization, CHILD 12 months – 8 years, initially 0.25 mg daily; If previously on
bronchodilators alone, 0.5 mg daily administered as a single dose or divided twice daily (maximum daily dose, 0.5
mg); If previously on inhaled corticosteroids, 0.5 mg daily administered as a single dose or divided twice daily
(maximum daily dose, 1 mg); If previously on oral corticosteroids, 1 mg/day administered as a single dose or divided
twice daily (maximum daily dose, 1 mg). NOTE: Titrate to lowest effective dose once patient is stable. Conversion
from oral systemic to orally inhaled corticosteroid. Initiation of oral inhalation therapy should begin in patients
whose asthma is reasonably stabilized on oral corticosteroids (OCS). A gradual dose reduction of OCS should begin
~7 to 10 days after starting inhaled therapy.

Page 1 of 2



April 24, 2026, 10:48:56 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

RESPIR › Inhalation: DPI: 100 micrograms/dose x 200 doses with appropriate accompanying dispenser (Rx)

For dry powder inhalers (DPI): Hold inhaler in upright position (mouthpiece up) to load dose. Do NOT shake prior to
use. Unit should be primed prior to first use only. It will not need to be primed again, even if not used for a long
time. Place mouthpiece between lips and inhale forcefully and deeply. Do NOT exhale through inhaler. Do NOT use
a spacer. Dose indicator does NOT move with every dose, usually only after 5 doses. Discard when dose indicator
reads “0”. Rinse mouth with water after each use to reduce incidence of candidiasis. For respiratory solutions for
nebulization: Shake well before use. Use with jet nebulizer connected to an air compressor. Administer with
mouthpiece or facemask. Do NOT use ultrasonic nebulizer. Do NOT mix with other medications in the nebulizer.
Rinse mouth following treatments to decrease risk of oral candidiasis. Wash face if face mask was used. See
Budesonide under Nasal Preparations –Decongestants and other nasal preparations for topical use in 12:
Respiratory System for other information.

Indications

Maintenance and prophylactic treatment of asthma; includes patients who require oral corticosteroids and those
who may benefit from systemic dose reduction or elimination

Contraindications

Primary treatment of status asthmaticus and acute episodes of asthma

NOTE: NOT for the relief of acute bronchospasm.

Mechanism of Action

A corticosteroid with potent glucocorticoid activity and weak mineralocorticoid activity. It controls the rate of
protein synthesis, depresses the migration of polymorphonuclear leukocytes, fibroblasts, and reverses capillary
permeability and lysosomal stabilization at the cellular level to prevent or control inflammation.

appropriate accompanying dispenser (Rx)

RESPIR › DPI: 160 micrograms budesonide + 4.5 micrograms formoterol (as fumarate dihydrate) x 60 doses with

accompanying dispenser (Rx)

120 doses with appropriate accompanying dispenser (Rx)

RESPIR › MDI: 160 micrograms budesonide + 4.5 micrograms formoterol (as fumarate dihydrate) x 120 doses with
appropriate accompanying dispenser (Rx)

Dosage

RESPIR › DPI: 80 micrograms budesonide + 4.5 micrograms formoterol (as fumarate dihydrate) x 60 doses with appropriate
accompanying dispenser (Rx)

NOTE: Recommended starting dose combination is determined according to asthma severity. Consider higher
dose combination if not adequately controlled on the lower combination dose following 1–2 weeks of therapy.
Asthma, by aerosol inhalation, ADULT, 2 inhalations (80/4.5mcg or 160/4.5mcg) twice daily (maximum, 4 inhalations
daily). COPD, by aerosol inhalation, ADULT, 2 inhalations (160mcg/4.5mcg) twice daily (maximum, 4 inhalations daily).

Page 1 of 5



April 24, 2026, 11:05:53 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

RESPIR › DPI: 80 micrograms budesonide + 4.5 micrograms formoterol (as fumarate dihydrate) x 60 doses with appropriate
accompanying dispenser (Rx)

Prior to first use, “load” the inhaler by turning the grip on the inhaler as far as it will move in 1 direction, then turning
in the opposite direction as far as it will go. The inhaler is “loaded” with a dose, indicated by a “click”. Loading
procedure should be done twice. It does not need to be repeated with subsequent uses even when not used
regularly. Place mouthpiece gently between teeth, closing lips around inhaler. Inhale deeply and hold breath for 5–
10 seconds. The amount of drug delivered is small, and the individual will not sense the medication as it is inhaled.
Remove mouthpiece prior to exhalation. Do NOT breathe out through the mouthpiece. After use of the inhaler,
rinse mouth or oropharynx with water and spit out rinse solution. Withdrawal and discontinuation of a
corticosteroid should be done slowly and carefully. Use with caution when transferring from systemic to inhaled
corticosteroids.

Indications

Treatment of asthma in patients ≥12 years of age where combination therapy is indicated; maintenance treatment of
airflow obstruction associated with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), including chronic bronchitis and
emphysema

Contraindications

As primary treatment of status asthmaticus, acute episodes of asthma or COPD requiring intensive measures, and
potentially worsening or rapidly deteriorating asthma or COPD.

Precautions

WARNING: Long-acting beta2-adrenergic agonists (LABAs) increase the risk of asthma-related death and
asthma_x0002_related hospitalization in pediatric and adolescent patients. Use only in patients not adequately
controlled on a long-term asthma control medication or if disease severity clearly warrants treatment with both an
inhaled corticosteroid and a LABA. Once asthma control is achieved and maintained, assess the patient at regular
intervals and step-down therapy if possible without loss of asthma control, and maintain the patient on a
long_x0002_term asthma control medication.

Adrenal suppression; diabetes; Bone density; osteoporosis;
Bronchospasm; asthma (not for acute bronchospasm);
COPD (not use for acute episodes of COPD); Hypersensitivity reactions; immunosuppression (restrict use in active
TB); lower respiratory infections (monitor patients closely); oral candidiasis; Serious effects or fatalities; Vasculitis;
cardiovascular disease; Renal impairment; hepatic impairment (monitor closely due to hepatic metabolism of both
agents); Hypokalemia; Ocular disease (consider routine eye exams in chronic users); Seizure disorders; Thyroid
disease (changes in thyroid status may necessitate dose adjustments;metabolic clearance of corticosteroids
increases in hyperthyroid patients and decreases in hypothyroid ones); Children (titrate to lowest effective dose to
minimize the systemic effects; growth should be routinely monitored); Pregnancy and lactation (refer to individual
agents).

Page 2 of 5



April 24, 2026, 11:05:53 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Headache, dizziness, abdominal distress, oral candidiasis, vomiting, influenza, back pain, upper and lower
respiratory tract infection, pharyngolaryngeal pain, sinusitis, bronchitis, nasal congestion

Less Common: Agitation, anaphylaxis, angina pectoris, angioedema, anxiety, atrial arrhythmia, behavioral changes,
bronchospasm, bruise, cataract, cough, decreased linear skeletal growth rate (children), depression, dermatitis,
extrasystoles, glaucoma, hypercorticoidism signs and symptoms, hyperglycemia, hypersensitivity reaction,
hypertension, hypokalemia, hypotension, immunosuppression, increased intraocular pressure, insomnia, muscle
cramps, nausea, nervousness, palpitations, pruritus, restlessness, skin rash, tachycardia, throat irritation, tremor,
urticaria, ventricular arrhythmia, voice disorder

Page 3 of 5



April 24, 2026, 11:05:53 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

drugs:

Drug Classification

ANATOMICAL

Cardiovascular System

THERAPEUTIC

Diuretics

High-Ceiling Diuretics

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › 1 mg tablet (Rx)

IM › IV › 500 micrograms/mL, 4 mL ampule (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › 1 mg tablet (Rx)

Heart failure, by mouth, ADULT, 0.5–2 mg 1–2 times daily; may repeat in 4–5 hours for up to 2 doses (maximum dose,
10 mg daily); by IM or IV injection, 0.5–1 mg per dose; may repeat in 2–3 hours for up to 2 doses (maximum dose, 10
mg daily). Edema, by mouth, ADULT, 0.5–2 mg 1–2 times daily; may repeat in 4–5 hours for up to 2 doses (maximum
dose, 10 mg daily); by IM or IV injection, 0.5–1 mg per dose; may repeat in 2–3 hours for up to 2 doses (maximum
dose, 10 mg daily; by mouth, IM or IV injection, CHILD and INFANT, 0.015–0.1 mg/kg per dose every 6–24 hours
(maximum dose, 10 mg daily). NOTE: Adjust doses based on individual patient’s needs. Dose equivalency for
patients with normal renal function (approximate): Furosemide 40 mg = Bumetanide 1 mg = Torsemide 20 mg =
Ethacrynic Acid 50 mg

Administration

ORAL › 1 mg tablet (Rx)

For oral administration, take without food. Administering with food slows the rate of absorption and reduces the
extent of absorption and may reduce diuretic efficacy. May require increased intake of potassium-rich foods.
Administer an alternate-day schedule or a 3–4 daily dosing regimen with rest periods of 1–2 days in between. For IV
administration, administer slowly, over 1–2 minutes.

Page 1 of 4



April 24, 2026, 11:15:11 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Indications

Management of congestive heart failure; management of edema secondary to heart failure or hepatic or renal
disease, including nephrotic syndrome

Contraindications

Anuria; hepatic coma or states of severe electrolyte depletion until the condition improves or is corrected

Precautions

WARNING: Bumetanide is a potent diuretic. If given in excess, can lead to a profound diuresis with water and
electrolyte depletion. Careful medical supervision is required.

Fluid or electrolyte loss (can lower serum calcium concentrations; can predispose a patient to serious cardiac
arrhythmias),
Hyperuricemia; Nephrotoxicity (monitor fluid status and renal function to prevent oliguria, azotemia, and reversible
increases in BUN and creatinine),
Ototoxicity (may occur with rapid IV administration, renal impairment, excessive doses, and concurrent use of other
ototoxins),
Sulfonamide allergy (may stimulate T-cell-mediated type IV reactions),
Cirrhosis and ascites (sudden changes in fluid and electrolyte balance and acid / base status may lead to hepatic
encephalopathy; increased risk of precipitating hepatic coma),
Renal impairment (may exhibit diminished effects; increased risk of adverse effects).
Diuretic resistance (can be overcome by IV administration, use of two diuretics together, or the use of a diuretic with
a positive inotropic agent; monitor serum electrolytes very closely).
Surgical patients (may render the patient volume depleted and blood pressure may be labile during general
anesthesia if given the morning of surgery),
Elderly (excess amounts can lead to profound diuresis with fluid and electrolyte loss; severe loss of sodium and/or
increase in BUN can cause confusion); neonates (potent displacer of bilirubin in critically ill patients),
Pregnancy (adverse events have been observed in animal reproduction studies); lactation (not known if excreted in
breastmilk; can decrease milk volume and suppress lactation; breastfeeding is not recommended),

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Dizziness, hyperuricemia, hypochloremia, hypokalemia, hyponatremia, hyperglycemia, phosphorus change,
variations in bicarbonate and CO2 content, abnormal serum calcium, abnormal lactate dehydrogenase, azotemia,
muscle cramps, increased serum creatinine
Less Common: Abdominal pain, arthritic pain, asterixis, auditory impairment, brain disease (preexisting liver
disease), chest pain, dehydration, diaphoresis, diarrhea, dyspepsia, ECG changes, erectile dysfunction, fatigue,
glycosuria, headache, hyperventilation, hypotension, musculoskeletal pain, nausea, nipple tenderness, orthostatic
hypotension, otalgia, ototoxicity, premature ejaculation, proteinuria, pruritus, renal failure, skin rash, Stevens-
Johnson syndrome, thrombocytopenia, toxic epidermal necrolysis, urticaria, vertigo, vomiting, weakness,
xerostomia

Mechanism of Action

A loop diuretic that inhibits reabsorption of sodium and chloride in the ascending loop of Henle and proximal renal
tubule, interfering with the chloride-binding cotransport system, thus causing increased excretion of water, sodium,
chloride, magnesium, phosphate, and calcium.

April 24, 2026, 11:15:17 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dosage

P-NEURAL › 0.5% (as hydrochloride), 5 mL vial (Rx)

NOTE: Dose varies with anesthetic procedure, area to be anesthetized, vascularity of tissues, number of neuronal
segments to be blocked, depth of anesthesia, degree of muscle relaxation required, duration of anesthesia
desired, individual tolerance, and the physical condition of the patient. Administer the smallest dose and
concentration required to produce the desired result. Local anesthesia local infiltration, by SC or IM injection,
ADULT, 0.25% up to a maximum of 175 mg; perform aspiration prior to each injection, however, absence of blood in
the syringe does not guarantee that IV injection has been avoided. Caudal block, by injection into the caudal end of
the spinal canal, ADULT, 15–30 mL of 0.25% or 0.5%. Epidural block other than caudal block, by epidural injection,
ADULT, 10–20 mL of 0.25% or 0.5% in 3–5 mL increments, allowing sufficient time to detect toxic manifestations of
inadvertent IV or intrathecal administration; or 10–20 mL of 0.5% solution, up to 1%; Non-obstetric surgery, by
epidural injection, ADULT, 10–20 mL of 0.75% solution up to 1.5% as a single dose. Surgical procedures requiring a
high degree of muscle relaxation and prolonged effects only, except obstetrical cases, by epidural injection,
ADULT, 10–20 mL of 0.75%. Peripheral nerve block, by injection onto or near the nerve, ADULT, 5 mL of 0.25% or
0.5% (maximum total daily dose, 400 mg). Sympathetic nerve block, by injection onto or near the nerve, ADULT, 20–
50 mL of 0.25%. Retrobulbar anesthesia, by injection into retrobulbar space, ADULT, 2–4 mL of 0.75%. Spinal
anesthesia, by intrathecal injection, ADULT, preservative-free solution of 0.75% in 8.25% dextrose; for lower
extremity and perineal procedures, 1 mL; for lower abdominal procedures, 1.6 mL; for normal vaginal delivery, 0.8
mL, higher doses may be required in some patients; for Caesarean delivery, 1-–1.4 mL.

Administration

P-NEURAL › 0.5% (as hydrochloride), 5 mL vial (Rx)

Do NOT use solutions containing preservatives for epidural or caudal blocks. Avoid rapid injection of large volume.
Use fractional (incremental) doses when feasible. Use an infusion pump to slowly administer local anesthetics to
or around surgical wound sites and/or near peripheral nerves for postoperative analgesia. Avoid IV injections.
Perform aspiration prior to administration. Re-position the needle until no return of blood can be elicited by
aspiration. However, absence of blood in the syringe does NOT guarantee that IV injection has been avoided.
Intravenous regional anesthesia (Bier block) is not recommended; Mixing or the prior or intercurrent use with
other local anesthetic is not recommended due to insufficient data on the clinical use of such mixtures. NOTE: A
test dose is recommended prior to epidural administration (prior to initial dose) and all reinforcing doses with
continuous catheter technique. Duration of anesthetic effect may be prolonged by the addition of epinephrine.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric, Acutely ill Patients, and Debilitated Patients:
Use with caution. Dose reduction may be required.

Hepatic Impairment and/or Cardiac Disease:
Reduce doses.

Indications

Infiltration of local anesthesia, caudal block (preservative-free), epidural block (preservative-free), peripheral nerve
block, sympathetic nerve block, retrobulbar anesthesia, spinal anesthesia.

Page 3 of 5



April 24, 2026, 11:15:17 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Obstetrical paracervical block anesthesia; use as IV regional anesthesia (Bier block).

Precautions

WARNING: Should be administered by clinicians welltrained in the diagnosis and management of emergencies that
may arise from the use of these agents. Resuscitative equipment, oxygen, and other resuscitative drugs should be
available for immediate use.

The 0.75% concentration is NOT for use as obstetrical anesthesia. Its use Cardiac arrest with difficult resuscitation
or death has been reported with its use for epidural anesthesia in obstetrical patients. Cardiac arrest has occurred
after convulsions resulting from systemic toxicity, presumably following unintentional IV injection.

Reserve use of 0.75% concentration for surgical procedures where a high degree of muscle relaxation and
prolonged effect are necessary.

Cardiac arrest and death have occurred with IV regional anesthesia administration (Bier block).

Delay in proper management of dose-related toxicity, underventilation from any cause and/or altered sensitivity may
lead to the development of acidosis, cardiac arrest, and possibly death.

Cardiovascular effects; Cardiovascular disease;

Cross-sensitivity (cross-sensitivity with amide type local anesthetics have been reported);

Chondrolysis (primarily of the shoulder joint, has occurred following continuous intra-articular infusion; destruction
of articular cartilage has occurred when infused directly into the shoulder);

Seizures (convulsions due to systemic toxicity leading to cardiac arrest have been reported);

Respiratory arrest (have been associated with rare occurrences of sudden respiratory arrest, especially when
administered near the head or neck);

Hepatic impairment; Renal impairment (substantially excreted by the kidney; increased risk of toxic reactions);

Sulfite allergy (may contain sodium metabisulfite; use with caution);

Elderly (patients >65 years may be at an increased risk for developing hypotension while undergoing anesthesia with
bupivacaine);

Children (insufficient data available on the use in patients <12 years; use with caution);

Pregnancy (no adequate and well-controlled studies);

Lactation (excreted in breastmilk).

Page 4 of 5



April 24, 2026, 11:15:17 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Anxiety, anaphylactoid reaction, apnea, angioedema, blurred vision, bradycardia, cardiac arrest, chills,
chondrolysis, CNS excitation and/or depression, constriction of pupils, decreased cardiac output, depression of
myocardium, dizziness, drowsiness merging into unconsciousness, heart block, hypotension, hypoventilation,
miosis, nausea, palpitations, pruritus, respiratory arrest, restlessness, tinnitus, tremors, urticaria, ventricular
arrhythmia, vomiting, weakness.

Less Common: Backache, convulsions, cranial nerve palsy, fecal incontinence, headache, loss of anal sphincter
control, loss of perineal sensation, paralysis of lower extremities, meningismus, paresthesia, persistent anesthesia,
seizure, septic meningitis, sexual disorder (loss of function), slowing of labor, urinary incontinence, urinary retention.

Rare: Erythema, angioneurotic edema, tachycardia, sneezing, syncope, excessive sweating, elevated temperature,
anaphylactoid-like symptoms.

NOTE: Incidence of adverse reactions is difficult to define. Most effects are dose-related and are often due to
accelerated absorption from the injection site, unintentional IV injection, or slow metabolic degradation. The
development of any CNS symptoms may be an early indication of more significant toxicity (seizure).

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Bupivacaine: Lidocaine, Mexiletine (additive systemic effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Antiarrhythmics (myocardial depression)
Reduces diagnostic effect of Technetium Tc 99m Tilmanocept

Avoid concomitant use with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Neuromuscular-blocking Agents (neuromuscular-blocking effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Bupivacaine: Hyaluronidase
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Blood pressure-lowering agents; Hypotensive
agents (hypotension); Bupivacaine, Liposomal [administer at least 20 minutes or more apart]; Ergot Alkaloids,
Vasopressor drugs (severe, persistent hypertension or cerebrovascular accidents)

Mechanism of Action

Bupivacaine blocks the generation and the conduction of nerve impulses, by binding to the intracellular portion of
sodium channels and blocks sodium influx into nerve cells. This leads to lose of sensation for pain, temperature,
touch and proprioception, and eventually skeletal muscle tone.

Butorphanol causes inhibition of the ascending pain pathways, altering the perception of and response to pain,
producing analgesia, respiratory depression, and sedation similar to opioids.

Page 1 of 2



April 24, 2026, 11:44:00 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Known or suspected disorder of calcium metabolism; erythrodermic, exfoliative and pustular psoriasis; viral lesions
of the skin, fungal or bacterial skin infections, parasitic infections, skin manifestations in relation to TB, perioral
dermatitis, atrophic skin, striae atrophicae, fragility of skin veins, ichthyosis, acne vulgaris, rosacea, ulcers and
wounds

Precautions

Diabetes mellitus (use with caution); Lactation.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common and Less Common: Allergic contact dermatitis, pruritus, headache, nasopharyngitis, psoriasis, rash,
influenza, erythema, pain, burning sensation, eye irritation, dry skin, skin atrophy, folliculitis, reversible hypothalamic-
pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis suppression, manifestations of Cushing’s syndrome, hyperglycemia, glucosuria
Rare: Hypercalcemia, hypercalciuria, angioedema, facial edema

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Steroids

Mechanism of Action

Calcipotriol is a synthetic vitamin D3 derivative that binds to vitamin D receptors to induce differentiation and
suppress proliferation of keratocytes. Betamethasone is a topical corticosteroid with anti-inflammatory, antipruritic,
and vasoconstrictive properties. It depresses the formation, release, and activity of endogenous chemical mediators
of inflammation through the induction of phospholipase A2 inhibitory proteins (lipocortins) and subsequent
inhibition of the release of arachidonic acid.

Management of Hypocalcemia due to secondary hyperparathyroidism, and resultant metabolic bone disease;

Contraindications

Accidental exposure; arteriosclerosis; breastfeeding; cardiac disease; dehydration; geriatric; hypercalcemia;
hyperphosphatemia; hypervitaminosis D; occlusive dressing; ocular exposure; pregnancy; renal failure; sarcoidosis;
sunlight (UV) exposure

Precautions

Renal impairment; Hepatic and biliary disease; Excessive doses; Immobilized patients.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Hypervitaminosis D, hypercalcemia, fatigue, weakness, headache, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain,

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Decreases intestinal absorption of Calcitriol: Cholestyramine, Colestipol, Mineral Oil, Orlistat
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Calcium-containing Antacids (hypercalcemia);

and toxicity); Vitamin D Analogues (additive effects and toxicity)

Mechanism of Action

Also known as 1,25dihydroxy-cholecalciferol, it is the active form of vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol). Calcitriol is
structurally similar to calcifediol and plays an important role in maintaining calcium balance and in the regulation of
parathyroid hormone (PTH). It is the preferred form for the management of hypocalcemia in dialysis-dependent renal
failure patients.

Indications

Adjunct inosteoporosis; therapeutic supplementation (e.g., lactose intolerance or with milk allergy; established
vitamin D dependent osteomalacia; post-menopausal women; chronic kidney disease with or without renal
replacement therapy); hyperphosphatemia.

Contraindications

Hypercalcemia; hyperparathyroidism; renal calculi; hypophosphatemia; vitamin D toxicity; abnormal sensitivity to the
effects of vitamin D; malabsorption syndrome; renal failure or severe renal impairment; known hypersensitivity to
the preparation or any of the excipients (e.g. soya oil).

Precautions

Should not be used in osteoporosis due to prolonged immobilization, renal stones, or severe hypercalciuria;
Mild to moderate renal failure or mild hypercalciuria (supervise carefully, including periodic checks of plasma calcium
levels and urinary calcium excretion);
History of renal stones;
Patients receiving treatment for cardiovascular diseases;
Rare hereditary problems of glucose-galactose malabsorption or fructose intolerance;
Achlorhydria;
Hypercalcemia and hypercalciuria.

NOTE: Allowances should be made for calcium and vitamin D supplements from other sources.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Less Common: Hypercalcemia, hypercalciuria
Rare: Abdominal pain, acid rebound, anorexia, arrhythmia, bone or muscle pain, constipation or diarrhea, headache,
hypophosphatemia, flatulence, loss of appetite, metallic taste, milk-alkali syndrome, nausea, pruritus, rash, urticaria,
vomiting, xerostomia

FOOD INTERACTION:
Foods that are rich in oxalic acid (e.g., spinach) and phytic acid (e.g., whole cereals) may reduce calcium absorption
due to formation of insoluble calcium salts. Do NOT take calcium products within 2 hours of eating such foods.

Mechanism of Action

A two-component dietary supplement of calcium and fat-soluble vitamin D, which serves to increase the calcium
pool available for GI absorption and assimilation into bones.

Administration

IM / IV › 10 mg/mL, 5 mL ampule/vial (Rx)

Administer by IV or IM route as a rescue after folate antagonists. Administer by IM injection, IV push, or by IV
infusion for 15 minutes to 2 hours. Do NOT administer at a rate >160 mg/minute due to calcium content. Do NOT
administer intrathecally. Do NOT administer IV solutions at a rate >160 mg/minute due to calcium content. NOTE:
Do NOT administer concurrently with methotrexate. It is commonly initiated 24 hours after the start of
methotrexate. Toxicity to normal tissues may be irreversible if leucovorin is not initiated by ~40 hours after the
start of methotrexate. Fluorouracil is usually given after, or at the midpoint, of leucovorin infusion by IV bolus
injection or short IV infusion over 10–120 minutes. Other administration schedules have been used; refer to
individual protocols. See Calcium Folinate (Leucovorin Calcium) under Antineoplastic Agents – Adjuncts to
Antineoplastic Chemotherapy in Chapter 8: Antineoplastic and Immunomodulating Agents for other information.

Indications

Rescue agent after high-dose methotrexate treatment; to diminish toxicity and counteract the effects of impaired
methotrexate elimination and of inadvertent overdosage of folic acid antagonists; adjunct cofactor therapy in
methanol toxicity; antidote for formaldehyde and methyl alcohol poisoning

Contraindications

Pernicious anemia and other megaloblastic anemias secondary to vitamin B12 deficiency

Mechanism of Action

A reduced form of folic acid that supplies the necessary cofactor blocked by methotrexate. It actively competes with
methotrexate for transport sites, displaces methotrexate from intracellular binding sites, and restores active folate
stores required for DNA/RNA synthesis. It aids in the elimination of formic acid by serving as a source of
tetrahydrofolate.

Administration

IV › 10%, 10 mL ampule/vial (Rx)

For IV use only. No preservative added. Discard unused portion. Inspect visually for particulate matter and
discoloration. Use only if solution is clear and seal intact. For direct IV administration, administer undiluted at a
rate of 0.5 mL or a fraction thereof over 1 minute. Do NOT exceed 2 mL/minute. For intermittent or continuous IV
administration, administer diluted in 1,000 mL of NS slowly. Do NOT exceed 200 mg/minute, through a small-bore
needle into a large vein to avoid possibility of extravasation and resultant necrosis. Do NOT administer by SC or IM
injection as this may cause severe necrosis and sloughing. IM injection may cause local necrosis and abscess
formation. NOTE: Injection should be stopped if patient complains of any discomfort. Patient should be advised to
remain in bed for at least 15–30 minutes following injection, depending on response. Avoid scalp veins in children.
Avoid rapid infusion. High concentrations of calcium suddenly reaching the heart can cause fatal cardiac arrest.

Indications

Treatment of conditions arising from calcium deficiencies such as hypocalcemic tetany, hypocalcemia related to
hypoparathyroidism, and hypocalcemia due to rapid growth or pregnancy; to antagonize cardiac toxicity in patients
not receiving digitalis.

Contraindications

Ventricular fibrillation; metastatic bone disease; injection into myocardium; renal calculi; hypercalcemia and
predisposition to hypercalcemia (e.g., hyperparathyroidism, certain malignancies); above normal serum calcium
levels; concomitant administration of ceftriaxone in neonates.

Precautions

WARNING: May cause asystole when given by rapid IV administration.

Cardiovascular effects (monitor cardiac rate); Concomitant digitalis therapy; Sarcoidosis or renal or cardiac disease
(use with caution); history of lithiasis; renal impairment; Immobilized patients (use with caution); Premature
neonates; Lactation (use with caution).

NOTE: Supersaturated solutions are prone to precipitation. If crystallization has occurred, warm in a 60°C water bath
for 15–30 minutes with occasional shaking. Cool to body temperature before use.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Tingling sensation, sensations of heat waves (peripheral vasodilation), fainting, constipation, chalky taste,
increased gastric acid secretion, hypotension, bradycardia, cardiac arrhythmias, cardiac arrest, pain and burning at IV
site, severe venous thrombosis, extravasation (with necrosis and sloughing)

Page 2 of 3



April 24, 2026, 11:45:48 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Decreases absorption of the following drugs:
Magnesium, Quinolone

Avoid concomitant use with:
Decreases therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Tetracycline (forms Tetracycline-Calcium complex), Calcium Channel Blockers, e.g., Verapamil

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
Ceftriaxone Sodium Injection (may form precipitates), Digitalis Glycosides e.g digoxin (arrhythmias)

Mechanism of Action

The calcium salt of gluconic acid, produced as an oxidation product of glucose. Calcium is an essential element for
regulating the excitation threshold of nerves and muscles, for blood clotting mechanisms, cardiac function (rhythm,
tonicity, contractility), maintenance of renal function, for body skeleton and teeth, as well as in regulating storage
and release of neurotransmitters and hormones, regulating amino acid uptake and absorption of vitamin B12, gastrin
secretion, and in maintaining structural and functional integrity of cell membranes and capillaries. Calcium gluconate
increases cardiac muscle tone and force of systolic contractions (positive inotropic effect).
