tissue necrosis. Following IV injection, the patient should remain recumbent for a short time. Visually inspect
parenteral products for particulate matter and discoloration prior to administration. Close monitoring of serum
calcium concentrations is essential during IV administration of calcium. Oral administration of calcium
supplements or calciumrich foods should replace IV calcium therapy as soon as possible. NOTE: Do NOT administer
by IM or SC route. Closely monitor serum calcium concentrations during IV administration of calcium. Oral
administration of calcium supplements or calcium-rich foods should replace IV calcium therapy as soon as
possible. See General Information on Calcium listed under "Calcium" for other information.

Dose Adjustment

Renal Impairment:
For CrCl 30–50 mL/minute, administer 75% of dose.
For CrCl <30 mL/minute, use is contraindicated.

Obesity:
Use actual body weight (full weight) for calculation of BSAbased or weight-based dosing, especially if the intent of
therapy is curative.
If dose reduction is done due to toxicity, consider resumption of full weight-based dosing with subsequent cycles,
especially if cause of toxicity is resolved.

North American descent:
Initiate at 1,000 mg/m2 twice daily for 2 weeks, every 21 days. Increased incidence of capecitabine toxicities,
particularly hand-and-foot syndrome.

Dihydropyrimidine Dehydrodeganase (DPD) Mutations:
May require therapy interruption or permanent discontinuation. Partial, total, or near total absence of DPD are at an
increased risk for acute early-onset toxicity (potentially severe, life-threatening, or fatal), which may include
mucositis or stomatitis, diarrhea, neutropenia, and neurotoxicity.

Toxicity:
Monitor closely for toxicity and adjust dose as needed.
Doses reduced should not be increased later on.
For combination therapy, monitor and adjust doses for docetaxel as well.
If treatment delay is required for either capecitabine or docetaxel, withhold both agents until appropriate.

Indications

Palliative treatment of metastatic breast cancer in patients resistant to paclitaxel and not candidates for further
anthracycline therapy; (in combination with docetaxel) treatment of metastatic breast cancer in patients that failed
to respond to or have relapsed following anthracycline chemotherapy; adjuvant therapy for patients with stage III
(Dukes’ C) colon cancer after complete resection of the primary tumor; first-line treatment of metastatic colorectal
cancer

Page 2 of 5



April 24, 2026, 11:46:09 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Severe renal impairment (CrCl <30 mL/minute)

Precautions

WARNING: May increase the anticoagulant effects of warfarin. Bleeding events, including death, have occurred with
concomitant use. An increased risk of coagulopathy is correlated with a cancer diagnosis and age >60 years. Monitor
PT and INR frequently and adjust anticoagulant dose accordingly. In patients with colorectal cancer, treatment with
capecitabine immediately following 6 weeks of fluorouracil/leucovorin (FU/LV) therapy has been associated with an
increased incidence of grade =3 toxicity, when compared to patients receiving the reverse sequence, capecitabine
(two 3week courses) followed by FU/LV.

WARNING: Hazardous agent; use appropriate precautions for handling and disposal.

Bone marrow suppression (use with caution, dose reduction may be required; not recommended in patients with
baseline platelets <100,000/mm3 and/or neutrophils <1,500/mm3)
Cardiotoxicity (e.g. myocardial infarction, ischemia, angina, dysrhythmias, cardiac arrest, cardiac failure, sudden
death, cardiomyopathy, ECG changes)
Dermatologic toxicity (e.g. SJS, TEN); (permanently discontinue use if severe dermatologic or mucocutaneous
reaction occurs).
Gastrointestinal toxicity (NEC, severe prolonged diarrhea, dehydration); (antidiarrheal therapy, e.g. loperamide is
recommended; adequately hydrate prior to initiation of treatment)
Hand-and-foot syndrome (numbness, dysesthesia or paresthesia, tingling, painless or painful swelling, erythema,
desquamation, blistering, and severe pain may occur).
Hepatotoxicity (grade 3 and 4 hyperbilirubinemia, transaminase, alkaline phosphatase elevations may occur; use with
caution in mild-to-moderate hepatic impairment due to liver metastases).
Renal toxicity (dehydration may occur and result in possibly fatal acute renal failure; use with caution in mild-
tomoderate renal impairment).
Elderly (increased risk for adverse events; may experience a greater incidence of grade 3 or 4 adverse events, e.g.,
diarrhea, hand-and-foot syndrome, nausea, vomiting; at higher risk for dehydration).

Administration

OPHTHALM › 0.01% intraocular solution, 1.5 mL vial (Rx)

For single-dose intraocular use only. Use only if container is undamaged. Discard unused portion. Aseptically
remove the sterile vial from the blister package by peeling the backing paper and dropping the vial onto a sterile
tray. Withdraw the contents into a dry sterile syringe and replace the needle with an atraumatic cannula. Gently
instill no more than ½ milliliter into the anterior chamber to produce satisfactory miosis. It may be instilled before
or after securing sutures. Miosis is usually maximal within 2–5 minutes.

Dose Adjustment

No information found

Indications

For obtaining miosis during surgery; for reduction of the intensity of intraocular pressure elevation in the first 24
hours following cataract surgery.

A sterile balanced salt solution of carbachol for intraocular injection. It is a potent cholinergic (parasympathomimetic)

Drug Classification

Nervous System

ORAL › 400 mg MR tablet (Rx)

ORAL › 100 mg/5 mL syrup, 120 mL (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › 200 mg tablet (Rx)

NOTE: MR tablets are designed to be taken twice daily due to the controlled release of the active substance.
Epilepsy, by mouth, ADULT, initially 100–200 mg once or twice daily, slowly raise until an optimal response is
obtained (generally at 400 mg twice or thrice daily); ADOLESCENT >12 years, 5–10 mg/kg daily divided every 12
hours then increase gradually to maintenance of 10– 30 mg/kg daily divided every 8 or 6 hours (maximum, 1 g daily
for 12–15 years old and 1.2 g daily if >16 years old); CHILD 6–12 years, initially 10 mg/kg daily in 2–4 divided doses,

Page 1 of 2



April 24, 2026, 11:47:47 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

ORAL › 200 mg tablet (Rx)

May be taken during, after, or between meals. For the syrup formulation, shake well before use. Administer tablets
with little liquid. Swallow MR tablets unchewed with a little liquid. For existing anticonvulsant therapy, add
carbamazepine gradually while existing anticonvulsant is maintained or gradually decreased, except with
phenytoin, which may need to be increased or decreased depending on phenytoin serum levels. See
Carbamazepine under Analgesics – Other Analgesics and Antipyretics: Drugs for Neuropathic Pain in Nervous
System for other information.

Indications

Management of epilepsy; complex or simple partial seizures with or without secondary generalization; generalized
tonic-clonic seizures; mixed forms of seizures.

NOTE: NOT effective in absence seizures (petit mal) and myoclonic seizures.

Contraindications

Atrioventricular block; bone marrow depression; acute intermittent porphyria; combination with MAO Inhibitors e.g.,
Phenezline, Selegeline.

Mechanism of Action

Carbamazepine is an iminostilbene that limits the firing of action potentials evoked by a sustained depolarization in
cortical neurons. It acts by limiting influx of sodium ions or by slowing the rate of recovery of Na+ channels.

Dosage

ORAL › 200 mg tablet (Rx)

NOTE: MR tablets are designed to be taken twice daily due to the controlled release of the active substance.
Idiopathic trigeminal neuralgia, by mouth, ADULT, initially 100 mg 2 times daily, slowly raise the dosage until
freedom from pain is achieved (normally at 200 mg 3–4 times daily), then gradually reduce dose to the lowest
possible maintenance level; usual maintenance dose is 400–800 mg daily in divided doses (maximum, 1.2 g daily).
Painful diabetic neuropathy, by mouth, ADULT, initially 100 mg 1–2 times a day, gradually increase depending on
response; average dose is 200 mg 2–4 times daily.

ORAL › 100 mg capsule (Rx)

Postherpetic neuralgia, by mouth, ADULT, Day 1: 300 mg; Day 2: 300 mg twice daily; Day 3: 300 mg 3 times daily;
titrate dose as needed for pain relief, usually ranging from 1,800–3,600 mg daily in divided doses. Neuropathic pain,
by mouth, ADULT, initially, 300 mg on the 1st day, 300 mg twice a day on the 2nd day and 300 mg 3 times a day on the
3rd day; alternatively, 900 mg daily in 3 divided doses; dose may increase in increments of 300 mg every 2–3 days
(maximum 3,600 mg daily).

Administration

ORAL › 200 mg tablet (Rx)

May be taken during, after, or between meals. For the syrup formulation, shake well before use. Administer tablets
with little liquid. Swallow MR tablets unchewed with a little liquid.

ORAL › 100 mg capsule (Rx)

May be taken with or without food. Administer first dose on first day at bedtime to avoid somnolence and
dizziness. When given 3 times daily, the maximum time between doses should not exceed 12 hours. Do NOT
discontinue abruptly because of the possibility of increasing seizure frequency. Withdraw gradually to minimize
the potential of increased seizure frequency, unless safety concerns require a more rapid withdrawal.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Select doses with caution. Generally, lower doses are started.

Renal Impairment:
Use with caution. In moderate to severe impairment, reduce doses.

Hepatic Impairment:
Drug is primarily metabolized in the liver. Use with caution in advanced liver disease as metabolism is impaired.

Indications

Idiopathic trigeminal neuralgia and painful diabetic neuropathy.

Page 2 of 4



April 24, 2026, 11:47:53 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Atrioventricular block; bone marrow depression; acute intermittent porphyria; combination with MAO Inhibitors e.g.
Selegeline

Precautions

WARNING: Neurologic. Must be used with caution in patients with mixed seizures, including absences, which can be
exacerbated by carbamazepine. In case of exacerbation, the drug must be discontinued.

Hematologic effects (Obtain pretreatment complete blood counts with platelet counts at baseline and periodically
thereafter; discontinue if there is significant bone marrow depression); Dermatologic effects (discontinue if signs
and symptoms of severe skin reaction appear); psychosis (may activate latent psychosis); Hepatic effects (evaluate
baseline and periodic hepatic function particularly in those with a history of liver disease; may cause or aggravate
liver disease; withdraw immediately in case of aggravated liver dysfunction or active liver disease); Breakthrough
bleeding (may be seen in women taking oral contraceptives); may affect reliability of oral contraceptives, women of
childbearing age must be advised to use alternative forms of birth control.

Elderly (increased risk for adverse effects, including liver dysfunction, and confusion and agitation); Pregnancy
(increased risk for developmental disorders has been reported; rare reports of developmental disorders, including
spina bifida, has been reported; can aggravate folic acid deficiency so supplementation is recommended before and
during pregnancy; administer Vitamin K1 to the mother during the last weeks of pregnancy and to the newborn to
prevent bleeding disorders in the offspring); lactation (excreted in breastmilk; monitor infants closely for adverse
reactions).

SKILLED TASKS. May impair ability to perform tasks, which require mental alertness, like driving or operating
machinery.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Ataxia, dizziness, drowsiness, fatigue, headache diplopia, blurred vision, allergic reactions, urticaria (may
be severe), leucopenia, thrombocytopenia, eosinophilia, elevated gamma-GT (usually not clinically relevant), elevated
alkaline phosphatase, nausea, vomiting, mouth dryness, edema, increase in weight, hyponatremia, reduced plasma
osmolality.

Less Common: Abnormal involuntary movements, exfoliative dermatitis, erythroderma, diarrhea, constipation.

Rare: Orofacial dyskinesia, oculomotor disorders, slurred speech, peripheral neuritis, paresthesia, muscle
weakness, hallucinations, depression, restlessness, agitation, confusion, loss of appetite, lupuserythematosus like
syndrome, pruritus, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, acne, purpura, leukocytosis, lymphadenopathy, folic acid
deficiency, hypertension or hypotension, delayed multiorgan hypersensitivity, disturbance in cardiac conduction,
agranulocytosis, aplastic anemia, pure red cell aplasia, megaloblastic anemia, pancreatitis, anaphylactic reactions,
arrhythmias, renal or urinary dysfunction.

NOTE: Particularly at the start of treatment, or if the initial dose is too high, or in the elderly, certain adverse
reactions (e.g., CNS and GI symptoms, allergy) commonly occur.

April 24, 2026, 11:47:53 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Decreases bioavailability of Paracetamol
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Diuretics, e.g., Hydrochlorothiazide, Furosemide
(symptomatic hyponatremia) Isoniazid (isoniazid-induced hepatotoxicity) Neuroleptics, e.g., Haloperidol (neurological
adverse reactions)

Avoid concomitant use with:
Decreases serum concentration of Oral Contraceptives
Incompatible with the following products: MAO Inhibitors e.g. Phenelzine
Reduces therapeutic effect of Oral Contraceptives

Mechanism of Action

Carbamazepine is an anti-seizure drug also used for neuropathic pain. It limits the firing of action potentials evoked
by a sustained depolarization in cortical neurons by slowing the rate of recovery of voltage-activated Na+ channels
from inactivation.

Hypersensitivity to carbetocin, oxytocin, or any component of the formulation; administration prior to delivery of

children.

Precautions

May cause antidiuretic effect and bleeding;
Use in caution in patients with asthma, cardiovascular disease, epilepsy, and migraines.

Mechanism of Action

Carbetocin binds oxytocin receptors located in uterine smooth muscle producing rhythmic uterine contractions
characteristic to deliver, as well as increasing both the frequency of existing contractions and uterine tone. It
enhances uterine involution early in postpartum.

Dosage

NOTE: Doses for adults are commonly calculated by the target AUC using the Calvert formula, where: Total dose
(mg) = Target AUC x (GFR + 25) If estimating glomerular filtration rate (GFR) instead of a measured GFR, consider
capping estimated GFR at a maximum of 125 mL/minute to avoid potential toxicity. Ovarian cancer, advanced, by IV
infusion, ADULT, as a single agent, 360 mg/m2 every 4 weeks; in combination with cyclophosphamide 300 mg/m2
every 4 weeks; as a single agent in previously-treated patients, target AUC 4–6. Neuroblastoma, induction and
post induction, CHILD, 500 mg/m2/d for 2 days on 1 cycle. Retinoblastoma, CHILD < 36 months, 18.6 mg/kg/dose
(single dose each cycle); CHILD > 36 months, 560 mg/m2/dose (single dose each cycle). Recurrent soft tissue
sarcoma, CHILD, 150 mg/m2/d as 3h IV infusion for 2 days.

Page 1 of 4



April 24, 2026, 11:48:04 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

IV › 150 mg powder, vial (Rx)

For IV administration. Infuse over 15–60 minutes. Some protocols may require infusions up to 24 hours. Do NOT use
needles or IV administration sets that contain aluminum during preparation or administration. Aluminum an react
with carboplatin resulting in precipitate formation and loss of potency. NOTE: Avoid extravasation. Ensure proper
needle or catheter position prior to and during administration. If extravasation occurs, discontinue infusion
immediately and apply ice to the affected area. Associated with a high emetic potential. Antiemetics are
recommended to prevent nausea and vomiting. When administered as sequential infusions, with taxane
derivaties, administer taxane derivatives before platinum derivatives to limit myelosuppression and to enhance
efficacy.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Calculate dose based on Calvert formula:
Total dose (mg) = Target AUC x (GFR + 25)

Obesity:
Consider dosing based on GFR. GFR should not exceed 125 mL/minute.
Use actual body weight (full weight) for calculation of BSA-based dosing for hematopoietic stem cell transplant
conditioning regimens in adults.

Renal Impairment:
Dose determination with Calvert formula uses GFR and, therefore, inherently adjusts for renal dysfunction.
For baseline CrCl 41–59 mL/minute, initiate at 250 mg/m2 and adjust subsequent doses based on bone marrow
toxicity.
For baseline CrCl 16–40 mL/minute, initiate at 200 mg/m2 and adjust subsequent doses based on bone marrow
toxicity.
In hemodialysis, administer on a non-dialysis day, hemodialysis should occur between 12–24 hours after
administration. Calculate dose as follows:
Carboplatin dose (mg) = Target AUC x 25

Bone Marrow Suppression:
Reduce dose. Delay cycles until WBC and platelet counts have recovered.

Toxicity:
If platelets <50,000 cells/mm3 or ANC <500 cells/mm3, administer 75% of dose.

Indications

Initial treatment of advanced ovarian cancer in combination with other established chemotherapy agents; palliative
treatment of recurrent ovarian cancer after prior chemotherapy. Childhood solid malignancy: neuroblastoma,
retinoblastoma; relapsed Non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma (NHL); recurrent soft tissue sarcomas

Page 2 of 4



April 24, 2026, 11:48:04 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

History of severe allergic reaction to carboplatin, cisplatin, other platinum-containing formulations, mannitol, or any
component of the formulation; severe bone marrow depression; significant bleeding

Precautions

WARNING: Should be administered under the supervision of an experienced cancer chemotherapy physician.
Administer in a facility sufficient to appropriately diagnose and manage complications.

WARNING: Hazardous agent; use appropriate precautions for handling and disposal.

Bone marrow suppression (increased risk with prior myelosuppressive therapy, renal dysfunction); neurotoxicity

Hypersensitivity or anaphylactoid reactions (risk increased in patients previously exposed to platinum therapy;
consider skin testing or desensitization protocols).

Liver function abnormalities (may be caused by high doses of >4 times the recommended dose)

Ototoxicity (may occur when given together with aminoglycosides; long-term audiology monitoring is recommended)

Renal toxicity (limited unless given together with aminoglycosides)

Vision loss (reported with higher than recommended doses; usually reversible weeks after discontinuation)

Elderly (increased incidence of peripheral neuropathy and severe thrombocytopenia; increased incidence of
neurotoxicity; considered inappropriate for elderly use)

Children (children <6 months are at an increased risk of ototoxicity)

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Abdominal pain, alopecia, anemia, pain, bleeding complications, constipation, decreased creatinine
clearance, bone marrow depression (dose related; dose limiting), dysgeusia, hemorrhage, hypersensitivity,
hypocalcemia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hyponatremia, diarrhea, infection, leukopenia, mucositis, nausea,
neurotoxicity, neutropenia, ototoxicity, peripheral neuropathy, stomatitis, thrombocytopenia, visual disturbance,
vomiting, weakness

Less Common: Anaphylaxis, bronchospasm, cardiac failure, cerebrovascular accident, dehydration, anorexia,
embolism, febrile neutropenia, erythema, hemolytic anemia (acute), HUS, hypertension, hypotension, injection site
reaction (pain, redness, swelling), limb ischemia (acute), malaise, metastases, pruritus, skin rash, tissue necrosis
(associated with extravasation), urticaria, vision loss

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Carboplatin: Trastuzumab (neutropenic effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Carboplatin: Denosumab (serious infections), Aminoglycosides [e.g.

Reduces diagnostic effect of Coccidioides immitis Skin Test

A platinum compound alkylating agent, which covalently binds to DNA, interfering with DNA function by producing

CARVEDILOL

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

C07AG02

ANATOMICAL

Cardiovascular System

THERAPEUTIC

Beta-Blocking Agents

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Alpha and Beta Blocking Agents

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › 6.25 mg tablet (Rx)

ORAL › 25 mg tablet (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › 6.25 mg tablet (Rx)

Heart failure, by mouth, ADULT, 3.125 mg twice daily for 2 weeks; if tolerated, may increase to 6.25 mg twice daily;
double the dose every 2 weeks to the highest dose tolerated by patient. Mild-to-moderate heart failure, by mouth,
ADULT >85 kg, maximum dose, 50 mg twice daily; ADULT <85 kg, maximum dose, 25 mg twice daily. Severe heart
failure, by mouth, ADULT, 25 mg twice daily (maximum dose, 80 mg once daily). Left ventricular dysfunction
following MI, by mouth, ADULT, initially 3.125–6.25 mg twice daily; increase dose incrementally at intervals of 3–10
days, based on tolerance, to a target dose of 25 mg twice daily (maximum dose, 50 mg twice daily). NOTE: Use with
caution in patients who require inotropes during their hospital course. Increase dose gradually and monitor for
congestive signs and symptoms of HF making every effort to achieve target.

Administration

ORAL › 6.25 mg tablet (Rx)

Administer with food to minimize the risk of orthostatic hypotension. Initiate only in stable patients or hospitalized
patients after volume status has been optimized and IV diuretics, vasodilators, and inotropic agents have all been
successfully discontinued. Stabilize other heart failure medications and minimize fluid retention prior to initiating
therapy.

Page 1 of 5



April 24, 2026, 11:48:25 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Lower initial dose and titrate to response. Dose reductions may be necessary.

Patients with Hypotension or Syncope:
Lower initial dose and titrate to response. Lower blood pressure at a rate appropriate for the patient's clinical
condition.

Indications

Management of heart failure

Contraindications

Decompensated cardiac failure requiring intravenous inotropic therapy; bronchial asthma or related bronchospastic
conditions; secondor third-degree AV block, sick sinus syndrome, and severe bradycardia (except in patients with a
functioning artificial pacemaker) ; cardiogenic shock; severe hepatic impairment

Precautions

Anaphylactic reactions
Diabetes (may potentiate and/or mask signs and symptoms of hypoglycemia)
Pheochromocytoma (adequate alpha-blockade should be initiated prior to use of any betablocker).
Bradycardia (may occur; reduce dose if heart rate drops to <55 beats/minute); Hypotension or syncope (may occur
within the first 30 days of therapy); Angina
Heart failure (may experience a worsening of renal function; worsening heart failure or fluid retention may occur
during upward titration).
Floppy iris syndrome (has been observed in cataract surgery patients who were on or were previously treated with
alpha1 blockers);
Myasthenia gravis; peripheral vascular disease (may precipitate or aggravate symptoms of arterial insufficiency).
Bronchospastic disease (use with caution and close monitoring)
Hepatic impairment
Psoriasis (associated with induction or exacerbation of psoriasis)
Thyroid disease (may mask signs of hyperthyroidism; abrupt withdrawal may exacerbate symptoms of
hyperthyroidism or precipitate thyroid storm)
Abrupt withdrawal (may cause acute tachycardia, hypertension, and/or ischemia; severe exacerbation of angina,
ventricular arrhythmias, and myocardial infarction have been reported; chronic betablocker therapy should not be
routinely withdrawn prior to major surgery)
Elderly (bradycardia may be observed more frequently).
Pregnancy (increased risk of cardiovascular defects was observed); lactation (not known if excreted in breastmilk;
may cause serious adverse reactions in the nursing infant).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Hypotension, bradycardia, syncope, peripheral edema, generalized edema, angina, dependent edema, AV
block, cerebrovascular accident, hypertension, hypervolemia or hypovolemia, orthostatic hypotension, palpitation,
dizziness, fatigue, headache, depression, fever, hypoesthesia, hypotonia, insomnia, malaise, somnolence, vertigo,
hyperglycemia, hypercholesterolemia, hypertriglyceridemia, diabetes mellitus, gout, hyperkalemia, hyperuricemia,
hypoglycemia, hyponatremia, weight gain, diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, melena, periodontitis, weight
loss, impotence, anemia, decreased prothrombin, purpura, thrombocytopenia, increased alkaline phosphatase and
transaminases, increased GGT, weakness, back pain, arthralgia, arthritis, muscle cramps, paresthesia, blurred vision,
increased BUN and non-protein nitrogen, albuminuria, increased creatinine, glycosuria, hematuria, renal
insufficiency, cough, nasopharyngitis, rales, dyspnea, pulmonary edema, rhinitis, nasal congestion, sinus congestion,
injury, allergy, flulike syndrome, sudden death
Less Common: Anaphylactoid reaction, alopecia, angioedema, aplastic anemia, amnesia, asthma, bronchospasm,
bundle branch block, cholestatic jaundice, concentration decreased, diaphoresis, erythema multiforme, exfoliative
dermatitis, GI hemorrhage, decreased hearing, hypersensitivity reaction, hypokalemia, hypokinesia, interstitial
pneumonitis, leukopenia, libido decreased, migraine, myocardial ischemia, nervousness, neuralgia, nightmares,
pancytopenia, paresis, peripheral ischemia, photosensitivity, pruritus, rash, respiratory alkalosis, seizure, Stevens-
Johnson syndrome, tachycardia, tinnitus, toxic epidermal necrolysis, urinary incontinence, urticaria, xerostomia

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Decreases metabolism of Carvedilol:
Aminoquinolines e.g. Antimalarial drugs; Phenothiazine Antipsychotic Agents, CYP2D6 Inhibitors (Moderate)

Decreases metabolism of the following drugs:
Phenothiazine Antipsychotic Agents

Enhances therapeutic effect of Carvedilol:
Bradycardic effect: Acetylcholinesterase Inhibitors e.g., Neostigmine, Amiodarone, Anilidopiperidine Opioids e.g.,
Fentanyl (bradycardic effect; antihypertensive effect), Bretylium (also AV blockade), Cardiac Glycosides e.g., Digoxin,
Dipyridamole, Disopyramide, Other Bradycardia-causing Agents, Regorafenib, Ruxolitinib, Tofacitinib

Antihypertensive effect: Anilidopiperidine Opioids e.g. Fentanyl, Phenothiazine Antipsychotic Agents, Barbiturates
e.g. Phenobarbital, Brimonidine (Topical), Calcium Channel Blockers, Non-Dihydropyridine [except Bepridil],
Diazoxide, MAO Inhibitors [except Linezolid, Tedizolid], Molsidomine, Nicardipine, Nicorandil, Nifedipine (also
inotropic effects), Other Antihypertensive Agents , Pentoxifylline, Phosphodiesterase-5 Inhibitors e.g. Sidenafil,
Prostacyclin Analogues e.g. Epoporestenol, Reserpine; Propafenone (possesses independent beta blocking activity)

Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Bradycardic effect: Fingolimod, Ivabradine, Midodrine
Hypoglycemic effect: Insulin, Sulfonylureas e.g., Gliclazide
Disopyramide (negative inotropic effect)
Lacosamide (AV blocking effect)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Carvedilol:
Amiodarone (cardiac arrest), Calcium Channel Blockers, Non-Dihydropyridine [except Bepridil] (bradycardia; signs of
heart failure), MAO Inhibitors [except Linezolid, Tedizolid] (orthostatic hypotension), Nicardipine (may precipitate
signs of heart failure), Other Antihypertensive Agent

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
Cholinergic Agonists e.g., Betanechol (cardiac conduction abnormalities; bronchoconstriction), Duloxetine
(orthostatic hypotension), Levodopa (orthostatic hypotension), Risperidone (hypotensive effect)

Reduces therapeutic effect of Carvedilol:
Antihypertensive effect: Methylphenidate, Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatory Agents

Avoid concomitant use with:
Decreases metabolism of Carvedilol:
CYP2D6 Inhibitors

Enhances therapeutic effect of Carvedilol:
Bradycardic effect: Dronedarone, Rivastigmine

Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Alpha / Beta Agonists, Direct-Acting [except Dipivefrin] (vasopressor effect), Ceritinib (bradycardic effect), Ergot
Derivatives e.g., Ergotamine (vasoconstricting effect)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Carvedilol:
Alpha2 Agonists e.g., Clonidine (AV-blocking and sinus node dysfunction effects; rebound hypertension),

Floctafenine

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
Alpha1 Blockers e.g. Tamsulosin (orthostatic hypotension), Amifostine (hypotensive effect), Grass Pollen Allergen
Extract / 5 Grass Extract (inhibits the ability to effectively treat severe allergic reactions with Epinephrine),
Methacholine, Obinutuzumab (hypotensive effect), Rituximab (hypotensive effect)

Increases serum concentration of Carvedilol:
Abiraterone Acetate, Dronedarone, Panobinostat

Increases serum concentration of the following drugs:
Afatinib [reduce Afatinib by 10 mg], Bosutinib, Colchicine (increase distribution into certain tissues, e.g., brain),
Cyclosporine (Systemic), Dabigatran Etexilate, Doxorubicin (Conventional), Edoxaban, Everolimus. Pazopanib,
Silodosin, Topotecan, Vincristine (Liposomal)

Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Alpha / Beta Agonists, Direct-Acting [except Dipivefrin] (beta-adrenoceptor-mediated effects, including anti-
anaphylactic effects of epinephrine), Beta2 Agonists (bronchodilatory effect), Theophylline Derivatives
(bronchodilatory effect)

Mechanism of Action

A racemic mixture, carvedilol has non-selective beta-adrenoreceptor and alpha-adrenergic blocking activity. It
causes exercise-induced or beta-agonist-induced tachycardia, a reduction of cardiac output and reflex orthostatic
tachycardia, vasodilation, decreased peripheral and renal vascular resistance, reduced plasma renin activity, and
increased levels of atrial natriuretic peptide

CASTOR OIL

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

A06AB05

ANATOMICAL

Alimentary Tract and Metabolism

THERAPEUTIC

Drugs for Constipation

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Laxatives

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › USP Grade (OTC)

Dosage

ORAL › USP Grade (OTC)

Bowel evacuation / constipation: by mouth, ADULT, 15 to 60 mL as a single dose; CHILD 2–11 years, 5 to 15 mL as a
single dose.

Administration

ORAL › USP Grade (OTC)

For oral administration, should be administered on an empty stomach, with juice or carbonated beverages. Do NOT
administer at bedtime because of rapid onset of action. Shake emulsions well before use. May be mixed with 120–
240 mL of water, milk, fruit juice, or soft drink before administration.

Dose Adjustment

N/A

Indications

Temporary relief of occasional constipation; bowel evacuation.

Page 1 of 2



April 24, 2026, 11:48:38 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting

Precautions

WARNING: Do NOT use for >1 week or when abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, or rectal bleeding are present unless
directed by health care provider.
Elderly (e.g., severe fluid and electrolyte loss, which may affect mental function);
Pregnancy (associated with induction of labor).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Dizziness, electrolyte disturbance, abdominal cramps, diarrhea, nausea, pelvic congestion

Drug Interactions

No known significant interactions

Mechanism of Action

A fixed oil obtained from the seeds of Ricinus communis that is used as a stimulant laxative. It is hydrolyzed to
ricinoleic acid in the small intestine, which reduces net absorption of fluid and electrolytes and stimulates
peristalsis.

Pregnancy & Remarks

PREGNANCY CATEGORY

Not available

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Refer to adult dosing.

Renal Impairment:
Adults:
ClCr 35-54mL/minute or SrCr 1.6-3mg%: Full dose every 8 hours or longer; ClCr 11-34mL/minute or SrCr 3.1-4.5mg%:
1/2 usual dose every 12 hours; ClCr ≤10mL/minute or SrCr ≥4.6mg%: 1/2 usual dose every18-24 hours.
Pediatrics:
ClCr 41-70mL/minute: 60% of usual dose given in equally divided doses every 12 hours; ClCr 21-40mL/minute: 25% of
usual dose given in equally divided doses every 12 hours; ClCr ≤5-20mL/minute: 10% of usual dose given in equally
divided doses every 24 hours.

Indications

Treatment of staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome, osteomyelitis, septic bursitis, prosthetic joint infection, native
valve infective endocarditis, central-line associated bloodstream infection, dacryocystitis, and dental prophylaxis

Contraindications

Known allergy to the cephalosporin group of antibiotics.

Precautions

Monitor patients for: Elevated INR, Penicillin Allergy, Superinfection;
Use with caution in patients with: Renal Impairment, Seizure Disorders.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Diarrhea, oral candidiasis, stomach cramps, anorexia, anaphylaxis, eosinophilia, itching, skin rash, leukopenia,
thrombocytopenia, hepatitis, genital or anal pruritus

Drug Interactions

Increases serum concentration of Cefazolin:

1st: First-generation cephalosporins include cefazolin, cefadroxil, cephalexin, cephalothin, cephapirin, and

Page 1 of 3



April 24, 2026, 11:48:53 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Refer to adult dosing.

Renal Impairment:
Intermittent hemodialysis (IHD) (administer after hemodialysis on dialysis days): IV: Initial: 1g (single dose) on day 1.
Maintenance: 0.5-1g every 24 hours or 1-2g every 48-72 hours. Note: Dosing dependent on the assumption of 3
times/week, complete IHD sessions.
Peritoneal dialysis (PD): Removed to a lesser extent than hemodialysis; administer normal recommended dose every
48 hours.
Continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT): Drug clearance is highly dependent on the method of renal
replacement, filter type, and flow rate. Appropriate dosing requires close monitoring of pharmacologic response,
signs of adverse reaction due to drug accumulation, as well as drug concentrations in relation to target trough (if
appropriate). The following are general recommendations only (based on dialysate flow/ultrafiltration rates of 1-
2L/hour and minimal residual renal function) and should not supersede clinical judgment:
CVVH: Loading dose of 2g followed by 1-2g every 12 hours.
CVVHD/CVVHDF: Loading dose of 2g followed by either 1g every 8 hours or 2g every 12 hours. Note: Dosage of 1g
every 8 hours results in similar steady-state concentrations as 2g every 12 hours and is more cost effective.

Indications

Treatment of complicated urinary tract infections, including pyelonephritis caused by E. coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae,
or Proteus mirabilis; monotherapy for febrile neutropenia; complicated skin and skin structure infections caused by
S. pyogenes or methicillin-susceptible staphylococci; moderate-to-severe pneumonia caused by S. pneumoniae, P.
aeruginosa, Klebsiella pneumoniae, or Enterobacter species; complicated intra-abdominal infections caused by
E.coli, P. aeruginosa, K. pneumoniae, Enterobacter species against methicillin-susceptible staphylococci,
Enterobacter sp, and many other gram negative bacilli.

Precautions

Use with caution in patients with a history of penicillin allergy, especially IgE- mediated reactions; Prolonged use may
result in fungal or bacterial superinfection, including C. difficile-associated diarrhea (CDAD) and pseudomembranous
colitis, CDAD has been observed >2 months post-antibiotic treatment; Use with caution in patients with a history of
gastrointestinal disease, especially colitis; Use with caution in patients with renal impairment; dosage adjustments
recommended.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Positive Coombs’ test without hemolysis.
Less Common: Fever, headache, rash, pruritus, hypophosphatemia, diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, inflammation,
phlebitis.
Rare: Agranulocytosis, anaphylactic shock, colitis, coma, confusion, encephalopathy, hallucinations,
hyper/hypocalcemia, hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, leucopenia, moniliasis, stupor, vaginitis.

Page 2 of 3



April 24, 2026, 11:48:53 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
BCG, Typhoid vaccine (Only the live attenuated Ty21a strain is affected.)

Increases serum concentration of Cefepime:
Probenecid

Mechanism of Action

4th: Cefepime belongs to the fourth-generation cephalosporin. It is more resistant to hydrolysis by chromosomal β
lactamases. Cefepime has good activity against P aeruginosa, Enterobacteriaceae, S. aureus, and S. pneumoniae. It is
highly active against Haemophilus and Neisseria sp.

Dose Adjustment

Renal Impairment:
CrCl 21-60 mL/ minute: Administer 75% of the standard dose.
CrCl <20 mL/minute: Administer 50% of the standard dose.
10% removed by hemodialysis.

Indications

Second line therapy for uncomplicated typhoid fever, salmonella gastroenteritis, uncomplicated gonococcal
infections of the cervix, urethras, rectum and conjunctiva. Step down therapy for severe/complicated typhoid fever

Precautions

WARNING:
Use with caution in patients with a historyof penicillin allergy, especially IgE-mediated reactions.
Prolonged use may result in fungal or bacterial superinfection, including C. difficile-associated diarrhea (CDAD) and
pseudomembranous colitis. CDAD has been observed >2 months post-antibiotic treatment; Use with caution in
patients with renal impairment; modify dosage.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Diarrhea

Less Common: Abdominal pain, nausea, dyspepsia, flatulence, loose stools.

Rare: Acute renal failure, anaphylactic reactions, angioedema, candidiasis, dizziness, drug fever, eosinophilia,
erythema multiforme, facial edema, fever, headache, hepatitis, hyperbilirubinemia, jaundice, leukopenia,
neutropenia, pseudomembranous colitis, rash, seizure, serum sickness-like reaction, SJS, thrombocytopenia, toxic
epidermal necrolysis, urticaria, vaginitis, vomiting.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Refer to adult dosing.

Renal Impairment:
Note: Renal function may be estimated using CockcroftGault formula for dosage adjustment purposes.

ClCr <20mL/minute/1.73 m2: dose should be decreased by 50%.
Alternative recommendation:
Adults:
Glomerular Filtration Rate >50mL/minute: Administer every 6 hours; Glomerular Filtration Rate 10-50mL/minute:
Administer every 6-12 hours; Glomerular Filtration Rate <10mL/minute: Administer every 24 hours or decrease the
dose by 50% (and administer at usual intervals); Intermittent hemodialysis: Administer 1-2g every 24 hours (on
dialysis days, administer after hemodialysis).
NOTE: Dosing dependent on the assumption of 3 times a week, complete intermittent hemodialysis sessions.
Peritoneal dialysis: 1g every 24 hours
Continuous renal replacement therapy: Drug clearance is highly dependent on the method of renal replacement,
filter type, and flow rate. Appropriate dosing requires close monitoring of pharmacologic response, signs of adverse
reactions due to drug accumulation, as well as drug concentrations in relation to target trough (if appropriate). The
following are general recommendations only (based on dialysate flow ultrafiltration rates of 1-2L/h and minimal
residual renal function) and should not supersede clinical judgment: Continuous veno-venous hemofiltration: 1-2g
every 8-12 hours; Continuous veno-venous hemodialysis: 1-2g every 8 hours; Continuous venovenous
hemodiafiltration: 1-2g every 6-8 hours.

Children:
NOTE: Glomerular filtration rate should be estimated using an acceptable pediatric method (eg, Schwartz equation,
TraubJohnson equation, or a height/weight nomogram).

Glomerular filtration rate 30-50mL/minute/1.73m2: 35-70mg/kg/dose every 8-12 hours; Glomerular filtration rate 10 to
29mL/minute/1.73m2: 35-70mg/kg/dose every 12 hours; Glomerular filtration rate <10mL/minute/1.73m2: 35 to
70mg/kg/dose every 24 hours; Intermittent hemodialysis: 35-70mg/kg/dose every 24 hours; Peritoneal dialysis: 35-
70mg/kg/dose every 24 hours; Continuous renal replacement therapy: 35-70mg/kg/dose every 12 hours.

Indications

Treatment of serious bone and joint infections, meningitis and other central nervous system infections, serious
lower respiratory tract infections (including pneumonia), bacteremia/septicemia, serious skin and skin structure
infections, multi drug resistant or complicated typhoid fever and other infections caused by Salmonella, infections
caused by Vibrio parahaemolyticus or V. vulnificus.

Precautions

Monitor patients for: arrhythmia, granulocytopenia, penicillin allergy, tissue inflammation, superinfection;
Use with caution in patients with colitis and renal impairment.

Injection-site reactions (eg, pain, induration, tenderness, inflammation).

IM / IV › [Watch] 250 mg (as pentahydrate), vial (Rx)

Page 2 of 4



April 24, 2026, 11:50:02 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
IM, IV: Dosage should be based on renal function with a dosing interval not more frequent than every 12 hours.

Renal Impairment:
ClCr 30-50mL/minute: Administer every 12 hours.
ClCr 10-30mL/minute: Administer every 24 hours.
ClCr <10mL/minute: Administer every 48-72 hours.
Intermittent hemodialysis (IHD) (administer after hemodialysis on dialysis days): Dialyzable (50% to 100%): 0.5-1g
every 24 hours or 1-2g every 48-72 hours. Note: Dosing dependent on the assumption of 3 times/week, complete IHD
sessions.
Peritoneal dialysis (PD): Loading dose of 1g, followed by 500mg every 24 hours.
Continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT): Drug clearance is highly dependent on the method of renal
replacement, filter type, and flow rate. Appropriate dosing requires close monitoring of pharmacologic response,
signs of adverse reactions due to drug accumulation, as well as drug concentrations in relation to target trough (if
appropriate). The following are general recommendations only (based on dialysate flow/ultrafiltration rates of 1-
2L/hour and minimal residual renal function) and should not supersede clinical judgment:
CVVH: Loading dose of 2g followed by 1-2g every 12 hours.
CVVHD/CVVHDF: Loading dose of 2g followed by either 1g every 8 hours or 2g every 12 hours. Note: Dosage of 1gg
every 8 hours results in similar steady-state concentrations as 2g every 12 hours and is more cost effective. Dosage
of 2g every 8 hours may be needed for gram-negative rods with MIC ≥4mg/L.
Note: For patients receiving CVVHDF, some recommend giving a loading dose of 2g followed by 3g over 24 hours as a
continuous IV infusion to maintain concentrations ≥4 times the MIC for susceptible pathogens.

Indications

Treatment of documented susceptible Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection and infections due to other susceptible
aerobic gram-negative organisms; neonatal sepsis, osteomyelitis, bone and joint infections, necrotizing otitis
externa, dacryocystitis, acute sinusitis, infective endocarditis.

Precautions

WARNING: Use with caution in patients with a history of penicillin allergy, especially IgE-mediated reactions.

Prolonged use may result in fungal or bacterial superinfection, including C. difficile-associated diarrhea (CDAD) and
pseudomembranous colitis; CDAD has been observed >2 months post-antibiotic treatment;
Use with caution in patients with renal impairment modify dosage in severe impairment); Use with caution in patients
with a history of seizure disorder, high levels, particularly in the presence of renal impairment, may increase risk of
seizures.

Less Common: Diarrhea, pain at injection site, hypersensitivity reactions.
Rare: Anaphylaxis, angioedema, asterixis, BUN increased, candidiasis, creatinine increased, dizziness,
encephalopathy, eosinophilia, erythema multiforme, hyperbilirubinemia, pruritus, toxic epidermal necrolysis,
vaginitis, vomiting.

Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
BCG, Typhoid vaccine (Only the live attenuated Ty21a strain is affected.)

Probenecid

cefixime, cefpodoxime proxetil, cefdinir, cefditoren pivoxil, ceftibuten, and moxalactam. They are active against
Citrobacter, S marcescens, and Providencia.

Administration: Do not admix with aminoglycosides in same bottle/bag. Do not reconstitute, admix, or co-
administer with calciumcontaining solutions. Infuse intermittent infusion over 30 minutes

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Refer to adult dosing.

Indications

Treatment of lower respiratory tract infections, acute bacterial otitis media, skin and skin structure infections, bone
and joint infections, intra-abdominal and urinary tract infections, pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), uncomplicated
gonorrhea, bacterial septicemia, and meningitis; used in surgical prophylaxis.

Page 3 of 5



April 24, 2026, 11:50:07 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Do NOT use in hyperbilirubinemic neonates, particularly those who are premature since ceftriaxone is reported to
displace bilirubin from albumin binding sites; concomitant use with intravenous calcium-containing
solutions/products in neonates (≤28 days).

Precautions

Severe cases (including some fatalities) of immune-related hemolytic anemia have been reported in patients
receiving cephalosporins, including ceftriaxone;
Secondary to biliary obstruction, pancreatitis has been reported rarely;
Use with caution in patients with a history of penicillin allergy, especially IgE-mediated reactions;
Prolonged use may result in fungal or bacterial superinfection, including C. difficile-¬associated diarrhea (CDAD) and
pseudomembranous colitis; CDAD has been observed >2 months post-antibiotic treatment.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Induration, tightness, warmth.

Less Common: Rash, diarrhea, eosinophilia, thrombocytosis, leukopenia, tenderness at injection site, pain.

Rare: Abdominal pain, agranulocytosis, allergic dermatitis, allergic pneumonitis, anaphylaxis, anemia, basophilia,
biliary lithiasis, bronchospasm, chills, colitis, diaphoresis, dizziness, dysgeusia, dyspepsia, edema, epistaxis,
erythema multiforme, exanthema, fever, flatulence, flushing, gallbladder sludge, gallstones, glossitis, hemolytic
anemia, jaundice, Lyell’s syndrome, monocytosis, oliguria, palpitation, renal and pulmonary ceftriaxone-calcium
precipitations, seizure, serum sickness Stevenson-Johnson syndrome, stomatitis, toxic epidermal necrolysis,
vaginitis, vomiting.

Drug Interactions

Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
BCG, Typhoid vaccine (Only the live attenuated Ty21a strain is affected.)

Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Vitamin K antagonists e.g. warfarin

Mechanism of Action

3rd: Third-generation cephaosphorins include cefoperazone, cefotaxime, ceftazidime, ceftizoxime, ceftriaxone,
cefixime, cefpodoxime proxetil, cefdinir, cefditoren pivoxil, ceftibuten, and moxalactam. They are active against
Citrobacter, S marcescens, and Providencia.

Dosage

ORAL › [Watch] 500 mg tablet (as axetil) (Rx)

Acute otitis media, no anaphylaxis, by mouth, CHILD, 30 mg/kg every 12 hours for 10 days (<2 years old), 7 days (2-5
years old), 5-7 days (>5 years old); ADULT, 500mg-1g in divided doses every 12 hours for 7 days. Acute otitis media,
clinical failure after 3 days, by mouth, CHILD, 30 mg/kg in divided doses every 12 hours for 10 days (2yrs old OR
severe symptoms regardless of age), 5-7 days (>2yrs with mild or moderate disease). Acute bacterial exacerbation
of chronic bronchitis (ABECB), by mouth, ADULT, 500mg twice a day for 5-10 days Community-acquired pneumonia,
non-severe, by mouth, CHILD >40 kg, 20-30 mg/kg in divided doses every 12 hours for 7days; Severe, with no Hib
vaccination or incomplete or unknown vaccination history, by mouth, CHILD, 100 mg/kg in divided doses every 8
hours. With stable co-morbid illness, by mouth, ADULT, 500 mg twice a day for 5-7 days or 3-5 days if using with
azithromycin. Recurrent pharyngitis, by mouth, CHILD, 20 mg/kg in divided doses every 12 hours for 10 days;
ADULT, 500mg-1g every 12 hours for 10 days. Deep neck abscess/ Retropharyngeal abscess, by mouth, CHILD, 20-
30 mg/kg in divided doses every 12 hours with metronidazole 10 mg/kg in divided doses every 6 hours for at least 7
days; ADULT, 500mg twice daily with metronidazole 500mg every 8 hours for at least 7 days. Acute bacterial
rhinosinusitis, with severe penicillin allergy, by mouth, CHILD, 30 mg/kg in divided doses every 12 hours for 10 days;
ADULT, 500 mg twice daily for 5-7 days. Acute sinusitis (clinical failure after 3 days), by mouth, ADULT, 500mg twice
daily for 7-10 days.

Administration

ORAL › [Watch] 500 mg tablet (as axetil) (Rx)

For Oral suspension: Administer with food. Shake well before use. For IM: Inject deep IM into large muscle mass.
For IV: Inject direct IV over 3-5 minutes. Infuse intermittent infusion over 15-30 minutes.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Refer to adult dosing.

Renal Impairment:
CrCl 10-20mL/minute: Administer every 12 hours.
CrCl <10mL/minute: Administer every 24 hours.
Hemodialysis: Dialyzable (25%).
Peritoneal dialysis: Dose every 24 hours.
Continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT): 1g every 12 hours.

Indications

Treatment of infections caused by methicillin sensitive staphylococci, group B streptococci, H. influenzae (type A and
B), E. coli, Enterobacter, Salmonella, and Klebsiella; treatment of susceptible infections of the upper and lower
respiratory tract, otitis media

Page 2 of 4



April 24, 2026, 11:50:12 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to cefuroxime, any component of the formulation, or other cephalosporins.

Precautions

WARNING: Use with caution in patients with a history of penicillin allergy.

May be associated with increased INR, especially in nutritionally-deficient patients, prolonged treatment, hepatic or
renal disease; Prolonged use may result in fungal or bacterial superinfection, including C. difficile-associated
diarrhea (CDAD) and pseudomembranous colitis; CDAD has been observed >2 months post-antibiotic treatment.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Diarrhea
Less Common: Diaper rash, alkaline phosphatase increased, lactate dehydrogenase increased, nausea, vomiting,
vaginitis, eosinophilia, hemoglobin and hematocrit decreased, transaminases increased, thrombophlebitis.
Rare: Anaphylaxis, angioedema, chest pain, cholestasis, colitis, dyspnea, erythema multiforme, fever, GI bleeding,
hemolytic anemia, hepatitis, hives, hyperbilirubinemia, hypersensitivity, interstitial nephritis, jaundice, leukopenia,
neutropenia, pain at injection site, pancytopenia, positive Coombs’ test, pseudomembranous colitis, rash, renal
dysfunction, seizure, SJS, stomach cramps, tachycardia, thrombocytopenia, tongue swelling, toxic epidermal
necrolysis, urticaria.

Drug Interactions

Decreases serum concentration of Cefuroxime:
Antacids

Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
BCG, Typhoid vaccine (Only the live attenuated Ty21a strain is affected.)

Increases serum concentration of Cefuroxime:
Probenecid

Reduces absorption of Cefuroxime:
H2-antagonists e.g. Cimetidine (Separate oral doses by at least 2 hours.)

Mechanism of Action

2nd: Second-generation cephalosporins include cefaclor, cefamandole, cefonicid, cefuroxime, cefprozil, loracarbef,
and ceforanide; and the structurally related cephamycins cefoxitin, cefmetazole, and cefotetan, which have activity
against anaerobes.

Geriatric:

Indications

Page 2 of 5



April 25, 2026, 1:51:20 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Active GI disease or bleeding; heart failure, angina, peripheral arterial disease or cerebrovascular disease;
perioperative setting of Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG) surgery; third trimester of pregnancy; breastfeeding;
severe liver impairment or active liver disease; severe renal impairment; inflammatory bowel disease; known
hyperkalemia

Precautions

WARNING: Associated with an increased risk of serious (and potentially fatal) adverse cardiovascular events,
including MI, stroke, ischemic heart disease, cerebrovascular disease, and congestive heart failure. This risk may
increase with duration of use.

Patients with cardiovascular disease or risk factors for cardiovascular disease may be at greater risk.

NSAIDs cause an increased risk of serious GI adverse events, including bleeding, ulceration, and perforation of the
stomach or intestines, which can be fatal. These events can occur at any time during their use and without warning
symptoms.

Pre-existing renal impairment increases the risk of NSAID-induced impairment.

Hypertension, Congestive heart failure or cardiovascular disease;

GI diseases or bleeding (use with caution);

Vitamin K deficiency, or hypoprothrombinemia,

Anaphylactoid reactions;

Asthma, especially with rhinitis or nasal polyps;

Persistent urinary symptoms, hematuria, or cystitis;

Renal impairment;

Elderly;

Pregnancy;

Women (reconsider use if planning for pregnancy).

Page 3 of 5



April 25, 2026, 1:51:20 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Abdominal pain, back pain, cough, diarrhea, dizziness, dyspepsia, fever, flatulence, headache, insomnia,
nasopharyngitis, nausea, peripheral edema, pharyngitis, rash, rhinitis, sinusitis, upper respiratory tract infection,
vomiting

Less Common: Alopecia, anemia, angina, anorexia, anxiety, arthrosis, bradycardia, bronchospasm, cellulitis, chest
pain, constipation, cystitis, depression, dermatitis, dryness of skin, dysphagia, dyspnea, facial edema, fatigue,
gastroesophageal reflux, GI ulcer, hypercholesterolemia, hypertension, hypokalemia, MI, migraine, myalgia,
nervousness, pain palpitations, paresthesia, photosensitivity, pruritus, sinus tachycardia, somnolence, tinnitus,
urinary frequency, urticaria, ventricular hypertrophy, vertigo, xerostomia

Rare: Acute renal failure, agranulocytosis, anaphylactoid reactions, angioedema, aplastic anemia, cerebrovascular
accidents, CHF, cholelithiasis, colitis, DVT, erythema multiforme, esophageal perforation, exfoliative dermatitis,
gangrene, GI bleeding, hepatic failure and necrosis, hepatitis, hypoglycemia, hyponatremia, intestinal obstruction
and perforation, intracranial hemorrhage, jaundice, leukopenia, pancreatitis, pancytopenia, pulmonary embolism,
renal papillary necrosis, sepsis, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, suicide, syncope, taste disturbance, thrombocytopenia,
thrombophlebitis, toxic epidermal necrolysis, vasculitis, ventricular fibrillation

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Decreases excretion of the following drugs: Aminoglycosides e.g. Streptomycin
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Anticoagulants, Antidepressants, Benzodiazepines e.g.
Midazolam
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Celecoxib: Angiotensin II Receptor Blockers e.g. Losartan (significant
decrease in renal function)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:ACE Inhibitors e.g. Enalapril (significant decrease in
renal function), Agents with Antiplatelet Properties e.g., NSAIDs, SSRIs (bleeding), Anticoagulants (serious bleeding),
Haloperidol, Quinolone Antibiotics e.g. Levofloxacin (neuroexcitatory and/or seizurepotentiating effect)
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: ACE Inhibitors e.g. Enalapril, Angiotensin II Receptor Blockers
(antihypertensive effect), Diuretics e.g., Potassium-sparing Diuretics, Thiazide Diuretics, Beta Blockers
(antihypertensive effect)

Avoid concomitant use with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Vitamin K Antagonists, e.g., Warfarin (anticoagulant effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Celecoxib: Aspirin (increased rate of GI ulceration or other
complications), Other NSAIDs
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Vitamin K Antagonists, e.g., Warfarin (serious bleeding)
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Loop Diuretics e.g. Furosemide (antagonism of prostaglandin-
mediated effect)

Mechanism of Action

A diaryl-substituted, pyrazole-derived, selective COX-2 inhibitor, which is associated with less GI side effects and
susceptibility to bruising and bleeding than nonselective NSAIDs.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric (≥77 years):
Use lower dose (5 mg daily).

Renal and Hepatic Impairment:
For mild-to-moderate impairment, dose reduction is warranted (5 mg daily).

Indications

Perennial and seasonal allergic rhinitis; other allergic symptoms such as hay fever, conjunctivitis, and chronic
idiopathic urticaria

Contraindications

Known hypersensitivity reactions to cetirizine, levocetirizine, or hydroxyzine or any component of the formulation;
severe renal impairment

Precautions

WARNING: May cause CNS depression, which may impair physical or mental abilities.

CNS stimulation may occur with antihistamines, especially in children (caution is advised in patients suffering from
epilepsy); Children and elderly (risk of sedation and anticholinergic effects are increased; not recommended
for children <12 months); Renal impairment; hepatic impairment; excess alcohol intake; avoid use of other sedative
drugs; Lactation (excreted into breast milk).

SKILLED TASKS. May impair ability to perform certain tasks, e.g., operating machinery or driving.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common:
Dizziness, drowsiness, dryness of mouth, fatigue, headache, insomnia, malaise, nausea, pharyngitis, somnolence

Less Common:
Abdominal pain, anorexia, arthralgia, chest pain, diarrhea, dyspepsia, dyspnea, epistaxis, fever, flushing, increased
appetite, myalgia, tachycardia, thirst, vomiting, weight gain

Rare:
Dystonia, hemolytic anemia, hepatitis, hypersensitivity (including anaphylaxis and bronchospasm), hypotension, rash,
thrombocytopenia

Page 2 of 3



April 24, 2026, 11:50:17 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with: Enhances therapeutic effect of Cetirizine: Anticholinergics e.g., Ipratropium, Tiotropium
(anticholinergic effect), CNS Depressants e.g., Alcohol, Sedatives (sedative effect)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
Analgesics, Opioids (constipation; urinary retention), CNS Depressants e.g., Alcohol, Sedatives, Warfarin
(increased INR; epistaxis)

Reduces therapeutic effect of Cetirizine:
Acetylcholinesterase Inhibitors (Central)

Avoid concomitant use with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Cetirizine:
Doxylamine (CNS depression; psychomotor retardation)

Mechanism of Action

A piperazine-derived, long-acting, second-generation H1 receptor antagonist, which is often better tolerated than
sedating antihistamines due to less sedating and less anticholinergic effects.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Initiate at the lower end of dosing range. Toxicity to immunosuppressants is increased in the elderly.

Renal Impairment:
For patients with CrCl 10–50 mL/minute, administer 75% of dose.
For patients with CrCl <10 mL/minute and patients undergoing peritoneal dialysis, administer 50% of dose.

Hepatic Impairment:
Chlorambucil is primarily metabolized in the liver. Consider dose reduction.

Indications

Chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL); Hodgkin lymphoma; non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma (NHL)

Contraindications

Prior (demonstrated) resistance to chlorambucil

Precautions

WARNING: Chlorambucil is a carcinogen in humans and can severely suppress bone marrow function.

WARNING: Hazardous agent; use appropriate precautions for handling and disposal.

Please see other precautions under Alkylating Agents - Nitrogen Mustards (General Information)

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Drug fever, fever, angioneurotic edema, rash, urticaria, amenorrhea, infertility, skin hypersensitivity,
azoospermia, neutropenia, bone marrow failure (irreversible), bone marrow suppression, cystitis, leukemia
(secondary), leukopenia, lymphophenia, pancytopenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia, hepatotoxicity, jaundice,
peripheral neuropathy, interstitial pneumonia, pulmonary fibrosis, allergic reactions, malignancies (secondary)
Less Common: Diarrhea, nausea, oral ulceration, vomiting
Rare: Agitation, ataxia, confusion, focal / generalized seizure, hallucinations, erythema multiforme, Stevens-Johnson
syndrome, toxic epidermal necrolysis, SIADH, flaccid paralysis, muscular twitching, myoclonia, tremor

Page 2 of 3



April 24, 2026, 11:16:10 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Chlorambucil: Denosumab (serious infections), Trastuzumab
(neutropenic effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Leflunomide (hematologic toxicity, including pancytopenia,
agranulocytosis, and/or thrombocytopenia)
Reduces diagnostic effect of Coccidioides immitis Skin Test

Avoid concomitant use with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Chlorambucil: Roflumilast (immunosuppressive effect)
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Tofacitinib [except antirheumatic doses of Methotrexate,
nonbiologic DMARD] (immunosuppressive effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Chlorambucil: Dipyrone (agranulocytosis), Pimecrolimus, Tacrolimus
(Topical)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Clozapine (agranulocytosis), Natalizumab
(concurrent infection), Vaccines (Live)
Reduces therapeutic effect of Chlorambucil: Echinacea
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: BCG (Intravesical), Vaccines (Inactivated) [complete all age-
appropriate vaccinations at least 2 weeks prior to starting Chlorambucil; if vaccinated during Chlorambucil therapy,
revaccinate at least 3 months after discontinuation], Vaccines (Live) [do not administer vaccines for at least 3 months
after Chlorambucil therapy]

Mechanism of Action

A nitrogen mustard analogue that interferes with DNA replication and RNA transcription through alkylation and
crosslinking of DNAstrands producing cytotoxic effects.

Indications

Treatment of brain abscess in the presence of dental infection, meningitis, uncomplicated typhoid fever, salmonella
gastroenteritis. It is associated with serious hematological side-effects when given systemically and should
therefore be reserved for the treatment of life-threatening infections

Contraindications

Treatment of trivial or viral infections; bacterial prophylaxis.

Precautions

WARNING: Serious and fatal blood dyscrasias (aplastic anemia, hypoplastic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and
granulocytopenia) have occurred after both short-term and prolonged therapy. Monitor CBC frequently in all
patients.

Characterized by circulatory collapse, cyanosis, acidosis, abdominal distention, myocardial depression, coma, and
death. Reaction appears to be associated with serum levels ≥50mcg/mL. May result from drug accumulation in
patients with impaired hepatic or renal function;
Prolonged use may result in fungal or bacterial superinfections, including C. difficile-associated diarrhea (CDAD) and
pseudomembranous colitis;
Use with caution in patients with hepatic impairment (reduced dosage recommended);
Use with caution in patients with renal impairment.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Confusion, delirium, depression, fever, headache, angioedema, rash, urticaria, diarrhea, enterocolitis, glossitis,
nausea, stomatitis, vomiting, aplastic anemia, bone marrow suppression, granulocytopenia, hypoplastic anemia,
pancytopenia, thrombocytopenia, optic neuritis, anaphylaxis, hypersensitivity reactions, Gray syndrome.

Page 2 of 3



April 24, 2026, 11:50:23 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Decreases serum concentration of Chloramphenicol:
Anticonvulsants e.g. Phenytoin

Decreases metabolism of the following drugs:
Anticonvulsants e.g. Phenytoin, Barbiturates, Sulfonylureas

Increases serum concentration of the following drugs:
Voriconazole

Increases metabolism of Chloramphenicol:
Barbiturates, Rifampin

Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
BCG, Cyanocobalamin, Typhoid Vaccine

Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Vitamin K Antagonists e.g. warfarin

Mechanism of Action

Chloramphenicol is a potent inhibitor of microbial protein synthesis by reversibly binding to the 50S subunit of the
bacterial ribosome, thus preventing peptide bond formation by peptidyl transferase. It has both bacteriostatic and
bactericidal action against H. influenzae, N. meningitidis and S. pneumoniae.

Dosage

TOPIC › 0.12% (as gluconate) 50 mL (Rx)

Gingivitis, by mouth, ADULT, as 0.12% preparation, swish 1 tablespoon (15 mL) in mouth undiluted for 30 seconds,
then spit out; rinse twice daily, morning and evening, after tooth brushing Surgical hand scrub, by topical
application, ADULT, as 4% preparation, wet hands and forearms under running water for 30 seconds; scrub hands
and forearms with 5 mL of product with or without wet brush for 3 minutes; pay close attention to the nails,
cuticles, and interdigital spaces; rinse thoroughly under running water for 30 seconds; dry thoroughly. Health care
personnel hand wash, by topical application, ADULT, as 4% preparation, wet hands with water; wash hands with 5
mL of product in a vigorous manner for 15 seconds; rinse and dry thoroughly. Preoperative skin preparation, by
topical application, ADULT, as 4% preparation, apply liberally to surgical site and swab for at least 2 minutes; dry
with a sterile towel; repeat cleansing for an additional 2 minutes and dry with a sterile towel. Skin wound and
general cleansing, by topical application, ADULT, as 4% preparation, thoroughly rinse with water the area to be
cleansed; apply the minimum amount of product necessary to cover the skin or wound area and wash gently; rinse
thoroughly.

Administration

TOPIC › 0.12% (as gluconate) 50 mL (Rx)

Avoid contact w/ brain, meninges, middle ear or sensitive tissues and eyes. Do NOT inject or use in body cavities.
For 0.12% preparation: NOT intended for ingestion. Should be expectorated after rinsing. Do NOT rinse with water,
or other mouthwashes, brush teeth, or eat immediately after. Initiate oral rinse therapy directly following a dental
prophylaxis. Reevaluate patients and give a thorough prophylaxis at intervals no longer than 6 months. For 4%
preparation: For external use only. Do NOT use in the genital area. Do NOT use as a preoperative skin preparation
on the head or face. Keep out of reach of children. If swallowed, get medical help or contact a poison control
center immediately

Indications

Treatment of gingivitis; surgical hand scrub; health care personnel hand wash; preoperative skin preparation; skin
wound and general cleansing

Precautions

Gingivitis and periodontitis; Staining of oral surfaces; Alteration in taste perception; Children; Lactation

Adverse Drug Reactions

disturbance, skin sensitivity, irritation of conjunctiva, mucosal irritation, burning sensation of tongue, anaphylaxis
Less Common: Aphthous ulcer, grossly obvious gingivitis, trauma, ulceration, erythema, desquamation, coated
tongue, keratinization, geographic tongue, mucocele, short frenum, stomatitis, glossitis, dry mouth, hypesthesia,
glossal edema, paresthesia, parotid gland swelling, sialadenitis

No known significant interactions

Mechanism of Action

A very potent cationic chemoprophylactic agent with broad-spectrum of activity against gram-positive and gram-
negative bacteria. It is bactericidal at high concentrations by altering the bacterial cell osmotic equilibrium and
leakage of potassium and phosphorus.

Indications

Prophylaxis of malaria; treatment of non-chloroquine-resistant acute malaria caused by P. vivax and P. ovale
(followed in P. vivax and P. ovale infections by primaquine to eliminate intrahepatic forms).

Contraindications

In patients with history of epilepsy; retinal damage or impaired visual field

Mechanism of Action

An aminoquinoline antimalarial, which has rapid schizontocidal activity against blood forms of Plasmodium ovale and
P. malariae, and against susceptible strains of P. vivax and P. falciparum. It influences hemoglobin digestion by
increasing intravesicular pH in malaria parasite cells and interferes with the nucleoprotein synthesis of the patient.

April 25, 2026, 1:50:55 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

ORAL › 50 mg tablet (as hydrochloride) (Rx)

For oral administration, may be taken with or without food. May be taken with meals to reduce GI discomfort. For
direct IV injection, admqinister diluted solution by slow IV at a rate not to exceed 0.5 mg/minute in children and 1
mg/minute in adults. Patients must remain lying down during and for 30 minutes after administration to reduce the
risk of hypotension. For IM administration, inject slowly, deep into the upper outer quadrant of buttock. Do NOT
administer by SC injection to prevent tissue damage and irritation. Avoid skin contact with solution to prevent
contact dermatitis. NOTE: Do NOT discontinue abruptly because of the possibility of withdrawal and rebound
psychosis. Discontinue gradually to avoid physical withdrawal symptoms, including anorexia, anxiety, diaphoresis,
diarrhea, dizziness, dyskinesia, headache, myalgia, nausea, paresthesia, restlessness, tremulousness, and
vomiting. For the treatment of intractable hiccups, infuse as a slow IV infusion.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Start with small doses in severe renal impairment because of increased cerebral sensitivity.

Hepatic Impairment:
Can precipitate coma; phenothiazines are hepatotoxic.

Indications

Management of schizophrenia; bipolar mania; nausea; vomiting; restlessness and apprehension before surgery;
acute intermittent porphyria; manifestations of manic type of manic-depressive illness; tetanus (adjunct);
intractable hiccups; combativeness and/or explosive hyperexcitable behavior (children); hyperactive children who
show excessive motor activity with accompanying conduct disorders consisting impulsivity, difficulty sustaining
attention, aggressivity, mood lability, poor frustration tolerance, and/or psychosis.

Page 3 of 5



April 25, 2026, 1:50:55 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to chlorpromazine, other phenothiazines, sulfites, or any component of the formulation; comatose
states; Reye’s syndrome; blood dyscrasias; bone marrow suppression; subcortical brain injury, CNS depression,
pheochromocytoma, hypothyroidism.

Precautions

WARNING: Elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis are at an increased risk of death. Closely monitor for
clinical worsening and for the emergence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors.

Cardiovascular disease; anticholinergic effects (e.g., constipation, xerostomia, blurred vision, urinary retention);
blood dyscrasias (e.g., leukopenia, neutropenia, and agranulocytosis, some fatal); seizures.

Hepatic impairment; masking (may mask signs of other disorders or overdose); renal impairment; respiratory
disease; Reye syndrome; ocular effects (pigmentary retinopathy, and lenticular and corneal deposits);
photosensitivity (mild urticarial-type rash or photosensitivity); glaucoma.

Elderly (tardive dyskinesia; mortality and cerebrovascular accidents; SIADH or hyponatremia); children (suicidal
thoughts and behavior; safety and efficacy have not been established).

Pregnancy (abnormal muscle movements and withdrawal symptoms in newborns with use during the third
trimester); lactation (monitor infant for possible adverse effects).

SKILLED TASKS. May impair ability to perform tasks, which require mental alertness, like driving or operating
machinery.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Neuroleptic-induced deficit syndrome, akathisia, priapism, extrapyramidal symptoms, parkinsonism, acute
dystonia, galactorrhea, dry mouth, amenorrhea, gynecomastia, dizziness, sedation, impaired memory, constipation,
urinary retention, blurred vision, decreased sweating, sexual dysfunction, hypotension, tachycardia, syncope,
tardive dyskinesia, maculopapular rash, weight gain, erythema multiforme, urticaria, photosensitivity reactions, urine
discoloration (red to red-brown), photosensitivity, cholestatic jaundice, long-term skin pigmentation changes.

Less Common: Jaundice, changes in body thermoregulation.

Rare: Cornea and lens pigmentation, retinitis pigmentosa, blood dyscrasias (leukocytosis or leukopenia, eosinophilia,
thrombocytopenia, agranulocytosis), seizures, increased risk of death and cerebrovascular events (elderly with
dementia-related psychosis), neuroleptic malignant syndrome.

Page 4 of 5



April 25, 2026, 1:50:55 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Decreases metabolism of Phenytoin
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Antihypertensives [except Guanethidine] Beta Blockers [except
Atenolol, Nadolol] CNS Depressants (central effects) Vasopressors, e.g. Epinephrine (lowers blood pressure)
Thiopental (hypotensive effect; CNS excitatory effect) Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Chlorpromazine:
Methylphenidate, Metoclopramide, Lithium (neurotoxic effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Diuretics, Hypotension-associated Agents
(hypotension) Glucagon (GI adverse effects) Iohexol, Iomeprol, Iopamidol (seizures) Mequitazine (arrhythmogenic
effect) Methylphenidate, Mirabegron Photosensitizing Agents (photosensitizing effect) QTc-prolonging Agents
(torsades de pointes) Serotonin Modulators (neuroleptic malignant syndrome; serotonin syndrome) SSRIs
(psychomotor impairment) Topiramate (hyperthermia)
Reduces absorption of Chlorpromazine: Antacids
Reduces absorption of Nitroglycerin (decreases dissolution of sublingual nitroglycerin tablets)
Reduces therapeutic effect of Chlorpromazine: Anticholinergics (anticholinergic effect) Methylphenidate (inhibits
antipsychotic and moodstabilizing activity)
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Anticoagulants, Dopamine Agonists, Prokinetics, Secretin
Amphetamines (stimulant effect) Guanethidine (antihypertensive effects) Methylphenidate (inhibits stimulatory
effects)

Avoid concomitant use with:
Decreases serum concentration of Tamoxifen
Delayed gastric emptying with Potassium Salts (Oral)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Nilotinib (QTc prolongation; torsades de pointes)
Other Antipsychotics (increased hospitalization rates and length of stay; increased mortality; QTc prolongation;
torsades de pointes)

Mechanism of Action

Chlorpromazine is a dimethylamine derivative of phenothiazine. It has weak anticholinergic and strong
antiadrenergic activity and possesses slight ganglionic, antihistaminic, and anti-serotonin activity. It blocks at least
60% of D2 receptors in the mesolimbic pathway of the brain to produce a therapeutic effect on the positive
symptoms of schizophrenia.

meropenem; ADULT, 400 mg every 8 hours with amikacin/ gentamicin; MDR Acinetobacter, by IV infusion, CHILD,
20-30 mg/kg in divided doses every 12 hours; Klebsiella pneumoniae, by IV infusion, CHILD, 20-30 mg/kg in divided
doses every 12 hours; Burkholderia cepacia, by IV infusion, CHILD, 20-30 mg/kg in divided doses every 12 hours;
ADULT, 400 mg every 12 hours; Enterobacter, by IV infusion, CHILD, 20-30 mg/kg in divided doses every 12 hours.

Administration

ORAL › [Watch] 250 mg tablet (as hydrochloride) (Rx)

May be taken with or without food; do not take with antacids, iron or dairy products.

Dose Adjustment

Renal Impairment:
For mild-to-moderate renal impairment, dose reduction is warranted; for severe impairment, the patient should be
referred to a specialist.

Hepatic Impairment:
Same dosage as in patients with normal hepatic function may be used in patients with mild-to-moderate hepatic
impairment; for severe impairment, refer to a specialist.

Indications

Restricted use of treatment of severe infections of the GI tract including MDR typhoid fever and Shigella dysentery,
chancroid; febrile neutropenia, alternative treatment of acute uncomplicated cystitis; meningococcal meningitis
prophylaxis.

Contraindications

Known history of tendon disorders related to quinolone use; children; concurrent tizanidine use.

Precautions

WARNING:
Associated with an increased risk of tendinitis and tendon rupture; this risk is further increased in older patients,
usually those older than 60 years; in kidney, heart, and lung transplant recipients; and with
the concomitant use of steroid therapy. If tendinitis is suspected, the quinolone should be discontinued
immediately. Quinolones are contraindicated in patients with a history of quinolone-related tendon
disorders.
May exacerbate muscle weakness in patients with myasthenia gravis; avoid quinolones with known history of
myasthenia gravis.
History of epilepsy or conditions that predispose to seizures, myasthenia gravis, G6PD deficiency (increased risk of
hemolytic anemia),
Risk of crystalluria (avoid excessive alkalinity of urine and ensure adequate fluid intake)

Renal impairment;
photosensitivity (discontinue if photosensitivity occurs);
Pregnancy (avoid use; arthropathy reported in animal studies; safer alternatives are available);
Breastfeeding (amount too small to be harmful; use alternative drugs if possible).

USE IN CHILDREN:
Ciprofloxacin causes arthropathy in weight-bearing joints of immature animals, and is therefore generally not
recommended for use in children and growing adolescents. However, the significance of this effect in humans is
uncertain and in some specific circumstances, short-term use of ciprofloxacin in children may be justified.

SKILLED TASKS:
May impair ability to perform skilled tasks, e.g., operating machinery or driving.

Page 3 of 5



April 24, 2026, 11:50:46 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common:
Abdominal pain, diarrhea, dyspepsia, flatulence, itch, nausea, rash, vomiting.

Less Common:
agitation, anorexia, arthralgia, arthritis, confusion, decreased appetite and food intake, depression, dizziness,
drowsiness, hallucinations, fever, headache, myalgia, superinfections, fever, headache, myalgia, superinfections,
restlessness, sleep and taste disorders, urticaria.

Rare:
Agranulocytosis, anaphylaxis, bone marrow depression, C. difficile-associated disease, dyspnea, edema, erythema
multiforme, erythema, hematuria, fixed drug eruptions, hearing loss, hemolytic anemia, interstitial nephritis,
jaundice, leukopenia, neutropenia, pancreatitis, pancytopenia, petechiae, photosensitivity reactions, psychotic
reactions, renal impairment, seizures, serum sickness-like reactions, SJS, syncope, tachycardia, tendonitis,
thrombocytopenia, toxic epidermal necrolysis, vasculitis.

Drug Interactions

NOTE:
Ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2, and may affect drugs metabolized by this enzyme.

Monitor closely with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Ciprofloxacin: Ibuprofen
Reduces therapeutic effect of Phenytoin
Decreases metabolism of Theophylline
Enhances therapeutic effect of Warfarin
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Warfarin (risk of bleeding) [monitor INR]

Avoid concomitant use with:
Reduces absorption of Ciprofloxacin:
Antacids (e.g. aluminum or magnesium hydroxide) [give it at least 2 hours, before, or 4-6 hours after, the antacid],
Calcium [separate doses by at least 2 hours], Iron
[separate doses by at least 2 hours], Sucralfate, Zinc salts [separate doses by at least 2 hours]
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Ciprofloxacin:
Seizure-inducing drugs, Artemether + Lumefantrine

Mechanism of Action

Quinolones are broad-spectrum antibacterial agents. They act by blocking bacterial DNA synthesis by inhibiting
bacterial topoisomerase II and topoisomerase IV. Inhibition of topoisomerase II prevents the relaxation of positively
supercoiled DNA while inhibition of topoisomerase IV interferes with separation of replicated chromosomal DNA.
