Page 1 of 3



April 24, 2026, 11:54:40 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Indications

Paucibacillary and multibacillary leprosy; dermatitis herpetiformis.

Contraindications

Severe anemia; porphyria.

Precautions

Sulfonamide allergy (may predispose to dapsone allergy; begin with low dose of dapsone and monitor closely for
adverse effects);
Cardiopulmonary disease or HIV and PCP (tolerate hemolytic anemia and methemoglobinemia);
Susceptibility to hemolysis, including G6PD deficiency, or in patients receiving drugs capable of inducing hemolysis
(including breastfeeding affected infants);
Hemoglobin M deficiency;
Methemoglobin reductase deficiency;
In patients with hepatic or renal disease; dermatologic reactions (serious reactions are rare, but potentially
occurring);
Peripheral neuropathy (motor loss and muscle weakness have been reported with use);
Prolonged use may result in fungal or bacterial superinfection;
Pregnancy (supplementation with folic acid may be necessary if use is warranted; possible risk of hyperbilirubinemia
in neonates);
Breastfeeding (avoid use)

BLOOD DISORDERS. On long-term treatment, patients and their caregivers should be told how to recognize blood
disorders, and advised to seek immediate medical attention of symptoms, such as fever, sore throat, rash, mouth
ulcers, purpura, bruising, or bleeding develop.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Asymptomatic methemoglobinemia, GI symptoms, hemolytic anemia (occurs in most patients taking more
than 200 mg daily); rash (in HIV patients).
Less Common: Allergy, anorexia, fever, headache, insomnia, nausea, nervousness, vomiting.
Rare: Agranulocytosis, albuminuria, aplastic anemia, blurred vision, Dapsone syndrome (resembling mononucleosis),
hepatitis, Jarisch-Herxheimer reactions, neutropenia, paresthesia, peripheral neuropathy (with doses more than
200 mg daily); psychosis, severe skin reactions (e.g. exfoliative dermatitis).

Page 2 of 3



April 24, 2026, 11:54:40 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Avoid concomitant use with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects Dapsone:
Antimalarial agents (chloroquine and/or primaquine)

Reduces therapeutic effect of Clofazimine

Mechanism of Action

Antimycobacterial drugs are used in the treatment of diseases caused by members of the Mycobacterium genus,
including tuberculosis (TB) and leprosy. Combination chemotherapy is essential to kill heterogeneous populations of
bacterial cells located in different conditions within the host, and to prevent drug resistance.

Dosage

ORAL › 125 mg dispersible tablet (Rx)

Chronic iron overload, transfusion-related, by mouth, ADULT and CHILD >5 to 17 years, initially 20 mg/kg once daily,
adjust dose in increments or decrements of 5–10 mg/kg every 3–6 months when needed (maximum, 40 mg/kg
daily); CHILD 2–5 years, initially 20 mg/kg once daily, may require higher titration doses.

Administration

ORAL › 125 mg dispersible tablet (Rx)

Should be taken on an empty stomach, at least 30 minutes before meals, preferably at the same time daily. Do NOT
swallow the tablet whole. Disperse tablet completely by stirring in 100–200 mL of water, apple juice, or orange
juice until a fine suspension is obtained. Disperse <1 g in 105 mL and ≥1 g in 210 mL of liquid. Consume contents
completely. Rinse the glass with a little water or juice to resuspend any residue and drink the resulting
suspension. Do NOT disperse in fizzy drinks or milk. Do NOT chew, break, or crush the tablet.

Renal Impairment:
In patients with CrCl of 40–60 mL/minute, reduce starting

Do not administer if CrCl <60 mL/minute.

Hepatic Impairment:
In patients with moderate impairment, considerably reduce
dose followed by progressive increase up to a limit of
50%.
Do not administer in patients with severe hepatic

Indications

Management of chronic iron overload due to
repetitive blood transfusions in patients > 2 years.

High-risk myelodysplastic syndromes;
advanced malignancies; platelet counts <50,000
cells/mm3; renal impairment (CrCl <60 mL/min); severe
hepatic impairment

Precautions

WARNING: May cause acute renal failure, hepatic
failure, and death. GI hemorrhages may be fatal,
especially in the elderly.

Bone marrow suppression (e.g. neutropenia,
agranulocytosis, worsening anemia, and
thrombocytopenia, including fatal events);
Hypersensitivity (e.g. anaphylaxis and angioedema within
the first month of treatment);
Skin reactions (e.g. severe cutaneous adverse reactions;
erythema multiforme);
Auditory abnormalities (e.g. high frequency hearing loss,
and decreased hearing);
Ocular abnormalities (e.g. lens opacities, cataracts,
elevations in intraocular pressure, and retinal disorders);
Elderly;
Children;
Lactation.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common:
Gastrointestinal disturbance, headache, fatigue, dizziness, rashes, skin pigmentation, erythema multiforme, pyrexia,
pruritus, anxiety, sleep disorders, infections, pharyngolaryngeal pain, edema,
leucocytoclastic vasculitis, urticaria, alopecia, renal tubulopathy, proteinuria, hearing loss, visual disorders

Less Common: Stevens-Johnson syndrome, hypersensitivity
reactions, urticaria, alopecia, toxic epidermal necrolysis,
acute renal failure, tubulointerstitial nephritis, hepatic
failure, GI perforation, worsening anemia
Potentially Fatal: Hepatitis, agranulocytosis,
neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, upper GI ulceration and
haemorrhage.

Drug Interactions

Avoid concomitant use with:
Decreases serum concentration of Deferasirox:
Bile Acid Sequestrants, e.g., Cholestyramine, CYP3A4
Substrates, e.g., Cyclosporine, Hormonal Contraceptives,
Simvastatin, UGT Inducers, e.g., Rifampicin, Phenytoin,
Phenobarbital, Ritonavir

Incompatible with the following products:
Aluminum-containing Antacids (chelates with aluminum)

Increases serum concentration of Deferasirox:
CYP1A2 Substrates, e.g., Duloxetine, Theophylline,
CYP2C8 Substrates, e.g., Carbamazepine, Rifampicin,
Phenytoin

Reduces therapeutic effect of Deferasirox:
UGT Inducers, e.g., Rifampicin, Phenytoin,
Phenobarbital, Ritonavir

Mechanism of Action

An orally active iron chelating agent that binds with ferric ion and removes it from the blood stream

IM › IV INFUSION › SC › 500 mg powder (as mesylate), vial (Rx)

Dosage

IM / IV INFUSION / SC › 500 mg powder (as mesylate), vial (Rx)

Acute iron toxicity, by IM injection, ADULT, initially 1,000 mg, may be followed by 500 mg every 4 hours for 2 doses,
subsequent doses of 500 mg have been administered every 4–12 hours based on clinical response (maximum
recommended dose, 6,000 mg daily); ADOLESCENT and CHILD, 90 mg/kg per dose every 8 hours (maximum, 6,000
mg in 24 hours); by IV infusion, ADULT, initially 1,000 mg at 15 mg/kg per hour, subsequent doses may be given over
4–12 hours at a rate not to exceed 125 mg/hour; ADOLESCENT and CHILD, 15 mg/kg per hour (maximum, 6,000 mg in
24 hours). Chronic iron overload, by IM injection, ADULT, 500 to 1,000 mg daily (maximum, 1,000 mg daily); by IV
infusion, ADULT, 40–50 mg/kg daily (maximum, 60 mg/kg daily) over 8–12 hours for 5–7 days per week; ADOLESCENT
and CHILD ≥3 years, 20–40 mg/kg daily over 8–12 hours for 5–7 days per week, not to exceed 40 mg/kg daily until
growth has ceased; by SQ injection, ADULT, 1,000–2,000 mg daily or 20–40 mg/kg daily over 8–24 hours;
ADOLESCENT and CHILD ≥3 years, 20–40 mg/kg daily over 8–12 hours (maximum daily dose, 1,000–2,000 mg).

Administration

IM / IV INFUSION / SC › 500 mg powder (as mesylate), vial (Rx)

Administer by IV injection when severe toxicity is evidenced by cardiovascular collapse or systemic symptoms
(coma, shock, metabolic acidosis, or GI bleeding) or potentially severe intoxications (peak serum iron level >500
micrograms/dL). IM injection may be used for patients with acute iron toxicity that do not exhibit severe symptoms,
and in the treatment of chronic iron toxicity. NOTE: Limit vitamin C supplements in adults at 200 mg daily in divided
doses, in children ≥10 years at 100 mg daily, and in children <10 years at 50 mg daily.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Initiate at the lower end of the dosing range.
Renal Impairment:
Deferoxamine is readily dialyzable.
In CrCl 10–50 mL/minute and CRRT, administer 25 to 50%
of normal dose.

In CrCl <10 mL/minute, hemodialysis, and peritoneal
dialysis, avoid use.

Indications

Adjunct in the treatment of acute iron
intoxication; treatment of chronic iron overload
secondary to multiple transfusions

Contraindications

Severe renal disease; anuria

Precautions

Acute respiratory distress syndrome, ARDS;
Auditory effects (eg. tinnitus, high frequency hearing loss);
Ocular effects (eg. blurred vision, cataracts, corneal
opacities, decreased visual acuity, impaired peripheral,
color, and night vision, optic neuritis, retinal pigment
abnormalities, scotoma, visual loss or defect);
Growth retardation;
Infection (increased susceptibility to infection with Yersinia
enterocolitica and Yersinia pseudotuberculosis);
Mucormycosis;
Renal effects (e.g., increases in serum creatinine, acute
renal failure and renal tubular disorders; monitor for
changes in renal function);
Urine discoloration;
Elderly (eg. hearing loss and ocular disorders);
Pregnancy;
Lactation.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common and Less Common:
Flushing, hypotension, shock,
tachycardia, brain disease, dizziness, headache,
neuropathy, paresthesia, seizure, skin rash, urticaria,
growth suppression (children), hyperparathyroidism
(aggravated), hypocalcemia, abdominal distress,
abdominal pain, diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, dysuria,
urine discoloration (reddish), dysplasia (metaphyseal;
children <3 years; dose related), leukopenia,
thrombocytopenia, anaphylaxis, hepatic insufficiency,
muscle spasm, angioedema, hypersensitivity, infection
(Yersinia, mucormycosis), injection site reaction,
arthralgia, myalgia, blurred vision, cataract,
chromatopsia, corneal opacity, decreased peripheral
vision, decreased visual acuity, nocturnal amblyopia,
optic neuritis, retinal pigment changes, scotoma, vision
loss, visual field defect, hearing loss, tinnitus, acute renal
failure, renal tubular disease, acute respiratory distress
(dyspnea, cyanosis, and/or interstitial infiltrates),
asthma, fever

Drug Interactions

Avoid concomitant use with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects Deferoxamine:
Ascorbic Acid (left ventricular dysfunction), Multivitamins
/ Fluoride with ADE, Multivitamins / Minerals with ADEK,
Folate, Iron (left ventricular dysfunction), Multivitamins /
Minerals with AE, no Iron

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Prochlorperazine
(prolonged loss of consciousness)

TEST INTERATIONS. TIBC may be falsely elevated with high
serum iron concentrations or deferoxamine therapy.

Mechanism of Action

A chelating agent that complexes with ferric trivalent ions to
form ferrioxamine, which is removed by the kidneys. This
slows accumulation of hepatic iron and retards or
eliminated progression of hepatic fibrosis.

Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Blood pressure-lowering agents; Hypotensionassociated
agents (hypotensive effect); Dopamine; Epinephrine (nasal; oral inhalation and systemic); Norepinephrine
(arrhythmogenic effect); Metyrosine; Ropinirole, Rotigotine, Pramipexole (sedative effect); Neuromuscular-blocking
Agents, Nondepolarizing, e.g., Atracurium (neuromuscular-blocking effect) QTc-prolonging Agents (QTc-prolonging
effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Desflurane: Ephedrine nasal and systemic (arrhythmogenic effect);
Methylphenidate (hypertensive effect); Other CNS Depressants; Rufinamide (sleepiness and dizziness)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Amifostine (hypotensive effect); Fenoterol,
Formoterol, Isoproterenol, Metaraminol (arrhythmogenic effect); SSRIs (psychomotor impairment)

Mechanism of Action

Desflurane is a highly fluorinated, volatile methyl ethyl esther used for maintenance of general anesthesia. It
enhances inhibitory postsynaptic activity and inhibits excitatory synaptic activity, resulting in reversible loss of
consciousness and of pain sensations, suppression of voluntary motor activity, reduction of autonomic reflexes, and
sedation of respiratory and the cardiovascular system.

Indications

For the treatment of central diabetes insipidus and primary nocturnal enuresis in children aged 5 years and above

Contraindications

For the treatment of central diabetes insipidus and primary nocturnal enuresis in children aged 5 years and above

Precautions

Allergic reactions;
Hyponatremia;
Thrombotic events (use with caution in patients predisposed to thrombus formation);
Von Willebrand disease type 2B; Habitual or psychogenic polydipsia (use with caution);
Primary nocturnal enuresis (interrupt treatment if the patient experiences an acute illness, e.g. fever, recurrent
vomiting, or diarrhea);
Vigorous exercise;
Any condition associated with increase in water consumption;
Elderly and children.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Flushing (facial), headache, skin rash, hyponatremia, water intoxication, abdominal cramps, burning
sensation at injection site, sore throat, erythema or swelling at injection site, cough, nasal congestion, upper
respiratory tract infection, increased serum transaminases (transient; with tablets),
Less Common: Abnormality in thinking, agitation, balanitis, chest pain, diarrhea, drowsiness, pain, coma, dyspepsia,
insomnia, edema, localized warm feeling, palpitations, photophobia, seizure, eye pruritus, tachycardia, vomiting,
vulvar pain
Rare: Anaphylaxis, hypersensitivity reaction

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Desmopressin:
Carbamazepine, Chlorpromazine, Chlorpropramide, Lamotrigine, Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatory Agents, Opioid
Analgesics e.g. morphine, fentanyl, Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors e.g. Fluoxetine, Sertraline, Tricyclic
Antidepressants e.g. Amitriptyline Imipramine, Urea

Reduces therapeutic effect of Desmopressin:
Demeclocycline, Epinephrine [large doses], Ethyl Alcohol, Heparin, Lithium

Avoid concomitant use with:
Reduces therapeutic effect of Desmopressin:
Tolvaptan

Mechanism of Action

A synthetic analogue of the antidiuretic hormone arginine vasopressin. It increases cAMP in renal tubular cells in a
dose dependent manner. This increases water permeability, resulting in decreased urine volume and increased urine
osmolality, as well as increases plasma levels of von Willebrand factor, factor VIII, and tPA contributing to a shortened
activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) and bleeding time.

INTRAVENOUS Volume expansion or shock: by IV infusion, ADULT, initially 500–1,000 mL at a rate of 20–40
mL/minute for not longer than 3 days (maximum dose, 20 mL/kg during the first 24-hour period and 10 mL/kg daily
thereafter).

blood coagulation. Monitor central venous pressure during the initial period of infusion to detect fluid overload.
Stop immediately if signs of anaphylactic reactions, or oliguria, or renal failure appear. Maintain hematocrit at 30%
or higher and observe for signs of bleeding complications.

Renal impairment; hemorrhage; chronic liver disease; patients at risk of developing pulmonary edema or heart
failure; Lactation (use with caution).

Note: Do not mix IV product with other medications. When given intravenously, continuous infusion is not
recommended because of precipitation in IV fluids and absorption of drug into infusion bags and tubing. In
children, do not exceed 1 to 2 mg/minute IVP; in adults 5 mg/minute. WARNING: Parenterally administered
diazepam may cause hypotension and/or respiratory depression, particularly if the drug is administered too rapidly
IV. When given IV, diazepam should be administered directly into a large vein to avoid thrombosis; if this is not
feasible, the drug should be given into the tubing of a flowing IV solution as close as possible to the vein insertion.
Small veins such as those of the wrist or the dorsum of the hand should not be used. Care should be taken to avoid
intra-arterial administration or extravasation. Alternatively, some clinicians have suggested IV administration of
dilute solutions of the drug to avoid extravasation; however, the drug may precipitate when diluted and the
manufacturers do not recommend this method of administration. Although diazepam may also be given by deep IM
injection, this route of administration of the drug is rarely justified because absorption is slow and erratic. Therapy
with oral diazepam should replace parenteral administration as soon as possible. WARNING: Diazepam is a
vesicant; ensure proper needle or catheter placement prior to and during infusion; avoid extravasation.
Extravasation management: If extravasation occurs, stop IV administration immediately and disconnect (leave
cannula/needle in place) ; gently aspirate extravasated solution (do NOT flush the line) ; remove needle/cannula;
elevate extremity. Apply dry cold compresses. See Diazepam under Other Antiepileptics in Antiepileptics for other
information.

Dose Adjustment

Debilitated Patients/Geriatric:
Oral: Initially 2-2.5 mg once or twice daily. Slowly increase as needed and tolerated.

NOTE: Due to its long-acting metabolite, diazepam is not considered a drug of choice in the elderly. Long-acting
benzodiazepines have been associated with falls in the elderly.

Renal Impairment:
Initially 2-2.5 mg once or twice daily. Slowly increase as needed and tolerated.

NOTE: Hemodialysis – Not dialyzable (0%-5%); supplemental dose is not necessary.

Hepatic Impairment:
Initially 2-2.5 mg once or twice daily and slowly increase as needed and tolerated for mild to moderate hepatic
impairment. Contraindicated in severe hepatic impairment. If treatment is necessary, benzodiazepines with shorter
half lives are safer, such as temazepam or oxazepam.

Page 2 of 5



April 24, 2026, 11:55:14 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Indications

Short-term management of anxiety disorders, symptoms of anxiety, ethanol withdrawal symptoms, skeletal muscle
relaxant, convulsive disorders (adjunct), preoperative or preprocedural sedation and amnesia or perioperative
anxiety (injection only), anxiety during endoscopic procedures (adjunct, injection only), anxiety relief prior to
cardioversion (IV), acute agitation, tremor, impending or acute delirium tremens, hallucinosis in acute alcohol
withdrawal, skeletal muscle spasm due to reflex spasm to local pathology, spasticity caused by upper motor neuron
disorder, athethosis, Stiffman syndrome, initial treatment of status epilepticus

NOTE: Diazepam does not have analgesic, antidepressant, or antipsychotic properties.

Page 3 of 5



April 24, 2026, 11:55:14 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Myasthenia gravis; severe respiratory insufficiency; severe hepatic insufficiency; sleep apnea syndrome; acute
narrow-angle glaucoma; not for use in infants <6 months of age (oral).

Precautions

WARNING: Concomitant use of benzodiazepines and opioids may result in profound sedation, respiratory depression,
coma, and death. Reserve concomitant prescribing of these drugs for use in patients for whom alternative treatment
options are inadequate. Limit dosages and durations to the minimum required. Follow patients for signs and
symptoms of respiratory depression and sedation.

When given IV, there is a risk of propylene glycol toxicity; monitor closely if using for prolonged periods or at high
doses.

To be prescribed and dispensed through Yellow Rx. Only one (1) drug preparation shall be prescribed in one single
prescription form. Partial filling allowed. STRICTLY NO REFILL.

Diazepam 5 mg requires an S-2 license for physicians to be able to prescribe it and an S-1 license with a triplicate
prescription for pharmacists to dispense it.

Anterograde amnesia; CNS depression;
Opioids–should be reduced by approximately one third when diazepam is added;
Psychiatric and paradoxical reactions (discontinue if reactions occur);
Convulsive disorders (abrupt withdrawal - seizures); Depression;
Drug abuse; tolerance, psychological and physical dependence may occur with prolonged used (generally
>10 days);
Hepatic and renal impairment; Impaired gag reflex;
Post-traumatic Stress Disorder;
Respiratory disease (lower dose is recommended for chronic insufficiency).

Special populations:
Debilitated/elderly patients; Obese patients (prolonged action when discontinued).
Psychotic patients (use of diazepam is not recommended in place of appropriate therapy.
Pregnancy (not recommended during pregnancy, especially during the first trimester. Drug should be tapered if
discontinued. Women who have seizures in the second half of pregnancy should be assessed for eclampsia before
any change is made to antiepileptic treatment.
Lactation (Because diazepam and its metabolites may be present in breast milk for prolonged periods following
administration, it is recommended to discontinue breast- feeding for an appropriate period of time.).
Benzyl alcohol and derivatives (potentially fatal toxicity “gasping syndrome” in neonates).

MONITORING PARAMETERS: Respiratory, cardiovascular, and mental status; check for orthostasis. /For critically ill
mechanically-ventilated service patients: Monitor depth of sedation with either the Richmond Agitation Sedation
Scale (RASS) or Sedation-Agitation Scale (SAS).

STORAGE AND STABILITY: Injection: Store at 20°C to 25°C (68°F to 77°F) ; excursions permitted to 15°C to 30°C (59°F
to 86°F). Protect from light. Potency is retained for up to 3 months when kept at room temperature. Most stable at pH
48; hydrolysis occurs at pH <3. Tablet: Store at 15°C to 30°C (59°F to 86°F).

Page 4 of 5



April 24, 2026, 11:55:14 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

NOTE: Adverse reactions may vary by route of administration.

Common: Localized phlebitis, vasodilatation, amnesia, ataxia, confusion, depression, drowsiness the next day,
lightheadedness the next day, dysarthria, muscle weakness, constipation, diarrhea, nausea, pain at injection site;
tremor, asthma, bradypnea; paradoxical increase in aggression.

Less common: Changes in libido (in adults), dizziness, dysarthria, gastro-intestinal disturbances, gynecomastia,
headache, hypotension (in adults), incontinence, salivation changes, slurred speech, tremor, urinary retention,
vertigo, visual disturbances.

Rare: Apnea, changes in libido (in children) respiratory depression (overdose), hepatic dysfunction, renal
dysfunction, blood disorders, hallucinations, mania, hypotension (in children), jaundice, skin reactions.

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances the toxic/adverse effects of SSRIs (psychomotor impairment)

Enhances the toxic/adverse effects of diazepam: Cosyntropin (hepatotoxic)
CNS depressants (central effects)
Flumazenil (precipitate seizures)

Enhances the sedative effect of the following drugs: Pramipexole, Ropinirole, Rotigotine

Decreases therapeutic effect of diazepam: Theophylline derivatives e.g. Aminophylline

FOOD INTERACTIONS: Diazepam serum concentrations may be decreased if taken with food. Grapefruit juice may
increase diazepam serum concentrations. Avoid concurrent use of grapefruit juice. Maintain adequate hydration,
unless instructed to restrict fluid intake.

TEST INTERACTIONS: False-negative urinary glucose determinations when using Clinistix® or Diastix®.

Mechanism of Action

Diazepam is a benzodiazepine that exerts anxiolytic, sedative, muscle- relaxant, anticonvulsant and amnestic effects
due to its facilitation of the action of gamma aminobutyric acid (GABA), an inhibitory neurotransmitter in the central
nervous system.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Reduce dose.

Renal Impairment:
Reduce dose if used for prolonged periods.

Hepatic Impairment:
Reduce maintenance dose.

Indications

Skeletal muscle relaxant; preoperative or preprocedural sedation and amnesia

Contraindications

Respiratory depression; marked neuromuscular respiratory weakness, including unstable myasthenia gravis; acute
pulmonary insufficiency; sleep apnea syndrome; phobic or obsessional states; hyperkinesis; severe hepatic
impairment

Precautions

WARNING: Overdose may produce coma and respiratory depression.

When given intravenously, agents for correcting hypotension and facilities for supporting ventilation (e.g., AMBU bag,
oxygen) in cases of respiratory depression should be available. In such cases, the patient should be referred to the
nearest hospital immediately;

Hepatic impairment and renal impairment (avoid prolonged use and abrupt withdrawal);

Respiratory disease;

Muscle weakness may worsen;

History of alcohol or drug abuse, marked personality disorder (use only if the indication is clear, such as seizure
control)

Porphyria;

Pregnancy (high doses during late pregnancy or labor may cause neonatal hypothermia, hypotonia, and respiratory
depression)

Breastfeeding (monitor infant for drowsiness).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Coma or sensorial depression, hypotension, respiratory depression

Rare: Allergic reactions including anaphylaxis, jaundice, transient elevated liver function tests

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Diazepam: Amlodipine (hypotensive effect)
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: CNS Depressants, e.g., Alcohol, Sedatives, Tranquilizers (CNS
depression; sedative effect), Enalapril, Furosemide, Hydrochlorothiazide, Methyldopa, Nitrates, e.g., Isosorbide
Dinitrate (hypotensive effect),
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Diazepam: Azole Derivatives, e.g., Fluconazole
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Chlorphenamine, Chlorpromazine (sedative effect),
Seizure-inducing Drugs (lowers seizure threshold)
Reduces therapeutic effect of Diazepam: Theophylline (sedative effects)

Avoid concomitant use with:
Reduces therapeutic effect of Diazepam: Rifampicin

Mechanism of Action

A benzodiazepine that binds to stereospecific benzodiazepine receptors on the postsynaptic GABA neuron which
results to enhanced inhibitory effect of GABA on neuronal excitability. This results to increased neuronal membrane
permeability to chloride ions.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Initiate therapy using the lowest recommended dose and frequency.

Renal Impairment:
In patients with advanced renal disease or significant renal impairment, use is not recommended.

Hepatic Impairment:
May require dose adjustment. Drug undergoes extensive hepatic metabolism.

Indications

Management of mild to moderate acute pain and moderate to severe acute pain.

Contraindications

Patients who have experienced asthma, urticaria, or other allergic-type reactions after taking aspirin or other
NSAIDs; treatment of perioperative pain in the setting of coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) surgery; moderate to
severe renal impairment in the perioperative period and who are at risk for volume depletion.

Mechanism of Action

A reversible cyclooxygenase1 and 2 (COX1 and 2) enzyme inhibitor resulting in decreased formation of prostaglandin
precursors. It exhibits antipyretic, analgesic, and anti-inflammatory properties.

Indications

Treatment of patients with functional bowel or irritable bowel syndrome; adjunct in GI disorders characterized as
functional disturbances of gastrointestinal motility.

Contraindications

Obstructive diseases of the GI tract; severe ulcerative colitis; reflux esophagitis; unstable cardiovascular status in
acute hemorrhage; obstructive uropathy; glaucoma; myasthenia gravis; breastfeeding; infants <6 months of age;
hypersensitivity to dicycloverine or any component of the formulation.

Precautions

WARNING: Do NOT administer to children <6 months.
Diarrhea;
Hot weather and/or exercise (heat exhaustion may occur); Psychosis and delirium;
Elderly (avoid long-term use);
Infants (serious respiratory reactions, central nervous system symptoms, and deaths have been reported).
SKILLED TASKS: May cause drowsiness or blurred vision. Avoid performing tasks, which require mental alertness,
e.g., operating machinery or driving.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Dizziness, xerostomia, nausea, blurred vision, somnolence, nervousness, weakness.

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Dicycloverine: Anticholinergic Agents e.g., Atropine
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Opioid Analgesics (constipation; urinary retention)
Avoid concomitant use with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Dicycloverine: Ipratropium, Oral Inhalation (anticholinergic effect)

Mechanism of Action

Dicyclomine is a weak muscarinic receptor antagonist that also has nonspecific direct spasmolytic effects on smooth
muscle of the GI tract. It is occasionally used in the treatment of diarrhea-predominant irritable bowel syndrome.

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Diphenhydramine: CNS Depressants, e.g., Alcohol, Anxiolytic Sedatives,
Barbiturates, Hypnotics, Neuroleptics, Opioid Analgesics (sedative effect)
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Beta Blockers, e.g., Labetalol, Metoprolol, Propranolol
(cardiovascular effects)

Mechanism of Action

Diphenhydramine is a monoethanolamine-derived, firstgeneration H1 receptor antagonist, which exhibits
antimuscarinic and pronounced sedative property, with low incidence of GI side effects.

DIPHTHERIA, TETANUS, PERTUSSIS, HEPATITIS B

COMBINED VACCINE

Administration

Indications

Active immunization against diphtheria, tetanus, pertussis, hepatitis B, and invasive disease caused by Haemophilus
influenzae type b

Contraindications

vaccination; thrombocytopenia or any coagulation disorders

Mechanism of Action

A combination vaccine that promotes active immunity to diphtheria, tetanus, pertussis, hepatitis B and Haemophilus
influenzae type b (Hib) by inducing production of specific antibodies and antitoxins.

DIPHTHERIA-TETANUS TOXOID (Td)

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

J07AM51

ANATOMICAL

Anti-Infectives for systemic use

THERAPEUTIC

Vaccines

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Bacterial Vaccines

CHEMICAL CLASS

Tetanus Vaccines

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IM › ≥ 2 IU diphtheria toxoid + ≥ 40 IU tetanus toxoid per 0.5 mL ampule (For 7 yrs. old and above) (Rx)

IM › ≥ 2 IU diphtheria toxoid + ≥ 40 IU tetanus toxoid per 0.5 mL , multidose vial (10 doses per vial) (Rx)

Dosage

IM › ≥ 2 IU diphtheria toxoid + ≥ 40 IU tetanus toxoid per 0.5 mL ampule (For 7 yrs. old and above) (Rx)

Primary immunization, by IM injection, ADULT (NOT previously immunized), 2 primary doses of 0.5 mL each, given at
an interval of 8 weeks, then a third (reinforcing) dose of 0.5 mL 6–8 months later. Booster immunization, by IM
injection, ADULT (with complete primary immunization series), 0.5 mL every 10 years; CHILD 11–12 years, 0.5 mL as a
single dose when at least 5 years have elapsed since last dose of toxoid-containing vaccine; subsequent routine
doses are not recommended more often than every 10 years [NOTE: If Tdap is given as part of catchup dosing at 7–
10 years of age, the 11–12 year booster is not needed; regular Td booster immunizations should begin 10 years after
the last dose of the primary series]. Tetanus prophylaxis in wound management should be based on if the status
of the wound (clean or contaminated) and the immunization status of the patient. Wound management includes
proper use of tetanus toxoid and/or tetanus immune globulin (TIG), wound cleaning, and (if required) surgical
debridement and the proper use of antibiotics. Clean Minor Wounds History of Immunization Doses: Uncertain or
<3 doses Tetanus Toxoid: Yes TIG: No History of Immunization Doses: 3 or more doses Tetanus Toxoid: No, Yes if
≥10 years since last dose TIG: No All Other Wounds History of Immunization Doses: Uncertain or <3 doses Tetanus
Toxoid: Yes TIG: Yes History of Immunization Doses: 3 or more doses Tetanus Toxoid: No, Yes if ≥5 years TIG: No

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 3:00:10 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

IM › ≥ 2 IU diphtheria toxoid + ≥ 40 IU tetanus toxoid per 0.5 mL ampule (For 7 yrs. old and above) (Rx)

For IM administration only into the deltoid muscle. Do NOT administer by IV or SC administration. Shake suspension
well before use. Do NOT mix with other vaccines or injections. Use different needles and syringes for each
injection. Administer while patient is seated or lying down to prevent syncope related injuries. NOTE: For patients
at risk of hemorrhage following IM injection, administer by IM only if, in the opinion of the physician familiar with the
patient's bleeding risk, the vaccine can be administered by this route with reasonable safety. Use a fine needle (23
gauge or smaller) and apply firm pressure to the site (without rubbing) for at least 2 minutes. If the patient
receives anti-hemophilia or other similar therapy, intramuscular vaccination can be scheduled shortly after such
therapy is administered. Patients on anticoagulant therapy should be considered to have the same bleeding risks
and treated as those with clotting factor disorders.

Indications

Tetanus and diphtheria toxoids adsorbed for adult use

NOTE: For 10 years old and above.

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to diphtheria, tetanus toxoid, or any component of the formulation

Precautions

Anaphylactoid or hypersensitivity reactions;
Arthus-type hypersensitivity; syncope (has been reported with use);
Acute illness;
Bleeding disorders, including thrombocytopenia;
Guillain-Barré syndrome;
Altered immunocompetence (may have a reduced response to vaccination);
Children (pediatric dosage form (DT) should only be used in patients 6 weeks to ≤6 years of age;
Administer Td for children ≥7 years of age; apnea has occurred following administration in premature infants).
Pregnancy (reproduction studies have not been conducted);
Lactation (not known if excreted into breast milk; inactivated vaccines do not affect the safety of breastfeeding for
the mother or the infant).

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 3:00:10 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Headache, tiredness, chills, fever, rash, lymph node swelling, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, injection site
reactions (pain, erythema, swelling), body ache, muscle weakness, sore or swollen joints
Less Common: Allergic reactions, anaphylactic reactions, arthralgia, dizziness, fatigue, injection site reactions
(cellulitis, induration, nodules, warmth), lymphadenopathy, musculoskeletal stiffness, myalgia, pain, pain in
extremities, paresthesia, peripheral edema, seizure, syncope, weakness

Drug Interactions

Avoid concomitant use with:
Reduces therapeutic effect of Diphtheria-Tetanus Toxoids:
Belimumab
Fingolimod [administer at least 2 weeks apart; if vaccinated during fingolimod therapy, revaccinate 2–3 months after
fingolimod discontinuation]
Immunosuppressants [Cytarabine, Liposomal] [administer at least 2 weeks apart; if vaccinated during
immunosuppressant therapy, revaccinate at least 3 months after immunosuppressant discontinuation]
Meningococcal Polysaccharide (Groups A / C / Y and W135) Tetanus Toxoid Conjugate Vaccine [administer the
meningococcal polysaccharide (groups A / C / Y and W135) tetanus toxoid conjugate vaccine either together with or
at least one month before a tetanus toxoids-containing vaccine]

Mechanism of Action

A combination vaccine that promotes active immunity to diphtheria and tetanus by inducing production of specific
antibodies.

Pregnancy & Remarks

PREGNANCY CATEGORY

C

DIPHTHERIA-TETANUS TOXOIDS (DT)

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

J07AM51

ANATOMICAL

Anti-Infectives for systemic use

THERAPEUTIC

Vaccines

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Bacterial Vaccines

CHEMICAL CLASS

Tetanus Vaccines

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IM › 30 IU diphtheria toxoid + 40 IU tetanus toxoid per 0.5 mL ampule (Rx)

Dosage

IM › 30 IU diphtheria toxoid + 40 IU tetanus toxoid per 0.5 mL ampule (Rx)

Primary immunization, by IM injection, CHILD 6 weeks to ≤6 years, 0.5-mL administered as follows: First 3 doses at
2, 4, and 6 months of age; may be given as early as 6 weeks of age and repeated every 4–8 weeks; Fourth dose at
~15 to 18 months of age, but at least 6 months after third dose; may be given as early as 12 months of age, but at
least 6 months after third dose and the fourth dose; Fifth dose at 4–6 years of age, prior to starting school or
kindergarten; if the fourth dose is given at ≥4 years of age, fifth dose may be omitted [NOTE: For use when
pertussis-containing vaccine is contraindicated]. Booster immunization, by IM injection, CHILD, refer to Diphtheria
and Tetanus Toxoids and Acellular Pertussis Vaccine monograph for additional information.

Administration

IM › 30 IU diphtheria toxoid + 40 IU tetanus toxoid per 0.5 mL ampule (Rx)

For IM administration only. Administer into the anterolateral aspect of the thigh or the deltoid muscle. Do NOT
inject in the gluteal area. Do NOT inject by IV or SC. Shake suspension well before use. Do NOT mix with other
vaccines or injections. Use different needles and syringes for each injection. Administer while patient is seated or
lying down to prevent syncope related injuries. NOTE: For patients at risk of hemorrhage following IM injection,
administer by IM only if, in the opinion of the physician familiar with the patient's bleeding risk, the vaccine can be
administered by this route with reasonable safety. Use a fine needle (23 gauge or smaller) and apply firm pressure
to the site (without rubbing) for at least 2 minutes. If the patient receives anti-hemophilia or other similar therapy,
intramuscular vaccination can be scheduled shortly after such therapy is administered. Patients on anticoagulant
therapy should be considered to have the same bleeding risks and treated as those with clotting factor disorders.

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 3:00:15 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

NOTE: For less than 10 years old

Acute illness;
Bleeding disorders, including thrombocytopenia;
Guillain-Barré syndrome;
Altered immunocompetence (may have a reduced response to vaccination);
Children (pediatric dosage form (DT) should only be used in patients 6 weeks to ≤6 years of age;
Administer Td for children ≥7 years of age; apnea has occurred following administration in premature infants).
Pregnancy (reproduction studies have not been conducted);
Lactation (not known if excreted into breast milk; inactivated vaccines do not affect the safety of breastfeeding for
the mother or the infant).

Less Common: Allergic reactions, anaphylactic reactions, arthralgia, dizziness, fatigue, injection site reactions
(cellulitis, induration, nodules, warmth), lymphadenopathy, musculoskeletal stiffness, myalgia, pain, pain in
extremities, paresthesia, peripheral edema, seizure, syncope, weakness

DIPHTHERIA-TETANUS TOXOIDS AND ACELLULAR PERTUSSIS
VACCINE (DTaP)

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

J07AJ52

ANATOMICAL

Anti-Infectives for systemic use

THERAPEUTIC

Vaccines

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Bacterial Vaccines

CHEMICAL CLASS

Pertussis Vaccines

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IM › 0.5 mL pre-filled syringe (Rx)

IM › 0.5 mL vial (Rx)

Dosage

IM › 0.5 mL pre-filled syringe (Rx)

NOTE: Whenever possible, use the same product for all doses. Interruption of recommended schedule does not
require starting the series over. A delay between doses should not interfere with final immunity. Primary
immunization, by IM injection, CHILD and INFANT 6 weeks to <7 years, five 0.5 mL doses administered as follows:
Three doses at 2, 4, and 5 months of age; may be given as early as 6 weeks of age and repeated every 4–8 weeks;
Fourth doses at ~15 to 20 months of age but at least 6 months after third dose; may be given as early as 12 months
of age; Fifth doses at 4–6 years of age, prior to starting school or kindergarten; if fourth dose is given at ≥4 years
of age, the fifth dose may be omitted. Booster immunization, by IM injection, ADULT ≥19 years, 0.5 mL as a single
dose 5 years after last dose of tetanus toxoid, diphtheria toxoid, and/or pertussis-containing vaccine [NOTE:
Administer a single dose to replace a single dose of the 10 year Td booster in patients who have not previously
received Tdap or for whom vaccine status is not known];CHILD ≥10 years and ADOLESCENT ≤18 years, 0.5 mL as a
single dose 5 years after last dose of tetanus toxoid, diphtheria toxoid, and/or pertussis-containing vaccine
[NOTE: Administer as a single booster dose at 11 or 12 years of age in adolescents who have completed a childhood
DTaP vaccination series, followed by booster doses of Td every 10 years. Adolescents who have not received Tdap
at age 11 or 12 should receive a single dose of Tdap in place of a single Td booster dose]. For Wound Management,
see Diphtheria-Tetanus Toxoid (Td) under Vaccines – Bacterial Vaccines in Chapter 7: Anti-infectives for Systemic
Use for other information.

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:59:59 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

IM › 0.5 mL pre-filled syringe (Rx)

For IM administration only into the anterolateral aspect of the thigh or the deltoid muscle. Do NOT administer by IV
or SC. Shake suspension well before use. Store at 2–8°C. Freezing is the most common cause of vaccine damage.
Do NOT use a defrosted vaccine unless freezing is the recommended storage condition. Administer vaccines
while patient is seated or lying down to prevent syncope related injuries. NOTE: For patients at risk of hemorrhage
following IM injection, administer by IM only if, in the opinion of the physician familiar with the patient's bleeding
risk, the vaccine can be administered by this route with reasonable safety. If the patient receives antihemophilia or
other similar therapy, IM vaccination can be scheduled shortly after such therapy is administered. Patients on
anticoagulant therapy should be considered to have the same bleeding risks and treated as those with clotting
factor disorders. Administer to travelers regardless of the limitations if time is short. See Diphtheria-Tetanus
Toxoids (DT) under Vaccines – Bacterial Vaccines in Chapter 7: Antiinfectives for Systemic Use for other
information.

Indications

Active immunization against diphtheria, tetanus, and pertussis

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to diphtheria, tetanus toxoids, pertussis, or any component of the formulation; progressive
neurologic disorder, including infantile spasms, uncontrolled epilepsy or progressive epilepsy; encephalopathy
occurring within 7 days of administration and not attributable to another cause

Precautions

Anaphylactoid or hypersensitivity reactions;
Arthus-type hypersensitivity; syncope (has been reported with use);
Acute illness;
Bleeding disorders, including thrombocytopenia;
Guillain-Barré syndrome;
Altered immunocompetence (may have a reduced response to vaccination);
Children (pediatric dosage form (DT) should only be used in patients 6 weeks to ≤6 years of age;
Administer Td for children ≥7 years of age; apnea has occurred following administration in premature infants).
Pregnancy (reproduction studies have not been conducted);
Lactation (not known if excreted into breast milk; inactivated vaccines do not affect the safety of breastfeeding for
the mother or the infant).

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:59:59 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Apathy, drowsiness, irritability, lethargy, headache, fatigue, chills, skin rash, increased arm circumference,
GI disease, adenopathy, injection site reactions (erythema, pain, edema, tenderness, swelling), crying (prolonged or
persistent), fever, fussiness, myalgia, arthralgia
Less Common: Anaphylaxis, angioedema, apnea, back pain, brain disease, bronchitis, cellulitis, cough, cyanosis,
diabetes mellitus, diarrhea, encephalitis, erythema, facial paralysis, fatigue, Guillain-Barré syndrome,
hypersensitivity reaction, headache, hypoesthesia, hypotonia, hypotonic or hyporesponsive episode, IgA vasculitis,
immune thrombocytopenia, infantile spasm, injection site reaction (bruising, induration, mass, nodule, pruritus, rash,
sterile abscess, warmth), intussusception, lymphadenitis, lymphadenopathy, myalgia, myocarditis, myositis, nausea,
nerve compression, otalgia, paresthesia, peripheral edema (extensive), pruritus, respiratory tract infection,
screaming, seizure, sudden infant death syndrome, syncope, thrombocytopenia, urticaria

Drug Interactions

Avoid concomitant use with:
Reduces therapeutic effect of Diphtheria-Tetanus Toxoids:
Belimumab
Fingolimod [administer at least 2 weeks apart; if vaccinated during fingolimod therapy, revaccinate 2–3 months after
fingolimod discontinuation]
Immunosuppressants [Cytarabine, Liposomal] [administer at least 2 weeks apart; if vaccinated during
immunosuppressant therapy, revaccinate at least 3 months after immunosuppressant discontinuation]
Meningococcal Polysaccharide (Groups A / C / Y and W135) Tetanus Toxoid Conjugate Vaccine [administer the
meningococcal polysaccharide (groups A / C / Y and W135) tetanus toxoid conjugate vaccine either together with or
at least one month before a tetanus toxoids-containing vaccine]

Mechanism of Action

A three-component preparation of diphtheria and tetanus toxoids, and acellular pertussis vaccine that promotes
active immunity to diphtheria, tetanus, and pertussis by inducing production of specific antibodies.

Pregnancy & Remarks

PREGNANCY CATEGORY

DIPHTHERIA-TETANUS TOXOIDS AND PERTUSSIS VACCINE
(DTP)

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

J07AJ51

ANATOMICAL

Anti-Infectives for systemic use

THERAPEUTIC

Vaccines

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Bacterial Vaccines

CHEMICAL CLASS

Pertussis Vaccines

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IM › 0.5 mL ampule (Rx)

IM › 0.5 mL pre-filled syringe (Rx)

IM › 0.5 mL vial (Rx)

IM › 5 mL vial (Rx)

IM › 7.5 mL vial (Rx)

IM › 10 mL vial (Rx)

Dosage

IM › 0.5 mL ampule (Rx)

Primary immunization, by IM injection, ADULT, 0.5 mL; CHILD 1–6 years of age, two 0.5 mL doses separated by an
interval of 2 months, administer a third dose after 6–12 months; INFANT ≥6 weeks, three 0.5 mL doses with an
interval of at least 4 weeks between each dose.

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 3:00:05 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

IM › 0.5 mL ampule (Rx)

For IM administration only into the anterolateral aspect of the thigh or the deltoid muscle. Do NOT administer by IV
or SC. Shake suspension well before use. Store at 2–8°C. Freezing is the most common cause of vaccine damage.
Do NOT use a defrosted vaccine unless freezing is the recommended storage condition. Administer vaccines
while patient is seated or lying down to prevent syncope related injuries. NOT recommended for adolescents and
adults. NOTE: For patients at risk of hemorrhage following IM injection, administer by IM only if, in the opinion of
the physician familiar with the patient's bleeding risk, the vaccine can be administered by this route with
reasonable safety. If the patient receives anti-hemophilia or other similar therapy, IM vaccination can be scheduled
shortly after such therapy is administered. Patients on anticoagulant therapy should be considered to have the
same bleeding risks and treated as those with clotting factor disorders. Administer to travelers regardless of the
limitations if time is short.

Indications

Active immunization against diphtheria, tetanus, and pertussis

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to the any component of the formulation; severe acute neurological illness within 7 days of
pertussis vaccination; thrombocytopenia or any coagulation disorders

Precautions

Whole cell pertussis component is associated with more frequent minor adverse effects than acellular pertussis
component.
Anaphylactoid or hypersensitivity reactions (immediate treatment, including epinephrine 1:1000 should be available
during vaccine use);
Arthus-type hypersensitivity; syncope (has been reported with use);
Acute illness;
Bleeding disorders, including Guillain-Barré syndrome;
Neurologic disorders;
Thrombocytopenia;
Immunodeficiency.
Elderly (frequency of adverse effects increases with age and number of injections);
Children with proven, or suspected, underlying active or progressive neurologic disorders;
Pregnancy (adult vaccine may be used if necessary)

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 3:00:05 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Crying, drowsiness, irritability, limb swelling, restlessness
Less Common: Fever, lethargy, malaise, myalgia, transient injection site reactions (pain, redness, itching, burning,
small hard lump that persists for some weeks)
Rare: Allergic reactions including anaphylaxis, collapse, encephalopathy, headache, hypotonic-hyporesponsive
episodes, seizure, urticaria, peripheral neuropathy,

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Other vaccines and immunoglobulins [administer at least 4 weeks apart]

Avoid concomitant use with:
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Anti-infectives (may interfere with the development of a protective immune response)
Immunosuppressive Agents e.g. Corticosteroids Antineoplastic (decreases antibody response)

Mechanism of Action

A three-component preparation of diphtheria, tetanus and pertussis (whole-cell) vaccines whose rates of local and
systemic reactions are higher than acellular pertussis vaccine.

Pregnancy & Remarks

PREGNANCY CATEGORY

B / C (product specific)

DIPYRIDAMOLE

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

B01AC07

ANATOMICAL

Blood and Blood Forming Organs

THERAPEUTIC

Antithrombotic Agents

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Platelet Aggregation Inhibitors, Excluding Heparin

ORAL › 25 mg tablet (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › 25 mg tablet (Rx)

ORAL Adjunctive therapy for prophylaxis of thromboembolism with cardiac valve replacement: by mouth, ADULT,
75–200 mg 4 times daily

Administration

ORAL › 25 mg tablet (Rx)

Administer with water 1 hour before meals. NOTE: Preferably used in combination with aspirin. When used
concomitantly with aspirin, consider the cautions, precautions, and contraindications associated with aspirin.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Avoid use due to risk of orthostatic hypotension.

Indications

Secondary prevention of stroke in patients with a history of non-cardioembolic ischemic stroke or TIA (preferably
with aspirin); to decrease thrombosis after artificial heart valve replacement (with warfarin).

Cardiovascular disease (use with caution in patients with hypotension, unstable angina, and/or recent MI); hepatic
impairment; Elderly (use with caution); Lactation (use with caution).

Less Common: Alopecia, arthritis, cholelithiasis, dyspepsia, fatigue, hepatitis, hypersensitivity reaction,
hypotension, laryngeal edema, malaise, myalgia, nausea, palpitations, paresthesia, tachycardia, thrombocytopenia.

For management of cardiogenic or vascular shock; inotropic agent; cardiac decompensation

Arrhythmias (e.g. ventricular arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death; may increase ventricular response rate)

blood pressure, increased heart rate, localized phlebitis, ventricular ectopy (increased), headache, paresthesia,
nausea, local inflammation, local pain (from infiltration), leg cramps, dyspnea, fever

April 25, 2026, 12:09:58 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

IV › 10 mg/mL solution for infusion, 2 mL vial (Rx)

Dilute with NS or D5W to a final concentration of 0.3-0.74 mg/mL, and prepare in a glass bottle, polypropylene, or
polyolefin plastic bag to prevent leaching of plasticizers. Non-PVC tubing must be used. Preparation instructions
may vary by manufacturer, refer to specific prescribing information. NOTE: Extravasation may cause significant
irritation. Ensure proper needle or catheter placement prior to and during infusion. If extravasation occurs,
discontinue immediately and initiate appropriate extravasation management, including local injection of
hyaluronidase and moderate heat application to the affected area. Use a separate vein to administer any remaining
portion of the dose. Some preparations contain alcohol, which may affect the CNS and cause symptoms of alcohol
intoxication. Pre-medicate with corticosteroids for 3 days, beginning 1 day prior to docetaxel administration, to
reduce the severity of hypersensitivity reactions and fluid retention. Patients being treated for prostate cancer
with concurrent prednisone should be premedicated with oral dexamethasone at 12 hours, 3 hours, and 1 hour
prior to docetaxel administration.

Dose Adjustment

Hepatic Impairment:
If total bilirubin is greater than the ULN, or if AST and/or ALT
>1.5 times ULN concomitant with alkaline phosphatase
>2.5 times ULN, use is not recommended.

Specific for gastric or head and neck cancer:
AST/ALT >2.5 to =5 times ULN and alkaline phosphatase =2.5 times ULN, administer 80% of dose.
AST/ALT >1.5 to =5 times ULN and alkaline phosphatase >2.5 to =5 times ULN, administer 80% of dose.
AST/ALT >5 times ULN and /or alkaline phosphatase >5 times ULN, discontinue use.

Obesity:
Use actual body weight (full weight) for calculation of BSAbased or weight-based dosing, especially if the intent of
therapy is curative.
If dose reduction is done due to toxicity, consider resumption of full weight-based dosing with subsequent cycles,
especially if cause of toxicity is resolved.

Indications

Treatment of locally advanced or metastatic breast cancer (adjuvant treatment of operable nodepositive); treatment
of advanced gastric adenocarcinoma; treatment of recurrent and/or metastatic squamous cell head and neck
cancer; treatment of locally advanced or metastatic NSCLC; treatment of metastatic ovarian cancer following failure
of first-lineor subsequent chemotherapy; treatment of hormone refractory, metastatic prostate cancer

Page 2 of 5



April 25, 2026, 12:09:58 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to other medications containing polysorbate 80; neutrophil count <1,500/mm3, severe hepatic
impairment; pregnancy; breastfeeding

Precautions

WARNING: Patients with abnormal liver function, those receiving higher doses, and patients with NSCLC and a
history of prior treatment with platinum derivatives who receive single-agent docetaxel at a dose of 100 mg/m2 are
at higher risk for treatment-related mortality.

Avoid use in patients with bilirubin exceeding upper limit of normal (ULN) or AST and/or ALT >1.5 times ULN in
conjunction with alkaline phosphatase >2.5 times ULN. Patients with isolated transaminase elevations >1.5 times
ULN at an increased risk of neutropenic fever, although no increased incidence of toxic death.

Do NOT give to patients with neutrophil counts of <1,500 cells/mm3. Perform frequent blood cell counts to monitor
occurrence of neutropenia.

Do NOT administer to patients with a history of severe hypersensitivity to docetaxel or polysorbate 80. Severe
hypersensitivity reactions, characterized by generalized rash or erythema, hypotension, bronchospasms, or
anaphylaxis may occur and may be fatal.

Severe fluid retention, characterized by pleural effusion, ascites, peripheral edema, dyspnea at rest, cardiac
tamponade, generalized edema, and weight gain, has been reported.

WARNING: Hazardous agent. Use appropriate precautions for handling and disposal.

Bone marrow suppression (neutropenia may occur; neutropenia is the dose limiting toxicity; platelets should be
>100,000/mm3 prior to treatment; patients with increased liver function tests may experience more episodes of
neutropenia with more frequent severe infections);

Cutaneous reactions (e.g. erythema with edema and desquamation)

Fluid retention (may begin as lower extremity peripheral edema and become generalized; weight gain may be around
2 kg; administer corticosteroid, starting 1 day prior to administration, to reduce the incidence and severity of fluid
retention).

Hepatic impairment (increased risk of grade 4 neutropenia, infections, stomatitis, severe thrombocytopenia, skin
toxicity, or toxic death).

Hypersensitivity reactions (severe reactions occur; minor reactions include flushing or localized skin reactions;
discontinue for severe reactions; administer corticosteroid, starting 1 day prior to administration, to prevent or
reduce the severity ofhypersensitivity reactions)

Neurosensory symptoms (e.g. paresthesia, dysesthesia, neuropathic pain; reversal of symptoms may be delayed
after discontinuation).

Ocular adverse effects (cystoid macular edema may occur; promptly administer a comprehensive ophthalmic exam if
vision impairment occurs; majority experience tearing which subsided after therapy completion)

April 25, 2026, 12:09:58 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Secondary malignancies (treatment-related AML or myelodysplasia)

Weakness (fatigue or weakness may occur; weakness may be severe, lasting for a few days up to several weeks).

Common: Alopecia, anemia (dose-dependent), arthralgia, CNS toxicity (including neuropathy), decreased left

thrombocytopenia (dose-dependent), vomiting, weakness

Less Common: Abdominal pain, acute myelocytic leukemia, acute respiratory distress, anorexia, anaphylactic shock,
ascites, atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, AV block, bronchospasm, cardiac arrhythmia, cardiac failure, cardiac
tamponade, bradycardia, chest pain, chest tightness, colitis, confusion, conjunctivitis, constipation, cystoid macular
edema, deepvein thrombosis, dehydration, disease of the lacrimal apparatus (duct obstruction), disseminated
intravascular coagulation, drug fever, duodenal ulcer, ECG abnormality, dyspnea, erythema multiforme, esophagitis,
gastrointestinal hemorrhage, GI obstruction, GI perforation, hearing loss, hemorrhagic diathesis, hepatitis,
hypertension, hyponatremia, intestinalobstruction, interstitial pulmonary disease, ischemic colitis, ischemic heart
disease, loss of consciousness (transient), lymphedema (peripheral), multiorgan failure, lacrimation, myelodysplastic
syndrome, pain, myocardial infarction, neutropenic enterocolitis, ototoxicity, palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia,
pericardial effusion, pleural effusion, pneumonia, pneumonitis, pulmonary edema,pulmonary embolism, pulmonary

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Docetaxel: Trastuzumab (neutropenic effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Docetaxel: Denosumab (serious infections)
Reduces diagnostic effect of Coccidioides immitis Skin Test

A taxane derivative that inhibits DNA, RNA, and protein synthesis by preventing the depolymerization of

Indications

Gastrointestinal motility disorders e.g., diabetic gastroparesis and gastritis.

Prolactin-releasing pituitary tumor (prolactinoma); known existing prolongation of cardiac conduction intervals,
particularly QT; significant electrolyte disturbances; underlying cardiac disease (e.g., heart failure); moderate or

with potent CYP3A4 inhibitors, macrolides (e.g., erythromycin), protease inhibitors, or nefazodone; concomitant use
with QT-prolonging drugs.

Precautions

WARNING: Prolongs QT interval and poses a risk of torsades de pointes. Consult with physician prior to concomitant
use of a vasoconstrictor. Use epinephrine, mepivacaine, and levonordefrin with caution.

Prolactinoma;

Less Common: Abdominal cramps, appetite changes, conjunctivitis, constipation, diarrhea, dizziness, drug

April 25, 2026, 12:11:06 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Renal and Hepatic Impairment:
For mild-to-moderate impairment, dose reduction may be warranted. Use with caution
For severe impairment, refer patient to a specialist.

Indications

Hypovolemic shock and hemorrhagic shock as adjuvant therapy to volume replacement; cardiogenic shock (where
systolic BP <90 in adults); septic shock

Contraindications

Ischemic heart disease; tachyarrhythmias, ventricular fibrillation; pheochromocytoma; hyperthyroidism

Precautions

WARNING: This may cause peripheral ischemia in patients with history of occlusive vascular disease (e.g.,
atherosclerosis and Raynaud’s syndrome). In case of extravasation causing peripheral ischemia, use phentolamine
for local infiltration.

NOTE: Dosage is critical – at low doses, it stimulates myocardial contractility and increases cardiac output; however,
higher doses (more than 5 micrograms/kg per minute) cause vasoconstriction, which increases blood pressure and
may also cause worsening of heart failure.

Close monitoring of arterial and venous pressure and continuous ECG should be performed; treatment with
sympathomimetics should be guided by hemodynamic monitoring (individualize treatment depending on clinical
response); use low dose in cardiogenic shock due to MI; prolonged use of sympathomimetics may result in
diminution of therapeutic effect (downregulation of receptors; disproportionate increase in diastolic pressure)
Correct hypovolemia before treatment and maintain blood volume during treatment; correct hypoxia, hypercapnia,
and metabolic acidosis before or at the start of treatment (may reduce effectiveness and/or increase incidence of
adverse effects of dopamine)
Pulmonary hypertension (may be worsened due to dopamine-induced pulmonary vasoconstriction); suppresses
pituitary secretion of thyroid-stimulating hormone, growth hormone and prolactin
Elderly; Pregnancy (limited human data available)

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Anginal pain, chest pain, dyspnea, ectopic beats, flushing, headache, hypotension with dizziness, nausea,
palpitations, peripheral vasoconstriction, vomiting, tachycardia
Less Common: Abnormal ventricular conduction, bradycardia, extravasation (may cause necrosis and sloughing of
surrounding tissue), fainting, gangrene, hypertension, mydriasis, piloerection, uremia
Rare: Allergic reactions, ventricular arrhythmia

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Dopamine:
MAO Inhibitors, e.g., Linezolid, Phenelzine, Tranylcypromine

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Dopamine:
Phenytoin (hypotension; bradycardia)

Reduces therapeutic effect of Dopamine:
Alpha-adrenergic Blocking Agents

Avoid concomitant use with:
Decreases serum concentration of Dopamine:
Metoclopramide (inhibits GI absorption)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Dopamine:
Vasoconstrictors, e.g., Ergot Alkaloids (peripheral ischemia)

Reduces therapeutic effect of Dopamine:
Beta-adrenergic Blocking Agents, e.g., Propranolol, Metoprolol (cardiac effects); Chlorpromazine (hypertensive
effects); Fluphenazine (hypertensive effects); Haloperidol (hypertensive effects); Vasoconstrictors, e.g., Ergot
Alkaloids (peripheral vasoconstriction)

Mechanism of Action

A direct-acting sympathomimetic, which acts on beta1 receptors in cardiac muscles, leading to increased
contractility with little effect on rate. Dopamine may be given for short periods in the treatment of severe heart
failure; it also causes renal vasodilation, thus preserving renal perfusion and renal function. It also has peripheral
vasoconstricting properties when given at higher doses.

IV › 10 mg powder (as hydrochloride), vial (Rx)

Breast cancer, by IV push, ADULT, in combination with cyclophosphamide, 50-60 mg/m2 on day 1 of a 14 or 21day
cycle for 4-6 cycles. Metastatic solid tumors, leukemia, or lymphoma, by IV push, ADULT, as single-agent, 60–75
mg/m2 every 21 days; in combination therapy, 40–75 mg/m2 every 21 to 28 days. Acute lymphoblastic leukemia,
acute myeloid leukemia, Wilms’ tumor. Ewing’s sarcoma, osteosarcoma, and soft tissue sarcoma, CHILD: Always
consult institutional protocols. Maximum cumulative dose in children in 450 mg/m2. Lifetime cumulative dose
above 550 mg/m2 are associated with an increased risk of cardiotoxicity.

Page 1 of 5



April 25, 2026, 12:11:18 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

IV › 10 mg powder (as hydrochloride), vial (Rx)

For IV administration only. Administer IV push over at least 3–10 minutes or by continuous IV infusion via a central
venous line. Rate of administration varies by protocol, refer to individual protocol for details. Do NOT administer by
IM or SC. Protect from light until completionof infusion. Avoid contact with alkaline solutions. Monitor for local
erythematous streaking along vein and/or facial flushing, which indicates rapid infusion rate. Decrease rate if this
occurs. NOTE: Avoid extravasation. Ensure proper needle or catheter position prior to and during administration.
If extravasation occurs, discontinue infusion immediately and apply ice to the affected area. Associated with a
high emetic potential. Antiemetics are recommended to prevent nausea and vomiting.

Dose Adjustment

Hepatic Impairment:
Reduce doses in patients with impaired hepatic function.
For transaminases 2–3 times Upper Limit of Normal (ULN), administer 75% of dose.
For transaminases >3 times ULN or serum bilirubin 1.2 to 3 mg/dL, administer 50% of dose.
For serum bilirubin 3.1–5 mg/dL, administer 25% of dose.
In severe hepatic impairment, i.e., Child-Pugh class C or bilirubin >5 mg/dL, use is contraindicated.

Obesity:
Use actual body weight (full weight) for calculation of BSAbased or weight-based dosing, especially if the intent of
therapy is curative.
If dose reduction is done due to toxicity, consider resumption of full weight-based dosing with subsequent cycles,
especially if cause of toxicity is resolved.

Inadequate Marrow Reserve:
Consider lower doses due to advanced age, prior treatment, or neoplastic marrow infiltration. Cumulative doses
above 550 mg/m2 are associated with an increased risk of cardiomyopathy. Discontinue in patients who develop
signs or symptoms of cardiomyopathy.

Indications

Adjuvant therapy in women with evidence of axillary lymph node involvement following resection of primary breast
cancer; treatment of acute lymphoblastic leukemia, acute myeloid leukemia,Wilms’ tumor, neuroblastoma, soft
tissue and bone sarcomas, breast cancer, ovarian cancer, thyroid carcinoma, transitional cell bladder carcinoma,
gastric carcinoma, Hodgkin’s lymphoma, non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma, and bronchogenic carcinoma in which thesmall
cell histologic type is the most responsive compared with other cell types

Page 2 of 5



April 25, 2026, 12:11:18 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to doxorubicin, other anthracyclines, e.g., daunorubicin, epirubicin, idarubicin, anthracenediones,
e.g., mitoxantrone, or any component of the formulation; severe myocardial insufficiency; recent MI (within 4–6
weeks); severe arrhythmia; previous therapy with high cumulative doses of doxorubicin, daunorubicin, idarubicin, or
other anthracyclines, e.g., epirubicin, and anthracenediones, e.g., mitoxantrone; severe persistent drug-induced
myelosuppression or baseline neutrophil count <1500/mm3; severe hepatic impairment, i.e., Child-Pugh class C or
bilirubin >5 mg/dL; G6PD deficiency

Precautions

WARNING: Should be administered under the supervision of an experienced cancer chemotherapy physician,
experienced in the use of cancerchemotherapeutic agents. Administer in a facility sufficient to appropriately
diagnose and manage complications.

The risk for myocardial toxicity increases with cumulative exposure and with concomitant cardiotoxic therapy. The
incidence of irreversible myocardial toxicity increases as the total cumulative (lifetime) doses approach 300–500
mg/m2 (with an every-3-week regimen). Assess left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) with either an
echocardiogram or MUGA scan before, during, and after therapy. Increase frequency of assessments as the
cumulative dose exceeds 300 mg/m2.

Secondary acute myelogenous leukemia (AML) and myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) have been reported following
treatment.

May cause severe myelosuppression, which may result in serious infection, septic shock, transfusion requirements,
hospitalization, and death. May cause hemolytic anemia with G6PD deficiency.

WARNING: Hazardous agent. Use appropriate precautions for handling and disposal.

Bone marrow suppression (e.g. leukopenia, neutropenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia; monitor blood counts at
baseline and regularly during therapy).

Cardiotoxicity (delayed cardiotoxicity, sub-acute effects, e.g., pericarditis or myocarditis, early toxicity, e.g.,
tachyarrhythmias, sinus tachycardia, premature ventricular contractions, ventricular tachycardia, or bradycardia, and
ECG changes; dose-limiting, delayed toxicity may occur late in treatment or months to years after therapy; total
cumulative dose should be taken into account before treating with other anthracyclinesor anthracenediones,
cardiotoxic agents, or irradiation of chest; irreversible cardiotoxicity may occur at any dose level).

Fertility impairment (e.g. damage to spermatozoa and testicular tissue, oligospermia, azoospermia, or loss of fertility
may occur in men; infertility, amenorrhea, premature menopause may occur in women of reproductive potential).

Secondary malignancy (may occur within 1-3 years of treatment)

Tumor lysis syndrome (tumor lysis syndrome or hyperuricemia; urinary alkalinization and antihyperuricemic
prophylaxis may be required; monitor electrolytes, renal function, and hydration status).

Concurrent radiation therapy (radiation recall may occur; may increase radiation-induced toxicity to the myocardium,
mucosa, skin, and liver).

Page 3 of 5



April 25, 2026, 12:11:18 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Extravasation (extravasation during IV use causes severe local tissue damage leading to tissue injury, blistering,
ulceration, and necrosis may occur, requiring wide excision of the affected area and skin grafting).

Children (at an increased risk for developing delayed cardiotoxicity;may contribute to prepubertal growth failure in
children; may also contribute to gonadal impairment, usually temporary; radiation recall pneumonitis has been
reported in children receiving concomitant dactinomycin and doxorubicin).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Abdominal pain, acute cardiotoxicity (AV block, bradycardia, bundle branch block, ECG abnormalities,
extrasystoles (atrial orventricular), sinus tachycardia, ST-T wave changes, SVT, VT, tachyarrhythmia), alopecia,
amenorrhea, anemia, anorexia, colon necrosis, dehydration, delayed cardiotoxicity (decreased LVEF, CHF,
myocarditis, pericarditis), diarrhea, fluid discoloration (saliva, sweat, tears, urine), GI ulceration, hyperuricemia,
infertility (may be temporary), itching, leukopenia or neutropenia, malaise, mucositis, nausea, photosensitivity,
radiation recall, rash, skin “flare” at injection site, thrombocytopenia, urticaria, vomiting, weakness

Less Common: Anaphylaxis, azoospermia, bilirubin increased, chills, coma (in combination with cisplatin or
vincristine), conjunctivitis, fever, gonadal impairment (children), growth failure (prepubertal), hepatitis,
hyperpigmentation (nail, skin, oral mucosa), keratitis, lacrimation, myelodysplastic syndrome, neutropenic fever,
infection, neutropenic typhlitis, oligospermia, onycholysis, phlebosclerosis, radiation recall pneumonitis (children),
secondary acute myelogenous leukemia, seizure (in combination with cisplatin or vincristine), sepsis, shock,
systemic hypersensitivity (including urticaria, pruritus, angioedema, dysphagia, and dyspnea), SJS, TEN, urticaria

Page 4 of 5



April 25, 2026, 12:11:18 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Doxorubicin: Bevacizumab (cardiotoxic effect), Cyclophosphamide
(cardiotoxic effect), Denosumab (serious infections)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Mercaptopurine (hepatotoxic effect)
Reduces diagnostic effect of Coccidioides immitis Skin Test

Avoid concomitant use with:
Decreases metabolism of Doxorubicin: Taxane Derivatives [e.g., Paclitaxel (except Docetaxel)]
Decreases serum concentration of Doxorubicin: Dabrafenib, Mitotane, P-glycoprotein / ABCB1 Inducers,
Enhances therapeutic effect of Doxorubicin: Roflumilast (immunosuppressive effect)
Enhances therapeutic effect of Tofacitinib (immunosuppressive effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Doxorubicin: Dipyrone (agranulocytosis, pancytopenia), Pimecrolimus,
Tacrolimus (Topical), Trastuzumab (cardiotoxic effects)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Clozapine (agranulocytosis), Leflunomide
(hematologic toxicity such as pancytopenia, agranulocytosis, and/or thrombocytopenia), Natalizumab (concurrent
infection), Taxane Derivatives (e.g. Paclitaxel, Docetaxel), Zidovudine
Increases serum concentration of Doxorubicin: Abiraterone Acetate, Cyclosporine, CYP2D6 Inhibitors (Moderate),
CYP2D6 Inhibitors (Strong), Fusidic Acid (Systemic), Mifepristone, P-glycoprotein / ABCB1 Inhibitors,
Reduces therapeutic effect of Doxorubicin: Dexrazoxane, Echinacea
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: BCG (Intravesical), Stavudine, Vaccines (immune response; live
virus vaccines may progress to infection), Zidovudine

Mechanism of Action

An anthracycline that inhibits DNA and RNA synthesis by intercalating with DNA and by inhibiting DNA repair through
topoisomerase II inhibition.

Dosage

ORAL › [Access] 100 mg capsule (as hyclate) (Rx)

Acute bacterial exacerbation of chronic bronchitis ABECB, mild-to-moderate, by mouth, ADULT, 100 mg twice daily.
Acute bacterial rhinosinusitis, ABRS, by mouth, ADULT, 100 mg every 12 hours for 5-7 days. Blepharitis with
associated acne rosacea, by mouth, ADULT, 100 mg twice daily for 2 weeks, then every 24 hours. Chlamydia
infections, by mouth, ADULT, 100 mg twice daily for 7 days Pneumonia due to Burkholderia pseudomallei, by mouth,
ADULT, 100 mg twice daily; CHILD, 4 mg/kg divided twice daily with Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole Juvenile
periodontitis, by mouth, CHILD ≥8 years, 200 mg daily for 7 days. Mycoplasma infections, by mouth, ADULT, 100 mg
twice a day for five to ten days Leptospirosis Clinical manifestations that should lead a health practitioner to
consider suspected leptospirosis: any individual with acute febrile illness of at least 2 days duration and residing
in a flooded area or has high-risk exposure (defined as wading in floods and contaminated water, contact with
animal fluids, swimming in flood water or ingestion of contaminated water, with or without cuts or wounds) and
with at least 2 of the following symptoms: myalgia, calf tenderness, conjunctival suffusion, chills, abdominal pain,
headache, jaundice, oliguria. For mild leptospirosis (defined as acute febrile illness and various manifestations
BUT with stable vital signs, anicteric sclera, good urine output and no evidence of meningismus or meningeal
irritation, sepsis or septic shock, difficulty of breathing, and jaundice, that is able to take oral medications) as first
line agent to be started as soon as the diagnosis is suspected regardless of the phase of the disease or duration
of symptoms, by mouth, ADULT, 100 mg twice daily for 7 days; CHILD ≥8 years, 2-4 mg/kg daily twice a day for 7 days
(maximum dose,200 mg daily). For moderate to severe leptospirosis, refer immediately to a higher level of
healthcare facility or the hospital. For pre-exposure and post-exposure prophylaxis: Preexposure prophylaxis is
not routinely recommended except for travelers, soldiers, those engaged in waterrelated recreational and
occupational activities in high endemic areas, by mouth, ADULT, 4 mg/kg single dose (maximum dose, 200 mg
regardless of age), take 100 mg twice daily if 200 mg daily is not tolerated. For post-exposure prophylaxis: Post-
exposure prophylaxis depends on type of risk and may be repeated once weekly if with continued exposure to risk
factors. - Low-Risk Exposure (defined as single history of wading in flood or contaminated water and the absence
of wounds, cuts, or open lesions of the skin), by mouth, ADULT, 200 mg within 24-72 hours from exposure. -
Moderate-Risk Exposure (defined as single history of wading in flood or contaminated water and the presence of
wounds, cuts, or open lesion in the skin, or the accidental ingestion of contaminated water), by mouth, ADULT, 200
mg once daily for 3-5 days to be started immediately within 24-72 hours from exposure. - High-Risk Exposure
(defined as continuous exposure – more than a single exposure or several days exposure – of wading in flood or
contaminated water with or without the presence of wounds, cuts, or open lesions of the skin; swimming in
flooded waters especially in areas infested with domestic / sewer rats and ingestion of contaminated water), by
mouth, ADULT, 200 mg once weekly until the end of the exposure. - For Pediatrics: by mouth, CHILD >8 years, 4
mg/kg as single dose (maximum, 200 mg); if child is exposed for more than 7 days, repeat the dose after one week.
NOTE: Antibiotic prophylaxis in the prevention of leptospirosis is NOT 100% effective. Protective measures should
still be used. The most effective preventive measure remains to be avoidance of high-risk exposure. If
unavoidable, use protective measures such as boots, goggles, overalls, or rubber gloves. WARNING: There is
currently NO recommended preexposure and post-exposute prophylaxis that is safe for pregnant or lactating
women. The Philippine Clinical Practice Guidelines on the Diagnosis, Treatment and Prevention of Leptospirosis in
Adults 2010, PSMID, Quezon City.

Administration

ORAL › [Access] 100 mg capsule (as hyclate) (Rx)

To be taken with food or after a meal. Capsules should be swallowed whole with plenty of fluid (a full glass of
water); remain sitting or standing (for at least ½ hour) to prevent esophageal irritation or damage. Do not give with
milk.
