ETOPOSIDE

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

L01CB01

ANATOMICAL

Antineoplastic and Immunomodulating Agents

THERAPEUTIC

Antineoplastic Agents

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Plant Alkaloids and Other Natural Products

CHEMICAL CLASS

Podophyllotoxin Derivatives

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IV › 20 mg/mL powder, 10 mL vial (Rx)

IV › 20 mg/mL, 5 mL ampule / vial (Rx)

IV › 100 mg Powder Vial (IV) (Rx)

Dosage

IV › 20 mg/mL powder, 10 mL vial (Rx)

Non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma (in combination with other agents), NSCLC (alone or in combination), SCLC (first-line in
combination; second-line alone or in combination), testicular cancer (in combination), by IV infusion, ADULT, 50–
100 mg/m2 daily for 5 days, administer doses >200 mg in 2 divided doses. Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis,
Acute myeloid leukemia, CHILD under 3 yrs, 3.3 mg/kg/day IV infusion for 4 days; CHILD above 3 yrs, 100 mg/m2/d IV
infusion for 4 days.

Administration

IV › 20 mg/mL powder, 10 mL vial (Rx)

Administration: For IV infusion, administer diluted over 30– 60 minutes to prevent occurrence of hypotension. Do
NOT administer by IV push, by IM or SC. Avoid extravasation. NOTE: Extravasation may cause significant irritation.
Tissue irritation and inflammation have occurred following extravasation. Concentrations >0.4 mg/mL may form
precipitate within a few minutes. For large doses, where dilution to =0.4 mg/mL is not feasible, consider slow
infusion of the undiluted drug through a running normal saline, dextrose or saline and dextrose infusion, or use
etoposide phosphate. An inline filter may be used to address the risk for precipitation. Etoposide solutions of 0.1–
0.4 mg/mL may be filtered through a 0.22 micron filter.

Page 1 of 4



April 25, 2026, 12:16:11 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Renal Impairment:
For CrCl >50 mL/minute, dose adjustment is not required.
For CrCl 15–50 mL/minute, administer 75% of dose.
For CrCl <15 mL/minute, consider further dose reductions. No data available.
Infants, children, and adolescents:
GFR >50 ml/min/1.73 m2: no dosage adjustment
GFR 10-50 ml/min/ 1.73 m2: administer 75% of dose
GFR < 10 ml/min/1.73 m2: administer 50% of dose

Hepatic Impairment:
For mild-to-moderate impairment, no recommended dose adjustment.
For severe impairment, use is contraindicated.
Infants, children, and adolescents:
Bilirubin 1.5-3 mg/dL or AST > 3 times ULN: administer 50% of dose
Bilirubin > 3mg/dL: administer 25% of dose or consider avoiding dose

Obesity:
Use actual body weight (full weight) for calculation of BSAbased
dosing.
Use adjusted body weight 25% (ABW25) for weight-based (mg/kg) dosing for hematopoietic stem cell transplant
conditioning regimens in adults.

ABW25:
Adjusted wt (kg) = Ideal body weight (kg) + 0.25 [actual wt (kg) –ideal body weight (kg)]
If dose reduction is done due to toxicity, consider resumption of full weight-based dosing with subsequent cycles,
especially if cause of toxicity is resolved.

Toxicity:
If hypersensitivity reaction occurs, interrupt infusion.
For severe non-hematologic adverse reactions, reduce dose or discontinue treatment.
If WBC 2,000–3,000/mm3 or platelets 75,000– 100,000/mm3, reduce dose by 50%.
If ANC <500/mm3 or platelets <50,000/mm3, withhold treatment until recovery.

Indications

Treatment of small cell lung cancer (SCLC; firstand second-line) ; treatment of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC);
treatment of non-Hodgkin’s lymphomas (first-line) ; treatment of testicular cancer (first-line); Hemophagocytic
Lymphohistiocytosis (HLH); Childhood solid malignancies (e.g. Retinoblastoma, Neuroblastoma)

Page 2 of 4



April 25, 2026, 12:16:11 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to etoposide or any component of the formulation; severe leukopenia or thrombocytopenia; severe
hepaticimpairment; severe renal impairment

Precautions

WARNING: Should be administered under the supervision of a qualified healthcare provider, experienced in the use
of cancer chemotherapeutic agents. Severe myelosuppression, with resulting infection or bleeding, may occur.

WARNING: Hazardous agent. Use appropriate precautions for handling and disposal.

Hypersensitivity reaction (anaphylactic-like reactions manifested by chills, fever, tachycardia, bronchospasm,
dyspnea, and hypotension may occur; facial or tongue swelling, coughing, chest tightness, cyanosis, laryngospasm,
diaphoresis, hypertension, back pain, loss of consciousness, and flushing); hypotension (may occur due to rapid
administration).

Secondary malignancies (secondary acute leukemias); low serum albumin (at an increased risk for toxicities).

Elderly (may be more likely to develop severe myelosuppression and/or GI effects); children (at an increased risk of
anaphylactic-like reactions when used at concentrations higher than recommended).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Abdominal pain, alopecia, anaphylactic-like reaction, anorexia, diarrhea, hepatic toxicity, anemia,
hypotension (infusion-related), nausea, vomiting, leukopenia (nadir, 7–14 days; recovery, by day 20), peripheral
neuropathy, stomatitis, thrombocytopenia (nadir, 9–16 days; recovery, by day 20)

Less Common: Amenorrhea, back pain, blindness (transient; cortical), constipation, cyanosis, cough, diaphoresis,
dysphagia, erythema, esophagitis, extravasation (induration; necrosis), facial swelling, fatigue, fever,
hyperpigmentation, hypersensitivity, hypersensitivity-associated apnea, interstitial pneumonitis, laryngospasm,
maculopapular rash, metabolic acidosis, MI, malaise, mucositis, myocardial ischemia, optic neuritis, ovarian failure,
perivasculitis, pruritus, pulmonary fibrosis, radiation-recall dermatitis, rash, reversible posterior
leukoencephalopathy syndrome (RPLS), seizure, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, tongue swelling, somnolence, toxic
epidermal necrolysis, toxic megacolon, urticaria, vasospasm, weakness

Associated with higher doses: Alopecia, ethanol intoxication, hepatitis, hypotension (infusion-related), metabolic
acidosis, nausea and vomiting (severe), secondary malignancy, mucositis, skin lesions (resembling Stevens-Johnson
syndrome)

Page 3 of 4



April 25, 2026, 12:16:11 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Etoposide: Trastuzumab (neutropenic effect)
Enhances therapeutic effect of Vitamin K Antagonists [e.g. Warfarin (anticoagulant effect)]
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Etoposide: Denosumab (serious nfections)
Reduces diagnostic effect of Coccidioides immitis Skin Test

Avoid concomitant use with:
Decreases metabolism of Etoposide: Cyclosporine (Systemic), CYP3A4 Inhibitors
Decreases serum concentration of Etoposide:
Dabrafenib, Mitotane,
Enhances therapeutic effect of Etoposide: Roflumilast (immunosuppressive effect)
Enhances therapeutic effect of Tofacitinib (immunosuppressive effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Etoposide: Dipyrone (agranulocytosis, pancytopenia), Pimecrolimus,
Tacrolimus (Topical)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Clozapine (agranulocytosis), Leflunomide
(hematologic toxicity such as pancytopenia, agranulocytosis, and/or thrombocytopenia), Natalizumab (concurrent
infection)
Increases serum concentration of Etoposide: Fusidic Acid (Systemic), Mifepristone
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: BCG (Intravesical), Vaccines (live virus vaccines may progress to
infection)

Mechanism of Action

A topoisomerase II inhibitor that arrests cells in the late S or early G2 phase of cell division.

ETHINYLESTRADIOL + NORETHISTERONE

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

G03AA05

ANATOMICAL

Genito Urinary System and Sex Hormones

THERAPEUTIC

Sex Hormones and Modulators of the Genital System

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Hormonal Contraceptives for Systemic Use

CHEMICAL CLASS

Progestins and Estrogens, Fixed Combinations

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › 35 micrograms ethinylestradiol + 400 micrograms norethisterone acetate per tablet as 21 active tablets or
28-day tablet with 21 active and 7 inactive pills (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › 35 micrograms ethinylestradiol + 400 micrograms norethisterone acetate per tablet as 21 active tablets or 28-day
tablet with 21 active and 7 inactive pills (Rx)

Contraception, by mouth, ADULT (female), 1 tablet (35 micrograms ethinylestradiol + 400 micrograms
norethisterone acetate) daily beginning on the 5th day of menstrual cycle for 21 days, followed by 7 tab-free days.

Administration

ORAL › 35 micrograms ethinylestradiol + 400 micrograms norethisterone acetate per tablet as 21 active tablets or 28-day
tablet with 21 active and 7 inactive pills (Rx)

Administer at the same time each day at intervals for not more than 24 hours. If one or more tablets are forgotten
for more than 12 hours, contraceptive protection will be reduced. Use for the shortest duration possible at the
lowest effective dose in accordance with treatment goals.

Dose Adjustment

Renal Impairment
In mild-to-moderate impairment, use with caution.
In severe impairment, refer patient to a specialist.

Page 1 of 4



April 25, 2026, 12:18:02 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Indications

Contraception

Contraindications

Breast cancer (current or recent); risk factors for venous thromboembolism and arterial disease; heart disease
associated with pulmonary hypertension or risk of embolism; migraine; history of subacute bacterial endocarditis;
ischemic cerebrovascular disease; liver disease, including disorders of hepatic secretion; porphyria; systemic lupus
erythematosus (SLE); history of hemolytic uremic syndrome; liver adenoma; gallstones; estrogen-dependent
neoplasm; neoplasm of genital tract; undiagnosed vaginal bleeding; history of pruritus during pregnancy, chorea,
deteriorating otosclerosis, cholestatic jaundice, or pemphigoid gestationis; after evacuation of hydatidiform mole;
smokers >35 years

Precautions

WARNING: NOT for use as prophylaxis of dementia. An increased incidence of dementia was observed in women ≥65
years of age taking conjugated estrogens alone or in combination with medroxyprogesterone acetate.

NOT for use in the prevention of cardiovascular disease. An increased risk of deep vein thrombosis (DVT), stroke,
pulmonary emboli (PE), and myocardial infarction (MI) was observed in postmenopausal women.

The use of unopposed estrogen in women with an intact uterus is associated with an increased risk of endometrial
cancer. Combination or inclusion of a progestin to estrogen treatment may decrease the risk of endometrial
hyperplasia, a precursor to endometrial cancer.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Acne, breakthrough bleeding, breast enlargement and tenderness, changes in libido, chloasma, fluid
retention, headache, increased BP, mood changes (e.g., depression), nausea, thrush, vomiting
Less Common: Alopecia, amenorrhea, contact lens intolerance, hirsutism, hyperinsulinemia, insulin resistance, rash
Rare: Allergy, breast cancer, cervical cancer, hypertension, jaundice, liver cancer, pancreatitis, photosensitivity,
stroke, VTE

Page 2 of 4



April 25, 2026, 12:18:02 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

NOTE: Combined oral contraceptives are metabolized by CYP3A4.

Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs:

Reduces contraceptive effect of Fixed Combinations of Progestins and Estrogens:
Cephalosporins, e.g., Ceftriaxone, Macrolide Antibiotics, e.g., Azithromycin, Erythromycin, Metronidazole, Penicillins,
e.g., Benzylpenicillin, Amoxicillin, Phenoxymethylpenicillin, Ampicillin, Quinolones, e.g., Ciprofloxacin, Levofloxacin,
Tetracyclines, e.g., Doxycycline

Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Anti-diabetic Agents, Thyroid Products

Avoid concomitant use with:
Decreases serum concentration of Fixed Combinations of Progestins and Estrogens:
Bile Acid Sequestrants [administer oral contraceptives at least 1-4 hours prior to or 4-6 hours after a Bile Acid
Sequestrant], Dabrafenib, Efavirenz, Exemestane, Griseofulvin, Lamotrigine, Topiramate

Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Tranexamic Acid (thrombogenic effect)

Increases metabolism of Fixed Combinations of Progestins and Estrogens:
Carbamazepine, CYP2C19 Inducers, CYP3A4 Inducers, Phenobarbital, Rifampicin

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
Anti-hepaciviral Combination Products (hepatotoxic effect), Vitamin K Antagonists, e.g., Warfarin (thromboembolic
disorders)

Reduces therapeutic effect of Fixed Combinations of Progestins and Estrogens, leading to contraceptive failure:
Artemether, Barbiturates e.g. Phenobarbital, Carbamazepine, CYP2C19 Inducers, CYP3A4 Inducers, Fosphenytoin,
Mifepristone, Mycophenolate, Phenobarbital, Phenytoin, Protease Inhibitor [except Indinavir], Retinoic Acid
Derivatives [except Adapalene, Tretinoin (topical)], Rifamycin Derivatives, Rifampicin

Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Amlodipine, Enalapril, Isosorbide Dinitrate, Methyldopa (antagonizes hypotensive effect), Anastrozole, Anti-
coagulants e.g. Warfarin, Heparin (counteracts anticoagulant effects), Furosemide, Hyaluronidase,
Hydrochlorothiazide (antagonizes diuretic effect), Metformin (antagonizes hypoglycemic effect),

Mechanism of Action

A combined oral contraceptive, which contains estrogen as ethinylestradiol and progesterone as norethisterone. It
inhibits ovulation, reduces reception of endometrium to implantation, and thickens cervical mucus that serves as a

ETONOGESTREL

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

G03AC08

ANATOMICAL

Genito Urinary System and Sex Hormones

THERAPEUTIC

Sex Hormones and Modulators of the Genital System

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Hormonal Contraceptives for Systemic Use

CHEMICAL CLASS

Progestins

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

SC › 68 mg implant (Rx)

Dosage

SC › 68 mg implant (Rx)

Contraception, by subdermal implant, ADULT, 68 mg implant under the skin at the inner side of the non-dominant
upper arm (therapy can be started at any time in the menstrual cycle once it is determined that the woman is not
pregnant); back-up contraception is not needed if started within 5 days of onset of menstruation; if started >5
days after the onset of menstruation or at any time in a woman experiencing amenorrhea (not postpartum), back-
up contraception should be used for 7 days. Remove no later than the end of the third year. This can be replaced
with a new implant at time of removal if continued contraception is needed, after ruling out possibility of
pregnancy. Insertion can be done as follows: No hormonal contraceptives within past month: Insert between days
1 through 5 of menstruation, even if still bleeding Switching from combination hormonal contraceptive:
Transdermal system or vaginal ring Insert on the day of the removal of the transdermal system or vaginal ring or on
the day following the transdermal-free or ring-free interval Switching from a progestin-only contraceptive:
Implant or intrauterine device (IUD): Insert on same day as removal of implant or IUD ; Injection: Insert on day next
injection is due First trimester abortion or miscarriage: Insert within first 5 days following first trimester abortion
or miscarriage. Second trimester abortion or miscarriage: Insert between 21 and 28 days following second
trimester abortion or miscarriage. Postpartum: If not breast-feeding, insert between 21 to 28 days postpartum. If
breast-feeding, insert after the fourth post-partum week and use a second non-hormonal form of contraception
for the first 7 days of insertion.

Page 1 of 4



April 25, 2026, 3:10:09 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

SC › 68 mg implant (Rx)

Insert implant subdermally at the inner side of the non-dominant upper arm approximately 8–10 cm (3–4 inches)
above the medial epicondyle of the humerus just under the skin. Implant must be palpable after insertion. X-ray,
CT scan, ultrasound scanning, or MRI can be used to confirm the location of the implant if it is not palpable. Use of
a non-hormonal contraceptive is required until the verification of the presence of the implant. Confirm first that
the entire implant has been removed by measuring its length (4 cm), when removing. Get rid of all pieces if implant
has broken. A new implant may be inserted in the same arm through the same incision.

Indications

Contraception

Contraindications

Breast cancer or other estrogen-dependent or progestin-dependent neoplasms (current or a history of); hepatic
impairment; hepatic tumors or disease; pregnancy; thrombosis or thromboembolic disorders (current or history of);
undiagnosed abnormal genital bleeding

Precautions

Breast cancer;
Carbohydrate intolerance and diabetes;
Ovarian cysts;
Retinal vascular thrombosis (discontinue if there is loss of vision, proptosis, diplopia, papilledema, or retinal vascular
lesions);
Thromboembolism and other vascular events (e.g., deep vein thrombosis, myocardial infarction, pulmonary
embolism);
Changes in vaginal bleeding;
Cardiovascular diseases;
Depression;
Disease that may be exacerbated by fluid retention;
Gallbladder disease;
Hepatic adenomas or carcinomas;
Hepatic impairment;
Hyperlipidemia;
Hypertension;
Renal impairment;
Overweight women;
Wearers of contact lens;
Increased risk of cervical or ovarian cancer;
May change results of some laboratory tests (e.g., coagulation factors, lipids, glucose tolerance, binding proteins);
Does not protect against HIV infection or other sexually transmitted disease

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Headache, acne vulgaris, menstrual disease (<3 episodes or 90 days; prolonged menstrual bleeding lasting
>14 days: >5 episodes in 90 days) amenorrhea (no bleeding in 90 days); weight gain, abdominal pain, vaginitis,
mastalgia, pharyngitis
Less Common: Dizziness, emotional lability, depression, nervousness, pain, localized erythema, dysmenorrhea,
nausea, leukorrhea, hypersensitivity, application site reaction, local pain, hematoma at injection site, bruising at
injection site, back pain, flu-like symptoms
Rare: Alopecia, angioedema (including exacerbation of hereditary angioedema), cerebrovascular accident,
anaphylaxis, convulsions, migraine, hypertension, myocardial infarction, ovarian cyst, pulmonary embolism, seizure

Etonogestrel is a hormone that prevents ovulation by reducing reception of endometrium to implantation and
thickening of the cervical mucus that serves as a barrier to sperm.

Administration

ORAL › 250 micrograms tablet (Rx)

May be taken with or without food. Take consistently with regard to food to reduce variability. Swallow whole with a
glass of water. Do NOT break, chew, or crush. Do NOT administer crushed or broken tablets. Avoid contact with or
exposure to crushed or broken tablets. NOTE: Limit exposure to sunlight and ultraviolet light by wearing
protective clothing and using effective sunscreen to minimize the risk for skin cancer. For breast cancer,
pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors, renal cell cancer, renal angiolipoma, or SEGA, administer at the same time
each day. For liver transplantation, administer consistently ~12 hours apart, at the same time as tacrolimus. For
renal transplantation, administer consistently ~12 hours apart, at the same time as cyclosporine.

Dose Adjustment

Concomitant CYP3A4 Inducer or Inhibitor and/or Pglycoprotein Inhibitor use:
For patients receiving concomitant strong CYP3A4 and/or pglycoprotein inducers (e.g., carbamazepine,
phenobarbital, phenytoin, rifabutin, rifampin, rifapentine), double everolimus dose using increments of =5 mg with
careful monitoring up to a maximum dailydose of 20 mg. If inducer is discontinued, allow 3–5 days prior to reducing
the everolimus dose to recommended starting dose.
For patients receiving concomitant moderate CYP3A4 and/or p-glycoprotein inhibitors (e.g., amprenavir, aprepitant,
diltiazem, erythromycin, fluconazole, fosamprenavir, verapamil), reduce everolimus dose to 2.5 mg once daily;
increase from 2.5–5 mg once daily based on patient tolerance; further reductions may be necessary for adverse
reactions; if dose reduction is required for patients receiving the lowest available strength, consider alternate-day
dosing. If inhibitor is discontinued, allow 2–3 days prior to reducing the everolimus dose to recommended starting
dose.

Renal transplantation:
Adjust dose based on everolimus serum concentrations.

Hepatic Impairment:
Mild impairment (Child-Pugh class A): Breast cancer, PNET, RCC, renal angiomyolipoma, 7.5 mg once daily; may further
reduce to 5 mg once daily if not tolerated.

Liver or renal transplantation, reduce initial dose by ~33%; individualize subsequent dosing based on therapeutic
drug monitoring; target trough concentration is 3–8 nanograms/mL.

SEGA, 75% of usual dose based on calculated BSA, rounded to the nearest strength; target trough concentration is
5– 15 nanograms/mL; may increase dose within the target range to achieve higher concentrations as tolerated

Moderate impairment (Child-Pugh class B):

Breast cancer, PNET, RCC, renal angiomyolipoma, 5 mg once daily; may further reduce to 2.5 mg once daily if not
tolerated.

Liver or renal transplantation, reduce initial dose by ~50%; individualize subsequent dosing based on therapeutic
drug monitoring; target trough concentration is 3–8 nanograms/mL.

SEGA, 50% of usual dose based on calculated BSA, rounded to the nearest strength; target trough concentration is
5– 15 nanograms/mL; may increase dose within the target range if tolerated.

Severe impairment (Child-Pugh class C):
Breast cancer, PNET, RCC, renal angiomyolipoma, maximum dose is 2.5 mg once daily.

Liver or renal transplantation, reduce initial dose by ~50%; individualize subsequent dosing based on therapeutic
drug monitoring; target trough concentration is 3–8 nanograms/mL.

Indications

Prophylaxis of organ rejection in liver transplantation; prophylaxis of organ rejection in renal transplant patients at
low to moderate immunologic risk

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to everolimus

Precautions

WARNING: Should only be used by physicians experienced in immunosuppressive therapy and management of
transplant patients. Adequate laboratory and supportive medical resources must be readily available.

NOT recommended for use in heart transplant; has immunosuppressant properties which may result in infection. Use
may result in the development of malignancy, including lymphoma and skin cancer. An increased risk of renal arterial
and venous thrombosis has been reported with use in renal transplantation, which may result in graft loss.

Angioedema

Edema (generalized edema, including peripheral edema and lymphedema, and local fluid accumulation, e.g.,
pericardial effusion, pleural effusion, or ascites)

Bone marrow suppression; Malignancy

Fertility effects (e.g. menstrual irregularities, secondary amenorrhea, and increases in luteinizing hormone and
follicle-stimulating hormone in females may occur; azoospermia and oligospermia in males; may cause infertility).

Hepatic artery thrombosis; Hepatic impairment

Hereditary galactose intolerance (may result in diarrhea and malabsorption);

Metabolic effects (e.g. hyperglycemia, hyperlipidemia, and hypertriglyceridemia; increased risk for new-onset
diabetes)

Infections (sepsis, respiratory failure, hepatic failure, or fatality may occur; polyomavirus infection in transplant
patients may be serious and/or fatal; reactivation of hepatitis B)

Mucositis or stomatitis

Nephrotoxicity (e.g. elevations in serum creatinine, renal failure, and proteinuria; an increased incidence of rash,
infection and dose interruptions have been reported in patients with CrCl =60 mL/minute)

Pulmonary toxicity (e.g. noninfectious pneumonitis, interstitial lung disease (ILD), and/or noninfectious fibrosis,
some fatal)

Wound healing complication (may delay wound healing and increase the occurrence of wound-related complications)

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Everolimus: Trastuzumab (neutropenic effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Everolimus: Denosumab (serious infections)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: ACE Inhibitors, e.g., Captopril, Enalapril
(angioedema)
Reduces diagnostic effect of Coccidioides immitis Skin Test
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Antidiabetic Agents e.g. Gliclazide

Avoid concomitant use with:
Decreases serum concentration of Everolimus: Dabrafenib, Efavirenz
Enhances therapeutic effect of Everolimus: Roflumilast (immunosuppressive effect), Tofacitinib
(immunosuppressive effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Everolimus: Cyclosporine, Systemic (nephrotoxicity), Dipyrone
(agranulocytosis, pancytopenia); Pimecrolimus; Tacrolimus (Topical)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Clozapine (agranulocytosis), Leflunomide
(hematologic toxicity), Natalizumab, (concurrent infection), Vaccines (Live) (infection)
Increases serum concentration of Everolimus: Conivaptan, Cyclosporine (Systemic), Fusidic Acid (Systemic),
Idelalisib, Mifepristone, P-glycoprotein / ABCB1 Inhibitors
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: BCG (Intravesical), Vaccines

FOOD INTERACTION. Grapefruit Juice may increase the serum concentration of Everolimus.

Mechanism of Action

Macrolide immunosuppressant and a mechanistic target of rapamycin (mTOR) inhibitor which has antiproliferative

Dosage

ORAL › 10 mg (as Oxalate) Tablet (Rx)

Generalized anxiety disorder, by mouth, ADULT, initially 10 mg once daily. May increase to 20 mg once daily if

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 12:20:58 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric: 10 mg once daily; use with caution as per the Beers Criteria due to association of SSRIs with falls and
fractures in service users/patients 65 years and older and should be avoided such service users/patients who have a
history of falls or fractures, as well as having the potential to cause or exacerbate syndrome of inappropriate
antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH) or hyponatremia monitor sodium concentration closely when initiating or
adjusting the dose in older adults.

Renal Impairment: Use with caution

Hepatic Impairment: Initially 5 mg once daily. Increase to 10 mg once daily after 2 weeks

Indications

Management of major depressive disorder, generalized anxiety disorder.

Contraindications

Known QTc prolongation, congenital long QTc syndrome.

Precautions

WARNING: Antidepressants increased the risk of suicidal thinking and behavior (suicidality) in children, adolescents,
and young adults with major depressive disorder (MDD) and other psychiatric disorders. Balance the risk with the
clinical need. Appropriately monitor patients of all ages who are started on antidepressant therapy and closely
observe for clinical worsening, suicidality, or unusual changes in behavior. Advise families and caregivers of the need
for close observation and communication with the health care provider. Escitalopram is not approved for use in
pediatric service users/patients younger than 12 years.

Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT): Use with caution; no clinical studies have assessed the combined use of
escitalopram and electroconvulsive therapy; may increase the risks (e.g., cognitive adverse effects) associated with
electroconvulsive therapy; consider discontinuing, when possible, prior to ECT treatment.
Use with caution in patients with history of seizures.
Use with caution in patients with bipolar disorder unless treated with concomitant mood stabilizing agent.
Monitor patients for activation of suicidal ideation, especially children and adolescents.
Do not use use if patients is taking a MAO inhibitor

STORAGE AND STABILITY: Store at 25°C. Excursions permitted to 15-30°C. Protect from light.

See General Precautions under Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors in Chapter 10: Nervous System for other
precautions.

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 12:20:58 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Abnormal dreams, fatigue, paresthesia, pyrexia, restlessness, sinusitis, yawning.
Less Common: Alopecia, bruxism, confusion, epistaxis, menstrual disturbances, mydriasis, edema, pruritus,
syncope, tachycardia, taste disturbance, tinnitus.
Rare: Aggression, bradycardia, depersonalization.

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Tramadol, buspirone, tryptophan (serotonin syndrome)
NSAIDs (bleeding)
Oral anticoagulants (altered anticoagulant effects)
Drug-induced hypokalemia/hypomagnesaemia (malignant arrhythmias)
Concomitant administration or w/in 14 days of MAOIs withdrawal (serotonin syndrome; fatal)
QTc prolonging agents e.g., pimozide (additive effect; fatal; monitor)

Avoid concomitant use with:
Linezolid (may cause serotonin syndrome)
MAOIs (may cause serotonin syndrome)
Methylene blue (may cause serotonin syndrome)
Pimozide (may precipitate torsades de pointes)

Mechanism of Action

Escitalopram is a furancarbonitrile and S-stereoisomer of citalopram. It induces allosteric serotonin transporter
(SERT) inhibition which results in a significant increase of serotonin.

FACTOR IX COMPLEX CONCENTRATE (COAGULATION FACTORS
II, VII, IX, X)

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

B02BD04

ANATOMICAL

Blood and Blood Forming Organs

THERAPEUTIC

Antihemorrhagics

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Vitamin K and Other Hemostatics

CHEMICAL CLASS

Blood Coagulation Factors

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IV › 100 IU/mL, 5 mL vial (Rx)

IV › 100 IU/mL, 10 mL vial (Rx)

IV › 500 IU lyophilized powder vial + diluent (Rx)

Page 1 of 4



April 25, 2026, 12:58:08 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dosage

IV › 100 IU/mL, 5 mL vial (Rx)

NOTE: Dosage is expressed in units of factor IX activity. Individualize doses based on severity of factor IX
deficiency, extent and location of bleeding, and clinical status of patient. Closely monitor factor IX level to
determine proper dosage. When multiple doses are required, administer at 24¬-hour intervals unless otherwise
specified. INTRAVENOUS Control or prevention of bleeding in patients with factor IX deficiency: by IV injection,
ADULT and CHILD, [Indication]: Dental surgery, single tooth [Bebulin]: Usually 50–75 units/kg as a single infusion 1
hour prior to surgery [raise factor IX level to 40–60% of normal on day of surgery] [Profilnine]: Maintenance dose,
30–50 units/kg every 16–24 hours for 7–10 days following surgery or until healing has been achieved [raise factor IX
level to 50% of normal immediately prior to procedure; maintain factor IX levels at 30 to 50% of normal]
[Indication]: Dental surgery, multiple teeth [Bebulin]: Replacement therapy may be required for up to 1 week [raise
factor IX level to 40–60% of normal on day of surgery] [Profilnine]: Maintenance dose, 30–50 units/kg every 16–24
hours for 7–10 days following surgery or until healing has been achieved [raise factor IX level to 50% of normal
immediately prior to procedure; maintain factor IX levels at 30 to 50% of normal] [Indication]: Minor bleeding, e.g.,
early hemarthrosis, minor epistaxis, gingival bleeding, mild hematuria [Bebulin]: Initially 25–35 units/kg for 1 day
[raise factor IX level to 20% of normal] [NOTE: A single dose is usually sufficient, but a second dose may be given
after 24 hours] [Profilnine]: Initially 20–30 units/kg every 16–24 hours for 1–2 days for minor hemorrhage or until
hemorrhage stops and healing has been achieved [raise factor IX level to 20–30% of normal] [Indication]:
Moderate bleeding, e.g., severe joint bleeding, early hematoma, major open bleeding, minor trauma, minor
hemoptysis, hematemesis, melena, major hematuria [Bebulin]: Initially 50–65 units/kg for 2 days, or until adequate
wound healing [raise factor IX level to 40% of normal] [Profilnine]: Initially, 20–30 units/kg every 16–24 hours for 2–
7 days for moderate hemorrhage, or until hemorrhage stops and healing has been achieved [raise factor IX level to
20–30% of normal] [Indication]: Major bleeding, e.g., severe hematoma, major trauma, severe hemoptysis,
hematemesis, melena [Bebulin]: Initially 75–90 units/kg for 2–3 days or until adequate wound healing [raise factor
IX level to ≥60% of normal] [Profilnine]: Initially 30–50 units/kg every 16–24 hours (maintenance dose, 20 units/kg)
[raise factor IX level to 30–50% of normal] Following this treatment period, maintain factor IX levels at 20% of
normal for 3–10 days, or until healing has been achieved [Indication]: Minor surgical procedures [Bebulin]: Typical
dose, 50–75 units/kg [raise factor IX level to 40–60% of normal on day of surgery] Then lower dose to usually 26–65
units/kg for 1–2 weeks or until adequate wound healing [decrease factor IX level to 20 to 40% of normal during
initial postop period] Give preop dose 1 hour prior to surgery, initially every 12 hours, then every 24 hours postop
[Profilnine]: Initially 30–50 units/kg prior to surgery [raise factor IX level to 30–50% of normal] Maintenance dose,
30–50 units/kg every 16–24 hours for 7–10 days following surgery or until healing is achieved [maintain factor IX
levels at 30 to 50% of normal] [Indication]:Major surgical procedures [Bebulin]: Typical initial dose, 75–90 units/kg
[raise factor IX level to ≥60% of normal on day of surgery] Adjust dose to typically 25–75 units/kg for 1–2 weeks
[decrease factor IX level to 20–60% of normal during initial postop period] Adjust dose to typically 25–35 units/kg
during late postop period (≥3 weeks) and continue until adequate wound healing is achieved [further decrease to
maintain a factor IX level of 20% of normal] Give preop dose 1 hour prior to surgery, initially every 12 hours, then
every 24 hours postop [Profilnine]: Initially 30–50 units/kg prior to surgery [raise factor IX level to 30–50% of
normal] Maintenance dose, 30–50 units/kg every 16–24 hours for 7–10 days following surgery or until healing is
achieved [maintain factor IX levels at 30–50% of normal] Formula for units required to raise blood level %: NOTE:
Larger doses may be required, especially if treatment is delayed. [Bebulin] factor IX 1 unit/kg will increase the
plasma factor IX level by 0.8% Number of Factor IX units required = body weight (kg) x desired factor IX increase (as
% of normal) x 1.2 units/kg [Profilnine] factor IX 1 unit/kg will increase the plasma factor IX level by 1% Number of
factor IX units required = bodyweight (kg) x desired factor IX increase (as % of normal) x 1 unit/kg

Page 2 of 4



April 25, 2026, 12:58:08 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

IV › 100 IU/mL, 5 mL vial (Rx)

Administer by IV infusion. Infuse solution at room temperature at 2 mL/minute for Bebulin or 10 mL/minute for
Profilnine. Prior to reconstitution, bring diluent (SWFI) and Factor IX Concentrate to room temperature. Do NOT
exceed 37°C [98.6°F]. Gently rotate or agitate to dissolve. Do NOT shake. Use reconstituted solution within 3
hours. Do NOT refrigerate. Vials are intended for single use. Discard unused portion. Do NOT mix with other drugs
or solvents. Do NOT exceed the recommended infusion rates to prevent vasomotor reactions due to rapid
administration. Slowing the rate of infusion, changing the lot of medication, or administering antihistamines may
relieve some adverse reactions. NOTE: [Bebulin] Reconstituted product should be a colorless to slightly yellowish
and clear to slightly turbid solution. [Profilnine] A few particles may remain in solution following reconstitution.
The Mix2Vial set will remove the particles and the labeled potency will not be reduced.

Dose Adjustment

Hepatic Impairment:
No dose adjustment required. Monitor factor IX levels.

Indications

Prevention and control of bleeding in patients with factor IX deficiency (hemophilia B).

NOTE: NOT indicated for the treatment of other factor deficiencies, hemophilia A patient with inhibitors to factor VIII,
or bleeding caused by low levels of liver-dependent coagulation factors.

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity reactions to factor IX complex or any component of the formulation; known allergy to heparin;
history of heparin¬-induced thrombocytopenia.

Precautions

WARNING: Factor IX complex (factors II, IX, X) contains low or nontherapeutic levels of factor VII component. Do NOT
confuse with prothrombin complex concentrate (factors II, VII, IX, X, protein C, protein S), which contains therapeutic
levels of factor VII.

Antibody formation; Thrombotic events; Hepatic impairment (use with caution)

Page 3 of 4



April 25, 2026, 12:58:08 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Flushing, thrombosis, chills, fever, headache, lethargy, somnolence, rash, urticaria, nausea, vomiting,
disseminated intravascular coagulation, paresthesia, dyspnea, anaphylactic shock, development of clotting factor
antibodies, heparin-¬induced thrombocytopenia.

Mechanism of Action

A sterile lyophilized concentrate derived from human plasma. It is also known as prothrombin complex concentrate
and is composed of several Vitamin K-dependent clotting factors including factor IX, factor II (prothrombin), factor X,
and low levels of factor VII.

FACTOR VIII CONCENTRATE

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

B02BD02

ANATOMICAL

Blood and Blood Forming Organs

THERAPEUTIC

Antihemorrhagics

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Vitamin K and Other Hemostatics

CHEMICAL CLASS

Blood Coagulation Factors

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IV › 100 IU/g lyophilized powder, vial + diluent (Rx)

Dosage

IV › 100 IU/g lyophilized powder, vial + diluent (Rx)

NOTE: Individualize dose, dosing frequency, and duration based on location and severity of bleeding, target factor
VIII levels, and clinical condition of the patient. Plasma levels of factor VIII should not exceed 200% of normal or
200 units/dL. INTRAVENOUS Acquired hemophilia A, mild to moderate hemorrhage: by IV injection, ADULT, initially
200 units/kg to achieve factor VIII plasma level 50–100% of normal; titrate subsequent doses to maintain
recommended factor VIII trough levels and individual clinical response; dose every 4–12 hours; adjust frequency
based on clinical response or factor VIII levels. Acquired hemophilia A, major hemorrhage: by IV injection, ADULT,
initially 200 units/kg to achieve factor VIII plasma level 100–200% of normal for acute bleed or 50–100% of normal
after acute bleed is controlled, if required of normal; titrate subsequent doses to maintain recommended factor
VIII trough levels and individual clinical response; dose every 4–12 hours; adjust frequency based on clinical
response or factor VIII levels.

Administration

IV › 100 IU/g lyophilized powder, vial + diluent (Rx)

Administer via IV route at a rate of 1–2 mL/minute. Prior to reconstitution, warm vial and diluent to room
temperature. Gently swirl vial in a circular motion after adding diluent until dissolved. Do NOT administer in the
same tubing or container with other medicinal products.

Page 1 of 2



April 25, 2026, 12:58:13 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Indications

Treatment of bleeding episodes in adults with acquired hemophilia A.

NOTE: Not indicated for the treatment of congenital hemophilia A or von Willebrand disease.

Contraindications

Life-¬threatening hypersensitivity reactions to antihemophilic factor or any component of the formulation (including
traces of hamster proteins).

Precautions

Antibody formation (suspect an anti-porcine factor VIII antibody if the plasma factor VIII level does not increase as
expected or if bleeding is not controlled after administration); Hypersensitivity reactions (discontinue immediately if
allergic or anaphylactic¬-type reactions occur); Lactation (use with caution).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Antibody development
Less Common: Hypersensitivity reaction

Mechanism of Action

A plasma protein fraction that contains the glycoprotein coagulation factor VIII together with varying amounts of von
Willebrand factor. Factor VIII is required for clot formation and maintenance of hemostasis. It acts by activating factor
X, a coagulation factor necessary in thrombin and eventual fibrin formation.

Dosage

ORAL › 20 mg tablet (Rx)

Duodenal ulcer (Acute therapy): by mouth, ADULT, 40 mg daily at bedtime (or 20 mg twice daily) for 4-8 weeks.
Duodenal ulcer (Maintenance therapy): by mouth, ADULT, 20 mg daily at bedtime. Gastric ulcer (Acute therapy): by

GERD: by mouth, ADULT, 20 mg twice daily for 6 weeks; CHILD 1-16 years, 1 mg/kg daily divided twice daily
(maximum dose, 40 mg twice daily) (doses of up to 2 mg/kg/day have been used in clinical studies); CHILD 3- 12

due to GERD: by mouth, ADULT, 20 mg or 40 mg twice daily for up to 12 weeks. Hypersecretory conditions: by
mouth, ADULT, initially, 20 mg every 6 hours, may increase in increments up to 160 mg every 6 hours.
INTRAVENOUS/INTRAMUSCULAR Peptic ulcer: by IV injection, CHILD 1-16 years, 0.25 mg/kg every 12 hours
(maximum dose, 40 mg daily) (doses of up to 0.5 mg/kg have been used in clinical studies). Patients unable to take
oral medication: by IV injection, ADULT, 20 mg every 12 hours.

Administration

ORAL › 20 mg tablet (Rx)

For oral administration, may be taken with or without food. For IV injection or IV push, inject over at least 2 minutes.
For IV infusion, administer over 15–30 minutes.

Dose Adjustment

Renal Impairment: For creatinine clearance <50, reduce dose by half or increase dosing interval to 36–48 hours.

Indications

Relief and prevention of heartburn; treatment and maintenance of active duodenal ulcers, pathological
hypersecretory conditions (e.g., Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome or multiple endocrine adenomas), Gastroesophageal
Reflux Disease (GERD) and active benign gastric ulcer.

Contraindications

Lactation (excreted into breast milk).

Common: Headache, dizziness, constipation, diarrhea, nausea and vomiting, anxiety, confusion.

Less Common: Acne, pruritus, urticaria, dry skin, fever, hypertension, flushing, musculoskeletal pain, arthralgia,
tinnitus

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Use with caution. May need dose adjustment.

Renal Impairment:
For mild-to-moderate impairment, dose reduction is warranted. Initiate dose at 40–50 mg once daily in adults. Refer
child to pediatrician.
For severe impairment, fenofibrate is contraindicated.

Indications

Hypertriglyceridemia; dyslipidemia associated with type 2 diabetes; second-line treatment in hypercholesterolemia

Contraindications

Photosensitivity to ketoprofen; pancreatitis, unless due to hypertriglyceridemia; severe renal impairment, including
patients receiving dialysis; hepatic impairment; primary biliary cirrhosis; gallstones; gall bladder disease;
unexplained, persistent liver function abnormality; pregnancy; lactation

Precautions

NOTE: Fenofibrate has a uricosuric effect, which may reduce uric acid concentrations by about 25%.

Hyperlipidemia; myopathy, myositis, and rhabdomyolysis (have been reported; concomitant HMG-CoA reductase
inhibitor may potentiate rhabdomyolysis and lead to acute renal failure).
Increase in hepatic transaminases (monitor regularly and discontinue if enzyme levels persist 3 times above the
upper limit of normal); increase in serum creatinine (may occur; monitor renal function).
Avoid exposure of skin to sun, wear protective clothing and use sunscreen
Lack of optimal response (withdraw therapy if an adequate response is not obtained after 2–3 months of therapy at
the maximal daily dose).
Women and obese individuals (at high risk for biliary tract disease; modest increase in the risk of cholesterol
gallstones, reflecting an increase in the cholesterol content of bile; may cause cholelithiasis)
Patients at risk for venous thromboembolism (associated with pulmonary embolism and deep venous thrombosis).
Elderly (higher incident of renal impairment)
Pregnancy (embryotoxicity in animal studies); lactation.

April 25, 2026, 12:59:08 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Arthralgia, bronchitis, cough, dizziness, fatigue, headache, insomnia, GI disturbances (e.g., dyspepsia,
abdominal pain); hypertension, nausea, rhinitis, infections (e.g., urinary tract, respiratory tract)
Less Common: Pancreatitis, photosensitivity, pulmonary embolism, VTE
Rare: Agranulocytosis, anemia, arrhythmias, hepatitis, cholestatic jaundice, gallstones, hypersensitivity reactions
(e.g., angioedema, anaphylaxis, exfoliative dermatitis), hypokalemia, leukopenia, myopathy, rhabdomyolysis,
thrombocytopenia

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Insulins, Sulfonylureas e.g. Gliclazide (hypoglycemic effect)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
Statins e.g. Atorvastatin (myopathy; rhabdomyolysis)

Avoid concomitant use with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Anticoagulants, e.g., Warfarin (anticoagulant effect)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
Anticoagulants, e.g., Warfarin (bleeding)

Reduces absorption of Fenofibrate:
Bile Acid Sequestrants

Mechanism of Action

A peroxisome proliferator receptor alpha (PPARα) activator, which modulates lipoprotein synthesis and catabolism. It
reduces plasma triglyceride, moderately increases high-density lipoprotein (HDL) and has a variable effect on LDL
concentrations.

Dosage

ORAL › 100 mcg sublingual (SL) tablets (Rx)

Surgery, premedication, by slow IV infusion, ADULT, 50–100 micrograms 30–60 minutes prior to surgery; or 25–50
micrograms given shortly before induction. Surgery adjunct to general anesthesia, by slow IV infusion, ADULT, low
dose, 1–2 micrograms/kg depending on indication; moderate dose with a sedative / hypnotic, 25–50 micrograms
every 15–30 minutes (0.5–2 micrograms/kg per hour), discontinue infusion 30–60 minutes prior to the end of
surgery to allow adequate ventilation upon emergence from anesthesia; Surgery adjunct to general anesthesia, by
IV bolus, ADULT, high dose, 4–20 micrograms/kg, then 2–10 micrograms/kg per hour. Surgery adjunct to regional
anesthesia, by slow IV infusion, ADULT, 50–100 micrograms over 1–2 minutes. Surgery adjunct to anesthesia, by IV
infusion, ADOLESCENT and CHILD =2 years; 2–3 micrograms/kg per dose every 1–2 hours as needed. Surgery,
postoperative recovery, by slow IV infusion, ADULT, 50–100 micrograms every 1–2 hours as needed.

Administration

ORAL › 100 mcg sublingual (SL) tablets (Rx)

Administer by slow IV infusion over 3–5 minutes. Rapid IV infusion may result in skeletal muscular and chest wall
rigidity, impaired ventilation, or respiratory distress or arrest. Nondepolarizing skeletal muscle relaxant may be
required. Prolonged use may cause tolerance, psychological and physical dependence. Tailor each opioid-
containing analgesic regimen based on patient's needs, type of pain being treated, route of administration,
degree of tolerance for opioids, age, weight, and medical condition. The optimal analgesic dose varies widely
among patients. Titrate doses to pain relief or prevention. Concurrent use of mixed agonist / antagonist
analgesics or partial agonist analgesics may precipitate withdrawal symptoms and/or reduced analgesic efficacy in
patients following prolonged therapy with mu opioid agonists. Abrupt discontinuation following prolonged use
may lead to withdrawal symptoms. Gradually taper dose to avoid this risk.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric patients are twice as sensitive as younger patients to the effects of fentanyl. Determine dose based on age,
weight, physical status, underlying disease states, other drugs used, type of anesthesia used, and the surgical
procedure to be performed.

Indications

Adjunct to general or regional anesthesia.

Page 2 of 5



April 25, 2026, 12:59:14 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Septicemia; severe hemorrhage or shock; local infection at proposed injection site; disturbances in blood
morphology and/or anticoagulant therapy.

Precautions

WARNING: Cytochrome P450 3A4 Interaction. Use with strong or moderate CYP3A4 inhibitors may result in increased
effects and potentially fatal respiratory depression. Discontinuation of a concomitant CYP3A4 inducer may result in
increased fentanyl concentrations. Monitor patients receiving any CYP 3A4 inhibitor or inducer.

Life-threatening respiratory depression. Serious, life-threatening, or fatal respiratory depression may occur. Monitor
closely for respiratory depression, especially during initiation or dose escalation. Carbon dioxide retention from
opioidinduced respiratory depression can exacerbate the sedating effects of opioids.

Abuse, misuse, and diversion. Users are exposed to the risks of addiction, abuse, and misuse, potentially leading to
overdose and death. Assess each patient’s risk prior to prescribing. Monitor all patients for development of these
behaviors or conditions. Risk for opioid abuse is increased in patients with a personal or family history of substance
abuse, including drug or alcohol abuse or addiction, or mental illness.

See General Information on Natural Opium Alkaloids under Opioids (General Information) for other information.

SKILLED TASKS. May impair a bility to perform tasks, which require mental alertness, like driving or operating
machinery.

WARNING: May exacerbate GI bleeding. Use with caution in patients with GI disorders.

Not to be administered for longer than 6 months (monitor closely for potential complications);
Transfusion-dependent anemia;
Peptic ulcer;
Regional enteritis;
Ulcerative colitis;
Intestinal strictures;
Diverticula;
Pregnancy (avoid use in the first trimester; administer only in women with low-normal hemoglobin).

NOTE: In cases of overdose, initiate therapy with deferoxamine. Before administration of deferoxamine, the stomach
should be emptied by gastric lavage (with a wide-bore tube), preferable within one hour of ingesting a significant
quantity of iron or if radiography reveals tablets in the stomach.

Dosage

IV / SC › 150 micrograms/0.6 mL vial (Rx)

Myelosuppressive chemotherapy recipient with non-myeloid malignancies, by SC injection or IV infusion, ADULT/
CHILD/ ADOLESCENT, 5 micrograms/kg per day, may increase by 5 micrograms/kg for each chemotherapy cycle
according to the duration and severity of the neutropenia; continue for up to 14 days until the absolute neutrophil
count (ANC) reaches 10,000/mm3; discontinue if the ANC surpasses 10,000/mm3 after the expected
chemotherapy-induced neutrophil nadir. AML following induction or consolidation chemotherapy, by SC injection
or IV infusion, ADULT/ CHILD/ ADOLESCENT, 5 micrograms/kg per day, may increase by 5 micrograms/kg (for each
chemotherapy cycle) according to the duration and severity of the neutropenia; continue for up to 14 days until the
absolute neutrophil count (ANC) reaches 10,000/mm3; discontinue if the ANC surpasses 10,000/mm3 after the
expected chemotherapy-induced neutrophil nadir. Bone marrow transplantation, by IV infusion, ADULT, 10
micrograms/kg per day, =24 hours after chemotherapy and =24 hours after bone marrow infusion; adjust the dose
according to the duration and severity of neutropenia; If ANC >1,000/mm3 for 3 consecutive days, reduce dose to 5
micrograms/kg per day; If ANC remains >1,000/mm3 for 3 more consecutive days, discontinue; If ANC decreases to
<1,000/mm3, resume at 5 micrograms/kg per day; If ANC decreases to <1,000/mm3 during the 5 micrograms/kg per
day dose, increase dose to 10 micrograms/kg per day and follow the above steps. Hematopoietic radiation injury
syndrome, by SC injection, ADULT, 10 micrograms/kg once daily; begin as soon as possible after suspected or
confirmed radiation doses >2 gray (Gy) and continue until ANC remains >1,000/mm3 for 3 consecutive CBCs or ANC
exceeds 10,000/mm3 after the radiation-induced nadir. [NOTE: Initiate within 24 hours of exposure of a dose =2 Gy
and/or significant decrease in absolute lymphocyte count, or for anticipated neutropenia <500/mm3 for =7 days.]
Peripheral blood progenitor cell collection and therapy, by SC injection, ADULT, 10 micrograms/kg daily, usually for
6–7 days with apheresis occurring on days 5, 6, and 7; begin at least 4 days before the first apheresis and continue
until the last apheresis; discontinue for WBC >100,000/mm3. Severe chronic neutropenia, congenital, by SC
injection, ADULT/ADOLESCENT/ CHILD/INFANT 7 months & older, initially 6 micrograms/kg per day in 2 divided
doses; adjust dose based on ANC and clinical response; mean dose is 6 micrograms/kg daily; Severe chronic
neutropenia, idiopathic, by SC injection, ADULT/ADOLESCENT/ CHILD/INFANT 7 months & older, initially 5
micrograms/kg once daily; adjust dose based on ANC and clinical response; mean dose is 1.2 micrograms/kg daily;
Severe chronic neutropenia, cyclic, by SC injection, ADULT/ADOLESCENT/ CHILD/INFANT 7 months & older, initially
5 micrograms/kg once daily; adjust the dose based on ANC and clinical response; mean dose is 2.1 micrograms/kg
daily. Primary prophylaxis for children with high likelihood of febrile neutropenia (e.g., children receiving dose-
intense chemotherapy for Ewing’s sarcoma/Primitive neuroectodermal tumors, rhabdomyosarcoma), 5-10 mcg/kg
48-72 hours after completion of chemotherapy.

Administration

IV / SC › 150 micrograms/0.6 mL vial (Rx)

For IV administration, administer as a short infusion over 15–30 minutes for chemotherapy-induced neutropenia,
by continuous infusion for chemotherapy-induced neutropenia, or as an infusion of no longer than 24 hours for
bone marrow transplantation. For SC administration, administer into the outer upper arm, abdomen (except within
2 inches of navel), front middle thigh, or the upper outer buttocks area. Rotate the injection site. Do NOT inject
into areas that are tender, red, bruised, hardened, or scarred, or sites with stretch marks. Do NOT administer
earlier than 24 hours after or in the 24 hours prior to cytotoxic chemotherapy. Should NOT be routinely used in the
treatment of established neutropenic fever. Vials may be diluted in D5W to >15 mcg/mL for infusion. DO NOT dilute
with saline at any time, because the product may precipitate. For 5-15 mcg/mL dilution: Add human albumin to a
final concentration of 2 mg/mL to protect from adsorption to plastic materials

Page 2 of 5



April 25, 2026, 12:59:40 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Renal Impairment:
If patient develops glomerulonephritis, consider dose reduction or treatment interruption.

Indications

To decrease the incidence of infection (neutropenic fever) in patients with non-myeloid malignancies receiving
myelosuppressive chemotherapy associated with a significant incidence of severe neutropenia with fever; to
reduce the time to neutrophil recovery and the duration of fever following induction or consolidation chemotherapy
in adults with acute myeloid leukemia (AML); to reduce the duration of neutropenia and neutropenia-related events
in patients with nonmyeloid malignancies receiving myeloablative chemotherapy followed by marrow
transplantation; to increase survival in patients acutely exposed to myelosuppressive doses of radiation; for
mobilization of peripheral blood progenitor cells (PBPCs) for collection by leukapheresis in autologous or allogeneic
hematopoietic cell transplantation; long-term administration to reduce the incidence and duration of neutropenic
complications in symptomatic patients with congenital, cyclic, or idiopathic neutropenia.

Page 3 of 5



April 25, 2026, 12:59:40 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

History of serious allergic reactions to human granulocyte colony-stimulating factors, such as filgrastim or
pegfilgrastim, or any component of the formulation

Precautions

Allergic reactions (serious allergic reactions, including anaphylaxis may occur; may be managed

symptomatically with antihistamines, steroids, bronchodilators, or epinephrine; reactions may recur within days
after initial allergy management has been stopped; permanently discontinue in serious allergic reactions).

Alveolar hemorrhage (pulmonary infiltrates, hemoptysis may occur; resolved upon discontinuation)

Respiratory distress syndrome

Capillary leak syndrome (hypoalbuminemia, hypotension, edema, or hemoconcentration may occur; may vary in
frequency and severity; may be life-threatening if treatment is delayed)

Cutaneous vasculitis (generally occurs in patients with severe chronic neutropenia on chronic therapy; withhold
treatment if cutaneous vasculitis occurs)

Hematologic effects (excessive leukocytosis or thrombocytopenia may occur; discontinue when absolute neutrophil
count (ANC) exceeds 10,000/mm3 after the ANC nadir has occurred; monitor CBC twice weekly during therapy;
monitor platelet counts)

Severe chronic neutropenia (myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) and acute myeloid leukemia (AML) have been reported)

Sickle cell disorders (may precipitate severe sickle cell crises, some fatal; discontinue in patients undergoing sickle
cell crisis)

Nephrotoxicity (glomerulonephritis, resulting in azotemia, hematuria, or proteinuria may occur; if glomerulonephritis
is suspected, evaluate for cause).

Splenic rupture (if upper abdominal pain, left upper quadrant pain, or shoulder tip pain occurs, withhold treatment
and evaluate for enlarged spleen or splenic rupture).

Page 4 of 5



April 25, 2026, 12:59:40 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Alopecia, anemia, antibody development, arthralgia, back pain, cardiac arrhythmia, chest pain, dizziness,
dyspnea, epistaxis, erythema, cough, fatigue, fever, headache, hypertension, hypoesthesia, increased lactate
dehydrogenase and uric acid (mild-to-moderate; reversible), leukocytosis, limb pain, maculopapular rash,
musculoskeletal pain, MI, muscle spam, nausea, ostealgia (dose and cycle related), pain, petechia, splenomegaly, skin
rash, thrombocytopenia, transfusion reaction, upper respiratory tract infection, urinary tract infection, vomiting

Less Common: Anaphylaxis, capillary leak syndrome, cerebral hemorrhage, constipation, decreased bone mineral
density, decreased hemoglobin, diarrhea, ethema nodosum, exacerbation of psoriasis, facial edema, hematuria,
hemoptysis, hepatomegaly, hypersensitivity reaction, renal insufficiency, hypersensitivity angiitis, injection site
reaction, hypotension, malaise, mouth pain, oropharyngeal pain, osteoporosis, peripheral edema, proteinuria,
pulmonary hemorrhage, pulmonary infiltrates, severe sickle cell crisis, respiratory distress syndrome, splenic
rupture, Sweet’s syndrome, tachycardia, weakness, urticaria, wheezing

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Bleomycin (pulmonary toxicity), Cyclophosphamide

Avoid concomitant use with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Topotecan
Drugs that may potentiate the release of neutrophils‚ such as lithium‚ should be used with caution

DRUG-DIAGNOSTIC TEST INTERACTION. May interfere with bone imaging studies, increased hematopoietic activity of
the bone marrow may appear as transient positive bone imaging changes.

Mechanism of Action

A granulocyte colony stimulating factor (G-CSF) produced by recombinant DNA technology. It stimulates the
production, maturation, and activation of neutrophils.

Administration

ORAL › 50 mg capsule (Rx)

For IV: Infuse over ~1-2 hours; Do not exceed 200 mg/hr; Do not add additional medication. For oral: May be
administered without regard to meals

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Refer to adult dosing.

Renal Impairment:
NOTE: Renal function estimated using the Cockcroft-Gault formula.

For multiple dosing in adults, administer loading dose of 50- 400 mg, then adjust daily doses as follows (dosage
reduction in children should parallel adult recommendations):

CrCl >50 mL/minute: No dosage adjustment necessary
CrCl ≤50 mL/minute (no dialysis): Reduce dose by 50%

Endstage renal disease on intermittent hemodialysis: 100% of daily dose (according to indication) after each
dialysissession;
On nondialysis days, patient should receive a reduced dose according to their CrCl;
Alternate recommendations: doses of 200-400 mg every 48-72 hours or 100-200 mg every 24 hours have been
recommended.

NOTE: Dosing dependent on the assumption of 3 times per week, complete intermittent hemodialysis sessions.

Continuous renal replacement therapy: Drug clearance is highly dependent on the method of renal
replacement,filter type, and flow rate.
Appropriate dosing requires close monitoring of pharmacologic response, signs of adverse reactions due to drug
accumulation, as well as drug concentrations in relation to target trough (if appropriate). The following are general
recommendations only (based on dialysate flow ultrafiltration rates of 1 to 2 L/hour and minimal residual renal
function) and should not supersede clinical judgment:

Continuous Veno-Venous Hemofiltration: Loading dose of 400-800 mg followed by 200-400 mg every 24 hours

Continuous venovenous hemodialysis / continuous venovenous hemodiafiltration: Loading dose of 400-800 mg
followed by 400-800 mg every 24 hours (Continuous
venovenous hemodialysis or continuous veno-venous hemodiafiltration) or 800 mg every 24 hours (continuous
veno-venous hemodiafiltration).

NOTE:
Higher maintenance doses of 400 mg every 24 hours (Continuous veno-Venous Hemofiltration),
800 mg every 24 hours (continuous venovenous hemodialysis), and 500-600 mg every 12 hours (continuous veno-
venous hemodiafiltration) may be considered when treating
resistant organisms and/or when employing combined ultrafiltration and dialysis flow rates of ≥2 L/hour for
continuous venovenous hemodialysis/ continuous venovenous
hemodiafiltration.

Treatment of candidiasis (esophageal, oropharyngeal, peritoneal, urinary tract, vaginal);

Hypersensitivity to fluconazole or any component of the formulation (crossreaction with other azole antifungal
agents may occur);

Mechanism of Action

Azoles are synthetic compounds that can be classified as either imidazoles or triazoles according to the number of
nitrogen atoms. Azoles exhibit activity by reducing the ergosterol synthesis through inhibition of fungal cytochrome

WARNING: Use with extreme caution in patients with renal impairment.

Close monitoring of hematologic, renal and hepatic status of all patients is essential. Use with caution in patients
with: Bone Marrow Depression;
Hematologic Disease; Patients who have been treated with radiation or drugs that suppress the bone marrow;
Hepatic Impairment; Renal Dysfunction.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Myocardial toxicity, chest pain, dyspnea, rash, pruritus, urticaria, nausea, jaundice, renal failure, azotemia,
crystalluria, anemia, leukopenia, ataxia, confusion.

Drug Interactions

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
Amphotericin B, Ganciclovir, Norfloxacin, Valganciclovir, Zidovudine

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Flucytosine:
Clozapine, Dipyrone, Hydroxyurea, Fluconazole, Itraconazole

Reduces therapeutic effect of Flucytosine:
BCG Vaccine

Mechanism of Action

Azoles are synthetic compounds that can be classified as either imidazoles or triazoles according to the number of
nitrogen atoms. Azoles exhibit activity by reducing the ergosterol synthesis through inhibition of fungal cytochrome
P450 enzymes.

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances CNS depressant effect of Flunarizine: Doxylamine, Dronabinol, Hydroxyzine, Magnesium Sulfate,
Minocycline, Mirtazapine, Nabilone
Enhances therapeutic effect (sedative effect) of the following drugs: Ethyl Alcohol Pramipexole, Ropinirole,
Rotigotine
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Flunarizine: CNS Depressants, Rufinamide (sleepiness and dizziness)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of SSRIs (psychomotor impairment)

Avoid concomitant use with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Flunarizine: Droperidol, Methotrimeprazine [reduce flunarizine dose by 50% with
concomitant use] (CNS depressant effect)
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Hydrocodone [consider starting at a 20–30% lower hydrocodone
dose when used concomitantly], Methotrimeprazine [reduce flunarizine dose by 50% with concomitant use],
Orphenadrine, Paraldehyde, Perampanel, Sodium Oxybate, Tapentadol [start tapentadol at one-third to one-half of
the normal dose when used concomitantly], Thalidomide Zolpidem (CNS depressant effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Alvimopan [administer at least 7 days apart],
Azelastine (Nasal) (CNS depressant effect) Buprenorphine

Mechanism of Action

Flunarizine is a selective calcium channel blocker that prevents cellular calcium overload by reducing excessive
transmembrane calcium influx. It has calmodulin binding properties and histamine H1 blocking activity. It also appears
to act by blocking dopamine D2 receptors in a manner similar to antipsychotics.

Stress due to trauma, surgery, or severe infection (may require higher doses); Some dosage forms may contain benzyl

administering to breastfeeding women).

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

S01JA01

ANATOMICAL

Sensory Organs

THERAPEUTIC

Ophthalmologicals

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Diagnostic Agents

CHEMICAL CLASS

Colouring agents

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IV › 10% (100 mg/mL) (as sodium salt), 5 mL ampule (Rx)

OPHTHALM › 1 mg strips (as sodium salt) (Rx)

OPHTHALM › 9 mg strips (as sodium salt) (Rx)

Dosage

IV › 10% (100 mg/mL) (as sodium salt), 5 mL ampule (Rx)

Angiography of the retinal and iris vasculature, by antecubital vein, ADULT, 500 mg over 5–10 seconds; CHILD, 7.5
mg/kg. Aid in the fitting of hard contact lenses, detection of corneal lesions and foreign bodies, and diagnostic
ophthalmic procedures, into the eye, ADULT, instill into the desired eye(s), may be given in combination with a local
anesthetic as 0.25% solution with oxybuprocaine HCl or proxymetacaine HCl.

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 1:02:38 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

IV › 10% (100 mg/mL) (as sodium salt), 5 mL ampule (Rx)

For IV administration, inspect visually for particulate matter and discoloration prior to administration. Do NOT mix
or dilute with other solutions or drugs. Attach a syringe into a transparent tubing and a 23-gauge butterfly needle
for injection. Insert the needle and draw the patient’s blood to the hub of the syringe so that a small air bubble
separates the patient’s blood in the tubing from the fluorescein. With the room lights on, slowly inject the blood
back into the vein while watching the skin over the needle tip. If the needle has extravasated, blood will be seen to
bulge the skin over the needle tip. If this occurs, stop before any fluorescein is injected. When assured that
extravasation has not occurred, the room light may be turned off and the fluorescein injected rapidly (1
mL/second) into the antecubital vein. Luminescence usually appears in the retina and choroidal vessels in 7–14
seconds and can be observed by standard viewing equipment. Flush IV cannulas before and after drugs are
injected to avoid physical incompatibility reactions. For sterile ophthalmic strips, hold the package in the middle
and tear down toward the sterile strip. Turn the package over and tear down from the other side, taking care not to
tear the sterile strip. Grasp each end of the package firmly and pull apart. Do NOT touch the sterile strip. Moisten
the strip with 1–2 drops of sterile irrigating or saline solution prior to application to ensure full fluorescence and
patient comfort. Touch the conjunctiva or fornix as required with the moistened tip. Instruct patient to blink
several times after application. Contents may not be sterile if individual strips have been damaged or previously
opened.

Dose Adjustment

No information found

Indications

Angiography of the retina and iris vasculature; aid in the fitting of hard contact lenses; detection of corneal lesions
and foreign bodies; diagnostic ophthalmic procedures; applanation tonometry.

Precautions

WARNING: An emergency tray should be available prior to the injection of fluorescein.

Respiratory reaction;
Severe local tissue damage (e.g., severe pain in the arm,
sloughing of the skin, superficial phlebitis, subcutaneous
granuloma, and toxic neuritis along the median curve in
the antecubital area);
GI effects (e.g., nausea, vomiting, and GI distress commonly
occur within the first few minutes following injection);
Pregnancy (use only if clearly needed);
Lactation (excreted in breast milk for up to 4 days; following
fluorescein angiography, discontinue breastfeeding for
at least 4 days and the milk should be pumped off and
discarded during this period).

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 1:02:38 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common:
Nausea, vomiting, GI distress, strong taste, extravasation, hypersensitivity reactions, transient coloration of skin and
urine, staining of the skin or clothing, staining of soft contact lenses, headache, thrombophlebitis.

Rare:
Cardiac arrest, basilar artery ischemia, severe shock,
convulsions, syncope.

Drug Interactions

No information found

Mechanism of Action

A precursor of eosins that temporarily stains any cell it enters, marking damaged areas.

Pregnancy & Remarks

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 1:02:44 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Renal Impairment:
Use with extreme caution.
For patients with CrCl <50 mL/minute or on continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT), dose adjustment is not
necessary.
For patients undergoing hemodialysis, administer 50% of standard dose following hemodialysis.

Hepatic Impairment:
Use with extreme caution.
Administer <50% of dose, then increase if toxicity does not occur.
For patients with bilirubin >5 mg/dL, avoid use.

Obesity:
Use actual body weight (full weight) for calculation of BSA-based or weight-based dosing, especially if the intent of
therapy is curative.
If dose reduction is done due to toxicity, consider resumption of full weight-based dosing with subsequent cycles,
especially if cause of toxicity is resolved.

Dihydropyrimidine Dehydrodeganase (DPD) Mutations:
Genetic absence of DPD has beenprolonged clearance and increaseddiarrhea, neutropenia, neurotoxicity.
associated toxicity, with e.g. diarrhea, neutropenia, neurotoxicity

Indications

Treatment of breast cancer, colon cancer, rectal cancer, pancreatic cancer, and stomach (gastric) cancer

Contraindications

Poor nutritional states; depressed bone marrow function; potentially serious infections

Precautions

WARNING: Discontinue treatment for stomatitis or esophagopharyngitis, rapidly falling WBC count, leukopenia (WBC
<3,500/mm3), intractable vomiting, diarrhea, frequent bowel movements, GI ulcer or bleeding, watery stools,
thrombocytopenia (platelets <100,000/mm3), hemorrhage, or MI.

To be administered only by or under the supervision of an experienced cancer chemotherapy physician who is well
versed in the use of potent antimetabolites. Hospitalize patients during the initial course of therapy due to the
possibility of severe toxic reactions.

WARNING: Hazardous agent. Use appropriate precautions for handling and disposal.

Hand-and-foot syndrome (tingling sensation, pain, symmetrical swelling, erythema, tenderness, desquamation may
occur).

generalized allergic reactions, headache, heart failure, lacrimal duct stenosis, lacrimation, MI, leukopenia (nadir: days
9–14; recovery by day 30), mesenteric ischemia (acute), myocardial ischemia, nail changes (nail loss), palmarplantar
erythrodysesthesia syndrome, nausea, nystagmus, pancytopenia, photophobia, photosensitivity, sloughing, pruritic
maculopapular rash, stomatitis, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, stroke, thrombocytopenia, thrombophlebitis, toxic

Enhances therapeutic effect of Fluorouracil: Leucovorin Calcium-Levo-leucovorin, Trastuzumab (neutropenic
effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Fluorouracil: Denosumab (serious infections), Leucovorin CalciumLevo-
leucovorin
Reduces diagnostic effect of Coccidioides immitis Skin Test

Avoid concomitant use with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Fluorouracil: Roflumilast, Tofacitinib (immunosuppressive effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Fluorouracil: Dipyrone (agranulocytosis, pancytopenia), Pimecrolimus,
Tacrolimus (Topical)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Clozapine (agranulocytosis), Leflunomide
(hematologic toxicity such as pancytopenia, agranulocytosis, and/or thrombocytopenia), Natalizumab (concurrent
infection)
Increases serum concentration of the following drugs: CYP2C9 Substrates, Diclofenac (Systemic), Fosphenytoin,
Phenytoin, Vitamin K Antagonists, e.g., Warfarin
Reduces therapeutic effect of Fluorouracil: Echinacea
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: BCG (Intravesical), Vaccines (live virus vaccines may progress to
infection)

Mechanism of Action

A pyrimidine analogue antimetabolite that intercalates with RNA and inhibits thymidylate synthetase, subsequently
interfering with DNA synthesis and results to tumor cell cytotoxicity.

FLUOXETINE

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

N06AB03

ANATOMICAL

Nervous System

THERAPEUTIC

Psychoanaleptics

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Antidepressants

CHEMICAL CLASS

Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › 20 mg dispersible tablet/capsule (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › 20 mg dispersible tablet/capsule (Rx)

Bulimia nervosa, by mouth, ADULT, initially 60 mg once daily in the morning. Some patients may need to begin at
lower dose and titrate over several days. Depression associated with bipolar 1 disorder, by mouth, ADULT, initially
20 mg once daily in the evening or with the fixed-dose combination, 25 mg once daily in the evening. Slowly titrate
dose based on efficacy and tolerability up to a maximum of 50 mg once daily in the evening, CHILD≥10 years, initially
20 mg once daily in the evening or with the fixed-dose combination, 25 mg once daily in the evening. Slowly titrate
dose based on efficacy and tolerability. Safety of fluoxetine doses >50 mg in combination with doses >12 mg of
olanzapine has not been studied in pediatrics. Major depressive disorder, by mouth, ADULT, initially 20 mg once
daily in the morning. Allow a few weeks to assess effects before titrating dose slowly up to a maximum of 80 mg
once daily in the morning. Continue treatment for up to 4 to 9 months, CHILD≥8 years, initially 10 mg once daily in
the morning. Allow at least 1 week to assess effects before titrating dose slowly up to a maximum of 20 mg once
daily in the morning. Continue treatment for at least 4 to 9 months. Obsessive compulsive disorder, by mouth,
ADULT, initially 20 mg once daily in the morning. Allow a few weeks to assess effects before titrating dose slowly
up to a maximum of 80 mg once daily in the morning. Continue treatment for at least 1 to 2 years after initial
response. Panic disorder with or without agoraphobia, by mouth, ADULT, initially 10 mg once daily in the morning.
Increase to 20 mg once daily in the morning after 1 week. Allow a few weeks to assess effects before titrating dose
slowly up to a maximum of 60 mg once daily in the morning. Continue treatment for at least 12 months after initial
response. Premenstrual dysphoric disorder, by mouth, ADULT, initially 20 mg once daily in the morning through the
menstrual cycle or limited to the luteal phase. Treatment-resistant depression, in combination with olanzapine, by
mouth, ADULT, initially 20 mg once daily in the evening or with the fixed-dose combination, 25 mg once daily in the
evening. Slowly titrate dose based on efficacy and tolerability up to a maximum of 50 mg once daily in the evening.
NOTE: For all indications, allow a sufficient duration of therapy for around 4-8 weeks on the maximum tolerable
dose before switching the antidepressant.

Page 1 of 3



April 24, 2026, 11:17:03 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

ORAL › 20 mg dispersible tablet/capsule (Rx)

May be taken with or without food.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Decrease initial dose and increase dose intervals; some service users/patients may require an initial dose of 10
mg/day with dosage increases of 10 mg and 20 mg every several weeks as tolerated; should not be taken at night
unless service user/patient experiences sedation.

NOTE: Use with caution in patients >65 years, patients with history of falls or fractures and patients with a potential
to cause SIADH or hyponatremia.

Renal Impairment:
No dosage adjustment necessary. Accumulation of fluoxetine or norfluoxetine is possible due to chronic
administration in patients with severely impaired renal
function.

Hepatic Impairment:
Decrease initial dose and increase dose intervals; for service users/patients with cirrhosis, administer a lower dose
or less frequent dosing interval; for service users/patients with compensated cirrhosis without ascites, administer
50% of normal dose.

Indications

Management of major depressive disorder, depression associated with bipolar 1 disorder in combination with
olanzapine, treatment-resistant depression in combination with olanzapine, obsessive compulsive disorder,
premenstrual dysphoric disorder, bulimia nervosa, panic disorder with or without agoraphobia.

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to fluoxetine

Common: delayed ejaculation, erectile dysfunction; men and women: anorgasmia, urinary frequency, urination
