tremors, chills, emotional lability, headache, dizziness, headaches, anxiety, nervousness, yawning, fatigue, anorexia,
abnormal dreams, amnesia, confusion, QTc prolongation, palpitations, vasodilation, chest pain, hypertension,
sweating, skin rash, pruritis, hypermenorrhea, increased thirst, weight loss, weight gain, decreased sexual desire,
bruising, pharyngitis,hyperkinesia, asthenia, weakness , visual disturbance, otalgia, tinnitus, flu-like symptoms,
sinusitis, epistaxis.

Rare: bleeding, hyponatremia and SIADH, seizures, induction of mania, suicidality, falls, fractures and osteoporosis
(long-term use), abnormal hepatic function tests, acne vulgaris, acute abdominal condition, akathisia, albuminuria,
alopecia, amenorrhea, anaphylactoid reaction, anemia, angina pectoris, angle-closure glaucoma, aphthous
stomatitis, aplastic anemia, arthritis, asthma, ataxia, atrial fibrillation, bruise, bruxism, bursitis, cardiac arrhythmia,
cataract, cerebrovascular accident, cholelithiasis, cholestatic jaundice, colitis, congestive heart failure, dehydration,
delusions, depersonalization, dyskinesia, dysphagia, dysuria, ecchymoses, edema, eosinophilic pneumonitis,
equilibrium disturbance, erythema multiforme, erythema nodosum, esophagitis, euphoria, exfoliative dermatitis,
extrapyramidal reaction, gastritis, gastroenteritis, gastrointestinal ulcer, glossitis, gout, gynecological
gynecomastia, hallucination, hemolytic anemia, hepatic failure, hepatic necrosis, hepatitis, hiccups, hostility,
hypersensitivity reaction, hypertonia, hyperventilation, hypoglycemia, hypokalemia, hypotension, hypothyroidism,
immune thrombocytopenia, laryngeal edema, laryngospasm, leg cramps, lupus-like syndrome, malaise, melena,
memory impairment, migraine, mydriasis, myocardial infarction, myoclonus, neuroleptic malignant syndrome, optic
neuritis, orthostatic hypotension, ostealgia, pancreatitis, pancytopenia, paranoia, petechia, priapism, pulmonary
embolism, pulmonary fibrosis, pulmonary hypertension, purpuric rash, renal failure, serotonin syndrome, sexual
disorder (may persist after discontinuation), skin photosensitivity, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, syncope,
tachycardia, thrombocytopenia, toxic epidermal necrolysis, vasculitis, ventricular tachycardia (including torsades de
pointes), violent behavior

Mechanism of Action

Fluoxetine is a propylamine with a long half-life that, in addition to blocking SERT, also has antagonistic properties at
the 5-HT2C receptor and weak norepinephrine reuptake blockade. It blocks SERT and possesses antagonistic
properties at the 5-HT2C leading to disinhibition of dopamine and norepinephrine release resulting to precognitive
effects.

FLUPENTIXOL

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

N05AF01

ANATOMICAL

Nervous System

THERAPEUTIC

Psycholeptics

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Antipsychotics

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IM › 20 mg/mL (as decanoate) 1 mL ampule vial (Rx)

IM › 20 mg/mL (as decanoate) 10 mL vial (Rx)

Dosage

IM › 20 mg/mL (as decanoate) 1 mL ampule vial (Rx)

Maintenance therapy of schizophrenia and reduces symptoms of acute psychotic mania but not proven as a mood
stabilizer or as an effective maintenance treatment in bipolar disorder, by IM injection, ADULT (stabilized on oral
flupentixol), 20 mg into the gluteal muscle; continue oral flupentixol until 1 week after first injection while slowly
tapering; administer second 20 mg dose not earlier than 4–10 days after the first injection, followed by 20–40 mg
every 2–3 weeks maintenance dose; maximum of 200 mg every 1-4 weeks NOTE: Conversion of oral flupentixol to
long-acting injectable maintenance dose: x mg once daily is equivalent to: 4x mg decanoate every 2 weeks; or 8x
mg decanoate every 4 weeks Slowly titrate dose at maximum increments of =20 mg based on efficacy and
tolerability up to a maximum maintenance dose of 200 mg every 1–4 weeks.

Administration

IM › 20 mg/mL (as decanoate) 1 mL ampule vial (Rx)

Administer by deep IM injection into the gluteal muscle or deltoid muscle. Do NOT discontinue abruptly because of
the possibility of withdrawal and rebound psychosis. Discontinue or crosstitrate gradually to minimize the
potential of withdrawal, rebound psychosis, extrapyramidal side effects, cholinergic rebound, rebound insomnia,
and unwanted pregnancy, unless safety concerns require a more rapid withdrawal.

Page 1 of 5



April 25, 2026, 1:00:24 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Initially 5 mg by IM injection into the gluteal muscle. Recommended maintenance dose is 10–20 mg every 15 days.

Renal Impairment:
Recommended maintenance dose is 10–20 mg every 15 days.

Hepatic Impairment:
Can precipitate coma. Consider serum-flupentixol concentration monitoring

Indications

Management of chronic schizophrenia whose main manifestations do not include excitement, agitation or
hyperactivity; bipolar disorder

Page 2 of 5



April 25, 2026, 1:00:24 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Acute intoxication with ethanol, barbiturate, or opioid; psychoneurotic patients or geriatric patients with confusion
and/or agitation; suspected or established subcortical brain damage; CNS depression; blood dyscrasias; comatose
or brain damage; pheochromocytoma; liver damage; severe cardiovascular disorder; cerebrovascular insufficiency;
renal insufficiency.

Precautions

WARNING: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome. Has been associated with use. Monitor for mental status changes, fever,
muscle rigidity and/or autonomic instability, elevated creatine phosphokinase (CPK), myoglobinuria, and/or acute
renal failure. Discontinue treatment immediately with onset of NMS. Recurrence has been reported when
rechallenged with antipsychotic therapy.

Elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis treated with antipsychotic drugs are at an increased risk of death.

Anticholinergic effects (e.g., constipation, xerostomia, blurred vision, urinary retention; use is associated with
increased risk of paralytic ileus, bowel obstruction, fecal impaction, bowel perforation, and in rare cases death);
Impaired temperature regulation

CNS depression; extrapyramidal symptoms (e.g., acute dystonic reactions, pseudoparkinsonism, akathisia, and
tardive dyskinesia; risk of dystonia may be greater with increased doses); dementia; Parkinson disease; seizure
disorder; ocular effects (lens opacity has been reported rarely).

Esophageal dysmotility or aspiration; masking of toxicity of other drugs or conditions; diabetic ketoacidosis

Altered cardiac conduction (life-threatening arrhythmias); cardiovascular disease; orthostatic hypotension; blood
dyscrasias (agranulocytosis, neutropenia, leukopenia, and granulocytopenia); venous thromboembolism.

Glaucoma; hepatic impairment; renal impairment; prolactin-dependent tumors.

Surgical patients (monitor closely for hypotension).

Elderly (increased risk of tardive dyskinesia, especially among women; potentially inappropriate in the elderly; avoid
use in patients =65 years with dementia due to an increased risk of mortality and cerebrovascular accidents, a
greater rate of cognitive decline, and potential to cause or exacerbate SIADH or hyponatremia, unless all
nonpharmacologic options have failed or are not possible or the patient is threatening substantial harm to self or to
others).

Pregnancy (risk of abnormal muscle movements and withdrawal symptoms in newborns with fetal exposure during
the third trimester); lactation (excreted in breastmilk; tardive dyskinesia, dystonia, and sedation have been observed
in nursing infants).

SKILLED TASKS. May impair ability to perform tasks, which require mental alertness, like driving or operating
machinery.

Page 3 of 5



April 25, 2026, 1:00:24 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Neuroleptic-induced deficit syndrome, extrapyramidal symptoms, Parkinsonism, tardive dyskinesia
(duration and dose dependent), acute dystonia, insomnia, restlessness, agitation, galactorrhea, gynecomastia,
amenorrhea, weight gain, sedation, tachycardia, hypomania, photosensitivity, longterm skin pigmentation changes.

Less Common: ECG changes (broadened, flattened T waves, QT and PR interval prolongation, ST segment
depression), palpitations, syncope, depression, dizziness, drowsiness, headache, hyperreflexia, hypertonia,
hypomania, insomnia, opisthotonos, psychomotor agitation, contact dermatitis, diaphoresis, eczema, erythema,
exfoliative dermatitis, pruritus, seborrhea, skin rash, urticaria, amenorrhea, decreased libido, hyperprolactinemia,
increased libido, fatigue, weight changes, constipation, nausea, paralytic ileus, sialorrhea, xerostomia, impotence,
urination disorder, eosinophilia, jaundice, tremor, blurred vision, cataract, oculogyric crisis, accommodation
disturbance, abdominal pain, confusion, diarrhea, dyspepsia, dyspnea, ejaculation failure, erectile dysfunction,
flatulence, hot flash, hyperglycemia, impaired glucose tolerance, increased appetite, muscle rigidity, myalgia,
nervousness, prolonged QT interval, priapism, speech disturbance, torsades de pointes, urinary retention,
ventricular arrhythmia, ventricular fibrillation, ventricular tachycardia, vomiting, weakness.

Rare: Cholestatic jaundice, jaundice, neuroleptic malignant syndrome, seizures, changes in body thermoregulation,
blood dyscrasias (eosinophilia, leukocytosis or leukopenia, granulocytopenia, agranulocytosis, thrombocytopenia).

NOTE: Patients taking this drug may experience weight gain and/or sedation.

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Antihypertensives CNS Depressants (central effects)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Flupentixol: Methylphenidate, Metoclopramide (sleepiness and
dizziness) Lithium (neurotoxic effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Acetylcholinesterase Inhibitors (extrapyramidal
symptoms) Antihypertensives (hypotension) Glucagon (GI adverse effects) Iohexol, Iomeprol, Iopamidol (seizures)
Mequitazine (arrhythmogenic effect), Mirabegron, Topiramate QTc-prolonging Agents (torsades de pointes) Serotonin
Modulators (neuroleptic malignant syndrome; serotonin syndrome) SSRIs (psychomotor impairment)
Reduces absorption of Nitroglycerin (decreases dissolution of sublingual nitroglycerin tablets)
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Antidiabetics, Dopamine Agonists, Prokinetics, Secretin
Amphetamines (stimulant effect) Anticholinergics (anticholinergic effect) Epinephrine (reverses pressor effect)
Guanethidine (antihypertensive effects)

Avoid concomitant use with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of CNS Depressants [high dose] (CNS depression)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Other Antipsychotics (increased hospitalization
rates and length of stay; increased mortality; QTc prolongation; torsades de pointes)

Mechanism of Action

Flupentixol is a thioxanthene typical antipsychotic that inhibits dopamine-mediated effects by blocking postsynaptic
dopamine receptors in the CNS. It was formulated as a long-acting injectable for use in patients with schizophrenia
who have difficulty with adherence to oral medications.

FLUPHENAZINE

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

N05AB02

ANATOMICAL

Nervous System

THERAPEUTIC

Psycholeptics

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Antipsychotics

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IM › 25 mg/mL (as decanoate), 1 mL ampule vial (Rx)

IM › 25 mg/mL (as decanoate), 10 mL vial (Rx)

Dosage

IM › 25 mg/mL (as decanoate), 1 mL ampule vial (Rx)

Psychotic disorders, by IM injection, ADULT (stabilized on oral fluphenazine), 12.5 mg/0.5 mL to 25 mg/1 mL into the
deltoid or gluteal muscle; determine subsequent doses and intervals based on patient’s response; average dosing
is usually 6.25–25 mg every 2 weeks or 50 mg/2 mL not longer than every 4 weeks; if doses >50 mg are needed,
increase cautiously in 12.5 mg up to a maximum of 100 mg per dose.

Administration

IM › 25 mg/mL (as decanoate), 1 mL ampule vial (Rx)

Administer by IM injection using a dry syringe and needle of =21 gauge. A wet needle or syringe may cause the
solution to become cloudy. Z-track injection techniques are recommended to limit leakage after injections. Do
NOT discontinue abruptly because of the possibility of withdrawal and rebound psychosis. Discontinue or
crosstitrate gradually to minimize the potential of withdrawal, rebound psychosis, extrapyramidal side effects,
cholinergic rebound, rebound insomnia, and unwanted pregnancy, unless safety concerns require a more rapid
withdrawal.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Use with caution and initiate with lower dose

Renal Impairment:
Use with caution and titrate slower. Monitoring is recommended.

Hepatic Impairment:
Avoid in hepatic failure. Can precipitate coma.
Phenothiazines are hepatotoxic.

Indications

Management of psychotic disorders.

April 25, 2026, 1:07:26 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Comatose state; CNS depression; children <12 years; hepatic disease; blood dyscrasias; bone marrow suppression;
subcortical brain injury with or without hypothalamic damage; marked cerebral atherosclerosis; pheochromocytoma;
pregnancy (third trimester); lactation.

Precautions

WARNING: NOT approved for the treatment of patients with dementia-related psychosis. Elderly patients with
dementia-related psychosis treated with antipsychotic drugs are at an increased risk of death, mostly associated
with cardiovascular diseases (e.g. heart failure, sudden death) or infections (e.g. pneumonia).

Altered cardiac conduction (life-threatening arrhythmias); orthostatic hypotension; cardiovascular disease;
respiratory disorder; blood dyscrasias (agranulocytosis, neutropenia, and leukopenia, some fatal).

Anticholinergic effects (e.g., constipation, xerostomia, blurred vision, urinary retention); pheochromocytoma;
hyperprolactinemia; temperature regulation (impaired core body temperature regulation); neuroleptic malignant
syndrome.

CNS depression; extrapyramidal symptoms (pseudoparkinsonism, acute dystonic reactions, akathisia, and tardive
dyskinesia; risk of dystonia may be greater with increased doses); Parkinson’s disease; seizure disorder; ocular
effects (cause pigmentary retinopathy, and lenticular and corneal deposits); glaucoma.

Esophageal dysmotility or aspiration; hepatic effects (liver damage and jaundice of the cholestatic type of hepatitis);
hepatic impairment; renal impairment; masking.

Elderly (increased risk of tardive dyskinesia, especially among women; potentially inappropriate in the elderly; avoid
use in patients =65 years with dementia due to an increased risk of mortality and cerebrovascular accidents, a
greater rate of cognitive decline, and potential to cause or exacerbate SIADH or hyponatremia, unless all
nonpharmacologic options have failed or are not possible or the patient is threatening substantial harm to self or to
others).

Pregnancy (risk of abnormal muscle movements and withdrawal symptoms in newborns with fetal exposure during
the third trimester); lactation (tardive dyskinesia, dystonia, and sedation have been observed in nursing infants).

Additive effect of drugs with: CNS depressants

CNS excitatory effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Fluphenazine: Diuretics (hypotension) Epinephrine (low blood pressure)
Methylphenidate, Metoclopramide (sleepiness and dizziness) Lithium (neurotoxic effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Diuretics (hypotension) Glucagon (GI adverse
effects) Hypotension-associated Agents (hypotension) Iohexol, Iomeprol, Iopamidol (seizures) Mequitazine

Mechanism of Action

Fluphenazine is a phenothiazine typical antipsychotic formulated as a long-acting injectable for use in patients with
schizophrenia who have difficulty with adherence to oral medications. It blocks brain dopamine (D2) receptors in the
mesolimbic system and decreases the release of hypothalamic and hypophyseal hormones.

FLUPHENAZINE

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

N05AB02

ANATOMICAL

Nervous System

THERAPEUTIC

Psycholeptics

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Antipsychotics

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IM › 25 mg/mL (as decanoate), 1 mL ampule vial (Rx)

IM › 25 mg/mL (as decanoate), 10 mL vial (Rx)

Dosage

IM › 25 mg/mL (as decanoate), 1 mL ampule vial (Rx)

Psychotic disorders, by IM injection, ADULT (stabilized on oral fluphenazine), 12.5 mg/0.5 mL to 25 mg/1 mL into the
deltoid or gluteal muscle; determine subsequent doses and intervals based on patient’s response; average dosing
is usually 6.25–25 mg every 2 weeks or 50 mg/2 mL not longer than every 4 weeks; if doses >50 mg are needed,
increase cautiously in 12.5 mg up to a maximum of 100 mg per dose.

Administration

IM › 25 mg/mL (as decanoate), 1 mL ampule vial (Rx)

Administer by IM injection using a dry syringe and needle of =21 gauge. A wet needle or syringe may cause the
solution to become cloudy. Z-track injection techniques are recommended to limit leakage after injections. Do
NOT discontinue abruptly because of the possibility of withdrawal and rebound psychosis. Discontinue or
crosstitrate gradually to minimize the potential of withdrawal, rebound psychosis, extrapyramidal side effects,
cholinergic rebound, rebound insomnia, and unwanted pregnancy, unless safety concerns require a more rapid
withdrawal.

Page 1 of 5



April 25, 2026, 1:07:52 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Use with caution and initiate with lower dose

Renal Impairment:
Use with caution and titrate slower. Monitoring is recommended.

Hepatic Impairment:
Avoid in hepatic failure. Can precipitate coma.
Phenothiazines are hepatotoxic.

Indications

Management of psychotic disorders.

Page 2 of 5



April 25, 2026, 1:07:52 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Comatose state; CNS depression; children <12 years; hepatic disease; blood dyscrasias; bone marrow suppression;
subcortical brain injury with or without hypothalamic damage; marked cerebral atherosclerosis; pheochromocytoma;
pregnancy (third trimester); lactation.

Precautions

WARNING: NOT approved for the treatment of patients with dementia-related psychosis. Elderly patients with
dementia-related psychosis treated with antipsychotic drugs are at an increased risk of death, mostly associated
with cardiovascular diseases (e.g. heart failure, sudden death) or infections (e.g. pneumonia).

Altered cardiac conduction (life-threatening arrhythmias); orthostatic hypotension; cardiovascular disease;
respiratory disorder; blood dyscrasias (agranulocytosis, neutropenia, and leukopenia, some fatal).

Anticholinergic effects (e.g., constipation, xerostomia, blurred vision, urinary retention); pheochromocytoma;
hyperprolactinemia; temperature regulation (impaired core body temperature regulation); neuroleptic malignant
syndrome.

CNS depression; extrapyramidal symptoms (pseudoparkinsonism, acute dystonic reactions, akathisia, and tardive
dyskinesia; risk of dystonia may be greater with increased doses); Parkinson’s disease; seizure disorder; ocular
effects (cause pigmentary retinopathy, and lenticular and corneal deposits); glaucoma.

Esophageal dysmotility or aspiration; hepatic effects (liver damage and jaundice of the cholestatic type of hepatitis);
hepatic impairment; renal impairment; masking.

Elderly (increased risk of tardive dyskinesia, especially among women; potentially inappropriate in the elderly; avoid
use in patients =65 years with dementia due to an increased risk of mortality and cerebrovascular accidents, a
greater rate of cognitive decline, and potential to cause or exacerbate SIADH or hyponatremia, unless all
nonpharmacologic options have failed or are not possible or the patient is threatening substantial harm to self or to
others).

Pregnancy (risk of abnormal muscle movements and withdrawal symptoms in newborns with fetal exposure during
the third trimester); lactation (tardive dyskinesia, dystonia, and sedation have been observed in nursing infants).

SKILLED TASKS. May impair ability to perform tasks, which require mental alertness, like driving or operating
machinery.

April 25, 2026, 1:06:59 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Severe hepatic impairment

Precautions

WARNING: Hazardous agent. Use appropriate precautions for handling and disposal. Hospitalization and death (rare)
due to liver failure have been reported. Use is not recommended in patients with ALT values greater than 2 times
ULN.

Gynecomastia (may occur when administered in combination with medical castration)

Hepatic impairment (e.g. elevated serum transaminase levels, jaundice, hepatic encephalopathy, and acute hepatic
failure; hepatotoxicity is reversible; monitor serum transaminase levels periodically).

Cardiovascular disease (may increase risk of cardiovascular disease)

Hemoglobin M disease (increased risk of toxicities associated with aniline exposure).

Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency and smoking (increased risk of toxicities associated with
aniline exposure)

Women (should not be used in women, particularly for nonserious or nonlife-threatening conditions).

Pregnancy (may cause fetal harm); lactation (no information available; use is not recommended).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Edema, hypertension, anxiety, confusion, depression, dizziness, drowsiness, headache, insomnia,
nervousness, skin rash, ecchymoses, pruritus,hot flash, decreased libido, increased lactate dehydrogenase,
gynecomastia, diarrhea, vomiting, nausea, proctitis, anorexia, tumor flare, constipation, dyspepsia, increased
appetite, impotence, cystitis, breast tenderness, gastric distress, hematuria, rectal hemorrhage, anemia, anemia,
leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, herpes zoster, weakness

Less Common: Cholestatic jaundice, hemolytic anemia, hepatic encephalopathy, hepatic failure, hepatic necrosis,
hepatitis, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, jaundice, macrocytic anemia, MI, malignant neoplasm of breast (male), skin
methemoglobinemia, oligospermia, pulmonary embolism, photosensitivity, thrombophlebitis, sulf-hemoglobinemia,
urine discoloration (amber, yellowgreen)

Administer twice daily every day by the orally inhaled route only. After inhalation, rinse the mouth with water and
spit out the water without swallowing.

No information found

Indications

Prophylaxis of asthma

Contraindications

Prophylaxis of asthma

Precautions

Patient with active or latent TB; Fungal, bacterial or systemic viral infection; Cataracts and/or glaucoma; Untreated
localized infection of the nasal mucosa; Untreated cutaneous infection, rosacea, acne vulgaris, perioral dermatitis,
perianal or genital pruritus, pruritus without inflammation; Hepatic impairment; Avoid abrupt withdrawal when
switching from systemic to oral or orally inhaled corticosteroid; Children; Pregnancy and lactation.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common and Less Common: Eosinophilic conditions, adrenal insufficiency, growth retardation, Cushing's syndrome,
decreased bone density, cataract, glaucoma, paradoxical bronchospasm (potentially fatal)

Rare: Psychological effects

April 25, 2026, 1:06:36 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

RESPIR › DPI: 100 micrograms fluticasone (as propionate) + 50 micrograms salmeterol (as xinafoate) x 28 doses and 60 doses
with appropriate accompanying dispenser (Rx)

Should be administered twice daily every day by the orally inhaled route only. After inhalation, the patient should
rinse the mouth with water and spit out the water without swallowing.

Indications

For asthma patients aged 4 years and older; maintenance treatment of airflow obstruction, and reduction of
exacerbations in patients with COPD

NOTE: This should be used only if the asthma is deemed to be not well-controlled with a long-term asthma control
medicine (e.g., inhaled corticosteroids).

Contraindications

As primary treatment of status asthmaticus, acute episodes of asthma or COPD requiring intensive measures, and
potentially worsening or rapidly deteriorating asthma or COPD.

Precautions

Deterioration of disease and acute episodes: do not initiate in acutely deteriorating asthma or to treat acute
symptoms; Do NOT combine LABAs because of risk of overdose; Localized infections; Immunosuppression;
Pneumonia; Cardiovascular and CNS disorders; Hypercorticolism and adrenal suppression; Decreased bone mineral
density; Growth effects; Glaucoma and cataracts; Metabolic effects (be alert to eosinophilic conditions, hypokalemia
and hyperglycemia); Patients with convulsive disorders, thyrotoxicosis, diabetes mellitus and ketoacidosis.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Dizziness, dysphonia, headache, nausea, palpitations, throat irritation, upper respiratory tract infection
and inflammation, tremor, vomiting
Less Common: Agitation, hyperactivity in children, hyperglycemia (high doses), insomnia, tachycardia
Rare: Allergic reactions (including urticaria, angioedema, anaphylaxis), hypokalemia, lactic acidosis, paradoxical
bronchospasm

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 1:06:36 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Avoid concomitant use with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Fluticasone + Salmeterol:
CYP450 3A4 Inhibitors e.g. Ritonavir (may cause
systemic corticosteroid and cardiovascular effects)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Fluticasone +
Salmeterol:
Corticosteroids (serious hypokalemia), Diuretics e.g., Furosemide, Hydrochlorothiazide (serious hypokalemia), Drugs
that increase blood glucose concentration e.g., Glucocorticoids, Antipsychotics, Calcineurin Inhibitors e.g.,
Tacrolimus, high-dose Thiazide Diuretics e.g. Hydrochlorothiazide, Somatropin (hyperglycemia), Drugs that reduce
potassium concentration e.g., Amphotericin B, Theophylline (serious hypokalemia)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following
drugs:
Sympathomimetic Amines e.g., Ephedrine, Phenylephrine, Pseudoephedrine (sympathetic adverse effects e.g.,
tremor, tachycardia, headache)

Increases serum concentration of Fluticasone + Salmeterol:
CYP450 3A4 Inhibitors e.g. Ritonavir

Reduces therapeutic effect of Beta Blockers (may
precipitate asthma)

Mechanism of Action

A combination product containing an inhaled corticosteroid, and a long-acting beta2-adrenoceptor agonist, which is
used for controlling the symptoms of asthma.

Anemia, by mouth, IV, IM, or SC injection, ADULT, 0.4 mg daily; PREGNANT AND LACTATING WOMEN, 0.8 mg daily;

Administration

ORAL › 400 micrograms tablet/capsule (OTC)

May be administered by deep IM injection. For IV injection or infusion, administer =5 mg dose undiluted over =1
minute or dilute =5 mg in 50 mL of NS or D5W and infuse over 30 minutes. May be added to IV maintenance
solutions and given as an infusion.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric: Vitamin B12 deficiency must be ruled out before initiating folate therapy due to frequency of combined
nutritional deficiencies. RDA requirements, 400 micrograms daily (minimum, 0.4 mg).

Indications

Treatment of megaloblastic and macrocytic anemias due to folate deficiency; used for diarrhea in pediatric patients

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to folic acid or any component of the formulation

Precautions

Anemia (not appropriate for monotherapy with pernicious, aplastic, or normocytic anemias when anemia is present
with vitamin B12 deficiency); pernicious anemia (doses >0.1 mg daily may obscure pernicious anemia with continuing
irreversible nerve damage progression);
Depressed hematopoiesis, alcoholism, and deficiencies of other vitamins.
Elderly;
Neonates;
Lactation (excreted in breastmilk; increased folate requirement).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Flushing, malaise, erythema, pruritus, rash, bronchospasm, allergic reaction

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 1:12:24 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Avoid concomitant use with:
Reduces therapeutic effect of Raltitrexed

Mechanism of Action

Also known as Vitamin B9, it is reduced in the body to tetrahydrofolate, which is a coenzyme for various metabolic
processes, including the synthesis of purine and pyrimidine nucleotides, and hence in the synthesis of DNA. It is also
involved in some amino acid conversions, and in the formation and utilization of formate. Folic acid is also used in
women of childbearing potential and pregnant women to protect against neural tube defects in their offspring.

FOMEPIZOLE (4-METHYLPRAZOLE)

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

V03AB34

ANATOMICAL

Various

THERAPEUTIC

All Other Therapeutic Products

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Specific Antidotes

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IV › 1 mg/mL, 1.5 mL vial (Rx)

Dosage

IV › 1 mg/mL, 1.5 mL vial (Rx)

Methanol or ethylene glycol poisoning, by slow IV infusion, ADULT, 15 mg/kg loading dose, followed by 4 doses of 10
mg/kg every 12 hours, then 15 mg/kg every 12 hours.

Administration

IV › 1 mg/mL, 1.5 mL vial (Rx)

Administer each dose as a slow IV infusion over 30 minutes. Do NOT administer undiluted or by bolus injection.
Using sterile technique, draw the appropriate dose from the vial with a syringe and inject into at least 100 mL of
sterile 0.9% sodium chloride injection or dextrose 5% injection. Mix well. Infuse the resulting solution over 30
minutes. Visually inspect parenteral products for particulate matter and discoloration prior to administration. Do
NOT use solutions showing haziness, particulate matter, precipitate, discoloration, or leakage. NOTE: Begin
fomepizole treatment immediately upon suspicion of ethylene glycol or methanol ingestion, or a documented
serum ethylene glycol or methanol concentration greater than 20 mg/dL. Discontinue treatment when ethylene
glycol or methanol concentrations are undetectable or have been reduced below 20 mg/dL, and the patient is
asymptomatic with normal pH. Consider hemodialysis to remove ethylene glycol or methanol and the respective
toxic metabolites of these alcohols in patients with high ethylene glycol or methanol concentrations (≥50 mg/dL),
significant metabolic acidosis, or renal failure. Dialyze to correct metabolic abnormalities and to lower the
ethylene glycol concentrations below 50 mg/dL. Fomepizole solidifies at temperatures <25°C (77°F). If this occurs,
liquefy the solution by running the vial under warm water or by holding in the hand. Solidification does not affect
the efficacy, safety, or stability of fomepizole. Fomepizole diluted as stated above remains stable and sterile for at
least 24 hours when stored in a refrigerator or at room temperature. Do NOT use beyond 24 hours. Fomepizole
does NOT contain preservatives.

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 1:05:43 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Renal Impairment: In patients undergoing hemodialysis: If at the beginning of hemodialysis and ≥6 hours since last
fomepizole dose, administer next scheduled dose. During hemodialysis, administer dose every 4 hours. At
completion of hemodialysis, administer next scheduled dose after 3 hours. If between 1–3 hours since end of
hemodialysis, administer ½ of the next scheduled dose. If <1 hour since end of hemodialysis, do NOT administer
dose.

Off hemodialysis, administer next scheduled dose 12 hours from last dose as maintenance.

Indications

Antidote for methanol and ethylene glycol poisoning

Precautions

Local site irritation; Allergic reactions; Elderly and children; Lactation.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common:
Headache, nausea, dizziness, increased
drowsiness, bad or metallic taste

Less Common:
Abdominal pain, fever, multi-organ system
failure, pain or inflammation at injection site, lumbalgia
or backache, hangover, bradycardia, phlebosclerosis,
tachycardia, phlebitis, shock, hypotension, vomiting,
diarrhea, dyspepsia, heartburn, decreased appetite,
transient transaminitis, hypereosinophilia or
eosinophilia, lymphangitis, disseminated intravascular
coagulation, anemia, lightheadedness, seizure,
agitation, feeling drunk, facial flush, vertigo, nystagmus,
anxiety, feeling of strangeness, decreased
environmental awareness, hiccups, pharyngitis,
application site reaction, rash, abnormal smell, speech
or visual disturbances, transient blurred vision, roar in
ear

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 1:05:43 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with: Decreases rate of elimination of Ethanol

Mechanism of Action

A competitive inhibitor of alcohol dehydrogenase. It prevents the metabolism of ethylene glycol and methanol to
their toxic metabolites.

FONDAPARINUX

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

B01AX05

ANATOMICAL

Blood and Blood Forming Organs

THERAPEUTIC

Antithrombotic Agents

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Other Antithrombotic Agents

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IM › IV › SC › 2.5 mg/0.5 mL solution (as sodium salt) (Rx)

Dosage

IM / IV / SC › 2.5 mg/0.5 mL solution (as sodium salt) (Rx)

NOTE: PT and aPTT are insensitive measures of Fondaparinux activity. If unexpected changes in coagulation
parameters or major bleeding occur, discontinue fondaparinux. SUBCUTANEOUS UA or NSTEMI: by SC injection,
ADULT, 2.5 mg once daily for the duration of hospitalization or up to 8 days, initiate as soon as possible after
presentation. INTRAVENOUS STEMI: by IV injection, ADULT, 2.5 mg once; for subsequent doses, starting the
following day, by SC injection, 2.5 mg once daily for the duration of the hospitalization up to 8 days, or until
revascularization [NOTE: Discontinue Fondaparinux 24 hours prior to CABG surgery and administer UFH instead,
as per institutional practice].

Administration

IM / IV / SC › 2.5 mg/0.5 mL solution (as sodium salt) (Rx)

Initiate therapy 6–8 hours following surgery. When used for DVT prophylaxis, early initiation (before 6 hours after
orthopedic surgery) has been associated with increased bleeding. For STEMI, administer via IV route. Administer
initial dose as IV push or mix in NS and infuse over 1–2 minutes. Flush tubing with NS after infusion to ensure
complete administration. If to be administered via SC route, alternate injection sites. Administer according to
recommended regimen. Do NOT expel air bubble from syringe before injection. Do NOT administer with other
agents that increase the risk of hemorrhage unless they are essential for the management of the underlying
condition. Do NOT administer via IM route. CONVERSION. To convert from IV UFH infusion to SC Fondparinux,
calculate specific dose for Fondaparinux based on indication. Discontinue UFH and begin Fondaparinux within 1
hour. To convert from SC Fondaparinux to IV UFH infusion, discontinue Fondaparinux, calculate specific dose for IV
UFH infusion based on indication. Omit Heparin loading dose. For SC Fondaparinux dosed every 24 hours, start IV
UFH infusion 22–23 hours after last Fondaparinux dose.

Page 1 of 4



April 25, 2026, 12:58:33 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Renal Impairment:
For CrCl >50 mL/minute, no dose adjustment is necessary, however, total clearance is reduced ~25%.
For CrCl of 30–50 mL/minute, use with caution. Total clearance ~40% lower compared to normal renal function. When
used for thromboprophylaxis, reduce dose 50% or use low-dose heparin.
For CrCl <30 mL/minute, use is contraindicated.

Hepatic Impairment:
For severe impairment, use with caution. Monitor closely for signs of bleeding.

Indications

Management of unstable angina (UA) or non-ST segment elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI); management of ST
segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI).

Contraindications

Severe renal impairment; body weight <50 kg; active major bleeding; bacterial endocarditis; Thrombocytopenia
associated with a positive in vitro test for antiplatelet antibodies in the presence of Fondaparinux.

Precautions

WARNING: Epidural or spinal hematomas may occur in patients who are anticoagulated with low molecular weight
heparins, heparinoids, or fondaparinux and are receiving neuraxial anesthesia or undergoing spinal puncture. This
may result in longterm or permanent paralysis. Monitor patients frequently for signs and symptoms of neurologic
impairment. If neurologic compromise is noted, urgent treatment is necessary.

Hemorrhage may occur at any site. Risk appears increased by several factors including renal dysfunction, age (>75
years), and weight (<50 kg).

Bleeding; thrombocytopenia; Hepatic impairment; Renal impairment; Patients <50 kg (use with caution; dose
reduction recommended); Elderly (use with caution ; increased risk of bleeding in patients >75 years of age);
Lactation (use with caution).

Page 2 of 4



April 25, 2026, 12:58:33 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Anemia, hypotension, insomnia, dizziness, confusion, increased wound secretion, skin blister,
hypokalemia, thrombocytopenia, purpura, hematoma, minor or major hemorrhage, postoperative hemorrhage,
increased serum ALT and AST, postoperative wound infection, epistaxis.
Less Common: Anaphylactoid reaction, anaphylaxis, angioedema, catheter site thrombosis, elevated aPTT
associated with bleeding, epidural hematoma, hemorrhagic death, injection site reaction (bleeding, skin rash,
pruritus), intracranial hemorrhage, reoperation due to bleeding, spinal hematoma, thrombocytopenia, severe
thrombocytopenia (<50,000/mm3).

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances anticoagulant effect of Fondaparinux:
Agents with Antiplatelet Properties, e.g., P2Y12 Inhibitors, NSAIDs, SSRIs; Omega-3 Fatty Acids; Other
Anticoagulants; Salicylates e.g. Aspirin; Sugammadex; Thrombolytic Agents; Tibolone; Vitamin E (increases overall
risk for bleeding)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Fondaparinux:
Ibrutinib; Prostacyclin Analogues

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
Deferasirox (GI ulceration or irritation; GI bleeding;); Deoxycholic Acid (bleeding or bruising in the treatment area)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
Bleeding: Nintedanib; Obinutuzumab; Iodine I131

Avoid concomitant use with:
Enhances anticoagulant effect of the following drugs:
Apixaban; Dabigatran Etexilate; Rivaroxaban

Increases risk of bleeding of Fondaparinux:
Herbs with anticoagulant or antiplatelet properties, e.g., Alfalfa, Anise, Bilberry;

Reduces anticoagulant effect of Fondaparinux:
Estrogen Derivatives, Progestins [except Tibolone]

Mechanism of Action

A synthetic activated factor X (Xa) inhibitor used as an anticoagulant. It increases the affinity of antithrombin III for
factor Xa, a key enzyme in the coagulation cascade.

April 25, 2026, 1:04:42 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Decreases serum potassium when given with the following drugs: Salbutamol, Aspirin, Atenolol, Betaxolol,
Bisoprolol, Bumetanide, Ephedrine, Epinephrine, Esmolol, Furosemide, Gentamicin, Hydrochlorothiazide,

Decreases sedation:
Salbutamol, Alprazolam, Aripiprazole, Caffeine, Ephedrine, Epinephrine, Haloperidol, Hydroxyzine, Norepinephrine,
Risperidone, Terbutaline

Increases QTc interval:
Azithromycin, Fluoxetine, Goserelin, Levofloxacin, Ofloxacin, Quinine, Risperidone, Co-trimoxazole, Voriconazole

Increases adrenergic effects of Formoterol:
Ephedrine, Epinephrine, Norepinephrine, Olodaterol, Terbutaline, Salbutamol

Decreases effects of Formoterol:
Atenolol, Betaxolol, Bisoprolol, Carvedilol, Esmolol, Metoprolol, Propranolol.

Avoid concomitant use with:

Fluphenazine, Haloperidol, Ketoconazole, Moxifloxacin, Octreotide, Ondansetron

Increases effect of Formoterol: Linezolid

Mechanism of Action

Formoterol is a long acting beta 2 agonist. It relaxes bronchial smooth muscle with little effect on heart rate.

FOSFOMYCIN

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

J01XX01

ANATOMICAL

Anti-Infectives for systemic use

THERAPEUTIC

Antibacterials for Systemic Use

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Nitrofuran Derivatives

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › [Access] 3 g granules for solution (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › [Access] 3 g granules for solution (Rx)

Cystitis, acute uncomplicated, by mouth, ADULT, 3 g as a single dose. Safety and effectiveness in the pediatric
population have not been established.

Administration

ORAL › [Access] 3 g granules for solution (Rx)

Always mix with 3 to 4 oz (90 to 120 mL) cool water before ingesting; do not administer in its dry form or mix with
hot water. May be administered without regard to meals.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Refer to adult dosing.

Indications

First-line treatment for acute uncomplicated cystitis

Page 1 of 2



April 25, 2026, 1:04:07 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Precautions

Prolonged use may result in fungal or bacterial superinfection, including C. difficile-associated diarrhea (CDAD) and
pseudomembranous colitis;
CDAD has been observed >2 months post-antibiotic treatment;
Potentially significant interactions may exist, requiring dose or frequency adjustment, additional monitoring, and/or
selection of alternative therapy.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: None.

Less Common: Headache, pain, dizziness, skin rash, diarrhea, nausea, abdominal pain, dyspepsia, vaginitis,
dysmenorrhea, back pain, weakness, rhinitis, pharyngitis.

Rare: Abnormal stools, anaphylaxis, angioedema, anorexia, aplastic anemia, cholestatic jaundice, constipation,
dermatological disease, drowsiness, dysuria, ear disease, exacerbation of asthma, fatigue, fever, flatulence, flu-like
symptoms, hearing loss, hematuria, hepatic necrosis, insomnia, lymphadenopathy, menstrual disease, migraine,
myalgia, nervousness, optic neuritis, paresthesia, pruritus, toxic megacolon, vomiting, xerostomia

Drug Interactions

Monitory closely with:
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Sodium Picosulfate, Typhoid Vaccine

Avoid concomitant use with:
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: BCG Vaccine, Cholera Vaccine

Mechanism of Action

Nitrofurans are bacteriostatic and bactericidal for many gram-negative and gram-positive organisms but may not
affect P. aeruginosa and many strains of proteus.

Edema, by mouth, ADULT, 40 mg daily in divided doses, may be increased gradually to 120 mg in resistant edema;

NEONATES or PREMATURE INFANTS, 1–4 mg/kg per dose once or twice daily due to poor bioavailability; by IV
injection, NEONATES or PREMATURE INFANTS, 1–2 mg/kg per dose every 12–24 hours; by continuous IV infusion,
NEONATES or PREMATURE INFANTS, 0.05 mg/kg per hour (maximum, 0.4 mg/kg per hour). Heart failure, by mouth,
ADULT, initially 20–40 mg; may repeat the same dose or increase dose in increments of 20–40 mg per dose at
intervals of 6–8 hours (maximum total daily dose, 600 mg); usual maintenance dose interval is once or twice daily;
adjust dosing frequency based on patient-specific needs; CHILD and INFANT, initially 2 mg/kg per dose, increased
in increments of 1–2 mg/kg per dose at intervals of 6–8 hours until a satisfactory response is achieved (maximum
dose, 6 mg/kg per dose); by IM or IV injection, ADULT, initially 20–40 mg per dose, may repeat the same dose or
increase dose in increments of 20 mg per dose and administer 1–2 hours after previous dose (maximum dose, 200
mg per dose); given once or twice daily; CHILD and INFANT, initially 1 mg/kg per dose, may increase dose in
increments of 1 mg/kg per dose and administer not sooner than 2 hours after previous dose, until a satisfactory
response is achieved; may administer maintenance dose at intervals of every 6–12 hours (maximum dose, 6 mg/kg
per dose); by continuous IV infusion, ADULT, initially administer an IV bolus dose 40–100 mg over 1 to 2 minutes,
followed by continuous IV infusion rate of 10–40 mg/hour; repeat loading dose before increasing infusion rate.
Acute pulmonary edema, by slow IV injection, ADULT, 40 mg initially, dose may be doubled every hour until a ceiling
dose of 160 mg; CHILD 1 mg/kg daily (maximum, 6mg/kg daily); by slow IV drip or infusion, ADULT, 40 mg/hour.
Oliguria with glomerular filtration rate <20 mL/minute, by slow IV infusion at a rate not exceeding 4 mg/minute,
ADULT, initially 250 mg over 1 hour; if urine output is not satisfactory during the hour after the first dose, infuse
500 mg over 2 hours then, if there is no satisfactory response during the hour after the second dose, infuse 1 g
over 4 hours; if there still is no response after the third dose, dialysis is probably necessary; may repeat effective
dose, up to 1 g, every 24 hours.

Administration

ORAL › 20 mg tablet (Rx)

For oral administration, take tablet once daily, in the morning. If to be taken twice daily, take the first dose in the
morning and the second dose at lunchtime. Advise the patient to get up gradually from sitting or lying to minimize
dizziness upon standing when taking the medicine. The patient should sit or lie down if dizziness occurs For IV
administration, administer no faster than 4 mg/minute to avoid ototoxicity. Dilute dose in a suitable amount of
infusion fluid, according to the hydration of the patient.

Page 2 of 5



April 25, 2026, 12:58:40 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Reduce dose.

Renal Impairment:
In acute renal failure, higher doses may be required to initiate desired response. Avoid use in oliguric states.
Not removed by hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis. Supplemental dose is not necessary.
In CrCl <25 mL/minute, use upper end of the initial infusion dosage range. If urine output is <1 mL/kg per hour, double
as necessary to a maximum of 80–160 mg/hour.
Monitor effects, particularly with high doses.

NOTE: Dose equivalency for patients with normal renal function (approximate): Furosemide 40 mg = Bumetanide 1 mg
= Torsemide 20 mg = Ethacrynic Acid 50 mg

Indications

Management of edema; pulmonary edema; cerebral edema; hepatic cirrhosis; nephrotic syndrome; oliguria due to
renal failure; congestive heart failure

Contraindications

Severe fluid and sodium depletion

Precautions

WARNING: Cases of tinnitus, hearing impairment, and deafness have been reported. Ototoxicity is related to rapid
injection, severe renal impairment, use of higher than recommended doses, hyponatremia, or concomitant therapy
with aminoglycoside antibiotics or other ototoxic drugs. If high doses are necessary, parenteral therapy with
controlled IV infusion is advisable.

Prostatic enlargement and/or obstruction (may precipitate acute urinary retention).
Gout (may be aggravated by diuretic-induced hyperuricemia).
Hypotension.
Diabetes (may see changes in glucose control); SLE (may cause SLE exacerbation or activation).
Oliguria (correct hypovolemia before administration).
Renal impairment (monitor electrolytes particularly potassium and sodium, as well as creatinine).
Hepatic impairment (hypokalemia may precipitate coma); cirrhosis (diminished natriuretic effect with increased
sensitivity to hypokalemia and volume depletion); alcoholic cirrhosis (increased risk of hypomagnesemia).
Severe urinary retention.
Patients at high risk for radiocontrast nephropathy (can lead to higher incidence of deterioration in renal function).
Elderly (more susceptible to electrolyte imbalance and orthostatic hypotension).
Pregnancy (avoid use; may cause electrolyte disturbance in fetus; possible neonatal thrombocytopenia; monitor fetal
growth because of potential for higher fetal birth weights); lactation (enters breastmilk; use with caution).

Page 3 of 5



April 25, 2026, 12:58:40 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Dehydration, dizziness, gout, hyperuricemia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hyponatremia, orthostatic
hypotension, syncope
Less Common: Dyslipidemia, hyperglycemia, hypocalcemia, increased creatinine concentration, rash
Rare: Acute pancreatitis, agranulocytosis, bone marrow depression, bullous eruptions, exfoliative dermatitis,
hemolytic anemia, interstitial nephritis, jaundice, photosensitivity, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, thrombocytopenia,
tinnitus, vertigo

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Furosemide:
Alcohol (antihypertensive effect), Thiazide Diuretics [e.g., Hydrochlorothiazide (profound diuresis; serious
electrolyte disturbance)]

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Furosemide:
Salbutamol (hypokalemia)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
ACE Inhibitors [e.g., Enalapril (severe hypotension with the first ACEi dose; ACEi-induced renal impairment)], ARBs
(excessive hypotension with the first ARB dose due to volume depletion), Drugs causing hypotension [e.g.,
Amlodipine, Diazepam, Hydrochlorothiazide, Methyldopa (hypotension)], Digoxin (cardiac toxicity due to hypokalemia),
NSAIDs including COX-2 Inhibitors (nephrotoxicity), Ototoxic Drugs [e.g., Gentamicin, Streptomycin (ototoxicity)]

Reduces therapeutic effect of Furosemide:
Selective and non-selective NSAIDs

Avoid concomitant use with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Furosemide:
Hydrocortisone (hypokalemia)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
Ibuprofen (nephrotoxicity)

Reduces therapeutic effect of Furosemide:
Hydrocortisone (antagonism of diuretic effect), Ibuprofen (antagonism of diuretic effect), Oral Contraceptives
(Estrogen antagonizes diuretic effect)

Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Lidocaine (antagonized by hypokalemia), Metformin (antagonism of hypoglycemic effect)

Mechanism of Action

A potent, high-ceiling, sulfonamide loop diuretic, which produces dose dependent diuresis of relatively short
duration

FUSIDATE SODIUM (FUSIDIC ACID) (ANTIBIOTICS FOR TOPICAL
USE)

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

D06AX01

ANATOMICAL

Dermatologicals

THERAPEUTIC

Antibiotics and Chemotherapeutics for Dermatological Use

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Antibiotics for Topical Use

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

TOPIC › [Access] 2% cream, 5 g tube (Rx)

TOPIC › [Access] 2% ointment, 15 g tube (Rx)

Dosage

TOPIC › [Access] 2% cream, 5 g tube (Rx)

Skin infection, by topical application, ADULT, apply to the affected area 2–3 times daily for 7 days; may be used with
or without covering dressing.

Administration

TOPIC › [Access] 2% cream, 5 g tube (Rx)

For external use only. Observe caution when applying in the eye region; may cause eye irritation

Indications

Treatment of skin infections caused by staphylococci, streptococci, Corynebacterium minutissimum; impetigo;
infected wounds; folliculitis; boils; sycosis barbae; carbuncles; hidradenitis; paronychia; erythrasma

Precautions

Hepatic disease (monitor liver function); Neonates; Lactation.

Page 1 of 2



April 24, 2026, 11:17:27 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Rashes, irritation
Less Common: Hypersensitivity reactions

Drug Interactions

Avoid concomitant use with:
Ciprofloxacin (antagonistic activity)

Mechanism of Action

An antibiotic derived from Fusidium coccineum, which is active against several gram-positive organisms and can
penetrate intact skin. It acts by disrupting translocation of peptide subunits and elongating the peptide chain of
susceptible bacteria, inhibiting protein synthesis.

Pregnancy & Remarks

PREGNANCY CATEGORY

Not Available

FUSIDATE SODIUM (FUSIDIC ACID) (ANTIINFECTIVES
(OPHTHALMOLOGICALS))

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

S01AA13

ANATOMICAL

Sensory Organs

THERAPEUTIC

Ophthalmologicals

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Antiinfectives (Ophthalmologicals)

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

OPHTHALM › [Access] 1% eye drops suspension (as sulfate), 5 g tube (Rx)

Dosage

OPHTHALM › [Access] 1% eye drops suspension (as sulfate), 5 g tube (Rx)

Ophthalmic infections, into the eye, ADULT, 1 drop in the conjunctival sac every 4 hours for the 1st day of
treatment, then reduce dose to 1 drop every 12 hours; continue treatment 2 days after the eye appears normal.
Surgical prophylaxis, into the eye, ADULT, 1 drop in the conjunctival sac every 12 hours, 24–48 hours before
operation.

Administration

OPHTHALM › [Access] 1% eye drops suspension (as sulfate), 5 g tube (Rx)

For ophthalmic use only. Keep out of reach of children. Do NOT use contact lenses during treatment.

Dose Adjustment

Hepatic Impairment:
Impaired biliary excretion may possibly increase risk of hepatotoxicity. Avoid use or reduce dose. Monitor liver
function.

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 1:01:17 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Indications

Bacterial infections of the eye caused by susceptible organisms in conjunctivitis, blepharitis, stye, keratitis,
dacryocystitis; prophylaxis in ophthalmic surgery and in connection with removal of foreign bodies.

Contraindications

Infections caused by non-susceptible
organisms.

Precautions

Should not be used for more than 10 days (e.g., fungal
superinfection with prolonged use; discontinue
treatment if superinfection occurs; evaluate and treat
appropriately);
Avoid extended or recurrent use;
Pregnancy;
Breastfeeding.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Stinging (transient)

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Reduces therapeutic effect of Penicillins

Reduces therapeutic effect of Ciprofloxacin (antagonistic effect)

Avoid concomitant use with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of HMG-CoA
Reductase Inhibitors (muscle toxicities, e.g., rhabdomyolysis)

Mechanism of Action

An aqueous sustained-release formulation of a narrow spectrum antibiotic isolated from Fusidium coccineus.
Fusidic acid is active against staphylococci (including methicillin-resistant strains) and streptococci.

GABAPENTIN (OTHER ANALGESICS AND ANTIPYRETICS)

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

N03AX12

ANATOMICAL

Nervous System

THERAPEUTIC

Analgesics

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Other Analgesics and Antipyretics

CHEMICAL CLASS

Drugs for Neuropathic Pain

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › 100 mg capsule (Rx)

ORAL › 300 mg capsule (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › 100 mg capsule (Rx)

Postherpetic neuralgia, by mouth, ADULT, Day 1: 300 mg; Day 2: 300 mg twice daily; Day 3: 300 mg 3 times daily;
titrate dose as needed for pain relief, usually ranging from 1,800–3,600 mg daily in divided doses. Neuropathic pain,
by mouth, ADULT, initially, 300 mg on the 1st day, 300 mg twice a day on the 2nd day and 300 mg 3 times a day on the
3rd day; alternatively, 900 mg daily in 3 divided doses; dose may increase in increments of 300 mg every 2–3 days
(maximum 3,600 mg daily).

Administration

ORAL › 100 mg capsule (Rx)

May be taken with or without food. Administer first dose on first day at bedtime to avoid somnolence and
dizziness. When given 3 times daily, the maximum time between doses should not exceed 12 hours. Do NOT
discontinue abruptly because of the possibility of increasing seizure frequency. Withdraw gradually to minimize
the potential of increased seizure frequency, unless safety concerns require a more rapid withdrawal.

Page 1 of 4



April 25, 2026, 1:12:42 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Dose reductions may be needed based on renal function.

Renal Impairment:
Adjust doses for renal function.
In CrCl =60 mL/minute, administer 300–1,200 mg 3 times daily.
In CrCl >30 to 59 mL/minute, administer 200–700 mg twice daily.
In CrCl >15 to 29 mL/minute, administer 200–700 mg once daily.
In CrCl 15 mL/minute, administer 100–300 mg once daily.
In CrCl <15 mL/minute, reduce daily dose in proportion to creatinine clearance based on dose for CrCl 15 mL/minute
(e.g., reduce dose by one-half [range: 50– 150 mg daily] for CrCl 7.5 mL/minute). In ESRD requiring hemodialysis,
adjust dose based on CrCl plus a single supplemental dose of 125–350 mg after each 4 hours of hemodialysis.

Indications

Management of postherpetic neuralgia (PHN) in adults; neuropathic pain.

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to gabapentin or any component of the formulation.

Precautions

Mixed seizures including absences; Acute pancreatitis (discontinue if acute pancreatitis develops); Renal
impairment; Hemodialysis; Not recommended for patients who need to sleep during daytime and remain awake at
night.

Elderly; Children; Pregnancy (crosses the placenta; adverse events have been observed in animal reproduction
studies); Lactation (use only if the benefits to the mother outweigh the potential risk to the infant).

SKILLED TASKS. May impair ability to perform tasks, which require mental alertness, like driving or operating
machinery.

Page 2 of 4



April 25, 2026, 1:12:42 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Peripheral edema, dizziness, drowsiness, vasodilatation, ataxia, fatigue, hostility, tremor, emotional lability,
hyperkinesia, abnormality in thinking, headache, abnormal gait, depression, amnesia, nervousness, hyperesthesia,
lethargy, pain, twitching, vertigo, pruritus, weight gain, skin rash, hyperglycemia, diarrhea, nausea and vomiting,
xerostomia, constipation, abdominal pain, dyspepsia, dry throat, dental disease, flatulence, increased appetite,
urinary tract infection, impotence, decreased white blood cell count, leukopenia, infection, weakness, back pain,
dysarthria, myalgia, bone fracture, limb pain, viral infection, nystagmus, diplopia, blurred vision, conjunctivitis,
nasopharyngitis, rhinitis, otitis media, bronchitis, pharyngitis, respiratory tract infection, cough, fever.

Less Common: Acute renal failure, altered serum glucose, anemia, angina pectoris, angioedema, aphasia, aspiration
pneumonia, blindness, blood coagulation disorder, bradycardia, brain disease, breast hypertrophy, bronchospasm,
cardiac arrhythmia (various), cerebrovascular accident, cardiac failure, colitis, confusion, Cushingoid appearance,
CNS neoplasm, DRESS syndrome, drug abuse, dyspnea, erythema multiforme, drug dependence, facial paralysis,
fecal incontinence, gastroenteritis, glaucoma, glycosuria, hearing loss, hematemesis, hematuria, hemiplegia, heart
block, hemorrhage, hepatitis, hepatomegaly, herpes zoster, hyperlipidemia, hypertension, hyperthyroidism,
hyponatremia, hypotension, hyperventilation, hypoventilation, hypothyroidism, jaundice, leukocytosis,
lymphadenopathy, joint swelling, lymphocytosis, memory impairment, meningism, migraine, movement disorder,
myocardial infarction, myoclonus (local), nerve palsy, nephrolithiasis, nephrosis, non-Hodgkin's lymphoma,
palpitations, pancreatitis, ovarian failure, paresthesia, peptic ulcer, pericardial effusion, pericardial rub, pericarditis,
peripheral vascular disease, pneumonia, psychosis, pulmonary thromboembolism, retinopathy, purpura,
rhabdomyolysis, seasonal allergy, skin necrosis, status epilepticus, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, subdural
hematoma, suicidal ideation, suicidal tendencies, syncope, tachycardia, thrombophlebitis, tumor growth,
thrombocytopenia, withdrawal syndrome.

Page 3 of 4



April 25, 2026, 1:12:42 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances CNS Depressant effect of Gabapentin: Brimonidine (Topical); Doxylamine; Dronabinol; Hydroxyzine;
Minocycline; Nabilone
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Ethyl alcohol; Mirtazapine (CNS depressant effect); Pramipexole,
Ropinirole, Rotigotine (sedative effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Gabapentin: CNS Depressants [except Levocabastine, Nasal];
Rufinamide (sleepiness and dizziness)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of SSRIs e.g. Fluoxetine (psychomotor impairment)

Avoid concomitant use with:
Decreases serum concentration of Gabapentin: Antacids [administer gabapentin at least 2 hours after antacid];
Magnesium Salts [administer gabapentin at least 2 hours after magnesium salts]; Mefloquine
Enhances therapeutic effect of Gabapentin: Droperidol; Magneisum salts; Methotrimeprazine [reduce gabapentin
dose by 50% with concomitant use]; Perampanel; Sodium oxybate; Tapentadol [start tapentadol at one-third to one-
half of normal dose if used concomitantly] (CNS depressant effect)
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Azelastine, Nasal; Buprenorphine; Hydrocodone [start with a
20–30% lower hydrocodone dose when used concomitantly]; Methotrimeprazine [reduce gabapentin dose by 50%
with concomitant use]; Orphenadrine; Paraldehyde; Suvorexant; Thalidomide; Zolpidem (CNS depressant effect)
Reduces therapeutic effect of Gabapentin: Mefloquine; Mianserin

TEST INTERACTION. May cause a false positive result with the Ames N-Multistix SG® dipstick test for urine protein.

Mechanism of Action

Gabapentin selectively binds to a2d subunits of voltagesensitive calcium channels, causing closure of N and P/Q
presynaptic calcium channels that diminishes excessive neuronal activity and neurotransmitter release.

GABAPENTIN (OTHER ANTIEPILEPTICS)

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

N03AX12

ANATOMICAL

Nervous System

THERAPEUTIC

Antiepileptics

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Antiepileptics

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › 100 mg capsule (Rx)

ORAL › 300 mg capsule (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › 100 mg capsule (Rx)

Seizures, partial onset, by mouth, ADULT or CHILD =12 years, initially 300 mg on the 1st day, 300 mg twice a day on
the 2nd day, and 300 mg 3 times a day on the 3rd day; or 300 mg 3 times daily on the 1st day, increase dose based on
response and tolerability; usual dose range is 900–1,800 mg daily administered in 3 divided doses; CHILD 5–11
years, initially 10–15 mg/kg daily in 3 divided doses, titrate to effective dose over approximately 3 days, increase
dosage based on response and tolerability; usual dose range is 25–35 mg/kg daily in 3 divided doses with maximum
interval of 12 hours (maximum, 50 mg/kg daily); CHILD 3–4 years, initially 10–15 mg/kg daily in 3 divided doses, titrate
to effective dose over approximately 3 days, increase dosage based on response and tolerability; usual dose range
is 40 mg/kg daily in 3 divided doses. NOTE: If gabapentin is discontinued or if another anticonvulsant is added to
therapy, it should be done slowly over a minimum of 1 week.

Administration

ORAL › 100 mg capsule (Rx)

May be taken with or without food. Administer first dose on first day at bedtime to avoid somnolence and
dizziness. When given 3 times daily, the maximum time between doses should not exceed 12 hours. Do NOT
discontinue abruptly because of the possibility of increasing seizure frequency. Withdraw gradually to minimize
the potential of increased seizure frequency, unless safety concerns require a more rapid withdrawal. See
Gabapentin under Analgesics – Other Analgesics and Antipyretics: Drugs for Neuropathic Pain in : Nervous System
for other information.

Page 1 of 2



April 25, 2026, 1:12:47 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Indications

Adjunct therapy in the treatment of partial seizures with and without secondary generalization in adults and pediatric
patients 3 years and older.

Mechanism of Action

Gabapentin is a leucine analog of GABA. Its therapeutic properties are due to its selective binding to a2d subunits of
voltage-sensitive calcium channels, causing closure of N and P/Q presynaptic calcium channels that diminishes
excessive neuronal activity and neurotransmitter release.

GADOBUTROL

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

V08CA09

ANATOMICAL

Various

THERAPEUTIC

Contrast Media

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Magnetic Resonance Imaging Contrast Media

CHEMICAL CLASS

Paramagnetic Contrast Media

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IV › 1.0 mmol/mL solution for injection, 15 mL vial (Rx)

IV › 1.0 mmol/mL solution for injection, 5 mL pre-filled syringe (Rx)

Dosage

IV › 1.0 mmol/mL solution for injection, 15 mL vial (Rx)

Magnetic resonance imaging, by IV injection, ADULT and CHILD, 0.1 mmol/kg (0.1 mL/kg).

Administration

IV › 1.0 mmol/mL solution for injection, 15 mL vial (Rx)

Do not administer other medications in the same IV line simultaneously.

Dose Adjustment

Renal impairment:
Dose adjustment is not recommended. However, risk for NSF development increases as renal function decreases.
Gadobutrol is removed by hemodialysis.

Page 1 of 2



April 24, 2026, 11:17:34 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Indications

Use in diagnostic magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) in adults and children.

Contraindications

Severe hypersensitivity reactions to gadobutrol or any component of the formulation.

Precautions

May cause extravasation, gadolinium retention, hypersensitivity reactions, nephrogenic systemic fibrosis;
Use in caution in patients with renal impairment and seizure disorder.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Less Common:
Headache and nausea.

Rare:
Injection site reactions, dysgeusia, feeling hot, dizziness, vomiting, rash, pruritus, erythema, dyspnea, paresthesia,
cardiac arrest, nephrogenic systemic fibrosis (NSF), and hypersensitivity/anaphylactoid reactions.

Drug Interactions

None known.

Mechanism of Action

Gadobutrol is a gadolinium-containing, nonionic paramagnetic agent. Exposure to an external magnetic field induces
a large local magnetic field in exposed tissues. The local magnetism disrupts water protons in the vicinity, resulting
in a change in proton density and spin characteristics which can be detected by imaging device.

including angiography.

Hypersensitivity to gadoteric acid or any component of the formulation.

Precautions

May cause extravasation, gadolinium-retention, hypersensitivity reactions, and nephrogenic systemic fibrosis;

Adverse Drug Reactions

Less Common:

Page 1 of 4



April 25, 2026, 1:14:44 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Renal Impairment:
Use with caution in patients with pre-existing renal dysfunction. Discontinue if severe renal toxicity or hemolytic
uremic syndrome (HUS) occur during treatment.
For mild-to-severe renal impairment, dose adjustment is not necessary.
For patients with ESRD on hemodialysis, initiate hemodialysis 6–12 hours after gemcitabine infusion.

Hepatic Impairment:
Use with caution. Discontinue if severe hepatotoxicity occurs during treatment.
For elevated transaminases with normal bilirubin, dose adjustment is not necessary.
For serum bilirubin >1.6 mg/dL, initiate at 800 mg/m2, may escalate dose if tolerated.

Obesity:
Use actual body weight (full weight) for calculation of BSAbased or weight-based dosing, especially if the intent of
therapy is curative.
If dose reduction is done due to toxicity, consider resumption of full weight-based dosing with subsequent cycles,
especially if cause of toxicity is resolved.

Indications

First-line treatment of metastatic breast cancer, in combination with paclitaxel after failure of adjunct chemotherapy
with an anthracycline (unless contraindicated); first-line treatment of inoperable, locally advanced or metastatic
(stage IIIA, IIIB, or IV) Nonsmall cell lung cancer (NSCLC), in combination with cisplatin; treatment of advanced ovarian
cancer, in combination with carboplatin that has relapsed at least 6 months following completion of platinum based
chemotherapy; first-line treatment of locally advanced or metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma (nonresectable
stage II, III, or stage IV)

Page 2 of 4



April 25, 2026, 1:14:44 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

No information found

Precautions

WARNING: Has radio-sensitizing activity when used concomitantly with radiation therapy or =7 days apart and may
cause radiation recall when administered >7 days apart. Radiation toxicity, including tissue injury, severe mucositis,
esophagitis, or pneumonitis, has been reported.

WARNING: Hazardous agent. Use appropriate precautions for handling and disposal.

Bone marrow suppression (e.g. neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, anemia); capillary leak syndrome (discontinue if
capillary leak syndrome occurs).

Hemolytic uremic syndrome (may lead to renal failure and dialysis, including fatalities; elevation of bilirubin or LDH,
reticulocytosis, severe thrombocytopenia, and/or renal failure; discontinue if HUS or severe renal
impairment occurs).

Hepatotoxicity (may lead to liver failure and death; use in patients with hepatic impairment or hepatic metastases
may worsen hepatic impairment; monitor hepatic function at baseline and periodically during treatment).

Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (blindness, confusion, headache, hypertension, lethargy, seizure, and
other visual and neurologic disturbances may occur; discontinue if posterior reversible encephalopathy diagnosis
confirmed).

Pulmonary toxicity (ARDS, interstitial pneumonitis, pulmonary edema, pulmonary fibrosis; may lead to possibly fatal
respiratory failure despite discontinuation; discontinue if unexplained dyspnea or other evidence of pulmonary
toxicity occurs).

Infusion duration >60 minutes and administration frequency more than once weekly have been shown to increase
toxicity.

Adverse Drug Reactions: Common: Alopecia, anemia, bronchospasm, diarrhea, drowsiness, dyspnea, edema, fever,

stomatitis, thrombocytopenia

Less Common: ARDS, anaphylactoid reaction, anorexia, arthralgia, bullous skin disease, capillary leak syndrome,
cardiac arrhythmia, cardiac failure, cellulitis, cerebrovascular accident, desquamation, digital vasculitis,
constipation, gangrene of skin or other tissue, hemolytic uremic syndrome, hepatic veno-occlusive disease, hepatic
failure, hypertension, hypocalcemia, MI, hypotension, interstitial pneumonitis, neuropathy, petechiae, pruritus,
pulmonary edema, pulmonary fibrosis, radiation recall phenomenon, renal failure, respiratory failure, reversible
posterior leukoencephalopathy syndrome, sepsis, supraventricular cardiac arrhythmia, thrombotic
thrombocytopenic purpura

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Gemcitabine: Trastuzumab (neutropenic effect)
Enhances therapeutic effect of Warfarin (anticoagulant effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Gemcitabine: Denosumab (serious infections)
Reduces diagnostic effect of Coccidioides immitis Skin Test

Enhances therapeutic effect of Tofacitinib (immunosuppressive effect)

A pyrimidine antimetabolite that inhibits DNA polymerase and ribonucleotide reductase subsequently inhibiting DNA

April 25, 2026, 1:15:46 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Neonates and Infants:
Adjust dosage; avoid prolonged use.

Geriatric:
For mild-to-moderate renal impairment, dose reduction or dose interval extension is warranted;
for severe impairment, the patient should be referred to a specialist.

Renal Impairment:
For mild-to-moderate renal impairment, dose reduction is warranted;
for severe impairment, the patient should be referred to a specialist.

Indications

Used in combination with beta lactams for the treatment of potential sepsis, sepsis neonatorum, immunocompetent
children due to specific sources, native valve infective endocarditis, prosthetic valve infective endocarditis,
bacterial purulent pericarditis, community acute bacterial meningitis, brain abscess in the presence of endocarditis,
chronic or recurrent mastoiditis, community-acquired pneumonia, hospital acquired pneumonia, and pathogen-
specific infections.

Contraindications

Perforated ear drum; hepatic impairment; myasthenia gravis.

Precautions

WARNING: Neurotoxicity, manifested as both bilateral auditory and vestibular ototoxicity, can occur in patients with
pre-existing renal damage and in patients with normal renal function treated at higher doses and/or for periods
longer than those recommended.

Aminoglycosides are potentially nephrotoxic. Risk is greater in patients with impaired renal function and in those
who receive high doses or prolonged therapy.
Use with caution premature infants and neonates because of renal immaturity and the resulting prolongation of
serum half-life of the drug. Decrease dose.
Neuromuscular blockade and respiratory paralysis have been reported following parenteral injection of
aminoglycosides. Use with caution when giving in patients with neuromuscular diseases e.g. myasthenia gravis.

Obesity (use ideal body weight to calculate dose and monitor serum gentamicin concentration closely);
Pregnancy (second and third trimesters: auditory or vestibular nerve damage; reserve for severe or life threatening
infections for which safer drugs are inappropriate; if administered, monitor serum gentamicin concentration),
Breastfeeding (amount probably too small to be harmful; monitor infant for thrush and diarrhea).

Page 3 of 4



April 25, 2026, 1:15:46 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Edema, gait instability, ototoxicity (auditory and vestibular damage), nephrotoxicity, reddening of the skin,
skin itching.

Less Common: Rash.

Rare: Anaphylaxis, antibiotic-associated colitis, blood disorders, bronchospasm, CNS effects, electrolyte
disturbances, nausea, neuromuscular blockade, oliguria, peripheral neuropathy, photosensitivity, stomatitis,
vomiting.

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Gentamicin: Ototoxic and nephrotoxic drugs e.g. Streptomycin

Avoid concomitant use with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Gentamicin: Diuretics e.g. Furosemide

Mechanism of Action

Aminoglycosides are used most widely in combination with a β-lactam antibiotic in serious infections with
gramnegative bacteria, in combination with vancomycin or a β-lactam antibiotic for gram-positive endocarditis, and
for treatment of tuberculosis. They are rapid bactericidal irreversible inhibitors of protein synthesis by binding on
the 30S ribosome.

Dosage

IM / IV / SC › 1 mg lyophilized powder (as hydrochloride) + solvent (Rx)

Severe hypoglycemia, by IV, IM, or SC injection, ADULT=25 kg, initially 1 mg; if not responsive within 10 minutes,
administer IV glucose; repeat glucagon dose if necessary; ADULT <25 kg, initially 0.5 mg; if not responsive within
10 minutes, administer IV glucose; repeat glucagon dose if necessary.

Administration

IM / IV / SC › 1 mg lyophilized powder (as hydrochloride) + solvent (Rx)

Administer parenterally because glucagon is destroyed in the GI tract. Reconstitute as per manufacturer’s
instructions. Use reconstituted glucagon immediately. Discard any unused portion. Do NOT use at concentrations
greater than 1 mg/mL (1 unit/mL). NOTE: Patients with Type 1 diabetes may have less of an increase in blood
glucose levels compared with a stable Type 2 patient. Administer supplementary carbohydrate as soon as

Administer by SC injection. Insert needle at a 30-to 45-degree angle into the anterior abdominal wall below the
navel line. Use caution while injecting into the anterior abdominal wall due to the proximity of underlying inferior
epigastric artery and its branches. Goserelin is an implant. Do NOT attempt to eliminate air bubbles prior to
injection to prevent displacing the implant. Do NOT attempt to aspirate prior to injection. If a large vessel is
penetrated, blood will be visualized in the syringe chamber. If this occurs, withdraw needle and inject elsewhere
with a new syringe. Do NOT penetrate muscle or peritoneum. Implant may be detected by ultrasound if removal is
required. Monitor for signs or symptoms of abdominal hemorrhage. Use extra care when administering to patents
with a low BMI and/or to patients receiving full dose anticoagulation. Pregnancy Category: X (endometriosis,
endometrial thinning); D (advanced breast cancer)

slowly increase by 0.5 mg once daily every 5–7 days to an average maintenance dose of 0.05–0.15 mg/kg daily in 2–3
