Administration

ORAL › 250 tablet (Rx)

For IV: Infuse intravenously over 30 to 60 minutes. Avoid contact of drug solution with equipment containing
aluminum. For oral: Immediate-release tablets and capsules may be administered with food to minimize stomach
upset. Extended-release tablets should be administered on an empty stomach (1 hour before or 2 hours after
meals); do not split, crush, or chew.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric: Refer to adult dosing

Renal Impairment:
Severe impairment: There are no dosage adjustments provided in the manufacturer’s labeling; metronidazole
metabolites may accumulate; monitor for adverse events.
ESRD requiring dialysis: Metronidazole metabolites may accumulate; monitor for adverse events. Accumulated
metabolites may be rapidly removed by dialysis:
be separated from hemodialysis, consider supplemental dose following hemodialysis.
Peritoneal dialysis (PD): No dosage adjustment necessary.

Hepatic Impairment:
Severe impairment (Child-Pugh class C):
Extended-release tablets: Use is not recommended.
Immediate-release capsules:
Amebiasis: 375 mg 3 times daily
Trichomoniasis: 375 mg once daily
Immediate-release tablets, injection:
Reduce dose by 50%

Indications

Eradication of H. pylori; Treatment of acute intestinal amebiasis (amebic dysentery) and amebic liver abscess;
Treatment of bacterial septicemia;
Treatment (adjunctive therapy) of bone and joint infections;
Treatment of CNS infections, including meningitis and brain abscess;
Treatment of gynecologic infections including endometritis, endomyometritis, tubo-ovarian abscess, or postsurgical
vaginal cuff infection;
Treatment of intra-abdominal infections, including peritonitis, intraabdominal abscess and liver abscess;
Treatment of lower respiratory tract infections, including pneumonia, empyema and lung abscess

Page 2 of 4



April 25, 2026, 2:02:05 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Precautions

WARNING:
Possibly carcinogenic based on animal data. Unnecessary use should be avoided. Clinical and laboratory monitoring
recommended in course lasting longer than 10 days;
Hepatic impairment and hepatic encephalopathy;
Renal impairment;
Congestive heart failure;
History of blood dyscrasias;
History of CNS disorders and seizures;
Severe neurological disturbances, including aseptic meningitis (may occur within hours of a dose), cerebellar
symptoms, convulsive seizures, encephalopathy, optic
neuropathy, and peripheral neuropathy (usually symmetric and of sensory type) have been reported;
CNS symptoms and CNS lesions are generally reversible within days to weeks of discontinuation of therapy;
peripheral neuropathy symptoms may be prolonged after discontinuation. (Monitor for neurologic symptoms and
discontinue therapy if any abnormal neurologic symptoms occur);
Prolonged use may result in fungal or bacterial superinfection, including C. difficile-associated diarrhea (CDAD) and
pseudomembranous colitis;
CDAD has been observed >2 months post-antibiotic treatment.
Candidiasis infection (known or unknown) may be more prominent during metronidazole treatment, antifungal
treatment required.
Pregnancy (avoid use in early pregnancy); lactation (significant amounts excereted in breastmilk; may cause
bitterness in milk)

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Headache, nausea, vaginitis

Less Common: Metallic taste, dizziness, genital pruritus, abdominal pain, diarrhea, xerostomia, dysmenorrhea, urine
abnormality, urinary tract infection, bacterial infection, candidiasis, flu-like symptoms, upper respiratory tract
infection, pharyngitis, sinusitis

Page 3 of 4



April 25, 2026, 2:02:05 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitory closely with:
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: BCG Vaccine, Lactobacillus and Estriol, Sodium Picosulfate,
Typhoid Vaccine
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Lithium, Lopinavir, Tipranavir
Enhances therapeutic effect of Vecuronium

Avoid concomitant use with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
Alcohol (Ethyl), Carbocisteine, Products Containing Propylene Glycol
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects Metronidazole: Disulfiram, Mebendazole, Ritonavir
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: BCG, Cholera Vaccine

Mechanism of Action

These drugs inhibit the biosynthesis of ergosterol, the main sterol in membranes of fungi. These agents also affect
the synthesis of triglycerides and phospholipids leading to an intracellular buildup of toxic concentrations of
hydrogen peroxide, contributing to the deterioration of subcellular organelles and to cell necrosis.

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to micafungin, other echincandins, or any component of the formulation.

Precautions

WARNING: Cases of hypersensitivity reactions including anaphylactoid reactions and anaphylaxis with shock have
been reported in patients who receive micafungin.

Use with caution to avoid anaphylactic reactions, hemolytic anemia, hepatic failure and renal impairment.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Fever, headache, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, mucosal inflammation,
constipation.

Less Common: Hypotension, tachycardia, hypertension, peripheral edema, edema, bradycardia, atrial fibrillation,
insomnia, anxiety.

Drug Interactions

No known significant interactions

Mechanism of Action

Azoles are synthetic compounds that can be classified as either imidazoles or triazoles according to the number of
nitrogen atoms. Azoles exhibit activity by reducing the ergosterol synthesis through inhibition of fungal cytochrome
P450 enzymes.

Indications

Prevent vitamin and mineral deficiencies; improve iron status and reduce anemia among infants and children 6-23
months of age.

Contraindications

Children with specific conditions, such as HIV infection or TB (effects and safety of the intervention have not been
evaluated).

Precautions

Programs involving the use of multiple micronutrient powders for fortification at home of foods should be preceded
by an evaluation of the nutritional status among children <5 years of age, and existing measures to control anemia
and vitamin A deficiency (e.g., hookworm control programs, provision of supplements, and use of other products for
home fortification of foods and fortified complementary foods), to ensure that daily micronutrient needs are met and
not exceeded.

Less Common to Rare: Darkening of the stools, staining of child’s teeth

No information found

Single-dose packets vitamins and minerals, in powder form, which can be sprinkled onto any ready-to-eat semisolid

by intravenous infusion, ADULT<60 years, Initial: Some service patients respond to doses as low as 1 mg; no more
than 2.5 mg should be administered over a period of 2 minutes. Additional doses of midazolam may be
administered after a 2-minute waiting period and evaluation of sedation after each dose increment. A total dose
>5 mg is generally notneeded. Maintenance: 25% of dose used to reach sedative effect. ADULT ≥60 years,
debilitated, or chronically ill, Refer to geriatric dosing. Alternate recommendations (American Society for
Gastrointestinal Endoscopy): Initial: 0.5 to 2 mg slow IV over at least 2 minutes; slowly titrate to effect by repeating
doses every 2 to 3 minutes if needed; usual total dose: 2.5 to 5 mg. Anesthesia, by intravenous infusion,
ADULTS<55 years, Induction: Unpremedicated service patients: 0.3 to 0.35 mg/kg over 20-30 seconds; after 2
minutes, may repeat if necessary at 25% of initial dose every 2 minutes, up to a total dose of 0.6 mg/kg in resistant
cases. Premedicated patients: Usual dosage range: 0.05-0.2 mg/kg. Use of 0.2 mg/kg administered over 5 to 10
seconds has been shown to safely produce anesthesia within 30 seconds and is recommended for ASA physical
status P1 and P2 patients. When used with other anesthetic drugs (i.e., coinduction), the dose is <0.1 mg/kg. ASA
physical status >P3 or debilitation: Reduce dose by at least 20%. Maintenance: 0.05 mg/kg as needed, or
continuous infusion 0.015 to 0.06 mg/kg/hour (0.25 to 1 mcg/kg/minute). Sedation in mechanically ventilated
service patients, by intravenous infusion, ADULT, Initial dose: 0.01 to 0.05 mg/kg (~0.5 to 4 mg); may repeat at 5-15

sedation is recommended unless clinically contraindicated. May consider a trial of daily awakening; if agitated

evaluate the sedative effect. NOTE: For pediatric patients: The dose of midazolam needs to be individualized

ORAL › 15 mg tablet (Rx)

WARNING: For deeply sedated pediatric service patients, an individual other than the clinician performing the
procedure should be dedicated to monitoring the service user/patient throughout the procedure. IM: Give deep IM
into large muscle. IV: Administer by slow IV injection over at least 2 to 5 minutes at a concentration of 1 to 5 mg/mL
or by IV infusion. For induction of anesthesia, administer IV bolus over 5 to 30 seconds. Continuous infusions
should be administered via an infusion pump. Oral: Swallow tablet whole with adequate fluid. Avoid intra-arterial
injection or extravasation of the drug. Do not administer intrathecally or epidurally. It is advisable to keep the
service user/patient supine during intravenous use and throughout the procedure. WARNING: Administer by
direct IV injection as the 1-mg/mL solution or dilute the 1 or 5mg/mL solution to facilitate slower administration.
Rapid IV administration may result in respiratory depression, airway obstruction, and/or respiratory arrest. Do not
administer by rapid IV injection in neonates. Severe hypotension and seizures have been reported following rapid

OTC drugs, and alcohol consumption. Inform clinician of any breathing problems experienced. Take only as
prescribed; do not increase dosage or duration of therapy unless otherwise instructed by a clinician. Avoid driving
and doing other tasks or actions that needs alertness. Avoid alcohol or other drugs and natural products that slow
down actions. Inform the clinician of any side effect (e.g., feeling sleepy, tired or weak, or memory problems or
loss) experienced with taking the drug. For women, inform clinicians if they are or planning to become pregnant or
plan to breast-feed.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
NOTE: The dose of midazolam needs to be individualized based on the patient's age, underlying diseases, and
concurrent medications. Consider reducing dose by 20%- 50% in elderly, chronically ill, or debilitated patients and
those receiving opioids or other CNS depressants.

Anesthesia, by intravenous infusion, ADULT >55 years, Induction: Unpremedicated service patients: Initial dose:
0.3 mg/kg; Premedicated service patients: Reduce dose by at least 20%. Maintenance: Refer to adult dosing.

Conscious sedation, by intravenous infusion, ADULT, Initial: 0.5 mg slow IV; give no more than 1.5 mg in a 2 minute
period; if additional titration is needed, give no more than 1 mg over 2 minutes, waiting another 2 or more minutes to
evaluate sedative effect; a total dose of >3.5 mg is rarely necessary.

Preoperative/pre-procedural sedation, ADULT >60 years (without concomitant opioid administration): by
intramuscular injection, 2 to 3 mg (or 0.02 to 0.05 mg/kg) 30 to 60 minutes prior to surgery/procedure; some may only
require 1 mg (or 0.01 mg/kg) if anticipated intensity and duration of sedation is less critical. By mouth, 7.5 mg 30 to 60
minutes prior to surgery/procedure.

Renal Impairment:
Oral: 7.5 mg; consider lower doses as needed.
IV, and Oral (Solution): There are no dosage adjustments provided; however, patients with renal failure receiving a
continuous infusion cannot adequately eliminate the active hydroxylated metabolites (e.g., 1-hydroxymidazolam)
contributing to prolonged sedation sometimes for days after discontinuation. Intermittent hemodialysis:
Supplemental dose is not necessary. Continuous venovenous hemofiltration (CVVH): Unconjugated 1-
hydroxymidazolam not effectively removed; 1-hydroxymidazolamglucuronide effectively removed; sieving
coefficient = 0.45. Peritoneal dialysis: Significant drug removal is unlikely based on physiochemical characteristics.

Hepatic Impairment:
Oral: Mild to moderate impairment: 7.5 mg; consider lower doses as needed. Severe impairment: Use is
contraindicated.
IV, and Oral (solution): Severe hepatic impairment (e.g., cirrhosis): Note: Use with caution in patients with any degree
of hepatic impairment; patients with hepatic encephalopathy likely to be more sensitive to midazolam. Single dose
(e.g., induction): No dosage adjustment recommended; patients with hepatic impairment may be more sensitive
compared to patients without hepatic impairment; anticipate longer duration of action. Multiple dosing or
continuous infusion: Expect longer duration of action and accumulation; based on patient response, dosage
reduction likely to be necessary.

Indications

Sedation in minor surgical or investigative procedures; ICU sedation (continuous infusion); preoperative sedation,
conscious prior to short diagnostics or endoscopic procedures; preoperative anxiolytic; drug-induced amnesia
premedication and sedation, and for induction and maintenance of general anesthesia; moderate sedation prior to
diagnostic or radiographic procedures. Sedation in pediatric patients

WARNING: Use only when adequate treatment
facilities for appropriate management of therapy and complications are available.

NOTE: Midazolam does not have analgesic, antidepressant, or antipsychotic properties. Does not protect against
increases in heart rate or blood pressure during intubation. Should not be used in shock, coma, or acute alcohol
intoxication.

Contraindications

Intrathecal or epidural injection of parenteral forms containing preservatives (i.e., benzyl alcohol); acute narrow
angle glaucoma; Severe respiratory depression; severe hepatic insufficiency; sleep apnea syndrome; myasthenia
gravis, CNS depression.

Precautions

WARNING: Associated with respiratory depression and respiratory arrest, especially when used for sedation in
noncritical-care settings. Death or hypoxic encephalopathy has resulted when respiratory depression was not
recognized promptly and treated effectively.

Initial doses in debilitated and elderly patients should be conservative; start at the lower end of dosing range.

Do not administer by rapid IV injection in neonates; severe hypotension and seizures have been reported; risk may be
increased with concomitant fentanyl use.

Midazolam 15 mg requires an S-2 license for physicians to be able to prescribe it and an S-1 license with a triplicate
prescription for pharmacists to dispense it.

Cardiac disease; children (particularly if cardiovascular impairment); concentration of midazolam in children
<15kg not to exceed 1 mg/mL.
Debilitated patients (reduce dose) (in children); hypothermia; hypovolemia (hypotension); neonates; airways
obstruction and hypoventilation in children <6 months (monitor respiratory rate and oxygen saturation);
vasocontriction.
Impaired gag reflex.
Renal impairment (use with caution). Respiratory disease (use with caution). Tolerance; withdrawal.
Pregnancy; Lactation (avoid breast-feeding for 24 hours after administration).

Concurrent drug therapy issues:
NOTE: See Drug Interactions Section for further information.

CNS depressants/psychoactive medications
CYP3A4 Inhibitors/Protease inhibitors (consider reducing midazolam dose; is contraindicated with both oral and
parenteral forms of midazolam)

Opioids and skeletal muscle relaxants (CNS depression; avoid)
Elderly/Debilitated patients (start at the lower end of dosing range); fall risk
Obese patients (use with caution; may have prolonged action when discontinued)
Neonates (do not administer by rapid IV injection in neonates; increased risk with concomitant fentanyl use) Benzyl
alcohol and derivatives (potentially fatal toxicity (“gasping syndrome”) in neonates; avoid or use dosage forms
containing benzyl alcohol with caution in neonates.)

MONITORING PARAMETERS: Respiratory and cardiovascular status, blood pressure, blood pressure monitor required
during IV administration. For critically ill patients: Monitor depth of sedation with either the Richmond Agitation-
Sedation Scale (RASS) or Sedation-- Agitation Scale (SAS).

STORAGE/STABILITY: Oral: Store at 25°C (77°F); excursions permitted to 15°C to 30°C (59°F to 86°F). Injection: Store at
20°C to 25°C (68°F to 77°F), excursions permitted to 15°C to 30°C (59°F to 86°F). The manufacturer states that

midazolam, at a final concentration of 0.5 mg/mL, is stable for up to 24 hours when diluted with D5W or NS. A final
concentration of 1 mg/mL in NS has been documented to be stable for up to 10 days. Admixtures do not require
protection from light for short-term storage.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Decreased tidal volume and/or respiratory rate decrease, apnea; hypotension; drowsiness, oversedation,
impaired recall, agitation, headache, seizurelike activity; nausea, vomiting; pain and local reactions at injection site;
myoclonic jerks (preterm infants); nystagmus; cough; physical and psychological dependence with prolonged use,
hiccups, paradoxical reaction; vital sign fluctuations.

Less Common: Acid taste, agitation, amnesia, bigeminy, bradycardia, bronchospasm, confusion, dyspnea, emergence
delirium, euphoria, excessive salivation, hallucinations, hyperventilation, laryngospasm, PVC, rash, tachycardia,
wheezing, respiratory arrest, cardiac arrest.

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Decrease therapeutic effect of Midazolam: Aminophylline
Anesthetic agents, inhalation (patients who have received midazolam as an induction agent may require reduced
amounts of inhalation agents during maintenance of anesthesia)
Anticonvulsants e.g., carbamazepine, phenobarbital, phenytoin; Use with caution; consider dosage adjustments, if
necessary
CCBs e.g., diltiazem, nifedipine, verapamil); use oral or parenteral midazolam with caution; reduce oral midazolam
dosage.
Clozapine (consider therapy modification; consider decreasing the dose of (or possibly discontinuing)
benzodiazepines prior to initiating clozapine)
CNS Depressants
Ketamine
Opiates e.g., fentanyl (use with caution; adjust midazolam dosage appropriately to avoid overdosage) Quinupristin
and Dalfopristin
SSRIs (psychomotor impairment)
Thiopental (induction of anesthesia reduced by about 15% in patients who received preoperative sedation with IM
midazolam)

Enchances the sedative effect of the following drugs: Pramipexole, Ropinirole, Rotigotine

Decrease the therapeutic effect of midazolam: Theophylline derivatives, Yohimbine

Avoid concomitant use with:
FOOD INTERACTIONS: Grapefruit juice may increase serum concentrations of midazolam. Management: Avoid
concurrent use of grapefruit juice with oral midazolam

Mechanism of Action

Midazolam is a short-acting benzodiazepine with general properties similar to those of diazepam, except that it has a
more potent amnestic action. It is mainly used for sedation in minor surgical or investigative procedures and in
intensive care, for premedication, and for induction of general anaesthesia.

Page 1 of 5



April 25, 2026, 2:03:49 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dosage

IM / IV › 1 mg/mL, 5 mL ampule/vial (Rx)

NOTE: Individualize dose based on patient’s age, underlying diseases, and concurrent medications. Initial dose for
sedation should not exceed 2.5 mg in a healthy adult. Consider reducing dose by 20–50% in patients receiving
opioids or other CNS depressants. Seizures, by IM injection, ADOLESCENT and CHILD, 0.2 mg/kg (maximum dose, 6
mg), may repeat every 10–15 minutes. Status epilepticus, convulsions >5 minutes or convulsions occurring after
intermittent seizures without regaining consciousness for >5 minutes, by IM injection, ADULT, ADOLESCENT, and
CHILD >40 kg, 10 mg once; or 0.2 mg/kg once (maximum dose, 10 mg); ADOLESCENT and CHILD 13–40 kg, 5 mg once.
Status epilepticus, refractory, by IV injection, ADULT, ADOLESCENT, CHILD, and INFANT, 0.2 mg/kg as loading dose
followed by a continuous IV infusion, 0.05–2 mg/kg per hour (0.83–33.2 micrograms/kg per minute), titrate to
cessation of electrographic seizures or burst suppression; if patient experiences breakthrough status epilepticus
while on the continuous infusion, administer a bolus of 0.1–0.2 mg/kg and increase infusion rate by 0.05–0.1 mg/kg
per hour (0.83–1.66 micrograms/kg per minute) every 3–4 hours. [NOTE: Withdraw continuous infusion after a
period of at least 24–48 hours of electrographic control; withdraw gradually to prevent recurrent status
epilepticus]; or ADOLESCENT, CHILD, and INFANT, 0.15–0.2 mg/kg followed by a continuous infusion of 1
microgram/kg per minute, titrated every 15 minutes by increments of 1 microgram/kg per minute until the
cessation of seizures (maximum dose, 5 micrograms/kg per minute).

Administration

IM / IV › 1 mg/mL, 5 mL ampule/vial (Rx)

For IM route, administer by deep IM injection into a large muscle. For IV route, administer by slow IV injection over
at least 2–5 minutes at a concentration of 1–5 mg/mL or by IV infusion via an infusion pump. For induction of
anesthesia, administer IV bolus over 5–30 seconds. For oral administration, swallow tablet whole with adequate
fluid. Rebound or withdrawal symptoms may occur following abrupt discontinuation or large decreases in dose.
Decrease doses slowly and monitor for withdrawal symptoms. NOTE: Midazolam 15 mg requires an S-2 license for
physicians to be able to prescribe it and an S-1 license with a triplicate prescription for pharmacists to dispense it.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric, Chronically Ill, or Debilitated Patients:
Consider reducing dose by 20–50%. Initial doses should be conservative. Initiate treatment at the low end of the
dosing range. Titrate subsequent doses slowly.

Renal Impairment:
Lower doses may be needed. Patients with renal failure receiving continuous infusion cannot adequately eliminate
the active hydroxylated metabolites, which may cause prolonged sedation.

Hepatic Impairment:
Lower doses as needed.

Indications

Management of seizures and status epilepticus.

Page 2 of 5



April 25, 2026, 2:03:49 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Intrathecal or epidural injection; acute narrow-angle glaucoma; concurrent use of potent inhibitors of CYP3A4 (e.g.,
amprenavir, atazanavir, ritonavir)

[Oral]: Severe respiratory insufficiency; severe hepatic insufficiency; sleep apnea syndrome; myasthenia gravis; use
in children and adolescents.

Precautions

WARNING: Respiratory Depression. Has been associated with respiratory depression and respiratory arrest,
especially when used for sedation in noncritical care settings. The immediate availability of specific reversal agents
(e.g., flumazenil) and size appropriate equipment for intubation and ventilation is highly recommended. Monitor vital
signs continuously during the recovery period. For deeply sedated patients, a dedicated individual, other than the
practitioner performing the procedure, should monitor the patient throughout the procedure.

Sedation precaution. Administer over at least 2 minutes and allow an additional 2 or more minutes to fully evaluate
the sedative effect. Use 1 mg/mL formulation or dilution of the 1 mg/mL or 5 mg/mL formulation to facilitate slower
injection. Doses of sedative medications in pediatric patients must be calculated on a mg/kg basis. Initial pediatric
dose for sedation, anxiolysis, or amnesia is dependent on age, procedure, and route.

Neonates [injection]. Do NOT administer by rapid injection in neonates. Rapid IV injection (<2 minutes) in neonates
has been associated with seizures and severe hypotension, particularly when used concomitantly with fentanyl.
Severe hypotension has also been observed in neonates receiving a continuous infusion of midazolam who then
receive a rapid IV injection of fentanyl.

Anterograde amnesia; CNS depression; paradoxical reactions (e.g., hyperactive behavior, particularly in adolescent
and pediatric patients; consider treatment with flumazenil).

Hypotension; hemodynamic events are more common in pediatric patients or patients with hemodynamic instability);
heart failure.

Impaired gag reflex; renal impairment (half-life may be prolonged); respiratory disease; fall risk; obesity (may have
prolonged action when discontinued).

Elderly; neonates (e.g., severe hypotension and seizures; risk may be increased with concomitant fentanyl use).

Pregnancy; (teratogenic effects have been observed; incidence of premature birth and low birth weights may be
increased with use; neonatal withdrawal symptoms may occur within days to weeks after birth; “floppy infant
syndrome” has been reported); lactation.

SKILLED TASKS. May impair ability to perform tasks, which require mental alertness, like driving or operating
machinery.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Hypotension, drowsiness, over-sedation, headache, seizure-like activity (children), pain and local reactions
at injection site, nausea, vomiting, myoclonic jerks (preterm infants), nystagmus, decreased tidal volume and/or
decreased respiratory rate, apnea, cough, physical and psychological dependence (prolonged use), hiccups,
paradoxical reaction (children).

Less Common: Acid taste, agitation, amnesia, bigeminy, bronchospasm, confusion, dyspnea, bradycardia, emergence
delirium, euphoria, hallucinations, hyperventilation, laryngospasm, excessive salivation, PVC, rash, tachycardia,
wheezing, respiratory arrest (lifethreatening),cardiac arrest (life-threatening).

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect (CNS depressant effect) of Midazolam: Brimonidine (Topical), Doxylamine, Dronabinol,
Hydroxyzine, Magnesium Sulfate, Minocycline, Nabilone
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Ethyl Alcohol, Mirtazapine (CNS depressant effect) Pramipexole,
Ropinirole, Rotigotine (sedative effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Midazolam: Other CNS Depressants Rufinamide (sleepiness and
dizziness)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of SSRIs (psychomotor impairment)

Avoid concomitant use with:
Decreases serum concentration of Midazolam: Dabrafenib, Mitotane
Enhances therapeutic effect of Midazolam: Droperidol, Methotrimeprazine [reduce dose by 50% when used
concomitantly], Tapentadol (CNS depressant effect) [start at one-third to one-half of the normal dose]
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Buprenorphine, Hydrocodone [consider starting with a 20–30%
lower dose when used concomitantly], Methadone, Methotrimeprazine [reduce dose by 50% when used
concomitantly], Orphenadrine, Paraldehyde, Perampanel, Sodium Oxybate, Thalidomide, Zolpidem (CNS depressant
effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Midazolam: Olanzapine
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Azelastine (Nasal), Clozapine (CNS depressant
effect)
Increases serum concentration of Midazolam: Anti-hepaciviral Combination Products, Boceprevir, Cobicistat, Fusidic
Acid (Systemic), Idelalisib, Itraconazole, Ketoconazole (Systemic), Macrolide Antibiotics [except Systemic
Azithromycin, Fidaxomicin, Spiramycin], Mifepristone [avoid midazolam during and 2 weeks following use], Protease
Inhibitors, Telaprevir
Increases serum concentration of Amodiaquine
Reduces therapeutic effect of Midazolam: Theophylline Derivatives

FOOD INTERACTION. Grapefruit juice may increase serum concentrations of midazolam.

Mechanism of Action

Midazolam is a short-acting benzodiazepine that binds to stero-specific receptors on the postsynaptic GABA neuron
at several sites within the CNS. This results to an enhanced inhibitory effect of GABA on neuronal excitability.

Antineoplastic Agents

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IV › 2 mg/mL solution, 5 mL (Rx)

IV › 2 mg/mL solution, 10 mL (Rx)

Dosage

IV › 2 mg/mL solution, 5 mL (Rx)

Acute leukemia, relapsed, induction, by slow IV infusion, ADULT, 12 mg/m2 once daily for 5 consecutive days, may
repeat once if needed. Acute myeloid leukemia (AML), induction, by slow IV infusion, ADULT, 12mg/m2/day IV on
days 1-3 with cytarabine 100mg/m2/day IV continuous infusion on days 1-7. May be given with etoposide. Second
induction with same doses of mitoxantrone for 2 days and cytarabine for 5 days may be given if incomplete
antileukemic response and no severe nonhematologic toxicity in first induction. AML, consolidation, ADULT, in
combination with cytarabine, 12 mg/m2 once daily for 2 days, repeat in 4 weeks; CHILD, 10 mg/m2 in combination
with high dose cytarabine Breast cancer, metastatic or lymphoma, initial, by slow IV infusion, ADULT, 14 mg/m2
every 21 days, reduce to =12 mg/m2 for myelosuppression due to previous treatment or for poor general health;
reduce to 10–12mg/m2 when used in combination with other agents. Hepatocellular cancer, initial, by slow IV
infusion, ADULT, 14 mg/m2 every 21 days; reduce initial dose to =12 mg/m2 for myelosuppression due to previous
treatment or for poor general health. Prostate cancer, advanced, hormone-refractory, by IV infusion, ADULT, in
combination with corticosteroids, 12–14mg/m2 every 3 weeks.

Page 1 of 5



April 25, 2026, 2:02:10 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

IV › 2 mg/mL solution, 5 mL (Rx)

For IV administration only. Dilute in at least 50mL of NS or D5W. Administer via a free-flowing IV, as a short IV
infusion over 5–15 minutes or continuous IV infusion over 24 hours. Do NOT infuse over <3 to 5 minutes. Must be
diluted prior to use. Do NOT administer by SC, IM, or intraarterial injection. Do NOT administer intrathecally to
prevent serious permanent neurologic damage. NOTE: Avoid extravasation. Ensure proper needle or catheter
position prior to and during administration. If extravasation occurs, discontinue infusion immediately and apply ice
to the affected area. Associated with a high emetic potential. Antiemetics are recommended to prevent nausea
and vomiting. May cause urine, saliva, tears, and sweat to turn bluegreen for 24 hours post-infusion; whites of
eyes may have blue-green tinge.

Dose Adjustment

Hepatic Impairment:
In mild-to-moderate impairment, consider dose adjustments and monitor closely.

Obesity:
Use actual body weight (full weight) for calculation of BSAbased or weight-based dosing, especially if the intent of
therapy is curative.
If dose reduction is done due to toxicity, consider resumption of full weight-based dosing with subsequent cycles,
especially if cause of toxicity is resolved.

Toxicity:
AML:
In severe or life-threatening nonhematologic toxicity, withhold treatment until toxicity resolves.

Hepatocellular cancer, lymphoma, or breast cancer (metastatic):
In WBC nadir >1,500/mm3 or platelet nadir >50,000/mm3, and recovery =21 days, repeat previous dose or increase
dose by 2mg/m2 if myelosuppression is inadequate.
In WBC nadir >1,500/mm3 or platelet nadir >50,000/mm3, and recovery >21 days, withhold treatment until recovery
then resume at previous dose.
In WBC nadir <1,500/mm3 or platelet nadir <50,000/mm3, withhold treatment until recovery then decrease previous
dose by 2 mg/m2.
In WBC nadir <1,000/mm3 or platelet nadir <25,000/mm3, withhold treatment until recovery then decrease previous
dose by 4 mg/m2.

Indications

Initial treatment of acute myelogenous leukemias; treatment of advanced hormone-refractory prostate cancer;
treatment of metastatic breast cancer, relapsed leukemia, lymphoma, and hepatocellular carcinoma

Page 2 of 5



April 25, 2026, 2:02:10 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Prior substantial anthracycline exposure and abnormal cardiac function prior to initiation of mitoxantrone therapy;
presence of severe myelosuppression due to prior chemo and/or radiotherapy; severe hepatic impairment;
intrathecal administration.

Precautions

WARNING: Should be administered under the supervision of an experienced cancer chemotherapy physician.
Administer in a facility sufficient to appropriately diagnose and manage complications.
Administer only if baseline neutrophil count <1,500 cells/mm3 (except in AML). Monitor blood counts and monitor for
infection due to neutropenia.
Use increases the risk of developing secondary acute myeloid leukemia (AML).
Congestive heart failure, potentially fatal, may occur during therapy or months to years after termination of therapy.
Cardiotoxicity risk increases with cumulative dose. Assess all patients for cardiac signs and symptoms by history,
physical examination, and ECG. Evaluate all patients for baseline quantitative evaluation of left ventricular ejection
fraction.
Patients >60 years who were undergoing induction therapy experienced heart failure, serious arrhythmias, chest
pain, MI, and asymptomatic declines in LVEF more frequently than younger patients.

WARNING: Hazardous agent; use appropriate precautions for handling and disposal.

Bone marrow suppression (use is not recommended in patients with pre-existing myelosuppression from prior
chemotherapy unless expected benefit outweighs risk)

Hyperuricemia (caused by rapid lysis of tumor cells)

Secondary malignancy (e.g. excessive bruising, bleeding, recurrent infections, which may be symptoms of secondary
malignancy; acute promyelocytic leukemia; risk for secondary leukemia is increased in patients who are heavily
pretreated, with higher doses, and with combination chemotherapy).

Elderly (decreased clearance; use with caution).

Page 3 of 5



April 25, 2026, 2:02:10 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Abdominal pain, abnormal urine, alkaline phosphatase increased, alopecia, amenorrhea, anemia or
decreased hemoglobin, anorexia, anxiety, aphthosis, arrhythmia, arthralgia, back pain, bleeding, blurred vision,
cardiac function changes, chills, conjunctivitis, CHF, constipation, cough, increasedcreatinine, cutaneous mycosis,
depression, diaphoresis, diarrhea, dyspepsia, dyspnea, ECG changes, edema, fatigue, fever, fungal infection,
granulocytopenia, headache, hematuria, hemorrhage, hyperglycemia, hypertension, pain, LVEF decreased,
hypocalcemia, hypokalemia, hyponatremia, impotence, infection, ischemia, jaundice, leukopenia, lymhopenia,
menorrhagia, menstrual disorder, mucositis, myalgia, nail bed changes, nausea, neutropenia, neutropenic petechiae
or bruising, pharyngitis, pneumonia, proteinuria, renal failure, rhinitis, secondary acute leukemias (including AML and
APL), fever, seizure, sepsis, sinusitis, skin infection, sterility, stomatitis, systemic infection, thrombocytopenia, upper
respiratory tract infection, urinary tract infection, vomiting, weakness, weight gain or loss
Less Common: Allergic reaction, anaphylactoid reactions, anaphylaxis, chest pain, dehydration, extravasation at
injection site (erythema, pain, burning, skin discoloration, swelling, or tissue necrosis), interstitial pneumonitis,
hypotension, hyperuricemia, phlebitis at the infusion site, rash, sclera discoloration (blue), tachycardia, urine
discoloration (blue-green), urticaria

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Mitoxantrone: Trastuzumab (neutropenic effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Mitoxantrone: Denosumab (serious infections)
Reduces diagnostic effect of Coccidioides immitis Skin Test

Avoid concomitant use with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Mitoxantrone: Roflumilast (immunosuppressive effect)
Enhances therapeutic effect of Tofacitinib (immunosuppressive effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Mitoxantrone: Dipyrone (agranulocytosis; pancytopenia), Pimecrolimus,
Tacrolimus (Topical)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Clozapine (agranulocytosis), Leflunomide
(hematologic toxicity such as pancytopenia, agranulocytosis, and/or thrombocytopenia), Natalizumab (concurrent
infection)
Increases serum concentration of Mitoxantrone: Cyclosporine (Systemic)
Reduces therapeutic effect of Mitoxantrone: Echinacea
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: BCG (Intravesical), Vaccines (immune response; live virus
vaccines may progress to infection)

Mechanism of Action

A cell-cycle nonspecific agent that intercalates into DNA and inhibits DNA topoisomerase II to inhibit DNA and RNA
synthesis.

hypotension, hyperuricemia, phlebitis at the infusion site, rash, sclera discoloration (blue), tachycardia, urine
discoloration (blue-green), urticaria

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Mitoxantrone: Trastuzumab (neutropenic effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Mitoxantrone: Denosumab (serious infections)
Reduces diagnostic effect of Coccidioides immitis Skin Test

Avoid concomitant use with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Mitoxantrone: Roflumilast (immunosuppressive effect)
Enhances therapeutic effect of Tofacitinib (immunosuppressive effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Mitoxantrone: Dipyrone (agranulocytosis; pancytopenia), Pimecrolimus,
Tacrolimus (Topical)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Clozapine (agranulocytosis), Leflunomide
(hematologic toxicity such as pancytopenia, agranulocytosis, and/or thrombocytopenia), Natalizumab (concurrent
infection)
Increases serum concentration of Mitoxantrone: Cyclosporine (Systemic)
Reduces therapeutic effect of Mitoxantrone: Echinacea
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: BCG (Intravesical), Vaccines (immune response; live virus
vaccines may progress to infection)

Mechanism of Action

A cell-cycle nonspecific agent that intercalates into DNA and inhibits DNA topoisomerase II to inhibit DNA and RNA
synthesis.

text:
﻿April 24, 2026, 11:19:13 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

MODIFIED FLUID GELATIN (POLYMERISATE OF DEGRADED
SUCCINYLATED GELATIN)

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

B05AA06

ANATOMICAL

Blood and Blood Forming Organs

THERAPEUTIC

Blood Substitutes and Perfusion Solutions

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Blood and Related Products

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IV › 3% solution, 500 mL bottle (Rx)

IV INFUSION › 4% solution, 500 mL bottle (Rx)

Dosage

IV › 3% solution, 500 mL bottle (Rx)

NOTE: Determine infusion rate and duration of administration based on individual requirements. Adjust rate and
duration to the current requirement by monitoring the usual circulation parameters. INTRAVENOUS Hypovolemia
or hypotension, prophylaxis: by IV infusion, ADULT, 500–1,000 mL. Mild hypovolemia, treatment: by IV infusion,
ADULT, 500–1,000 mL. Hypovolemia: by IV infusion, ADULT, 500–1000 mL. Severe hypovolemia, treatment: by IV
infusion, ADULT, 1,000–2,000 mL; Severe hypovolemia, emergencies with vital indications: by rapid IV infusion,
ADULT, 500 mL. Hemodilution, isovolemic: by IV infusion, ADULT, not more than 20 mL/kg body weight daily.
Extracorporeal circulation: by IV infusion, ADULT, 500–1,500 mL. NOTE: Infuse the first 20–30 mL slowly under
close observation to allow early recognition of allergic reactions (e.g., anaphylactic, anaphylactoid).

Administration

IV › 3% solution, 500 mL bottle (Rx)

Administer by IV route. Warm solution to body temperature prior to administration by pressure infusion.
Administer by rapid IV infusion under external pressure in emergency situations. Remove all air from containers
with air space before starting the infusion to prevent air embolism during infusion. Do NOT use if solution is not
clear or the container or its closure show visible signs of damage.

Page 1 of 2



April 24, 2026, 11:19:13 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Indications

Prophylaxis and treatment of absolute and relative hypovolemia (e.g., following shock due to hemorrhage or trauma,
perioperative blood losses, burns, sepsis); prophylaxis of hypotension (e.g., with induction of epidural or spinal
anesthesia); hemodilution; extracorporeal circulation (heart-lung machine, hemodialysis).

Contraindications

Hypervolemia; hyperhydration; severe cardiac insufficiency; severe disturbance of blood coagulation.

Precautions

Hypernatremia (use with great caution; additional sodium is administered with modified fluid gelatin); States of
dehydration; Disturbance of blood coagulation; Renal insufficiency; Hepatic impairment.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Less Common: Transient mild nausea or abdominal pain, transient mild rise of body temperature.
Rare: Anaphylactoid reactions, tremor, hypotension, hypertension, wheezing, dyspnea, hypoxia, urticarial reactions,
sweating, chills, pyrexia.

Drug Interactions

Incompatibilities can occur on mixing with other medicaments.

Mechanism of Action

A sterile non-pyrogenic solution for infusion of succinylated gelatin used as colloidal plasma substitutes. They
contain large molecules that do not readily leave the intravascular space where they exert osmotic pressure to
maintain circulatory volume.

Pregnancy & Remarks

PREGNANCY CATEGORY

N/A

MONOBASIC / DIBASIC SODIUM PHOSPHATE

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

A06AG01; A06AD17

ANATOMICAL

Alimentary Tract and Metabolism

THERAPEUTIC

Drugs for Constipation

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Enemas

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › 48 g/18 g per 100 mL solution, 45 mL bottle (OTC)

RECTAL › 19 g/7 g solution, 66 mL bottle (OTC)

RECTAL › 19 g/7 g solution, 133 mL bottle (OTC)

Dosage

ORAL › 48 g/18 g per 100 mL solution, 45 mL bottle (OTC)

Constipation: by mouth, ADULT, 15 mL (1 tablespoon) (maximum dose, 30–45 mL or 2–3 tablespoons); CHILD 10–11
years, 15 mL (1 tablespoon) (maximum dose, 15 mL or 1 tablespoon); CHILD 5–9 years, 7.5 mL (½ tablespoon)
(maximum dose, 7.5 mL or ½ tablespoon). NOTE: Not to be administered in children under 5 years. 1 tablespoon = 3
teaspoons RECTAL Constipation: by rectum, CHILD 5–11 years, one 66 mL bottle; CHILD 2–5 years, one–half 66 mL
bottle. NOTE: Not to be administered in children under 2 years.

Administration

ORAL › 48 g/18 g per 100 mL solution, 45 mL bottle (OTC)

For oral administration, recommended doses are to be diluted in 1 full glass (8 fl. oz.) of cool water, followed with at
least 1 additional full glass (8 fl. oz.) of cool water. NOTE: Drink plenty of water after use to prevent dehydration
associated with significant loss of liquid when using this product. For rectal administration, administer additional
liquid during therapy to ensure adequate hydration.

Dose Adjustment

Oral: Do not administer in severe renal impairment

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:04:45 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Indications

Management of constipation.
NOTE: Not for bowel cleansing.

Contraindications

Previous kidney problem, congestive heart failure, impaired renal functions, decreased intervascular volume,
dehydration, uncorrected electrolyte abnormalities, children under 2 years.

Precautions

WARNING: Taking more than the recommended dose in 24 hours can be harmful (Oral and rectal administration)

For Oral administration:
Low serum calcium concentrations;
Infected phosphate renal calculi;
Hyperkalemia;
Congestive heart failure;
Hypertension;
Edema;
GI obstruction;
Inflammatory bowel disease;
Conditions where there is likely to be increased colonic absorption;
Debilitated patients;
Patients with preexisting electrolyte disturbances;
Elderly.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Oral Administration:
Common and Less Common: Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain hyperkalemia, hypernatremia, dehydration,
hypokalemia, hyperphosphatemia and hypocalcemia (resulting in tetany and death), generalized tonic-clonic seizures
and/or loss of consciousness.
Rare: Acute renal failure, nephrocalcinosis.

Rectal Administration
Common and Less Common: Local irritation, hyperkalemia, hypernatremia, dehydration, hypokalemia,
hyperphosphatemia and hypocalcemia (resulting in tetany and death), rectal gangrene (elderly).

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:04:45 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Oral Administration
Monitor closely with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Calcium-containing Antacids (ectopic calcification),
Calcium Supplements (ectopic calcification)
Avoid concomitant use with:
Increases absorption of Phosphates: Vitamin D (decreases laxative effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Diuretics, Drugs that affect serum electrolytes
(hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia, hypernatremia), (Vitamin D (hyperphosphatemia)
Reduced therapeutic effects of Phosphates: Aluminum Salts, Calcium Salts, Magnesium Salts

No known drug ineractions for rectally administered formulation

Mechanism of Action

A saline laxative that promotes bowel movements by increasing water retention in the intestines.

Pregnancy & Remarks

PREGNANCY CATEGORY

Not available

REMARKS

Strength harmonized with FDA

Drug Classification

Respiratory System

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Other Systemic Drugs for Obstructive Airway Diseases

ORAL › 4 mg chewable tablet (as sodium salt) (Rx)

ORAL › 5 mg chewable tablet (as sodium salt) (Rx)

ORAL › 10 mg (as Sodium) Tablet (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › 4 mg granules (as sodium salt), sachet (Rx)

Prophylaxis of asthma, by mouth, ADULT and CHILD >15 years, 10 mg once daily in the evening; CHILD 6–15 years, 5
mg chewable tablet once daily in the evening; CHILD 2–6 years, 4 mg chewable tablet once daily in the evening;
INFANT 6–23 months, 1 packet (4 mg oral granules). Seasonal allergic rhinitis, by mouth, ADULT and CHILD >15 years,
10 mg daily in the evening.

Administration

ORAL › 4 mg granules (as sodium salt), sachet (Rx)

Take the drug at only one dose daily in the evening. Administer the oral granules (sachet) within 15 minutes after
opening the packet (with or without mixing with food). Administer the oral granules (sachet) within 15 minutes
after opening the packet (with or without mixing with food).

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:04:57 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Renal and Hepatic Impairment:
For severe impairment, refer patient to a specialist.

Indications

Prophylaxis and treatment of asthma (as an adjunctive therapy in combination with a corticosteroid); symptomatic
relief of seasonal allergic rhinitis in patients with asthma

NOTE: There is a place for montelukast in children aged 2–5 years with intermittent viral-induced asthma, where
leukotrienes play a minor role.

Precautions

WARNING: Montelukast has no role in the treatment of
status asthmaticus. It cannot be substituted for
inhaled or oral corticosteroids especially for
persistent asthma. It should not be used as
monotherapy for exercise-induced bronchospasm

Eosinophilia and vasculitis; neuropsychiatric events;
phenylketonuria; Patients are instructed to have
appropriate rescue treatment available while on
montelukast; when this is being given with systemic
corticosteroids, appropriate tapering of corticosteroids is
needed under medical supervision; Children; Pregnancy
(may be administered at normal dose in women who
have shown a significant improvement in asthma, which
is not achievable with other drugs before becoming
pregnant); breastfeeding (avoid use unless essential).

NOTE: Rarely, the reduction of a systemic corticosteroid
while on another leukotriene receptor antagonist has
been reported to manifest with eosinophilia, vasculitic
rash, worsening pulmonary symptoms, cardiac
complications, or neuropathy, sometimes presenting as
Churg-Strauss syndrome.

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:04:57 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common:
Abdominal pain and discomfort, cough, diarrhea, fatigue, fever, headache, hyperkinesia, otitis media, pharyngitis,
rash, sinusitis, thirst, upper respiratory infection, weakness

Less Common:
Abnormal dreams, arthralgia, bruising,
dizziness, dry mouth, dyspepsia, edema, epistaxis,hypesthesia, malaise, myalgia, paresthesia, pruritus,
seizures, sleep disturbances, sleepwalking

Rare: Allergy (including urticaria, angioedema, anaphylaxis), bleeding, Churg-Strauss syndrome, disorientation,
eosinophilia, erythema multiforme, erythema nodosum, hallucinations, hepatic disorders, mood or behavioral
changes e.g., anxiety, depression and aggression; neuropsychiatric effects e.g., nightmares and hallucinations;
palpitations, suicidal thoughts and behavior, tremor

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Reduces therapeutic effect of Montelukast by decreasing
AUC:
CYP450 Inducers e.g., Phenobarbital, Rifampicin

Mechanism of Action

A selective, leukotriene-receptor inhibitor, which antagonizes airway smooth muscle contraction and inflammation
caused by leukotrienes.

MORPHINE

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

N02AA01

ANATOMICAL

Nervous System

THERAPEUTIC

Analgesics

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Opioids

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › 10 mg tablet / capsule (as sulfate) (Rx)

ORAL › 20 mg tablet / capsule (as sulfate) (Rx)

ORAL › 30 mg tablet / capsule (as sulfate) (Rx)

ORAL › 10 mg MR tablet (as sulfate) (Rx)

ORAL › 30 mg MR tablet (as sulfate) (Rx)

ORAL › 60 mg MR tablet (as sulfate) (Rx)

IM › IV › SC › 10 mg/mL (as sulfate), 1 mL ampule (Rx)

IM › IV › SC › 16 mg/mL (as sulfate), 1 mL ampule (Rx)

Page 1 of 5



April 25, 2026, 2:05:08 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dosage

ORAL › 10 mg tablet / capsule (as sulfate) (Rx)

NOTE: Tailor dosing based on patient's needs, type of pain being treated, route of administration, degree of
tolerance for opioids, age, weight, and medical condition. The optimal analgesic dose varies widely among
patients. Titrate doses to pain relief/prevention. Acute pain, moderate to severe, by mouth, ADULT (opioid naïve),
as immediate-release tablet or capsule, initially 15–30 mg every 4 hours as needed; patients with prior opioid
exposure may require higher initial doses; CHILD >6 months and <50 kg, as immediate-release tablet or capsule,
0.15–0.3 mg/kg every 3–4 hours as needed; if pain is severe, an initial dose of 0.3 mg/kg may be administered; by IM
or SC injection, ADULT (opioid naïve), initially 5–10 mg every 4 hours as needed; usual range, 5–15 mg every 4 hours
as needed; patients with prior opioid exposure may require higher initial doses; CHILD >6 months and <50 kg, 0.1–
0.2 mg/kg; by IV injection, ADULT (opioid naïve), initially 2.5–5 mg every 3–4 hours; patients with prior opioid
exposure may require higher initial doses. [NOTE: Administer 2–3 mg every 5 minutes until pain relief or if
associated sedation, oxygen saturation <95%, or serious adverse event occurs, may be appropriate in treating
acute moderate-to-severe pain in immediate postoperative period or the emergency department; maximum
cumulative dose prompting reevaluation of continued morphine use and/or dose should be included as part of any
medication order intended for short-term use.]; CHILD >6 months and <50 kg, 0.05–0.3 mg/kg every 3–4 hours as
needed, not to exceed 10 mg per dose; by continuous IV or SC infusion, ADULT, 0.8–10 mg/hour; usual range is up
to 80 mg/hour; may administer a loading dose prior to initiating the infusion; ADULT (critically ill patients), usual
range Is 2–30 mg/hour; ADULT (patient-controlled analgesia), usual concentration is 1 mg/mL; usual demand dose
is 1 mg, ranging from 0.5–2.5 mg; lockout interval is 5–10 minutes; CHILD >6 months and <50 kg, initially 10–30
micrograms/kg per hour, titrate as needed to control pain; CHILD >6 months and <50 kg (patient-controlled
analgesia; opioid-naive), consider using lower end of dosing range; usual concentration is 1 mg/mL; usual demand
dose is 0.02 mg/kg per dose ranging from 0.01–0.03 mg/kg per dose; lockout interval is 8–10 minutes; usual basal
rate is 0–0.03 mg/kg per hour. Chronic pain, by mouth, ADULT (opioid-tolerant), as MR tablet, 90–120 mg once daily
at the same time each day; ADULT (opioid naïve), initially 30 mg once daily; adjust in increments =30 mg daily every
3–4 days (maximum dose, 1,600 mg daily).

Administration

ORAL › 10 mg tablet / capsule (as sulfate) (Rx)

For oral administration, swallow MR tablets whole. Do NOT crush, chew, or dissolve to prevent the rapid release of
a potentially fatal dose of morphine. Take consistently with regards to meals. Administration with food may
increase bioavailability. Morphine may cause GI upset. Take with food if GI upset occurs. Avoid alcohol. Do NOT
administer with alcoholic beverages or ethanol-containing prescription or nonprescription products. Establish a
patient's morphine requirement using immediate-release tablets or capsules. Conversion to MR tablets may be
considered when chronic, continuous treatment is required. Reserve the use of higher doses in opioid-tolerant
patients only. Reserve use of MR tablets in patients for whom alternative treatment options are ineffective, not
tolerated, or would be otherwise inadequate to provide sufficient management of pain. When administering by IV
push, dilute with sterile water or NS to a concentration of 1 mg/mL in adults or 0.1–1 mg/mL in children. Administer
slowly over 4–5 minutes. Repeated SC administration causes local tissue irritation, pain, and induration. The use of
IM injections is no longer recommended for repeated administration due to painful administration, variable
absorption, and lag time to peak effect. Do NOT use unless the solution is colorless or pale yellow. Protect from
light. NOTE: Conversion from parenteral to MR tablet. Substantial interpatient variability exists in relative potency.
Therefore, it is safer to underestimate a patient's daily oral morphine requirement and provide breakthrough pain
relief with immediate-release morphine. Consider the parenteral to oral morphine ratio. Parenteral to oral
morphine ratio. Between 2–6 mg of oral morphine may be required for analgesia equivalent to 1 mg of parenteral
morphine. An oral dose 3 times the daily parenteral dose may be sufficient in chronic pain settings.

Page 2 of 5



April 25, 2026, 2:05:08 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric and Debilitated Patients:
Use with caution. May require reduced doses.

Renal Impairment:
For CrCl 10–50 mL/minute and patients on CRRT, administer 75% of normal dose.
For CrCl <10 mL/minute and patients on peritoneal dialysis, administer 50% of normal dose.

Hepatic Impairment:
Substantial extrahepatic metabolism may occur. In cirrhosis, increases in half-life and AUC may require dose
adjustment.

Indications

[Oral]: Relief of moderate to severe acute and chronic pain for which use of an opioid analgesic is appropriate;
management of pain severe enough to require daily, around-the-clock, long-term opioid treatment and for which
alternative treatment options are inadequate.

[Injection]: Relief of severe pain, such as MI and severe injuries; relief of dyspnea of acute left ventricular failure and
pulmonary edema; preanesthetic medication; management of pain for extended periods without attendant loss of
motor, sensory, or sympathetic function.

Page 3 of 5



April 25, 2026, 2:05:08 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

[Oral]: GI obstruction and hypercarbia.

[Injection]: Severe respiratory depression; acute or severe asthma (in an unmonitored setting or without
resuscitative equipment); known or suspected paralytic ileus; presence of infection at infusion site; concomitant
anticoagulant therapy; uncontrolled bleeding diathesis; heart failure due to chronic lung disease; cardiac
arrhythmias; increased intracranial pressure; head injuries; brain tumors; acute alcoholism; deliriums tremens;
seizure disorders; use during labor when a premature birth is anticipated.

NOTE: Some contraindications are product specific. See detailed product prescribing information.

Precautions

WARNING: Risk of overdose. NOT recommended for single-dose IV, IM, or SC administration because of the very large
amount of morphine in the ampule and the associated risk of overdosage.

Naloxone injection and resuscitative equipment should be immediately available for use in case of life-threatening or
intolerable side effects and whenever therapy is being initiated, the reservoir is being refilled, or any manipulation of
the reservoir system is taking place.

Each ampule contains a large amount of a potent narcotic which has been associated with abuse and dependence
among healthcare providers. Due to the limited indications for this product, the risk of overdosage and the risk of its
diversion and abuse, it is recommended that special measures be taken to control this product within the hospital or
clinic, including wastage and access.

Page 4 of 5



April 25, 2026, 2:05:08 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Circulatory depression, flushing, shock, bradycardia, hypotension, atrial fibrillation, chest pain, edema,
hypertension, palpitation, syncope, tachycardia, vasodilation, dysphonia, physical and psychological dependence,
sedation, drowsiness, dizziness, confusion, amnesia, fever, agitation, anxiety, apathy, apprehension, ataxia, chills,
coma, delirium, depression, dream abnormalities, euphoria, false sense of well-being, hallucination, hypoesthesia,
insomnia, lethargy, malaise, nervousness, restlessness, seizure, slurred speech, somnolence, vertigo, pruritus (dose
related), dry skin, rash, urticaria, antidiuretic hormone release, hypogonadism, gynecomastia, hypokalemia,
hyponatremia, decreased libido, constipation, nausea, vomiting, abdominal distension, abdominal pain, anorexia,
biliary colic, diarrhea, dyspepsia, dysphagia, flatulence, gastroenteritis, GERD, GI irritation, paralytic ileus, rectal
disorder, taste perversion, weight loss, xerostomia, bladder spasm, dysuria, abnormal ejaculation, impotence,
decreased urination, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, pain at injection site, decreased bone mineral density,
weakness, arthralgia, back pain, bone pain, foot drop, gait abnormalities, paresthesia, rigors, skeletal muscle rigidity,
amblyopia, conjunctivitis, tremor, eye pain, vision problems or disturbance, decreased oxygen saturation, oliguria,
asthma, atelectasis, dyspnea, hiccups, pulmonary edema (noncardiogenic), hypoxia, hypercapnia, respiratory
depression, rhinitis, flulike syndrome, diaphoresis, infection, thirst, histamine release, voice alteration, withdrawal
syndrome.

Less Common: Amenorrhea, anaphylaxis, apnea, biliary tract spasm, bronchospasm, blurred vision, cardiac arrest,
decreased cough reflex, dehydration, intestinal obstruction, diplopia, disorientation, hemorrhagic urticaria,
intracranial pressure increased, laryngospasm, menstrual irregularities, miosis, myoclonus, nystagmus, paradoxical
CNS stimulation, respiratory arrest, sepsis, thermal dysregulation, toxic psychoses, urinary tract spasm.

Mechanism of Action

Morpine is a mu receptor agonist in the CNS and PNS. It binds to opioid receptors in the CNS, causing inhibition of
ascending pain pathways, altering the perception of and response to pain, as well as producing generalized CNS
depression.

Pregnancy & Remarks

PREGNANCY CATEGORY

Pre and postoperative sterilization, ADULT, instill 1 drop into operative eye every 5-15 minutes for five doses within
the hour prior to the start of the procedure.

April 25, 2026, 2:05:26 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to moxifloxacin, other quinolones, or to any component of the formulation.

Precautions

May cause severe hypersensitivity reactions including
anaphylaxis, angioedema, dermatologic reactions, and
superinfection.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Less Common:
Eye irritation, conjunctivitis, decreased
visual acuity, eye discomfort, eye pain, eye pruritus,
lacrimation, ocular hyperemia, subconjunctival
hemorrhage, and xerophthalmia.

Drug Interactions

None known.

Mechanism of Action

Moxifloxacin is a DNA gyrase inhibitor, and also inhibits topoisomerase IV. DNA gyrase is required for DNA replication
and transcription, DNA repair, recombination, and transposition; inhibition is bactericidal.

Drug Classification

ANATOMICAL

Anti-Infectives for systemic use

THERAPEUTIC

Antibacterials for Systemic Use

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Quinolone

CHEMICAL CLASS

Fourth-Generation Fluoroquinolones

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › [WATCH] 400 mg film coated tablet (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › [WATCH] 400 mg film coated tablet (Rx)

Acute bacterial rhinosinusitis, by mouth, ADULT, 400 mg every 24 hours for 10 days (manufacturer’s labeling) or 5 to
7 days. (Note: Recommended in patients with beta-lactam allergy; may also be used if initial therapy fails, in areas
with high endemic rates of penicillin non susceptible S. pneumoniae, those with severe infections, age >65 years,
recent hospitalization, antibiotic use within the past month, or who are immunocompromised). Chronic bronchitis,
acute bacterial exacerbation, by mouth, ADULT, 400 mg every 24 hours for 5 days Community-acquired pneumonia
(CAP) including MDRSP, by mouth, ADULT, 400 mg every 24 hours for 7 to 14 days Intra-abdominal infections,
complicated, by mouth, ADULT, 400 mg every 24 hours for 5 to 14 days (initiate with IV); Note: 2010 IDSA guidelines
recommend a treatment duration of 4 to 7 days (provided source controlled) for community-acquired, mild to
moderate IAI Plague, by mouth, ADULT, 400 mg every 24 hours for 10 to 14 days Skin and skin structure infections
(Complicated), by mouth, ADULT, 400 mg every 24 hours for 7 to 21 days Skin and skin structure infections
(Uncomplicated), by mouth, ADULT, 400 mg every 24 hours for 7 days

Administration

ORAL › [WATCH] 400 mg film coated tablet (Rx)

Administer without regard to meals. Administer at least 4 hours before or 8 hours after products containing
magnesium, aluminum, iron, or zinc, including antacids, sucralfate, multivitamins, and didanosine (buffered tablets
for oral suspension or the pediatric powder for oral solution).

Page 1 of 4



April 25, 2026, 2:05:22 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric: Refer to adult dosing.

Hepatic Impairment: No dosage adjustment necessary; however, use with caution in this patient population
secondary to the risk of QT prolongation.

Indications

Treatment of multidrug resistant tuberculosis (MDRTB); mild to moderate community-acquired pneumonia, including
multidrug-resistant Streptococcus pneumoniae; acute bacterial exacerbation of chronic bronchitis; acute bacterial
sinusitis; complicated and uncomplicated skin and skin structure infections; complicated intra-abdominal infections;
prophylaxis and treatment of plague, including pneumonic and septicemic plague, due to Yersinia pestis.

Precautions

WARNING:
Fluoroquinolones are associated with disabling and potentially irreversible serious adverse reactions that may occur
together, including tendinitis and tendon rupture, peripheral neuropathy, and CNS
effects. Discontinue moxifloxacin immediately and avoid use of fluoroquinolones in patients who experience any of
these serious adverse reactions.
Patients of any age or without pre-existing risk factors have experienced these reactions; may occur within hours to
weeks after initiation.
QTc prolongation (avoid use in patients with a history of QTc prolongation, uncorrected hypokalemia,
hypomagnesemia, or concurrent administration of other medications known to prolong the QT interval e.g. Class Ia
and Class III antiarrhythmics, cisapride, erythromycin, antipsychotics, and tricyclic antidepressants;
Hypoglycemia/hyperglycemia (Patients should be monitored closely for signs/symptoms of disordered glucose
regulation particularly severe hypoglycemia);
Severe hepatotoxicity (including acute hepatitis and fatalities) has been reported. (Discontinue therapy immediately
if signs and symptoms of hepatitis occur);
Severe hypersensitivity reactions, including anaphylaxis (Prompt discontinuation of drug should occur if skin rash or
other symptoms arise;
Photosensitivity (Discontinue use if photosensitivity occurs);
Prolonged use may result in fungal or bacterial superinfection, including C. difficile-associated diarrhea (CDAD) and
pseudomembranous colitis.

Drug Interactions

Monitory closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Blood Glucose Lowering Agents, Fluoxetine, Porfimer, QTc-
Prolonging Agents, Tacrolimus, Verteporfin, Vitamin K Antagonists (e.g. Warfarin)

Reduces absorption of Moxifloxacin: Antacids (except: sodium bicarbonate), Calcium Salts (except: calcium chloride),
Sevelamer
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: BCG Vaccine, Lactobacillus and Estriol, Sodium Picosulfate,
Typhoid Vaccine

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects Moxifloxacin: Corticosteroids
Decreases excretion of Moxifloxacin: Probenecid

Avoid concomitant use with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects Moxifloxacin: Nadifloxacin

Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: BCG, Cholera Vaccine

Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Hydroxychloroquine, Macimorelin, Mifepristone, Probucol,
Promazine, QTc-Prolonging Agents, Vinflunine

Decreases serum concentration of Moxifloxacin: Strontium Ranelate

Mechanism of Action

Quinolones are broad-spectrum antibacterial agents. They act by blocking bacterial DNA synthesis by inhibiting
bacterial topoisomerase II and topoisomerase IV.
Inhibition of topoisomerase II prevents the relaxation of positively supercoiled DNA while inhibition of
topoisomerase IV interferes with separation of replicated chromosomal DNA.

MUPIROCIN

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

D06AX09

ANATOMICAL

Dermatologicals

THERAPEUTIC

Antibiotics and Chemotherapeutics for Dermatological Use

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Antibiotics for Topical Use

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

TOPIC › 2% cream, 5 g sachet (Rx)

TOPIC › 2% cream, 15 g tube (Rx)

TOPIC › 2% ointment, 5 g tube (Rx)

TOPIC › 2% ointment, 15 g tube (Rx)

Dosage

TOPIC › 2% cream, 5 g sachet (Rx)

Secondary skin infection, by topical application, ADULT and CHILD ≥3 months, as cream, apply to affected area 3
times daily for up to 10 days; re-evaluate within 7 days if no clinical response. Impetigo, by topical application,
ADULT and CHILD ≥2 months, as ointment, apply to affected area 3 times daily for up to 5–10 days; re-evaluate after
3–5 days if no clinical response.

Administration

TOPIC › 2% cream, 5 g sachet (Rx)

For external use only. NOT for intranasal, ophthalmic, or other mucosal use. Avoid contact with eyes. In case of
accidental contact, rinse well with water. Apply a small amount to the affected area using a cotton swab or gauze
pad. If desired, cover the treated area with gauze dressing. Do NOT apply concurrently with any other lotions,
creams, or ointments.

Dose Adjustment

Renal Impairment:
Use with caution. Some products contain polyethylene glycol, which may be absorbed from open wounds and
damaged skin and secreted by the kidneys.

Indications

Management of impetigo; secondary skin infections (up to 10 cm in length or 100 cm2 in area) due to susceptible
Staphylococcus aureus and Steptococcus pyogenes.

Precautions

WARNING: Prolonged use may result in overgrowth of non-susceptible microorganisms, including fungi.

Extensive burns and wounds; Renal impairment; Local irritation (discontinue use when sensitization or severe local
irritation occurs); Lactation (not known if present in breastmilk, has effects on breastfed child and milk production).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common and Less Common: Burning, stinging, pruritus, pain, rash, erythema, dry skin, tenderness, cellulitis, pain or
bleeding secondary to eczema, secondary wound infection, urticaria, swelling, increased exudates, contact
dermatitis, furunculosis, exfoliative dermatitis
Rare: Systemic reactions (e.g., nausea, headache, dizziness, abdominal pain, ulcerative stomatitis, systemic allergic
reactions)

Drug Interactions

Avoid concomitant use with:
Reduces therapeutic effect of Mupirocin:
Chloramphenicol (interferes with antibacterial action of Mupirocin in RNA synthesis)

Mechanism of Action

A bacteriostatic that inhibits protein synthesis of the bacteria by binding to isoleucyl transfer RNA synthetase

MYCOPHENOLIC ACID

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

L04AA06

ANATOMICAL

Antineoplastic and Immunomodulating Agents

THERAPEUTIC

Immunosuppressants

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Selective Immunosuppressants

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › 500 mg tablet (as Mycophenolate Mofetil) (Rx)

ORAL › 360 mg tablet (as Mycophenolate Sodium) (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › 500 mg tablet (as Mycophenolate Mofetil) (Rx)

NOTE: Identify dosing with caution due to possibility of increased hepatic, renal, or cardiac dysfunction. Mofetil
Salt: Renal transplant, by mouth, ADULT, CHILD, and INFANT =3 months or BSA >1.5 m2, 1 g twice daily; doses>2 g
daily are not recommended; 1 g twice daily. Cardiac transplant or hepatic transplant, by mouth, ADULT, 1.5 g twice
daily. Sodium Salt: Renal transplant, by mouth, ADULT, 720 mg twice daily (total daily dose, 1440 mg); CHILD =5
years, usual dose, 400 mg/m2 per dose twice daily (maximum dose, 720 mg twice daily);

Administration

ORAL › 500 mg tablet (as Mycophenolate Mofetil) (Rx)

To be taken on an empty stomach, 1 hour before or 2 hours after meals, to avoid variability in mycophenolate
absorption. May be administered with food in stable renal transplant patients when necessary. MISSED DOSE:
Administer as soon as remembered. If it is close to the next scheduled dose, skip the missed dose and resume at
next regularly scheduled time. Do NOT double a dose to make up for a missed dose. Females of reproductive
potential should have a negative pregnancy test with a sensitivity of =25 mIU/mL immediately before therapy and
the test should be repeated 8–10 days later. Pregnancy tests should be repeated during routine follow-up visits.
They are also required to have contraceptive counseling and use acceptable birth control unless heterosexual
intercourse is completely avoided. Intrauterine device, tubal sterilization, or vasectomy of the female patient's
partner are acceptable contraceptive methods that can be used alone. Hormonal contraceptives require the use
of an additional barrier method because mycophenolate affects the effectiveness of hormonal contraceptive
agents. NOTE: Limit exposure to sunlight and ultraviolet light by wearing protective clothing and using effective
sunscreen to minimize the risk for lymphoproliferative disorders.

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to mycophenolic acid

Precautions

WARNING: Should only be used by physicians experienced in immunosuppressive therapy and management of
transplant patients. Adequate laboratory and supportive medical resources must be readily available.

Do NOT use mycophenolate mofetil and mycophenolic acid dosage forms interchangeably due to differences in
absorption.

May lead to an increased susceptibility to bacterial, viral, fungal, and protozoal infections, including opportunistic
infections.

May lead to an increased risk of development of lymphoma and other malignancies, i.e., skin.

Associated with an increased risk of congenital malformations and first trimester pregnancy loss when used by
pregnant women. Females of reproductive potential must be counseled about pregnancy prevention and planning.

Gastrointestinal disorders (e.g. gastric or duodenal ulcers, GI bleeding and/or perforation)

Hypoxanthine-guaninephosphoribosyl transferase deficiency

Lymphoproliferative disorders; Neutropenia; Pure red cell aplasia [PRCA]

Infections; New or reactivated viral infections (e.g. polyomavirus associated nephropathy (PVAN), JC virusassociated
progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML), cytomegalovirus (CMV) infections, and reactivation of hepatitis B
(HBV) or hepatitis C (HCV))

Renal impairment (increased toxicity).

Pregnancy (consider alternative agents for women planning pregnancy; external ear abnormalities, cleft lip and
palate, anomalies of the distal limbs, heart, esophagus, kidney, and nervous system have been reported;
spontaneous abortions have been noted; discontinue at least 6 weeks prior to trying to conceive).

Elderly (at an increased risk of certain infections, GI hemorrhage, and pulmonary edema).

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Mycophenolate: Trastuzumab (neutropenic effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Mycophenolate: Denosumab (serious infection)
Reduces diagnostic effect of Coccidioides immitis Skin Test

Mechanism of Action

It provides a cytostatic effect on T and B lymphocytes. It inhibits inosine monophosphate dehydrogenase (IMPDH),
which inhibits denovo guanosine nucleotide synthesis, thus limiting T and B lymphocyte proliferation.

N-ACETYL PENICILLAMINE

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

Not available

ANATOMICAL

Various

THERAPEUTIC

All Other Therapeutic Products

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Specific Antidotes

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › crystals, 25 g/bottle (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › crystals, 25 g/bottle (Rx)

NOTE: Consider dose reduction to 250 mg daily prior to surgical procedures. May resume normal recommended
dosing post-operatively once healing is complete. Lead poisoning, by mouth, ADULT, 900–1,500 mg daily in 3
divided doses for 1–2 weeks, then 750 mg daily in divided doses until blood lead concentration is <60
micrograms/dL or urinary lead excretion is <500 micrograms/L for 2 consecutive months; CHILD, 30–40 mg/kg daily
or 600–750 mg/m2 daily in 1–2 divided doses (maximum dose, 750 mg daily). Treat until blood lead concentration is
<40 micrograms/dL for 2 consecutive months AND decrease in erythrocyte protoporphyrin level to <3–5 times the
average normal level OR the excretion of coproporphyrin or delta– aminolevulinic acid decrease to the upper limit
of normal. Wilson’s disease, by mouth, ADULT, 750–1,500 mg daily in divided doses (maximum dose, 2,000 mg daily);
CHILD, 20 mg/kg daily in 2–3 divided doses, round off to the nearest 250 mg dose [NOTE: Dose that results in an
initial 24–hour urinary copper excretion of >2 mg daily should be continued for approximately 3 months. Establish
maintenance dose where serum copper levels are controlled at <10 micrograms/dL].

April 25, 2026, 2:07:20 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

ORAL › crystals, 25 g/bottle (Rx)

Administer on an empty stomach, 1 hour before or 2 hours after meals, and at least 1 hour apart from other drugs,
milk, antacids, and zinc or iron containing products. Penicillamine serum levels may be decreased if taken with
food. Certain disease states require further diet adjustment. Administer doses ≤500 mg as a single dose.
Administer doses >500 mg in divided doses. For patients who cannot swallow, contents of capsules may be
administered in 15–30 mL of chilled puréed fruit or fruit juice. Pyridoxine supplementation is recommended. NOTE:
Patients with Wilson's disease should receive 25–50 mg daily of pyridoxine. A multivitamin (without copper) may
also be considered. Decrease copper in diet to <1– 2 mg daily and omit chocolate, nuts, shellfish, mushrooms, liver,
raisins, broccoli, copper-enriched cereal, multivitamins with copper, and molasses. May consider short courses of
iron supplementation if dietary modifications result in iron deficiency. For lead poisoning, decrease calcium in
diet. Pediatric patients and menstruating women may be particularly susceptible to iron deficiency. Allow 2 hours
between administration of iron supplementation and penicillamine as iron may decrease drug absorption.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Initiate at low end of dosing range and “go slow” with dose
increase intervals. Do NOT exceed 750 mg daily.

Renal Impairment:
Avoid use in patients with CrCl<50 mL/minute.
In patients undergoing hemodialysis, administer 33% of
usual dose. Drug is dialyzable.

Pregnancy:
Limit daily dose to 750 mg daily.
If planned caesarian, limit dose to 250 mg daily during last
6 weeks of pregnancy and postoperatively until wound
healing is complete.

Indications

For copper, lead, and mercury poisoning

alveolitis, anetoderma, elastosis perforans serpiginosa,
lupus-like syndrome, l, lymphadenopathy, mammary
hyperplasia, positive ANA test
