NALBUPHINE

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

N02AF02

ANATOMICAL

Nervous System

THERAPEUTIC

Analgesics

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Opioids

CHEMICAL CLASS

Morphinan Derivatives

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IM › IV › SC › 10 mg/mL (as hydrochloride), 1 mL ampule (Rx)

Dosage

IM / IV / SC › 10 mg/mL (as hydrochloride), 1 mL ampule (Rx)

NOTE: Individualize doses based on severity of pain, patient response, prior analgesic treatment experience, and
risk factors for addiction, abuse, and misuse. Moderate to severe pain, by IM or SC injection, or by IV infusion,
ADULT, 10 mg every 3-6 hours as needed based on a 70 kg patient; may titrate dose to appropriate effect (maximum
single dose in nonopioid-tolerant patients, 20 mg; maximum daily dose, 160 mg).

Administration

IM / IV / SC › 10 mg/mL (as hydrochloride), 1 mL ampule (Rx)

For IM or SC injection, administer undiluted. For IV infusion, administer undiluted over at least 2–3 minutes. Larger
induction doses should be administered over 10–15 minutes. Inspect visually for particulate matter and
discoloration prior to administration whenever solution and container permit. NOTE: When discontinuing therapy,
decrease dose by 25– 50% every 2–4 days. Monitor carefully for signs or symptoms of withdrawal. If patient
displays withdrawal symptoms, increase dose to previous level and then reduce dose more slowly by increasing
interval between dose reductions, decreasing amount of daily dose reduction, or both.

Page 1 of 4



April 24, 2026, 11:19:21 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Initiate therapy with reduced doses and use with caution.

Renal and Hepatic Impairment:
Reduce dose.

Indications

Management of pain severe enough to require an opioid analgesic; supplement to balanced anesthesia;
preoperative and postoperative analgesia; obstetrical analgesia during labor and delivery.

Page 2 of 4



April 24, 2026, 11:19:21 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Significant respiratory depression; acute or severe bronchial asthma in an unmonitored setting or in the absence of
resuscitative equipment; known or suspected GI obstruction, including paralytic ileus.

Precautions

WARNING: Life-threatening respiratory depression. Serious, life-threatening, or fatal respiratory depression may
occur, particularly when used concomitantly with other opioids or CNS depressants. Monitor for respiratory
depression, especially during initiation of nalbuphine or following a dose increase.

Risks from concomitant use with benzodiazepines or other CNS depressants. May result in profound sedation,
respiratory depression, coma, and death. Reserve concomitant prescribing of nalbuphine and benzodiazepines or
other CNS depressants for use in patients for whom alternative treatment options are inadequate. Limit doses and
durations to the minimum required. Follow patients for signs and symptoms of respiratory depression and sedation.

Should be administered by personnel trained in the use of IV anesthetics and the management of the respiratory
effects of potent opioids. Resuscitative and intubation equipment and oxygen should be readily available.

Abuse, misuse, and diversion. Users are exposed to the risks of addiction, abuse, and misuse, potentially leading to
overdose and death. Monitor all patients for development of these behaviors or conditions. Risk for opioid abuse is
increased in patients with a personal or family history of substance abuse, including drug or alcohol abuse or
addiction, or mental illness.

Chronic pulmonary disease (monitor for decreased respiratory drive including apnea); Adrenal insufficiency (monitor
for nausea, vomiting, anorexia, fatigue, weakness, dizziness, and low blood pressure); Head injury and increased
intracranial pressure (may obscure the clinical course of patients with head injuries); Renal or hepatic impairment;
Biliary tract surgery (may cause spasm of the sphincter of Oddi); Myocardial infarction; Cardiovascular effects
(monitor for bradycardia); Cachectic or debilitated patients (life-threatening depression is more likely to occur);

Elderly (life-threatening depression is more likely to occur); Children (safety and effectiveness in patients <18 years
have not been established); Pregnancy (severe fetal bradycardia has been reported when used during labor;
sinusoidal fetal heart rate pattern associated with the use of nalbuphine has been reported); Lactation (monitor
nursing infants for excess sedation and respiratory depression).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Sedation, sweaty or clammy, nausea, vomiting, dizziness or vertigo, dry mouth, headache.

Less Common: Nervousness, restlessness, crying, depression, euphoria, floating, hostility, unusual dreams,
confusion, faintness, dysphoria, feeling of heaviness, hallucinations, numbness, tingling, unreality, bradycardia,
tachycardia, hypotension, hypertension, cramps, dyspepsia, bitter taste, depression, dyspnea, asthma, itching,
burning, urticaria, speech difficulty, urinary urgency, blurred vision, flushing and warmth, anaphylactic or
anaphylactoid and other serious hypersensitivity reactions.

Page 3 of 4



April 24, 2026, 11:19:21 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Butorphanol: Amphetamines (analgesic effect)
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Pramipexole, Ropinirole, Rotigotine (sedative effect); Selective
Modulators (serotonergic effect)
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Buprenorphine; Prokinetics
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Buprenorphine (opioid withdrawal); Desmopressin;
Diuretics; Ramosetron (constipating effect); Selective Modulators (serotonin syndrome); SSRIs (psychomotor
impairment)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Butorphanol: Anticholinergic Agents (constipation and urinary
retention); CNS Depressants; Rufinamide (sleepiness and dizziness); Succinylcholine (bradycardic effect)

Avoid concomitant use with:
Reduces therapeutic effect of Butorphanol: Nalmefene; Naltrexone; Other Opioid Analgesics [except
Buprenorphine, Butorphanol, Nalbuphine, Pentazocine]

Mechanism of Action

Nalbuphine is a synthetic morphine opioid agonistantagonist analgesic and a phenanthrene derivative that is a
potent analgesic essentially equivalent to morphine on a milligram basis and may produce the same degree of
respiratory depression.

Pregnancy & Remarks

NALIDIXIC ACID

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

J01MB02

ANATOMICAL

Anti-Infectives for systemic use

THERAPEUTIC

Antibacterials for Systemic Use

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Quinolone

CHEMICAL CLASS

First-Generation Quinolones (Non-Fluorinated)

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › 500 mg tablet (Rx)

ORAL › 250 mg/5 mL suspension, 60 mL (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › 500 mg tablet (Rx)

Shigellosis, by mouth, ADULT, 1 g every 6 hours for 5 days; CHILD ≥3 months, 15 mg/kg (maximum 1 g) every 6 hours
for 5 days

Administration

ORAL › 500 mg tablet (Rx)

Can be taken with or without meals. Patients should be advised to drink fluids liberally and not take antacids.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Risk of toxic reactions may be greater in patients with impaired renal function.

Renal Impairment:
The normal dosage of nalidixic acid may be employed in patients with creatinine clearance more than 20 mL/minute.
Dosage should be halved in patients with creatinine clearance 20 mL/minute or less.

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:08:30 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Indications

Treatment of acute or chronic urinary tract and gastrointestinal tract infections caused by susceptible gram-
negative organisms.

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to any quinolones, children under 3 months old and pregnant women.

Precautions

WARNING:
May cause toxic psychosis, convulsions, increased intracranial pressure, or metabolic acidosis.
Use with caution in patients with known or suspected CNS disorders e.g cerebral arteriosclerosis or epilepsy, or
other factors which predispose seizures Serious and occasionally fatal hypersensitivity (anaphylactoid) reactions,
some following the first dose, have been reported in patients receiving quinolone therapy.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common:
Nausea and vomiting.

Less Common:
Visual disturbances, headache, rashes, eosinophilia, and photosensitivity.

Rare: Hepatic and renal disturbances.

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:08:30 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitory closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Warfarin
Reduces therapeutic effect of Nalidixic Acid:
Probenecid
Reduces absorption of Nalidixic Acid:
Antacids
Avoid concomitant use with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Melphalan
Increases serum concentration of Cyclosporine

Mechanism of Action

Quinolones are broad-spectrum antibacterial agents. They act by blocking bacterial DNA synthesis by inhibiting
bacterial topoisomerase II and topoisomerase IV.
Inhibition of topoisomerase II prevents the relaxation of positively supercoiled DNA while inhibition of
topoisomerase IV interferes with separation of replicated chromosomal DNA.

NALOXONE

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

V03AB15

ANATOMICAL

Various

THERAPEUTIC

All Other Therapeutic Products

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Specific Antidotes

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IM › IV › SC › 400 micrograms/mL (as hydrochloride), 1 mL ampule (Rx)

Dosage

IM / IV / SC › 400 micrograms/mL (as hydrochloride), 1 mL ampule (Rx)

Opioid overdose (with standard ACLS protocols), by IV, IM, or SQ injection, ADULT, initially 0.4–2 mg, may need to
repeat doses every 2–3 minutes; ADOLESCENT, INFANT, and CHILD ≥5 years or >20 kg, 2 mg; if no response, repeat
every 2 to 3 minutes; ADOLESCENT, CHILD <5 years or ≤20 kg, and INFANT, 0.1 mg/kg per dose (maximum dose, 2
mg), repeat every 2–3 minutes if needed. [NOTE: After reversal, re-administration of dose(s) may be needed at a
later interval (i.e., 20–60 minutes) depending on type or duration of opioid. If no response is observed after 10 mg
total, consider other causes of respiratory depression]. Reversal of respiratory depression with therapeutic
opioid doses, by IV, IM, or SQ injection, ADULT, initially 0.04– 0.4 mg, may repeat until desired response achieved;
ADOLESCENT, CHILD, and INFANT, 0.001–0.015 mg/kg per dose; may be repeated as needed. [NOTE: If desired
response is not observed after 0.8 mg total, consider other causes of respiratory depression]. Reversal of
respiratory depression in opioid-dependent patients being treated for cancer pain, by slow IV push, ADULT, 40–80
micrograms every 30 to 60 seconds until improvement in symptoms. [NOTE: If no response is observed after total
naloxone dose 1 mg, consider other causes of respiratory depression.] Postoperative reversal, by IV injection,
ADULT, 0.1–0.2 mg every 2–3 minutes until desired response (adequate ventilation and alertness without
significant pain); repeat doses may be needed within 1– to 2-hour intervals depending on type, dose, and timing of
the last dose of opioid administered; ADOLESCENT, CHILD, and INFANT, 0.005–0.01 mg/kg, may repeat every 2–3
minutes as needed based on response (adequate ventilation without significant pain).

Page 1 of 3



April 24, 2026, 11:19:27 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

IM / IV / SC › 400 micrograms/mL (as hydrochloride), 1 mL ampule (Rx)

IV administration is the preferred route. Administer via IV push over 30 seconds as undiluted preparation or via
slow IV push as diluted preparation by diluting 0.4 mg (1 mL) ampule with 9 mL of normal saline for a total volume of
10 mL to achieve a concentration of 0.04 mg/mL. May also be administered via continuous IV infusion a diluted
preparation by diluting 2 mg in 500 mL of D5W or normal saline to make 4 micrograms/mL. May administer by IM or
SQ route if unable to obtain IV access.

Indications

For the complete or partial reversal of opioid
depression (including respiratory depression) induced by
natural and synthetic opioids (e.g., propoxyphene,
methadone, nalbuphine, butorphanol, pentazocine)

Precautions

Acute opioid withdrawal (e.g. precipitate acute withdrawal,
unmask pain);
Cardiovascular disease (e.g. pulmonary edema,
cardiovascular instability, including ventricular
fibrillation);
Seizures (use with caution);
Pregnancy (use with caution in mild-to-moderate
hypertension during labor; severe hypertension may
occur);
Lactation (consider opioid concentrations in the milk of a
breastfeeding mother and potential transfer of the
opioid to the infant).

Page 2 of 3



April 24, 2026, 11:19:27 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

NOTE: Adverse reactions are related to reversing
dependency and precipitating withdrawal. Withdrawal
symptoms are the result of sympathetic excess.

Common:
Cardiac arrest, fever, flushing, hypotension,
hypertension, tachycardia, ventricular fibrillation
ventricular tachycardia, agitation, hallucination, crying
(excessive; neonates), encephalopathy, irritability,
nervousness, restlessness, seizure (neonates),
tremulousness, abdominal cramps, coma, diarrhea,
nausea, vomiting, injection site reaction, ache,
hyperreflexia (neonates), paresthesia, piloerection,
tremor, weakness, dyspnea, hypoxia, pulmonary edema,
respiratory depression, rhinorrhea, sneezing,
diaphoresis, hot flashes, shivering, yawning

Drug Interactions

Avoid concomitant use with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Naloxone:
Methylnaltrexone (opioid withdrawal)

Mechanism of Action

A pure opioid antagonist that competes and displaces opioids at opioid receptor sites.

Pregnancy & Remarks

PREGNANCY CATEGORY

Antiinflammatory and Antirheumatic Products

ORAL › 550 mg (as sodium salt) (500 mg base) tablet (OTC)

Dosage

ORAL › 275 mg (as sodium salt) (250 mg base) (OTC)

Acute gout, by mouth, ADULT, initially 750 mg, followed by 250 mg every 8 hours until the attack subsides. Acute
musculoskeletal disorders, by mouth, ADULT, initially 500 mg, then 250 mg 2-3 times daily. Ankylosing spondylitis,

initially 500 mg, followed by 500 mg every 12 hours or 250 mg every 6-8 hours (maximum daily dose; Day 1: 1.25 g

naproxen sodium may be preferred due to more rapid absorption and onset.

April 24, 2026, 11:19:32 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Renal Impairment:
In mild impairment, reduce dose due to nephrotoxicity.
In moderate to severe impairment, avoid use.

Hepatic Impairment:
In severe impairment, avoid use due to hepatotoxicity.

Indications

Rheumatoid arthritis; osteoarthritis; ankylosing spondylitis; juvenile idiopathic arthritis; for the relief of signs and
symptoms of tendonitis and bursitis; acute gout; management of pain due to inflammation (e.g., period pain,
migraine); management of primary dysmenorrhea; inflammatory joint pain

Page 2 of 4



April 24, 2026, 11:19:32 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Treatment of perioperative pain in the setting of Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG) surgery; active peptic ulcers;
active GI inflammatory disease

Precautions

WARNING: NSAIDs are associated with an increased risk of adverse cardiovascular thrombotic events, including fatal
MI and stroke.

NSAIDs may increase risk of GI irritation, inflammation, ulceration, bleeding, and perforation.

Anaphylactoid reactions may occur (do not use in patients who experience bronchospasm, asthma, rhinitis, or
urticaria with aspirin or NSAID therapy);

mL ear drops solution, 5 mL bottle (Rx)

Dosage

OTHER › 3.5 mg neomycin (as sulfate) + 10,000 units polymyxin B (as sulfate) + 0.025% fluocinolone acetonide per mL ear
drops solution, 5 mL bottle (Rx)

Ear infections, into the ear, ADULT and CHILD, instill 3–4 drops 2–4 times daily. [NOTE: Limit treatment to 5–7 days.
Refer to ENT specialist if discharge continues].

Administration

OTHER › 3.5 mg neomycin (as sulfate) + 10,000 units polymyxin B (as sulfate) + 0.025% fluocinolone acetonide per mL ear
drops solution, 5 mL bottle (Rx)

Warm container in cup of warm water to reduce viscosity. The ears should be clean and dry. Lie down with the
affected ear upward. Gentle massage or pressure on the tragus (cartilage flap over the opening of the ear canal) to
aid penetration of the drops. Method of drug application based on degree of swelling in ear canal: For minimal
swelling, instill drops directly into the ear canal then remain in position for a few minutes. For swelling that
narrows the ear canal, saturate a sponge wick or ribbon gauze with drops and insert into the ear canal; review daily
until swelling settles.

Indications

Otitis externa, with or without associated otitis media; adjunct therapy to acute or chronic dermatoses of the
external ear; chronic suppurative otitis media; draining mastoidectomy cavities; otorrhea from ventilation tubes.

Dosage

Acute hypertension, by IV infusion, ADULT, 5 mg/ hour initially, may increase by 2.5 mg/hour every 5 minutes for
rapid titration, to every 15 minutes for gradual titration (maximum dose, 15 mg/hour); in rapidly titrated patients,
consider reduction to 3 mg/hour after response is achieved

Administration

IV › 1 mg/mL (as hydrochloride), 2 mL ampule (Rx)

Administer by slow continuous IV infusion via a central line or through a large peripheral vein. Avoid use of small
peripheral veins to minimize infusion site reactions. Do NOT combine or run in the same line as other medications.
Monitor infusion site closely to prevent extravasation. Peripheral venous irritation may be minimized by changing
the site of infusion every 12 hours. Evaluate cardiac status and blood pressure and monitor for rash, hypotension,
bradycardia, confusion, and nausea when starting, adjusting dose, or discontinuing. NOTE: Parenteral
administration is indicated only for short-term use. Teach patient orthostatic precautions.

Mechanism of Action

A calcium channel blocker that inhibits calcium ions from entering select voltage-sensitive areas of vascular smooth
muscle and myocardium. It produces a relaxation of coronary vascular smooth muscle and coronary vasodilation,
thus increasing myocardial oxygen delivery in patients with vasospastic angina.

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:10:41 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

ORAL › 1 mg Pastille (Rx)

Chewing-gum results in relatively slow absorption. Food and acidic drinks should be avoided 15 min before and
during use.

Dose Adjustment

Seek for a doctor's advice before using nicotine when taking prescription medicine for asthma or depression.

Indications

Aid to decrease withdrawal symptoms triggered by tobacco cessation (smoking and smokeless tobacco products)

Contraindications

Do not use when using other nicotine containing products

Precautions

Caution should be exercised by people with acute symptoms of cardiovascular disease (serious arrhythmia or
serious or worsening angina pectoris) or recent cardiac events (the 2-week post-myocardial infarction period), who
should use nicotine replacement Therapy only on the advice of a health professional.

With oral use gastritis (can be aggravated by swallowed nicotine).
Gum may also stick to and damage dentures. oesophagitis (can be aggravated by swallowed nicotine). peptic ulcers
(can be aggravated by swallowed nicotine)

Adverse Drug Reactions

Acute to severe nicotine poisoning onset of symptoms:

Nausea, salivation, abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhea, cold sweat, headache, dizziness, disturbed hearing and vision,
mental confusion, and marked weakness.

Faintness and prostration ensue; the blood pressure falls; breathing is difficult; the pulse is weak, rapid, and
irregular; and collapse may be followed by terminal convulsions.
Death may result within a few minutes from respiratory failure.

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:10:41 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

N/A

Mechanism of Action

Nicotine acts on a variety of neuroeffector and chemosensitive sites. This natural liquid alkaloid can both stimulate
and desensitize receptors, making nicotine’s effects complex and unpredictable. The ultimate response of any one
system represents the summation of stimulatory and inhibitory effects of nicotine. Nicotine can increase heart rate
by excitation of sympathetic ganglia or by paralysis of parasympathetic cardiac ganglia, and it can slow heart rate by
paralysis of sympathetic or stimulation of parasympathetic cardiac ganglia. The effects of the drug on the
chemoreceptors of the carotid and aortic bodies and on regions of the CNS also can influence heart rate, as can the
compensatory baroreceptor reflexes resulting from changes in blood pressure caused by nicotine. Finally, nicotine
can stimulate secretion of epinephrine from the adrenal medulla, which accelerates heart rate and raises blood
pressure.

Hypertension, by mouth, ADULT, initially 30–60 mg once daily, titrate to 30–90 mg daily (maximum, 90–120 mg daily).

titration may be done more rapidly with appropriate monitoring. Use same total daily dose when switching from
immediate-release to sustained-release formulation.

Administration

ORAL › 10 mg capsule (Rx)

May be taken with or without food. Capsules are rapidly absorbed orally if administered without food but may result
in vasodilator side effects. If flushing is problematic, administer low-fat meals to decrease flushing. Swallow
tablets whole. Do NOT crush, split, or chew. Teach patient orthostatic precautions. NOTE: For Adalat CC, Afeditab
CR, Nifediac CC, administer on an empty stomach (as per manufacturer labelling). Other extended-release
products may not have this recommendation; consult product labeling. Nifedipine serum levels may be decreased
if taken with food. Food may decrease the rate but not the extent of absorption of Procardia XL®.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Consider lower initial doses and titrate to response. Half-life is extended in elderly patients (6.7 hours) as compared
to younger subjects (3.8 hours).

Hepatic Impairment:
Use with caution. Clearance is reduced in cirrhotic patients, which may lead to increased systemic exposure. Monitor
closely for adverse effects or toxicity and consider dose adjustments.

Indications

Treatment of hypertension

Page 2 of 6



April 25, 2026, 2:10:54 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Concomitant use with strong CYP3A4 inducers (e.g., rifampin); cardiogenic shock; STEMI; Heart failure

Precautions

WARNING: Use with extreme caution due to rapid and prolonged fall in blood pressure.

Use is restricted for acute hypertensive emergencies in patients less than 18 years old.

Abrupt withdrawal (may cause rebound angina in patients with CAD).
Angina or MI (increased angina and/or MI have occurred; reflex tachycardia may occur resulting in angina and/or MI in
patients with obstructive coronary disease)
Hypotension or syncope (rarely occurs; blood pressure must be lowered at a rate appropriate for the patient's clinical
condition; death, cerebrovascular ischemia, syncope, stroke, acute myocardial infarction, and fetal distress, have
been reported)
Peripheral edema (most common side effect).
Aortic stenosis (may reduce coronary perfusion resulting in myocardial ischemia); Heart failure (avoid use)
Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with outflow tract obstruction (reduction in afterload may worsen symptoms
associated with this condition).
Gastrointestinal strictures (alterations in GI anatomy, e.g., severe GI narrowing, history of GI cancer, obstruction,
bowel resection, gastric bypass, vertical banded gastroplasty, and underlying hypomotility disorders have led to
bezoar formation with extended release forms); hepatic impairment.
Immediate-release formulations should not be used to manage primary hypertension (adequate studies have not
been conducted)
May contain lactose (do not administer in patients with galactose intolerance, Lapp lactase deficiency, or glucose-
galactose malabsorption syndromes).
Surgery (cardiopulmonary bypass, intraoperative blood loss or vasodilating anesthesia may result in severe
hypotension and/or increased fluid requirements; consider withdrawing nifedipine >36 hours before surgery).
Elderly (immediate-release tablets may cause hypotension and risk of precipitating myocardial ischemia; may
experience a greater hypotensive response; constipation may be more of a problem).
Pregnancy (crosses the placenta and small amounts can be detected in the urine of newborn infants; an increase in
perinatal asphyxia, cesarean delivery, prematurity, and intrauterine growth retardation have been reported);
lactation (excreted in breast milk; breastfeeding is not recommended).

Indications

Treatment of acute, uncomplicated cystitis in non-pregnant women, asymptomatic bacteruria; recurrent urinary
tract infection in non-pregnant women (for asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy, urine culture is necessary).

Contraindications

Previous history of cholestatic jaundice or hepatic dysfunction associated with prior nitrofurantoin use;
for infants <1 month of age (increased risk of hemolytic anemia);
G6PD-deficiency, including breast-feeding of affected infants (only small amounts in milk but may be enough to
produce hemolysis in G6PD-deficient infants);
pregnancy at term (third trimester: may produce neonatal hemolysis if used at term); porphyria.

Precautions

This is NOT indicated for the treatment of pyelonephritis; it is ineffective in alkaline urine;
Pulmonary toxicity and disorders (monitor lung and liver function on long-term therapy; discontinue use if the lung
function deteriorates; acute, subacute or chronic
pulmonary reactions have been observed);
Susceptibility to peripheral neuropathy (risk may be increased in patients with anemia, renal impairment, diabetes,
vitamin B deficiency, debilitating disease, or
electrolyte disturbance);
Risk of optic neuritis;
Hepatic reactions (monitor liver function tests periodically);
Hemolytic anemia;
Neurological or allergic disorder;
folate deficiency; prolonged use may result in fungal or bacterial superinfection; urine may be colored yellow or
brown.
Elderly (monitor closely for toxicities during use).
Pregnancy (when given during second trimester until 32 weeks age of gestation)

NOTE:
During long-term treatment, monitor:
Pulmonary function; Liver function every month for 3 months, then every 3 months, since onset of hepatotoxicity
may be insidious;
Renal function;
Development of paresthesia (withdraw to prevent severe neuropathy).

Abdominal pain, allergic skin reactions, anorexia, diarrhea, headache, nausea, vomiting.

The bioavailability of nitrofurantoin is usually increased by medicines that reduce gastric emptying time, such as

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Aldosteroneblocking agents e.g., eplerenone and spironolactone

Nitrofurans are bacteriostatic and bactericidal for many gram-negative and gram-positive organisms but may not

Dosage

IV › 1 mg/mL in 10 mL Ampul (IV infusion) (Rx)

Coronary artery disease: by IV injection, ADULT, 5 micrograms/minute, increase by 5 micrograms/minute every 3–5
minutes until 20 micrograms/minute; if no response at 20 micrograms/minute, may increase dose by 10-20
micrograms/minute every 3–5 minutes (maximum dose, 400 micrograms/minute). See Nitroglycerin (Glyceryl
Trinitrate) under Cardiac Therapy – Vasodilators used in Cardiac Disease in Chapter 3: Cardiovascular System for

Indications

Page 1 of 2



April 25, 2026, 2:13:11 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Mechanism of Action

An antianginal agent that forms free radical nitric oxide, which activates guanylate cyclase in smooth muscle,
increasing cGMP, leading to dephosphorylation of myosin light chains and smooth muscle relaxation. It produces a
more prominent vasodilator effect on the peripheral veins than arteries. It has blood pressure lowering effects and
also reduces cardiac oxygen demand by decreasing preload and afterload.

initially 10 micrograms/minute, increase by 10 micrograms/minute every 3–5 minutes until relief of symptoms; if no
response at 20 micrograms/minute, may increase by 10 micrograms/minute and later by 20 micrograms/minute.
NOTE: If nitroglycerin is prescribed, advise the patient to take 1 dose promptly in response to chest pain

Administration

IV › 1 mg/mL in 10 mL Ampul (IV infusion) (Rx)

For IV administration, administer via infusion pumps. Prepare in glass bottles, EXCEL, or PAB containers.
Adsorption occurs to soft plastic (e.g., PVC). Use administration sets intended for nitroglycerin. Prepare a 100
micrograms/mL solution by withdrawing 25 mL D5W from a 250-mL bottle of D5W and replace this volume with 25
mg (25 mL) of nitroglycerin. Prepare a 200 micrograms/mL solution by withdrawing 50 mL D5W from a 250-mL
bottle of D5W and replace volume with 50 mg (50 mL) of nitroglycerin.

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:13:18 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Indications

Management of unstable angina

Contraindications

Concurrent use with PDE5 Inhibitors or Riociguat; hypersensitivity to corn or corn products (solutions containing
dextrose); constrictive pericarditis; pericardial tamponade; restrictive cardiomyopathy; hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg
or ≥30 mmHg below baseline); marked bradycardia or tachycardia; right ventricular infarction

Precautions

Long-term therapy (may develop hemodynamic and antianginal tolerance; administer 10–12 hours nitrate-free
interval daily to prevent tolerance; gradually decrease dose to avoid withdrawal reaction).
Headache (dose-related; may occur during initial dosing); increased intracranial pressure (may precipitate or
aggravate increased intracranial pressure; may worsen clinical outcomes in patients with neurologic injury).
Hypotension or bradycardia (severe hypotension and orthostatic hypotension can occur; paradoxical bradycardia and
increased angina pectoris can accompany hypotension)
Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (may reduce preload, exacerbating obstruction and cause hypotension or syncope
and/or worsening of heart failure).
Elderly (hypotension is enhanced due to decreased baroreceptor response, decreased venous tone, hypovolemia, or
other hypotensive drugs).
Lactation (not known if excreted in breastmilk; limited information available; use with caution when administering to
nursing women)

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Bradycardia, flushing, hypotension, orthostatic hypotension, peripheral edema, syncope, tachycardia,
headache, dizziness, lightheadedness, nausea, vomiting, xerostomia, paresthesia, weakness, dyspnea, pharyngitis,
rhinitis, diaphoresis
Less Common: Allergic reactions, anaphylactoid reaction, blurred vision, cardiovascular collapse, crescendo angina,
exfoliative dermatitis, pallor, palpitation, rash, rebound hypertension, restlessness, shock, vertigo
Rare: Methemoglobinemia

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:13:18 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Nitroglycerin:
Antihypertensive effects: Alfuzosin, Barbiturates e.g. Phenobarbital, Molsidomine, Nicorandil
Vasodilatory effect: Ethyl alcohol

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Nitroglycerin:
Dapoxetine (orthostatic hypotension), Other Antihypertensive Agents

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
Methemoglobinemia: Dapsone, (Topical), Nitric Oxide, Prilocaine, Sodium Nitrite,
Orthostatic hypotension: Duloxetine, Levodopa
Risperidone (hypotensive effect), Rosiglitazone (ischemia)

Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Heparin (anticoagulant effect)

Avoid concomitant use with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of ISMN:
Phosphodiesterase-5 Inhibitors e.g. Sildenafil (vasodilatory effects), Ergot Derivatives (vasodilatory effects)

Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Riociguat (antihypertensive effects)

Increases serum concentration of the following drugs:
Ergot Derivatives

Mechanism of Action

An antianginal agent which releases nitric oxide. Nitric oxide produces a more prominent vasodilator effect on the
peripheral veins than arteries. In turn, it reduces cardiac oxygen demand by decreasing preload and afterload. It
likewise causes epicardial coronary arteries vasodilation

IV INFUSION › 1 mg/mL (as Bitartrate) in 2 mL Ampul (IV infusion) (Rx)

NOTE: Norepinephrine dose is stated in terms of its base form. Hypotension or shock: by continuous IV infusion,
ADULT, initially 8-12 micrograms/minute, titrate to desired response (usual maintenance dose, 2–4
micrograms/minute); dosage range varies greatly depending on clinical situation; CHILD, initially 0.05–0.1

micrograms/kg per minute, titrate to desired response.

April 25, 2026, 2:11:45 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

IV INFUSION › 1 mg/mL (as Bitartrate) in 2 mL Ampul (IV infusion) (Rx)

For IV route, administer as a continuous infusion with the use of an infusion pump. Dilute prior to use. Administer
via a central line. Do NOT administer alkaline solutions, i.e., sodium bicarbonate, through the same IV line as
norepinephrine. Inactivation of norepinephrine may occur. EXTRAVASATION MANAGEMENT: Norepinephrine is a
vesicant. Ensure proper needle or catheter placement prior to and during infusion. Infuse into a large vein to
prevent extravasation. Avoid infusion into leg veins. If extravasation occurs, stop infusion immediately and
disconnect. Leave cannula or needle in place then gently aspirate extravasated solution. Do NOT flush the line.
Remove needle or cannula and elevate extremity. Initiate phentolamine or alternative antidote. Apply dry warm
compresses.

Indications

For cardiogenic or vascular shock, persists after adequate fluid volume replacement; severe hypotension; first-
choice vasopressor for the treatment of sepsis and septic shock in adults

Contraindications

Hypotension from hypovolemia except as an emergency measure to maintain coronary and cerebral perfusion until
volume could be replaced; mesenteric or peripheral vascular thrombosis (unless life-saving procedure); during
anesthesia with cyclopropane or halothane (risk of ventricular arrhythmias)

Precautions

WARNING: If extravasation occurs, infiltrate the area with 5–10 mg Phentolamine diluted in 10–15 mL of saline using a
fine hypodermic needle. Administer Phentolamine within 12 hours after extravasation is noted to prevent sloughing
or necrosis.

Extravasation (extravasation may cause severe ischemic necrosis).
Pregnancy (animal reproduction studies have not been conducted; norepinephrine is an endogenous catecholamine
and crosses the placenta);
Lactation (not known if excreted in breast milk; use with caution when administering to nursing women).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Arrhythmias, bradycardia, peripheral ischemia, anxiety, headache (transient), skin necrosis, dyspnea,
respiratory difficulty

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Decreases metabolism of Norepinephrine:
COMT Inhibitors e.g., Entacapone

Enhances therapeutic effect of Norepinephrine:
MAO Inhibitors e.g., Selegeline (hypertensive effect; tachycardia)

Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Atomoxetine (hypertensive and tachycardic effects), Droxidopa (hypertensive effect)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
Doxofylline, Sympathomimetics

Reduces diagnostic effect of the following agents:
Ioflupane I123

Reduces therapeutic effect of Norepinephrine:
Alpha Blockers e.g., Phentolamine (vasodilating effect)], Spironolactone (vasoconstricting effect)

Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Alpha Blockers e.g., Phentolamine (vasoconstricting effect)

Avoid concomitant use with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Norepinephrine:
Beta Blockers (vasopressor effect), Ergot Derivatives [except Ergoloid Mesylates] (hypertensive and
vasoconstricting effects), Hyaluronidase (vasoconstricting effect), Linezolid (hypertensive effect) [reduce initial
dose], Serotonin / Norepinephrine Reuptake Inhibitors (tachycardic and vasopressor effects), Tricyclic
Antidepressants e.g., Amitryptilline (vasopressor effect)

Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Inhalational Anesthetics e.g., Sevoflurane (arrhythmogenic effect)

Reduces therapeutic effect of Norepinephrine:
Beta Blockers e.g., Propranolol

Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Iobenguane I123

Mechanism of Action

A direct-acting, mixed alpha- and beta-adrenoceptor agonist that stimulates beta1-adrenergic receptors and alpha-
adrenergic receptors causing increased contractility and heart rate, as well as peripheral vasoconstriction. This
results in an increased systemic blood pressure and coronary blood flow. Vasoconstricting effects on alpha-
receptors are greater than the inotropic and chronotropic effects on beta receptors.

Dosage

ORAL › 5 mg tablet (as acetate and as base) (Rx)

Abnormal uterine bleeding and amenorrhea, by mouth, ADULT, 2.5–10 mg once a day for 5–10 days; withdrawal
bleeding usually occurs within 3–7 days after the last dose. Endometriosis, by mouth, ADULT, 5 mg once a day for 14
days; increase dose at increments of 2.5 mg per day every 2 weeks until reaching 15 mg daily, continue for 6–9
months or until presence of breakthrough bleeding. Contraception, by mouth, ADULT, 350 micrograms once daily;
start on the first day of menstruation or after miscarriage or abortion; if switching from a combined oral
contraceptive, begin on the day after finishing the last active pill if the patient is not pregnant; if switching from
an UID, continue this or abstain or use an alternative emergency contraceptive for at least 2 days after
contraceptive is initiated; in case of missed dose, take as soon as remembered; if ≥3 hours late, use a backup
method of contraception for 48 hours. [NOTE: Back up contraception is needed if therapy is initiated within 5 days
of onset of menstruation. If started >5 days or at any time women experienced amenorrhea, back up contraception
should be used for 2 days.]

Administration

ORAL › 5 mg tablet (as acetate and as base) (Rx)

Administer tablets at the same time each day. NOT used for emergency contraception.

Indications

Management of abnormal uterine bleeding, amenorrhea, and endometriosis; contraception;

Contraindications

Current or history of deep vein thrombosis, active or recent history of arterial thromboembolic disease (e.g., stroke,
MI); as a diagnostic test for pregnancy; suspected or known pregnancy; hepatic impairment or tumors

Adverse Drug Reactions

Cerebral embolism, cerebral thrombosis, deep vein thrombosis, edema, pulmonary embolism, retinal thrombosis,
depression, dizziness, fatigue, headache, insomnia, migraine, emotional lability, nervousness, acne vulgaris,
alopecia, chloasma, pruritus, skin rash, urticarial, amenorrhea, hirsutism, hypermenorrhea, menstrual disease,
weight gain, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, breakthrough bleeding, breast hypertrophy, breast tenderness,
cervical erosion, change in cervical secretions, decreased lactation, genital discharge, mastalgia, spotting, vaginal
hemorrhage, hypersensitivity, cholestatic jaundice, hepatitis, arm pain, leg pain, optic neuritis (with or without vision
loss)

Mechanism of Action

Progestin-only contraception that acts by inhibiting ovulation through thickening of cervical mucus, inhibiting sperm
penetration; altering FSH and LH concentrations; slowing the movement of ovum through the fallopian tubes, and
modifying the endometrium.

April 25, 2026, 2:14:23 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Refer to adult dosing.

Renal Impairment:
After a normal initial dose, adjust as follows:
CrCl >20 mL/minute: Administer usual recommended dose every 24 hours.
CrCl <20 mL/minute: Administer half the usual recommended dose every 24 hours.
Intermittent hemodialysis (IHD): 100 to 200 mg after dialysis.
Peritoneal dialysis: 200 mg every 24 hours.
Continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT): 300 mg every 24 hours.

Hepatic Impairment:
Use with caution for patients with severe impairment (e.g., cirrhosis with or without ascites).

Indications

Treatment of acute exacerbations of chronic bronchitis, community-acquired pneumonia, skin and skin structure
infections (uncomplicated), urethritis and cervicitis (non-gonococcal) due to Chlamydia trachomatis infection, mixed
infections of the urethra and cervix, pelvic inflammatory disease (acute), cystitis (uncomplicated), urinary tract
infections (complicated), prostatitis.

Page 2 of 4



April 25, 2026, 2:14:23 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Precautions

WARNING: Significant hypoglycemia and certain mental health side effects. Low blood sugar levels can lead to coma;
and the mental health side effects to be added or updated across all fluoroquinolones are disturbances in attention,
disorientation, agitation, nervousness, memory impairment, and serious disturbances in mental abilities called
delirium.
Associated with an increased risk of tendinitis and tendon rupture in all ages. This risk is further increased in older
patients (usually older than 60 years), patients taking corticosteroid drugs, and patients with kidney, heart, or lung
transplants.
May exacerbate muscle weakness in persons with myasthenia gravis. Avoid ofloxacin in patients with a known history
of myasthenia gravis.

Fluoroquinolones may prolong QTc interval, avoid use in patients with a history of QTc prolongation, uncorrected
hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, or concurrent administration of other medications known to prolong the QT interval
(including Class Ia and Class III antiarrhythmics, cisapride, erythromycin, antipsychotics, and tricyclic
antidepressants);
Tremor, restlessness, confusion and very rare hallucinations, increased intracranial pressure or seizures may occur
(use with caution in patients with known or suspected CNS disorder. Discontinue in patients who experience
significant CNS adverse effects);
Severe, sometimes, fatal, hypersensitivity reactions, including anaphylaxis, have occurred with quinolone therapy.
(Prompt discontinuation of drug should occur if skin rash or other symptoms arise);
Peripheral neuropathy (discontinue if symptoms of sensory of sensorimotor neuropathy occur);
Photosensitivity (discontinue use if photosensitivity occurs);
Prolonged use may result in fungal or bacterial superinfection, including C. difficile-associated diarrhea (CDAD) and
pseudomembranous colitis.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Less Common: Chest pain, headache, insomnia, dizziness, fatigue, drowsiness, sleep disorder, nervousness, trunk
pain, pruritus, skin rash, genital pruritus, nausea, diarrhea, vomiting, abdominal cramps, constipation, decreased
appetite, dysgeusia, flatulence, gastrointestinal distress, xerostomia, vaginitis, visual disturbances, pharyngitis,
fever.
Rare: Abnormal dreams, anaphylaxis, anxiety, auditory disturbance, blurred vision, chills, cognitive dysfunction,
cough, depression, ecchymosis, edema, erythema nodosum, euphoria, exacerbation of myasthenia gravis, Gilles de
la Tourette’s syndrome, hallucination, hepatic insufficiency, hepatic failure, hepatitis, hepatotoxicity, hyperglycemia,
hypoglycemia, hypertension, increased intracranial pressure, polydipsia, interstitial nephritis, limb pain, malaise,
palpitations, paresthesia, peripheral neuropathy, photophobia, pneumonitis, pseudotumor cerebri, psychotic
reaction, rhabdomyolysis, rupture of tendon, seizure, skin photosensitivity, SJS, syncope, tendonitis, tinnitus,
torsades de pointes, toxic epidermal necrolysis, vasculitis, vasodilation, vertigo, weakness, weight loss.

Page 3 of 4



April 25, 2026, 2:14:23 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Glucose Lowering Agents e.g. Insulin
Delamanid, Fluoxetine, Porfimer, QTc-Prolonging Agents, Tacrolimus, Vitamin K Antagonists e.g. Warfarin

Reduces absorption of Ofloxacin:
Antacids (except: sodium bicarbonate), Calcium Salts (except: calcium chloride), Phosphate binder e.g. Sevelamer

Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
BCG Vaccine, Lactobacillus, Estriol, Sodium Picosulfate, Typhoid Vaccine

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects Ofloxacin:
Corticosteroids

Decreases excretion of Ofloxacin:
Probenecid

Avoid concomitant use with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects Ofloxacin:
Nadifloxacin

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Aminolevulinic Acid

Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
BCG, Cholera Vaccine

Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Hydroxychloroquine, Mifepristone, Probucol, QTc-Prolonging Agents

Decreases serum concentration of Ofloxacin:
Strontium Ranelate

Mechanism of Action

Quinolones are broad-spectrum antibacterial agents. They act by blocking bacterial DNA synthesis by inhibiting
bacterial topoisomerase II and topoisomerase IV. Inhibition of topoisomerase II prevents the relaxation of positively
supercoiled DNA while inhibition of topoisomerase IV interferes with separation of replicated chromosomal DNA.

April 25, 2026, 2:14:46 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

ORAL › 5 mg tablet (Rx)

Olanzapine orodispersible tablet may be placed on the tongue and allowed to dissolve, or dispersed in water,
orange juice, apple juice, milk or coffee. May be taken with or without food. Do NOT discontinue abruptly because
of the possibility of withdrawal and rebound psychosis. Perform discontinuation or cross-titration gradually to
minimize the potential of withdrawal, rebound psychosis, extrapyramidal side effects, cholinergic rebound,
rebound insomnia, and unwanted pregnancy, unless safety concerns require a more rapid withdrawal.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
May tolerate lower doses better.

Hepatic Impairment:
For moderate to severe hepatic impairment, consider initial dose of 5 mg daily. When used in combination with
fluoxetine, limit initial dose to 2.5–5 mg once daily. Increase dose with caution.

Debilitated Patients with a predisposition to Hypotensive reactions, who exhibit a combination of factors that may
result in slower metabolism, e.g., nonsmoking female patients =65 years), or who may be more pharmacodynamically
sensitive to olanzapine:
Initiate at 5 mg once daily, increase dose with caution as clinically indicated.

Indications

Management of schizophrenia; maintaining response in schizophrenia, acute phase schizophrenia; acute agitation
associated with schizophrenia, acute mania or mixed mania; bipolar maintenance; acute agitation associated with
bipolar I mania, bipolar depression; treatment-resistant depression (in combination with fluoxetine).

Page 2 of 4



April 25, 2026, 2:14:46 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Psychosis in Parkinson’s disease.

Precautions

WARNING: NOT approved for treatment of dementiarelated psychosis (increased risk of death)

Altered cardiac conduction, e.g., life-threatening arrhythmias; orthostatic hypotension; cardiovascular disease;
blood dyscrasias, e.g., leukopenia, neutropenia, and agranulocytosis; dyslipidemia; hyperglycemia;
hyperprolactinemia (dose-related); Anticholinergic effects, e.g., constipation, xerostomia, blurred vision, urinary
retention; esophageal dysmotility or aspiration; multiorgan hypersensitivity reactions (drug reaction with
eosinophilia and systemic symptoms [DRESS]); neuroleptic malignant syndrome; temperature regulation, e.g.,
impaired core body temperature regulation; cerebrovascular effects; CNS depression; extrapyramidal symptoms,
e.g., dystonic reactions, pseudoparkinsonism, akathisia, and tardive dyskinesia; greater risk for dystonia with
increased doses; greater risk for falls; suicidal ideation; Parkinson disease; seizures; weight gain; hepatic
impairment; renal impairment

Smokers (decreases olanzapine concentration; may require daily doses 30% higher to obtain an equivalent
concentration); Elderly (more susceptible to adverse effects; potentially inappropriate in the elderly; avoid use in
patients =65 years with dementia due to an increased risk of mortality and cerebrovascular accidents, a greater rate
of cognitive decline, and potential to cause or exacerbate SIADH or hyponatremia, unless all nonpharmacologic
options have failed or are not possible or the patient is threatening substantial harm to self or to others); children
(safety of doses >12 mg in combination with doses >50 mg of fluoxetine has not been studied; use in adolescents =13
years of age may result in increased weight gain, sedation, and increases in LDL cholesterol, total cholesterol,
triglycerides, prolactin, and liver transaminase levels).

SKILLED TASKS. May impair ability to perform tasks, which require mentalmachinery. alertness, like driving or
operating

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Arthralgia, hypercholesterolemia, hypertriglyceridemia, increased appetite, malaise, edema.

Less Common: Alopecia, amnesia, bradycardia, epistaxis.

Rare: Hepatitis, pancreatitis, rhabdomyolysis.

NOTE: Patients taking this drug may experience weight gain and/or sedation.

Page 3 of 4



April 25, 2026, 2:14:46 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Antihypertensives [except Guanethidine], CNS Depressants
(sedative effect), QTc-prolonging Agents (QTc-prolonging effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Olanzapine: Methylphenidate, Metoclopramide
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Glucagon (GI adverse effects) Hypotension-
associated Agents (hypotension) Iohexol, Iomeprol, Iopamidol (seizures) Mequitazine (arrhythmogenic effect)
Methylphenidate, Mirabegron Potassium Chloride (ulcerogenic effect) Serotonin Modulators (neuroleptic malignant
syndrome; serotonin syndrome) SSRIs (psychomotor impairment) Topiramate (hyperthermia)
Reduces absorption of Nitroglycerin (decreases dissolution of sublingual nitroglycerin tablets)
Reduces therapeutic effect of Olanzapine: Methylphenidate (antipsychotic and mood-stabilizing actions)
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Phenylephrine, Prokinetics, Secretin, Antidiabetics,
Anticholinergics Methylphenidate (stimulant effect) Dopamine Agonists (antagonism) Guanethidine
(antihypertensive effects)

Avoid concomitant use with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Other Antipsychotics (increased hospitalization
rates and length of stay; increased mortality; QTc prolongation; torsades de pointes)

Mechanism of Action

Olanzapine is a thienobenzodiazepine structurally related to clozapine. It is more potent and less sedating than
clozapine. It also has low risk for extrapyramidal symptoms, even at high doses. However, it has the greatest known
cardiometabolic risks, because it increases triglyceride levels and promotes insulin resistance.

prior to induction of anesthesia. For IM injection, administer undiluted. For IVPB, infuse diluted solution over 15–30
minutes. For IV push, single doses may be administered by IV injection as undiluted solution over 2–5 minutes. For
IV infusion, dilute in 50 mL D5W or NS.

Dose Adjustment

Severe hepatic impairment: Maximum daily dose for oral and parenteral administration is 8 mg.

Indications

Treatment of nausea and vomiting induced by cytotoxic chemotherapy and radiotherapy; prevention and treatment
of postoperative nausea and vomiting.

Contraindications

Concomitant use of apomorphine

QT prolongation (e.g., torsade de pointes); ileus or gastric distention.

sensation of cold, pruritus, skin rash, diarrhea, urinary retention, elevated ALT and AST, injection site reaction,
hypoxia, fever.
Less Common: Abdominal pain, accommodation disturbance, anaphylaxis, angina pectoris, angioedema, atrial
fibrillation, anaphylactoid reaction, bradycardia, bronchospasm, cardiac arrhythmia, cardiorespiratory arrest, bullous
skin disease, chest pain, chills, depression of ST segment on ECG, dyspnea, dystonic reaction, ECG changes,
extrapyramidal reaction, hypersensitivity reaction, hypokalemia, hepatic failure, hypotension, ischemic heart
disease, laryngeal edema, laryngospasm, mucosal tissue reaction, myocardial infarction, neuroleptic malignant
syndrome, oculogyric crisis, palpitations, positive lymphocyte transformation test, prolonged QT interval on ECG,
second-degree atrioventricular block, serotonin syndrome, shock, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, stridor,
supraventricular tachycardia, syncope, tachycardia, tonic-clonic seizures, torsade de pointes, toxic epidermal
necrolysis, transient blindness (≤48 hours), transient blurred vision (following infusion), urticaria, vascular occlusive
events, ventricular premature contractions, ventricular tachycardia, weakness, xerostomia.

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: QTc-prolonging agents, Serotonin Modulators (serotonergic

Avoid concomitant use with:

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Apomorphine (hypotensive effect), Ivabradine,

A selective type 3 serotonin (5-HT3) receptor inhibitor that acts as an antiemetic by blocking the effect of serotonin

Administration

IM / IV › [ACCESS] 250 mg (as sodium salt), vial (Rx)

For IM: Administer into muscle? avoid sciatic nerve injury. For IV: Administer around-the-clock to promote less
variation in peak and trough serum levels. Admin IV push over 10 minutes. Administer IV piggyback over 30
minutes.

Indications

Treatment of infections caused by susceptible penicillinaseproducing staphylococci.

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to oxacillin, any penicillin or any component of the formulation.

Precautions

WARNING: Serious and occasionally severe or fatal hypersensitivity reactions have been reported in patients on
penicillin therapy, especially with a history of beta-lactam hypersensitivity, history of sensitivity to multiple allergens,
or previous IgE-mediated reactions. Use with caution in asthmatic patients.

Monitor patients for anaphylactic/hypersensitivity reactions, hepatitis, superinfection;
Use with caution in patients with renal impairment;
Elderly;
Neonates.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Fever, rash, diarrhea nausea and vomiting, agranulocytosis, eosinophilia, leukopenia, neutropenia,
thrombocytopenia, hepatotoxicity, acute interstitial nephritis, hematuria, serum sicknesslike reactions

Drug Interactions

Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
BCG, Typhoid vaccine (Only the live attenuated Ty21a strain is affected.)

Reduces therapeutic effect of Oxacillin:
Fusidic acid, Tetracycline derivatives

Decreases excretion of the following drugs:
Methotrexate

Decreases serum concentration of the following drugs:
Mycophenolate

Increases serum concentration of Oxacillin:
Probenecid

Mechanism of Action

These semisynthetic penicillins are indicated for infection by β-lactamase-producing staphylococci, although
penicillin-susceptible strains of streptococci and pneumococci are also susceptible to these agents. Listeria
monocytogenes, Enterococci, and methicillin-resistant strains of Staphylococci are resistant.

Page 1 of 5



April 25, 2026, 2:16:26 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

IV INFUSION › 50 mg powder, vial (Rx)

Administer as IV infusion over 2 hours. Longer infusion time (6 hours) may mitigate acute toxicities (e.g.,
pharyngolaryngeal dysesthesia). Extend infusion time to 6 hours for acute toxicities. Do NOT use needles or IV
administration sets that contain aluminum during preparation or administration. Aluminum can react with
carboplatin resulting in precipitate formation and loss of potency. Avoid mucositis prophylaxis with ice chips to
prevent exacerbation of acute neurological symptoms associated with exposure to coldtemperatures, or
consumption of cold food or beverages during or within hours of administration. NOTE: Avoid extravasation.
Ensure proper needle or catheter position prior to and during administration. If extravasation occurs, discontinue
infusion immediately and apply ice to the affected area. Associated with a high emetic potential. Antiemetics are
recommended to prevent nausea and vomiting. Do NOT prepare using a chloride-containing solution such as NaCl.
Flush infusion line with D5W prior to, and following, administration of concomitant medications via same IV line.
When used in combination with a fluoropyrimidine (e.g., fluorouracil), infuse oxaliplatin first.

Dose Adjustment

Renal Impairment:
In CrCl <30 mL/minute, reduce dose to 65 mg/m2

Obesity:

Use actual body weight (full weight) for calculation of BSAbased or weight-based dosing, especially if the intent of
therapy is curative.
If dose reduction is done due to toxicity, consider resumption of full weight-based dosing with subsequent cycles,
especially if cause of toxicity is resolved.

Toxicity:
Neurosensory events:
For persistent grade 2 neurosensory events lasting >7 days, consider withholding oxaliplatin despite dose reduction:
In stage III colon cancer, reduce dose to 75 mg/m2. In advanced colorectal cancer, reduce dose to 65 mg/m2.
For persistent grade 3 neurosensory events lasting >7 days: In stage III colon cancer, discontinue use. In advanced
colorectal cancer, reduce dose to 65 mg/m2; if not resolved prior to the next cycle, discontinue use.
For persistent grade 4 neurosensory events: In advanced colorectal cancer, discontinue use.

Gastrointestinal toxicity (grade 3/4): In stage III colon cancer, delay next dose until recovery from toxicity, then
reduce dose to 75 mg/m2. In advanced colorectal cancer, delay next dose until recovery from toxicity, then reduce
dose to 65 mg/m2.

Hematologic toxicity (grade 3/4 neutropenia, grade 3/4 thrombocytopenia, or febrile neutropenia,): In stage III colon
cancer, delay next dose until neutrophils recover to =1,500/mm3 and platelets to =75,000/mm3, then reduce dose to
75 mg/m2. In advanced colorectal cancer, delay next dose until neutrophils recover to =1,500/mm3 and platelets to
=75,000/mm3, then reduce dose to 65 mg/m2.

April 25, 2026, 2:16:26 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Indications

Adjuvant treatment of stage III colon cancer after complete resectionof primary tumor; treatment of advanced
colorectal cancer

Contraindications

Pregnancy; breastfeeding; severe renal impairment

Precautions

WARNING: Anaphylactic-like reactions have been reported and may occur within minutes of administration.
Epinephrine, corticosteroids, and antihistamines have been employed to alleviate symptoms of anaphylaxis.

WARNING: Hazardous agent. Use appropriate
precautions for handling and disposal.

Hypersensitivity or anaphylactoid reactions (e.g. pruritus,bronchospasm, erythema, hypotension, rash, urticaria,

hypersensitivity)

Neuropathy (e.g. acute, reversible, peripheral sensory neuropathy which may interfere with daily activities (e.g.,
writing, buttoning, swallowing; cold-triggered neuropathy commonly recurs with subsequent doses and may last up
to 7 days after administration).

Hepatotoxicity (e.g. hepatitis and hepatic failure); Renal impairment

Pulmonary fibrosis (withhold treatment for unexplained pulmonary symptoms until interstitial lung disease or
pulmonary fibrosis are excluded)

Reversible posterior leukoencephalopathy syndrome (e.g. headache, mental status changes, seizure, blurred vision,
blindness, other vision changes)

Elderly (more sensitive to adverse events, particularly diarrhea, dehydration, hypokalemia, leukopenia, fatigue, and
syncope)

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Oxaliplatin: Trastuzumab (neutropenic effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Oxaliplatin: Denosumab (serious infections)
Reduces diagnostic effect of Coccidioides immitis Skin Test

Avoid concomitant use with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Oxaliplatin:
Roflumilast (immunosuppressive effect)
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Taxane Derivatives [e.g. Paclitaxel, Docetaxel
(myelosuppressive effect)], Tofacitinib (immunosuppressive effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Oxaliplatin: Dipyrone (agranulocytosis; pancytopenia), Pimecrolimus,
Tacrolimus (Topical)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
Clozapine (agranulocytosis), Leflunomide (hematologic toxicity such as pancytopenia, agranulocytosis, and/or
thrombocytopenia), Natalizumab (concurrent infection), Topotecan
Reduces therapeutic effect of Oxaliplatin: Echinacea
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: BCG (Intravesical), Vaccines (immune response; live virus
vaccines may progress to infection)

A platinum derivative that acts as an alkylating agent. It forms DNA cross links to inhibit DNA replication.

Indications

Management of partial-onset seizure in adults and children and partial seizure with secondary generalization.

Contraindications

No information found

Precautions

Hyponatremia; anaphylactic reactions and angioedema (eg., laryngeal edema can be fatal); serious dermatological
reactions (some fatal); bone metabolism.

Suicidal behavior and ideation; neuropsychiatric adverse events; DRESS or multi-organ hypersensitivity (some fatal);
hematologic events; glaucoma; hyponatremia.

HLA-B*1502 allele carrier (e.g., SJS or toxic epidermal necrolysis (SJS/TEN) during treatment).

Elderly (increased incidence of adverse effects).

Pregnancy (no adequate and well-controlled studies); lactation (excreted in breastmilk).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Dizziness, somnolence, diplopia, fatigue, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, abnormal vision, ataxia, tremor,
dyspepsia, abnormal gait, nystagmus, maculopapular rash, convulsions, rash, status epilepticus, fatigue, edema,
weight gain, asthenia, diarrhea, hypotension, hyponatremia, sprains, strains, muscle weakness, gastritis,
constipation, acne, headache, nervousness, agitation, dysmetria, abnormal coordination, speech disorder, rhinitis,
dry mouth, toothache, lymphadenopathy, rectal hemorrhage, infection, thirst, emotional lability, hypoesthesia,
pharyngitis, purpura, earache, ear infection, vaginitis, micturition frequency.

April 25, 2026, 2:15:33 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of CNS Depressants

Avoid concomitant use with:
Decreases serum concentration of Hormonal contraceptives
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: MAO Inhibitors (serotonin syndrome)
Reduces therapeutic effect of Hormonal contraceptives

Mechanism of Action

Oxcarbazepine is a prodrug related to carbamazepine. It works by binding to the open channel conformation of
voltage-sensitive sodium channels at a site within the channel itself at the a subunit.

ANATOMICAL

Nervous System

THERAPEUTIC

Analgesics

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Opioids

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › 5 mg capsule (Rx)

ORAL › 10 mg (as hydrochloride) tablet/prolonged-release or controlled release tablet (Rx)

ORAL › 20 mg (as hydrochloride) tablet/prolonged-release or controlled release tablet (Rx)

ORAL › 40 mg (as hydrochloride) tablet/prolonged-release or controlled release tablet (Rx)

ORAL › 80 mg (as hydrochloride) tablet/prolonged-release or controlled release tablet (Rx)

IV INFUSION › 10 mg/mL, 1 mL ampule (Rx)

IV INFUSION › 10 mg/mL, 2 mL ampule (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › 5 mg capsule (Rx)

Persistent moderate-to-severe acute and chronic pain, by mouth, ADULT, as immediate release form, initially 5–15
mg every 4–6 hours as needed with an average dosing range of 5–10 mg per dose; for severe chronic pain,
administer on a regularly scheduled basis, every 4–6 hours, at the lowest dose that will achieve adequate
analgesia; ADULT (opioid naïve), as controlled release form, initially 5–10 mg every 12 hours (maximum, 400 mg
daily); by IV injection, ADULT, 1–10 mg over 1–2 minutes, may repeat nor more often than every 4 hours; by IV
infusion, ADULT, initially 2 mg per hour, increase as needed. Patient-controlled analgesia (PCA), by IV infusion,
ADULT, 0.03mg/kg, administer with a minimum lock-out time of 5 minutes. NOTE: Oxycodone ER capsules are not
bioequivalent to ER tablets. Doses of ER capsules is expressed as oxycodone base. Doses of ER tablets is
expressed as oxycodone hydrochloride.

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:17:19 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

ORAL › 5 mg capsule (Rx)

For oral administration, may be taken with or without food. Administering with food may delay its onset. Swallow
tablet whole. Do NOT moisten, dissolve, cut, crush, break, or chew. Administer controlled release tablets one at a
time and each followed with water immediately after placing in the mouth. NOTE: Reserve use of 80 mg tablets for
opioid-tolerant patients only. See General Information on Natural Opium Alkaloids under Opioids (General
Information) forother information.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Initiate therapy at low end of dosing range and use caution.

Renal Impairment:
For CrCl <60 mL/minute, initiate at the low end of the dosing range and adjust dose as clinically indicated. Doses 33–
50% of usual initial dose have been recommended.

Hepatic Impairment:
Initiate at doses 33–50% of usual initial dose have been recommended. If reduced dose is less than smallest available
dosage form, consider alternative analgesic.

Indications

Persistent moderate-to-severe acute and chronic pain.

Contraindications

Significant respiratory depression; hypercarbia; acute or severe bronchial asthma in an unmonitored setting or in
the absence of resuscitative equipment; known or suspected GI obstruction, including paralytic ileus; suspected
surgical abdomen; any disease or condition that affects bowel transit; mild, intermittent, or short duration pain that
can be managed with other pain medications; acute pain (controlled release); chronic obstructive airway; status
asthmaticus; cor pulmonale; acute alcoholism; delirium tremens; convulsive disorders; severe CNS depression;
increased cerebrospinal or intracranial pressure; head injury; MAO inhibitors (concomitant use within 14 days of each
other); pregnancy; use during labor and delivery; breastfeeding.

Precautions

WARNING: Cytochrome P450 3A4 Interaction. The concomitant use of oxycodone with all CYP-450 3A4 inhibitors may
result in an increase in oxycodone plasma concentrations, which could increase or prolong adverse drug effects and
may cause potentially fatal respiratory depression. Discontinuation of a concomitantly used CYP3A4 inducer may
result in an increase in oxycodone plasma concentration.

Common: Physical dependence, tolerance, opioid-induced hyperalgesia, hypogonadism, opioid-induced
constipation, increased biliary tract pressures and/or spasm, respiratory depression (dose-related), delirium,
hypogonadism, urinary retention, edema, itching, sweating, injection site reaction, allergic reaction, nausea,
vomiting, drowsiness (dose-related), edema, flushing, hypertension, children and adolescents may experience the
following: tachycardia, headache, fatigue, lethargy, pain, decreased oxygen saturation, hypochloremia,
hyponatremia, weight loss, GERD, febrile neutropenia, limb pain and seroma. Orthostatic hypotension, drowsiness,
dizziness, abnormal dreams, abnormality in thinking, anxiety, confusion, drug withdrawal, dysphoria, euphoria, chills,
insomnia, irritability, nervousness, twitching, withdrawal syndrome, migraine, depression, hypoesthesia, agitation,
paresthesia pruritus, diaphoresis, excoriation, hyperhidrosis, skin rash, hyperglycemia, xerostomia, diarrhea,
anorexia, dyspepsia, abdominal pain, gastritis, hiccups, upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, dysuria, urinary
retention, constipation, weakness, arthralgia, back pain, musculoskeletal pain, myalgia, tremor, blurred vision,
cough, dyspnea, oropharyngeal pain, fever, cardiac failure, vasodilatation, neuralgia, personality disorder,
thrombophlebitis, skin photosensitivity, gout, gingivitis, glossitis, urinary tract infection, anemia, leukopenia,
hypersensitivity, infection, hemorrhage, arthritis, laryngospasm, neck pain, pathological fracture, bronchitis,
ostealgia, sepsis, epistaxis, respiratory depression, pharyngitis, pulmonary disease, rhinitis, sinusitis, accidental
injury.

Less Common: Abnormal gait, abnormal stools, aggressive behavior, amenorrhea, amnesia, anaphylactoid reaction,
anaphylaxis, chest pain, choking sensation; gag reflex and dysphagia (controlled release tablets), cholestasis, cough,
dehydration, dental caries, depersonalization, diverticulitis (exacerbation), drug abuse, disturbed sleep, drug
dependence, dysgeusia, emotional lability, exfoliative dermatitis, flatulence, eructation, hallucination, hematuria,
histamine release, hyperkinesia, hypertonia, hypogonadism, increased appetite, hyperalgesia, hyponatremia,
hypotonia, impotence, rase, increased heart rate, increased intracranial pressure, increased thirst, intestinal
obstruction, lymphadenopathy, memory impairment, mood changes, malaise, neonatal withdrawal, night sweats,
palpitations, pharyngeal edema, polyuria, restlessness, seizure, SIADH, speech disturbance, stomatitis, stupor,
suicidal ideation, suicidal tendencies, syncope, tinnitus, urticaria, vasodilatation, vertigo, visual disturbance, voice
disorder, xeroderma.

Mechanism of Action

Oxycodone binds to opiate receptors in the CNS, causing inhibition of ascending pain pathways, altering the
perception of and response to pain and produces generalized CNS depression.

Dosage

TOPIC › 0.05% nasal spray (as hydrochloride), 10 mL bottle (OTC)

Nasal congestion, into the nostril, ADULT and CHILD ≥6 years, spray 0.05% solution 1–3 times into each nostril
twice daily.

Administration

TOPIC › 0.05% nasal spray (as hydrochloride), 10 mL bottle (OTC)

Do NOT ingest. Keep out of reach of children. Shake well before use. If using for the first time, remove the
protective cap from the tip and prime metered pump by depressing the pump firmly several times. To spray, hold
bottle with thumb at base and nozzle between first and second fingers. Without tilting your head, insert the nozzle
into nostril. Fully depress rim with a firm, even stroke and sniff deeply. Wipe nozzle clean after use. Administer
each dose 10–12 hours apart. Do NOT exceed 2 doses in any 24-hour period. For children under 6 years of age, ask a
doctor prior to administration. Use only as directed. Do NOT use more than directed. Do NOT use for more than 3
days. Frequent or prolonged use may cause nasal congestion to recur or worsen. Do not share containers with
other persons to prevent the spread of infection

No information found

Precautions

reducing edema of the nasal mucosa.

Drug Classification

THERAPEUTIC

Pituitary and Hypothalamic Hormones and Analogues

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Posterior Pituitary Lobe Hormones

Oxytocin and Analogues

IM › IV › 5 IU/mL ampule, 1 mL ampule (Rx)

IM › IV › 10 IU/mL ampule, 1 mL ampule (Rx)

Dosage

IM / IV › 5 IU/mL ampule, 1 mL ampule (Rx)

Induction of labor, by IV infusion, ADULT and ADOLESCENT, initially 0.0005–0.001 IU/minute increased in 0.001–
0.002 IU/minute increments at intervals of 30 minutes until up to 3–4 contractions occur every 10 minutes
(maximum rate, 0.02 IU/minute). NOTE: Careful monitoring of fetal heart rate and uterine motility is essential for
dose titration. Discontinue immediately in uterine hyperactivity or fetal distress. Prevention of postpartum
hemorrhage, by IM injection, ADULT and ADOLESCENT, 10 IU when the anterior shoulder is delivered or
immediately after birth. Prevention of postpartum hemorrhage, by slow IM injection, ADULT and ADOLESCENT, 5 IU
when the anterior shoulder is delivered or immediately after birth. Treatment of postpartum hemorrhage, by slow
IV injection, ADULT, 10-40 IU in 1000 mL of IV fluid at a rate sufficient to control uterine atony; ADOLESCENT, 5-10
IU; by IM injection, ADULT and ADOLESCENT, 10 IU, followed in severe cases by a total of 40 IU, by intravenous
infusion, at a rate of 0.02-0.04 IU/minute; this should be started after the placenta is delivered.

Administration

IM / IV › 5 IU/mL ampule, 1 mL ampule (Rx)

For IV bolus injection, administer slowly. Avoid rapid injection due to risk of cardiovascular effects. Avoid bolus IV
injection during labor. For IV infusion, monitor fluid balance to prevent water intoxication. Avoid large volume
infusions and restrict fluid intake to prevent water intoxication and hyponatremia.

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:18:05 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Renal Impairment:
Use with caution (increased risk of water retention and oxytocin accumulation).

Indications

Prevention and treatment of postpartum and post-abortion hemorrhage; induction and augmentation of labor

Contraindications

Hypertonic uterine contractions; mechanical obstruction to delivery; fetal distress; any condition where
spontaneous labor or vaginal delivery is inadvisable; major cephalopelvic disproportion

Precautions

Oxytocin-resistant uterine inertia (avoid prolonged administration);
Severe pre-eclamptic toxemia;
Severe cardiovascular disease;
Previous uterine surgery, multiple pregnancy, or >4 previous births;
Induction or enhancement of labor in the presence of borderline cephalopelvic disproportion (avoid use if
significant);
Mild-to-moderate pregnancy-associated hypertension or cardiovascular disease;
Age >35 years;
History of low-uterine segment caesarean section;
Caudal block anesthesia;
Avoid tumultuous labor if fetal death or meconium-stained amniotic fluid

Adverse Drug Reactions

Less Common: Nausea, vomiting
Rare: Anaphylactoid reaction, arrhythmias, ECG changes, flushing, prolonged QT interval, rash, reflex tachycardia,
severe (tetanic) uterine spasm and contraction leading to uterine rupture or fetal distress, hypoxia and death,
seizures, transient hypotension, water intoxication

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:18:05 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs due to enhanced vasopressor effect:
Ephedrine, Epinephrine

Mechanism of Action

A synthetic nonapeptide identical with oxytocin, a peptide hormone, which participates in labor or delivery and
stimulates uterine contraction.

mg/m2 over 3 hours every 3 weeks; 80 mg/m2/IV Day 1 weekly. Non-small cell lung cancer, by IV infusion, ADULT, in
combination with cisplatin, 135 mg/m2 over 24 hours every 3 weeks. Ovarian cancer, advanced, previously treated,
by IV infusion, ADULT, 135 or 175 mg/m2 over 3 hours every 3 weeks. Ovarian cancer, advanced, previously
untreated, by IV infusion, ADULT, in combination with cisplatin, 175 mg/m2 over 3 hours every 3 weeks; or 135
mg/m2 over 24 hours administered every 3 weeks. Kaposi’s sarcoma, AIDS related, by IV infusion, ADULT, 135
mg/m2 over 3 hours every 3 weeks or 100 mg/m2 over 3 hours every 2 weeks (due to dose-related toxicity; use 100
mg/m2 dose only for patients with lower performance status).

Page 1 of 5



April 25, 2026, 2:18:18 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

IV / IV INFUSION › 6 mg/mL, 5 mL vial (Rx)

Administer by IV infusion over 3 or 24 hours, depending on indication or protocol, through a 0.22micronin-linefilter
and polyethylene-lined (non-PVC) administration set. Pre-medicate with corticosteroids, diphenhydramine, and H2
antagonists prior to infusion. NOTE: Pre-medication with dexamethasone (20 mg orally or IV at 12 and 6 hours
before the dose; reduce to 10 mg with advanced HIV disease), diphenhydramine (50 mg IV 30 to 60 minutes prior to
the dose), and cimetidine 300 mg, famotidine 20 mg, or ranitidine 50 mg (IV 30 to 60 minutes prior to the dose) is
recommended. Irritant with vesicant-like properties. Avoid extravasation. Ensure proper needle or catheter
position prior to administration.

Dose Adjustment

Hepatic Impairment:
NOTE: Recommendations are for the first course of therapy where the usual dose is 135 mg/m2 (over 24 hours) or 175
mg/m2 (over 3 hours). Dosing in subsequent courses should be based upon individual tolerance.

24-hour infusion:
If transaminases <2 times upper limit of normal (ULN) and bilirubin level =1.5 mg/dL, administer 135 mg/m2.
If transaminases 2 to <10 times ULN and bilirubin level =1.5 mg/dL, administer 100 mg/m2.
If transaminases <10 times ULN and bilirubin level 1.6 to 7.5 mg/dL, administer 50 mg/m2.
If transaminases =10 times ULN or bilirubin level >7.5 mg/dL, avoid use.

3-hour infusion:
If transaminases <10 times ULN and bilirubin level =1.25 times ULN, administer 175 mg/m2.
If transaminases <10 times ULN and bilirubin level 1.26 to 2 times ULN, administer 135 mg/m2.
If transaminases <10 times ULN and bilirubin level 2.01 to 5 times ULN, administer 90 mg/m2.
If transaminases =10 times ULN or bilirubin level >5 times ULN, avoid use.

Obesity:
Use actual body weight (full weight) for calculation of BSAbased or weight-based dosing, especially if the intent of
therapy is curative.
If dose reduction is done due to toxicity, consider resumption of full weight-based dosing with subsequent cycles,
especially if cause of toxicity is resolved.

Immunosuppression (in advanced HIV) or Toxicity:
NOTE: Reduce the dexamethasone premedication dose to 10 mg and initiate G-CSF treatment as clinically indicated.
For severe peripheral neuropathy or severe neutropenia (neutrophil <500/mm3for =7 days), reduce dose by 20%.
For neutrophil <1000 cells/mm3 or platelet <100,000/mm3, do not administer paclitaxel.
