April 25, 2026, 2:18:18 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Indications

Adjuvant treatment of node-positive breast cancer; treatment of metastatic breast cancer after failure of
combination chemotherapy or relapse within 6 months of adjuvant chemotherapy (prior therapy should have
included an anthracycline); second-line treatment of AIDS-related Kaposi’s sarcoma (KS); first-line treatment of non-
small cell lung cancer (in combination with cisplatin) in patients who are notcandidates for potentially curative
surgery and/or radiation therapy; subsequent therapy for treatment of advanced ovarian cancer; first-linetherapy of
ovarian cancer (in combination with cisplatin)

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to paclitaxel, polyoxyl 35 or polyoxyethylated castor oil, or any component of the formulation;
treatment of solid tumors in patients with baseline neutrophil counts <1,500/mm3; treatment of Kaposi’s sarcoma
inpatients with baseline neutrophil counts <1,000/mm3

Precautions

WARNING: Should be administered under the supervision of an experienced cancer chemotherapy physician,
experienced in the use of cancerchemotherapeutic agents. Administer in a facility sufficient to appropriately
diagnose and manage complications.

Severe bone marrow suppression, primarily neutropenia, with resulting infection or bleeding, may occur. Do NOT
administer if baseline neutrophil count is <1,500/mm3 for solid tumors or <1,000/mm3 for AIDS-related Kaposi’s
sarcoma.

Anaphylaxis and severe hypersensitivity reactions have occurred in 2–4% of patients in clinical studies. Some
reactions have been fatal. If severe hypersensitivity occurs, STOP INFUSION AND DO NOT RECHALLENGE.

WARNING: Hazardous agent. Use appropriate precautions for handling and disposal.

Bone marrow suppression (usually in the form of neutropenia; dose-dependent; dose-limiting toxicity)

Cardiovascular effects (e.g. infusion-related hypotension, bradycardia, and/or hypertension; rare but severe
conduction abnormalities).

Injection-site reactions (e.g. skin discoloration, tenderness, erythema, or swelling; more severe reactions, e.g.
phlebitis, cellulitis, skin exfoliation, necrosis, fibrosis, induration; skin reactions may occur despite administering
through a different IV site);

Hypersensitivity reactions (e.g. flushing, skin reactions, dyspnea, hypotension, or tachycardia);

Peripheral neuropathy (commonly occurs; patients with preexisting neuropathies may be at a higher risk).

Elderly (at an increased risk of toxicity, including severe neutropenia, neuropathy, and cardiovascular events).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Abdominal pain (with intraperitoneal paclitaxel), increased alkaline phosphatase, alopecia, anemia,
arthralgia or myalgia, bleeding, bradycardia, diarrhea, dyspnea, abnormal ECG, edema, febrile neutropenia, flushing,
hypersensitivity reaction, hypertension, hypotension, infection, injection site reaction (erythema, tenderness, skin
discoloration, swelling), leukopenia, mucositis, nail changes, nausea, vomiting, neutropenia, peripheral neuropathy,
rhythm abnormalities, rash, stomatitis (at doses >390 mg/m2), syncope, thrombocytopenia, tachycardia, weakness,
venous thrombosis

Less Common: Anaphylaxis, arrhythmia, ataxia, atrial fibrillation, AV block, back pain, cardiac conduction
abnormalities, cellulitis, CHF, chills, conjunctivitis, enterocolitis, extravasation recall, dehydration, hepatic
encephalopathy, hepaticnecrosis, induration, intestinal obstruction, intestinal perforation, interstitial pneumonia,
increased lacrimation, maculopapular rash, ischemic colitis, malaise, MI, myocardial ischemia, necrotic changes and
ulceration following extravasation, neuroencephalopathy, neutropenic enterocolitis, neutropenic typhlitis,
ototoxicity (tinnitus; hearing loss), pancreatitis, paralytic ileus, phlebitis, pneumonitis, pruritus, pulmonary embolism,
pulmonary fibrosis, radiation recall, radiation pneumonitis, renal insufficiency, seizure, skin edema (diffuse), skin
exfoliation, scleroderma exacerbation, skin fibrosis, skin necrosis, skin sclerosis,skin thickening, SJS, TEN
ventricular tachycardia (asymptomatic), supraventricular tachycardia, visual disturbances (scintillating scotomata)

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Paclitaxel: Denosumab (serious infections)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Vinorelbine (neurotoxic effect)
Reduces diagnostic effect of Coccidioides immitis Skin Test

Avoid concomitant use with:
Decreases metabolism of Doxorubicin (Conventional)
Decreases serum concentration of Paclitaxel: Dabrafenib, Mitotane
Decreases serum concentration of Aripiprazole
Enhances therapeutic effect of Paclitaxel: Platinum Derivatives [e.g., Cisplatin, Carboplatin, Oxaliplatin
(myelosuppressive effect)], Roflumilast (immunosuppressive effect)
Enhances therapeutic effect of Tofacitinib (immunosuppressive effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Paclitaxel: Dipyrone (agranulocytosis, pancytopenia), Pimecrolimus,
Sorafenib, Tacrolimus (Topical)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Antineoplastic Agents [Anthracycline, Systemic
(e.g., Doxorubicin, Epirubicin, Idarubicin)], Clozapine (agranulocytosis), Leflunomide (hematologic toxicity such as
pancytopenia, agranulocytosis, and/or thrombocytopenia), Natalizumab (concurrent infection)
Increases serum concentration of Paclitaxel: Atazanavir, Fusidic Acid (Systemic), Mifepristone
Reduces therapeutic effect of Paclitaxel: Echinacea
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: BCG (Intravesical), Vaccines (live virus vaccines may progress to
infection)

Mechanism of Action

A taxane derivative that stabilizes microtubule formation which interferes with the late G2 phase of cell division.

Dosage

IM › 50 mg/0.5 mL prolonged-release suspension for injection (Rx)

Maintaining stability in schizophrenia, by intramuscular injection, ADULT, stabilize service user/patient on oral
paliperidone. Initially, 150 mg injected into the deltoid muscle, followed after 1 week (±4 days) by 100 mg injected
into the deltoid muscle. Monthly maintenance doses (±7 days) may be administered after the second loading dose
in the gluteal muscle or the deltoid muscle and dosed based on the oral dose the service user/patient is stabilized
on: 12 mg by mouth once daily = 150 mg by intramuscular injection once a month 9 mg by mouth once daily = 100 mg
by intramuscular injection once a month 6 mg by mouth once daily = 75 mg by intramuscular injection once a month
3 mg by mouth once daily = 25-50 mg by intramuscular injection once a month. Schizoaffective disorder, by
intramuscular injection, ADULT, stabilize service user/patient on oral paliperidone. Initially, 150 mg injected into
the deltoid muscle, followed after 1 week (±4 days) by 100 mg injected into the deltoid muscle. Monthly
maintenance doses (±7 days) ranging from 25-150 mg may be administered after the second loading dose in the
gluteal muscle or the deltoid muscle and dosed based on the oral dose the service user/patient is stabilized on: 12
mg by mouth once daily = 150 mg by intramuscular injection once a month 9 mg by mouth once daily = 100 mg by
intramuscular injection once a month 6 mg by mouth once daily = 75 mg by intramuscular injection once a month 3
mg by mouth once daily = 25-50 mg by intramuscular injection once a month. Schizophrenia, by intramuscular
injection, ADULT/CHILD=12 years, stabilize service user/patient on oral paliperidone. Initially, 150 mg injected into
the deltoid muscle, followed after 1 week (±4 days) by 100 mg injected into the deltoid muscle. Monthly
maintenance doses (±7 days) may be administered after the second loading dose in the gluteal muscle or the
deltoid muscle and dosed based on the oral dose the service user/patient is stabilized on: 12 mg by mouth once
daily = 150 mg by intramuscular injection once a month 9 mg by mouth once daily = 100 mg by intramuscular
injection once a month 6 mg by mouth once daily = 75 mg by intramuscular injection once a month 3 mg by mouth
once daily = 25-50 mg by intramuscular injection once a month. Missed Doses – Second Initiation Dose <4 weeks
since first injection: Administer second dose of 100 mg by intramuscular injection into the deltoid muscle as soon
as possible, followed by a third dose of 75 mg by intramuscular injection into the deltoid or gluteal muscle 5 weeks
after the first injection (regardless of timing of second dose) 4-7 weeks since first injection: Administer second
dose of 100 mg by intramuscular injection into the deltoid muscle as soon as possible, followed by another 100 mg
by intramuscular injection into the deltoid muscle one week after the second dose. Resume regular monthly
dosing after. >7 weeks since first injection: Restart with recommended initiation dosing schedule. Missed Doses –
Maintenance Dose 4-6 weeks since last dose: Resume previous schedule and dose as soon as possible. >6 weeks
and ≤6 months since last injection: If maintenance dose <150 mg, administer the dose by intramuscular injection
into the deltoid muscle as soon as possible and administer a second previously stabilized dose after 1 week If
maintenance dose is 150 mg, administer a 100 mg dose by intramuscular injection into the deltoid muscle as soon
as possible and administer a second 100 mg dose after 1 week Continue with the previously stabilized dose 1 week
after the second injection. NOTE: Antipsychotics should not be discontinued abruptly because of the possibility of
withdrawal and rebound psychosis; discontinuation or cross-titration should be done gradually to minimize the
potential of withdrawal, rebound psychosis and extrapyramidal side effects, cholinergic rebound, rebound
insomnia, and unwanted pregnancy, unless safety concerns require a more rapid withdrawal.

Administration

IM › 50 mg/0.5 mL prolonged-release suspension for injection (Rx)

Administer by IM route only as a single injection (do not divide); do not administer by any other route. Avoid
inadvertent injection into vasculature. Do not mix with any other product or diluent. Prior to injection, shake
syringe for at least 10 seconds to ensure a homogenous suspension. Administer using only the needles that are
provided in the kit. The 2 initial injections should be administered in the deltoid muscle using a 11/2 inch, 22-gauge
needle for service patients =90 kg, and a 1 inch, 23-gauge needle for service patients <90 kg. The 2 initial deltoid
intramuscular injections help attain therapeutic concentrations rapidly. Alternate deltoid injections (right and left
deltoid muscle). The second dose may be administered 4 days before or after the weekly time point. Monthly
maintenance doses can be administered in either the deltoid or gluteal muscle. Administer injections in the
gluteal muscle using a 11/2 inch, 22-gauge needle (regardless of service user/patient weight) in the upperouter
quadrant of the gluteal area. Alternate gluteal injections (right and left gluteal muscle). The monthly maintenance
dose may be administered 7 days before or after the monthly time point.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Some patients may tolerate lower doses better; antipsychotics are considered to be potentially inappropriate in in
the elderly. and to be avoided in service patients 65 years and older with dementia due to an increased risk of
mortality, cerebrovascular accidents (stroke), a greater rate of cognitive decline with use, and potential to cause or
exacerbate or hyponatremia unless all nonpharmacologic options have failed or are not possible or the service
user/patient is threatening substantial harm toself or to others. It may be appropriate for use in schizophrenia.

Renal Impairment:
For mild renal impairment (CrCl=50 mL/min and <80 mL/min), reduce initiation doses to 100 mg by intramuscular
injection on day 1 and 75 mg by intramuscular injection after 1 week. Administer a maintenance dose of 50 mg by
intramuscular injection once a month.
Not recommended for use in service patients with moderate to severe renal impairment (CrCl<50 mL/min).

Hepatic Impairment:
No dose adjustment necessary in mild to moderate hepatic impairment. Not studied in severe hepatic impairment.

Indications

Maintenance in schizophrenia; maintaining stability in schizophrenia; schizoaffective disorder;

Page 3 of 5



April 25, 2026, 2:18:29 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

History of QTc prolongation or cardiac arrhythmia; recent acute myocardial infarction; uncompensated heart failure;
preexisting severe GI narrowing.

Precautions

WARNING: NOT approved for treatment of dementiarelated psychosis (increased risk of death)

STORAGE AND STABILITY: Store between 20-25°C. Excursions permitted to 15-30°C. Protect from moisture.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Dose-dependent extrapyramidal symptoms, hyperprolactinemia, anxiety, appetite changes, arthralgia,
depression, epistaxis, hypertension, infection, malaise, myalgia, edema, respiratory disorders, sleep disorders,
toothache, urinary disorders; may increase risk for diabetes and dyslipidemia.

Less Common: Alopecia, hypoesthesia, paresthesia, taste disturbances, tinnitus, visual disorders.

Rare: Inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretionobstruction, intra-operative floppy iris syndrome, pancreatitis,
pulmonary embolism, rhabdomyolysis.

NOTE: Patients taking this drug may experience weight gain and/or sedation.

Page 4 of 5



April 25, 2026, 2:18:29 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Reduces the therapeutic effects of the following drugs: Amphetamines, Antidiabetics, Dopamine agonists,
Levodopa
Enhances the therapeutic effects of CNS depressants e.g. Alcohol
Increases risk adverse/toxic effect of Paliperidone: Metoclopramide
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Iohexol, Iomeprol, Iopamidol (seizures) QTc-
prolonging agents, Methylphenidate Mequitazine (arrhythmogenic effect) Serotonin modulators (neuroleptic
malignant syndrome; serotonin syndrome) SSRIs (psychomotor impairment) Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors
(neurotoxic effect; severe extrapyramidal symptoms)

Avoid concomitant use with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Other Antipsychotics (increased hospitalization
rates and length of stay; increased mortality; QTc prolongation; torsades de pointes)

Mechanism of Action

Paliperidone is a benzisoxazole atypical antipsychotic, and a metabolite of risperidone. It is an antagonist at
dopamine (D2), serotonin (5-HT2A), adrenergic (a1 and a2), and histamine (H1) receptors. Paliperidone is not
hepatically metabolized and is eliminated via the urine, giving it less drug interactions.

Dose Adjustment

Renal Impairment:
For patients with CrCl of 10–50 mL/minute or patients on CRRT, administer 50% of normal dose. Avoid use in patients
with CrCl <10 mL/minute, or patients on hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis.

Hepatic Impairment:
Large doses may be required and problems may arise in antagonism.

Obesity:
Use ideal body weight for obese patients.

Indications

Facilitation of endotracheal intubation and relaxation of skeletal muscles during surgery; facilitation of mechanical
ventilation in ICU patients

Page 2 of 4



April 25, 2026, 2:18:44 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to pancuronium, bromide, or any component of the formulation

Precautions

WARNING: Should be administered by adequately trained individuals familiar with its use.

Cross-sensitivity (use extreme caution in patients with previous anaphylactic reactions);

Burn injury (resistance may occur if burns =20% of total body surface area);

Conditions which may antagonize neuromuscular blockade (e.g., respiratory alkalosis, hypercalcemia, demyelinating
lesions, peripheral neuropathies, denervation, and muscle trauma may occur);

Conditions which may potentiate neuromuscular blockade (e.g., electrolyte abnormalities, neuromuscular diseases,
metabolic acidosis, metabolic alkalosis, respiratory acidosis, Lambert-Eaton syndrome and myasthenia gravis may
occur).

Hepatic and renal impairment (use with caution);

Elderly;

Immobilized patients (resistance may occur).

Some dosage forms may contain benzyl alcohol (amounts =99 mg/kg per day of benzyl alcohol have been associated
with a potentially fatal toxicity called “gasping syndrome” in neonates; see manufacturer’s labeling for contents prior
to administration in neonates);

Pregnancy (may be used short-term in cesarean section; reduced doses recommended in patients also receiving
magnesium sulfate due to enhanced effects).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common and Less Common: Elevated blood pressure and cardiac output, tachycardia, edema, skin flushing,
circulatory collapse, rash, itching, erythema, burning sensation along the vein, excessive salivation, profound muscle
weakness, wheezing, bronchospasm, hypersensitivity reaction, acute quadriplegic myopathy syndrome (prolonged
use), anaphylactoid reactions, anaphylaxis, myositis ossificans (prolonged use)

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Pancuronium: Aminoglycosides (respiratory depressant effect), Calcium Channel

(Systemic), Inhalational Anesthetics, Lincosamide, Lithium, Loop Diuretics e.g., Furosemide, Magnesium Salts,
Minocycline, Procainamide, Quinidine, Spironolactone, Tetracycline Derivatives, Vancomycin
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Pancuronium: Ketorolac (apnea)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Cardiac Glycosides e.g., Digoxin (arrhythmogenic
effect)
Reduces therapeutic effect of Pancuronium: Acetylcholinesterase Inhibitors e.g., Neostigmine, Fosphenytoin-
Phenytoin, Loop Diuretics

Avoid concomitant use with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Pancuronium: Colistimethate, Polymyxin B, Quinine
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Pancuronium: Theophylline Derivatives e.g., Aminophylline
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Corticosteroids (Systemic) (increased muscle
weakness; polyneuropathies; myopathies)
Reduces therapeutic effect of Pancuronium: Theophylline Derivatives e.g., Aminophylline

Mechanism of Action

A non-depolarizing, neuromuscular-blocking agent, that induces muscle relaxation by restricting neural
transmission at the myoneural junction after binding to cholinergic receptor sites.

Administration

ORAL › 300 mg tablet (OTC)

For oral administration, may be taken with or without food. Taking with food may decrease GI upset. When
administering suspensions, shake well before use. For rectal administration, remove the wrapper and insert the
suppository well up into the rectum. For IV administration, administer by IV infusion only. Administer undiluted
over 15 minutes. Use within 6 hours of opening vial or transferring to another container. For doses <1,000 mg (<50
kg), withdraw the appropriate dose from the vial and place in a separate empty, sterile container prior to
administration. Small volume pediatric doses (up to 600 mg [60 mL]) may be placed in a syringe and infused over 15
minutes via syringe pump. For 1,000 mg doses (=50 kg), insert a vented IV set through vial stopper.

Dose Adjustment

Renal Impairment:
Longer dosing intervals and reduce total doses. Avoid prolonged or repeated use.

Hepatic Impairment:
Use with caution and avoid prolonged or repeated use in any type of liver disease. Contraindicated in severe active
liver disease

Indications

Management of mild-moderate pain, fever.

Contraindications

Severe hepatic impairment or severe active liver disease.

Precautions

WARNING: Massive overdose may cause hepatic necrosis and, less frequently, renal damage. Less overdoses can
frequently cause reversible jaundice.

Has been associated with cases of acute liver failure, at times resulting in liver transplant and death. Most are
associated with the doses that exceed the maximum daily limits, and often involve more than 1 paracetamol-
containing product.

Sodium restriction (soluble paracetamol products may contain large amounts of sodium);

Phenylketonuria (soluble products of paracetamol may contain aspartame);

Alcohol dependence; overdosage;

G6PD deficiency;

Hepatic impairment and renal impairment; (dose-related toxicity;

Avoid large doses, e.g., >4 g daily);

Pregnancy (not known to be harmful);

Lactation (small amount is present in milk; short courses are safe in usual dosage; monitor infant).

Adverse Drug Reactions

[Oral, Rectal]
Common and Less Common: Skin rash, nephrotoxicity (chronic overdose), anemia, hyperuricemia, leukopenia,
neutropenia, renal disease, pancytopenia

Rare: Hypersensitivity reaction

[Inj.]
Common: Hypertension, hypotension, peripheral edema, tachycardia, headache, insomnia, agitation, anxiety, fatigue,
trismus, pruritus, skin rash, hypervolemia, hypoalbuminemia, hypokalemia, hypophosphatemia, nausea, vomiting,
hypomagnesemia, constipation, abdominal pain, diarrhea, oliguria, anemia, infusion site reaction, limb pain, muscle
spasm, periorbital edema, atelectasis, abnormal breath sounds, dyspnea, hypoxia, pleural effusion, pulmonary
edema, stridor, wheezing, fever

Less Common: Anaphylaxis, hepatic injury (dose-related), hypersensitivity reaction, severe dermatological reaction
(acute generalized exanthematous pustulosis, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, toxic epidermal necrolysis)

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Vitamin K Antagonists, e.g., Warfarin (anticoagulant effect)
Increases metabolism of Paracetamol: Barbiturates e.g. Phenobarbital; Carbamazepine
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Paracetamol: Barbiturates; Carbamazepine; Ethyl alcohol (liver damage);
Isoniazid
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Dapsone, Topical; Fosphenytoin-Phenytoin;
Imatinib; Sorafenib (hepatotoxic effect); Nitric Oxide, Prilocaine, Sodium nitrite (significant methemoglobinemia)
Reduces therapeutic effect of Paracetamol: Barbiturates, Carbamazepine

Avoid concomitant use with:
Decreases absorption of Paracetamol: Cholestyramine Resin [administer 1 hour apart to minimize this effect]
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Dasatinib (hepatotoxic effect)
Increases serum concentration of Paracetamol: Dasatinib; Probenecid (increases the potential for formation of the
toxic NAPQI metabolite); Sorafenib

TEST INTERACTION. May cause false-positive urinary 5hydroxyindoleacetic acid.

Mechanism of Action

Inhibits COX-1 and COX-2 and has no significant antiinflammatory activity and is particularly useful for treating mild-
to-moderate pain, for reducing fever, and in patients where salicylates and other NSAIDs are contraindicated.

Dose Adjustment

Renal Impairment:
For patients with CrCl <30 mL/minute, 135 micrograms once weekly and monitor for toxicity.
For patients with end-stage renal disease (ESRD) requiring hemodialysis, 135 mcg once weekly and monitor for
toxicity. If severe adverse reactions or laboratory abnormalities occur, reduce dose to 90 micrograms once weekly
until adverse reactions resolve. If intolerance persists after dose adjustment, discontinue.

Hepatic Impairment:
For HCV or HBV in adult patients with ALT progressively rising above baseline, decrease dose to 135 micrograms once
weekly or temporarily discontinue. Monitor transaminases more frequently. If ALT continues to rise despite dose
reduction or ALT increase is accompanied by increased bilirubin or hepatic decompensation, discontinue therapy
immediately. Resume therapy after ALT flare subsides.
For HCV in children with ALT =5 but <10 x ULN, decrease interferon dose to 135 micrograms per 1.73 m2 x BSA once
weekly. Monitor weekly. Further modify dose if needed until ALT stabilizes or decreases. For persistent ALT =10 x
ULN, discontinue treatment.

Toxicity:
For HCV in adolescents and children =5 years with moderate-to severe adverse reactions, initially decrease to 135
micrograms/1.73 m2 x BSA once weekly. Further reduce dose to 90 micrograms/1.73 m2 x BSA once weekly; or 45
micrograms/1.73 m2 x BSA once weekly if reaction persists or recurs.

Based on hematologic parameters:
If ANC 750–999/mm3, week 1 to 2, 135 micrograms per 1.73 m2 x BSA once weekly; weeks 3 to 48, no modification.
If ANC 500–749/mm3, week 1 to 2, delay or hold dose until ANC >750/mm3 then resume dose with 135 micrograms/1.73
m2 x BSA once weekly. Assess WBC weekly for 3 weeks to verify ANC >750/mm3; weeks 3 to 48, 135 micrograms per
1.73 m2 x BSA once weekly.
If ANC 250 to 499/mm3, week 1 to 2, delay or hold dose until ANC >750/mm3 then resume dose with 90
micrograms/1.73 m2 x BSA once weekly; weeks 3 to 48, delay or hold dose until ANC >750/mm3 then resume dose
with 135 micrograms/1.73 m2 x BSA once weekly.
If ANC <250/mm3 or in febrile neutropenia, discontinue treatment.
If platelet count <50,000/mm3, 90 micrograms/1.73 m2 x BSA once weekly.

For HCV / HBV in adults with moderate-to-severe adverse reactions, decrease to 135 micrograms weekly for initial
dose reduction. Further dose reductions to 90 micrograms weekly may be necessary in some cases if reaction
persists or recurs.

Based on hematologic parameters:
If ANC <750/mm3, 135 micrograms once weekly.
If ANC <500/mm3, suspend therapy until ANC >1,000/mm3, then restart at 90 micrograms once weekly.
If platelet count <50,000/mm3, 90 micrograms once weekly.
If platelet count <25,000/mm3, discontinue therapy.

Management of HBeAg positive and negative in adults, chronic hepatitis B virus (HBV) infection who have

compensated liver disease; management of chronic hepatitis C in compensated liver disease, in patients with

Contraindications

Autoimmune hepatitis; hepatic decompensation in cirrhotic patients (Child-Pugh score >6, class B and C) before
treatment; hepatic decompensation with Child-Pugh score =6 in cirrhotic CHC coinfected with HIV before treatment;
neonates and infants (due to benzyl alcohol component); pregnancy; men whose female partners are pregnant

Precautions

WARNING: Peginterferon alfa-2a may cause or aggravate fatal or life-threatening neuropsychiatric, autoimmune,
ischemic, and infectious disorders. Withdraw therapy in patients with persistently severe or worsening signs or
symptoms of these conditions. Use with caution in the elderly. Certain adverse effects (e.g., neuropsychiatric,
cardiac, flu-like reactions) may be more severe.

Autoimmune disease (e.g. thyroiditis, thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura, idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura,
rheumatoid arthritis, interstitial nephritis, systemic lupus erythematosus, myositis, hepatitis, and psoriasis)

Hypersensitivity reactions

Bone marrow suppression (e.g. severe cytopenias and aplastic anemia)

Infectious disorders (interferon therapy is commonly associated with flu-like symptoms, including fever)

Cardiovascular disease (hypertension, chest pain, MI, and supraventricular arrhythmias)

Pulmonary effects (may cause or aggravate dyspnea, pulmonary infiltrates, pneumonia, bronchiolitis obliterans,
interstitial pneumonitis, pulmonary hypertension, and sarcoidosis, which may result in potentially fatal respiratory
failure)

CNS depression

Ischemic disorders (reported in patients without risk factors for stroke)

Neuropsychiatric disorders (e.g. depression, suicidal ideation, suicide attempt, homicidal ideation, drug overdose,
and relapse of drug addiction)

Dermatologic effects (e.g. serious cutaneous reactions, including vesiculobullous eruptions, SJS, and exfoliative
dermatitis)

Diabetes (may require adjustments in antidiabetic medications)

Growth impairment (height and weight may be decreased in pediatric use; closely monitor growth in children during
and after therapy until growth catch-up has occurred)

Thyroid disorders

Gastrointestinal effects (e.g. GI hemorrhage, ulcerative and
hemorrhagic/ischemic colitis; may be severe and/or life threatening;Pancreatitis

Hepatic effects (monitor hepatic function closely during use); Hepatitis B (transient and potentially severe increases

Drug Interactions

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Ribavirin (hemolytic anemia), Zidovudine

Mechanism of Action

An alpha interferon subtype, which binds to a cell wall receptor to initiate intracellular activity resulting in induction
of gene transcription, cellular growth inhibition, alteration of the state of cellular differentiation, interference with
oncogene expression, alteration of cell surface antigen expression, increase in phagocytic activity of macrophages,
and augmentation of cytotoxicity of lymphocytes for target cells.

Mechanism of Action

This class of antibiotics binds to penicillin binding proteins that catalyzes the synthesis of peptidoglycan and this
leads to the interruption of cell wall synthesis, leading to bacterial cell growth inhibition and cell lysis

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Refer to adult dosing.

Renal Impairment:
ClCr 10-50mL/minute: Administer every 8-12 hours.

Indications

Treatment of acute rheumatic fever, acute gingivitis, acute necrotizing ulcerative gingivitis, pericoronitis,
pharyngitis, erisipelas and tonsillitis.

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to penicillin or any component of the formulation.

Precautions

WARNING: Serious and occasionally severe or fatal hypersensitivity reactions have been reported in patients on
penicillin therapy, especially with a history of beta-lactam hypersensitivity, history of sensitivity to multiple allergens,
or previous IgE-mediated reactions. Use with caution in asthmatic patients.

Prolonged use may result in fungal or bacterial superinfection, including C. difficile-associated diarrhea (CDAD) and
pseudomembranous colitis;
Use with caution in patients with severe renal impairment (dosage adjustment necessary);
Use with caution in patients with a history of seizure disorder; high levels, particularly in the presence of renal
impairment, may increase risk of seizures.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Mild diarrhea, vomiting, nausea, oral candidiasis.
Rare: Acute interstitial nephritis, convulsions, hemolytic anemia, positive Coombs’ reaction.

Drug Interactions

Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
BCG vaccine, Typhoid vaccine

Reduces therapeutic effect of Penicillin:
Fusidic acid, Tetracycline derivatives

Decreases excretion of the following drugs:
Methotrexate

Decreases serum concentration of the following drugs:
Mycophenolate

Increases serum concentration of Penicillin:
Probenecid

Mechanism of Action

This class of antibiotics binds to penicillin binding proteins that catalyzes the synthesis of peptidoglycan and this
leads to the interruption of cell wall synthesis, leading to bacterial cell growth inhibition and cell lysis

Dosage

I-PERITON › Sterile with 1.5%, 2.3%, and 4.25% dextrose, 2 L bottle/bag (Rx)

Individualized according to clinical condition and based on blood chemistry results.

Administration

I-PERITON › Sterile with 1.5%, 2.3%, and 4.25% dextrose, 2 L bottle/bag (Rx)

For intraperitoneal infusion only. Do NOT administer by IV infusion. Observe aseptic technique throughout the
procedure. The drained fluid should be inspected for the presence of fibrin or cloudiness, which may indicate the
presence of peritonitis. Discard any unused solution. Prior to use, check for leaks by squeezing the inner bag
firmly. If leaks are found, do NOT use solution as sterility may be impaired. Check to see that solution is clear and
free of foreign matter. Solutions that are cloudy, discolored, contain visible particulate matter, or show evidence
of leakage should not be used or discarded. Heating the dialysis solution to 37°C while in the over pouch may
decrease discomfort and heat loss and increase urea clearance compared to solution at room temperature. Use
only dry heat (e.g. heating pad, warming plate). Do NOT heat solutions in water or in a microwave oven due to the
potential for patient injury or discomfort. When introducing additives, refer to directions for use accompanying
drugs to obtain full information on additives, and use aseptic techniques. Mix thoroughly. Do NOT store. NOTE:
1,000–2,000 international units of heparin per liter of solution may be added to the dialysis solution to prevent
catheter blockage in patients with peritonitis, or when the solution drainage contains fibrinous or proteinaceous
material.

Indications

Correct acid and electrolyte imbalance, and fluid overload; remove metabolites in renal failure.

Page 3 of 5



April 25, 2026, 2:20:56 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Known hypersensitivity to corn or corn products; abdominal sepsis; previous abdominal surgery; severe
inflammatory bowel disease; presence of abdominal devices; pre-existing severe lactic acidosis; uncorrectable
mechanical defects that prevent effective peritoneal dialysis or increase the risk of infection; documented loss of
peritoneal function or extensive adhesions that compromise peritoneal function.

Precautions

Encapsulating Peritoneal Sclerosis; azotemic diabetics (carefully monitor insulin requirements during and following
dialysis); Improper clamping or priming sequence may result in infusion of air into the peritoneal cavity; Abdominal
conditions, including disruption of the peritoneal membrane and diaphragm by surgery or from congenital anomalies
or trauma until healing is complete, abdominal tumors, extensive adhesions, bowel distension, undiagnosed
abdominal disease, abdominal wall infection, hernias or burns, fecal fistula, colostomy or ileostomy, frequent
episodes of diverticulitis, inflammatory or ischemic bowel disease, tense ascites, obesity, and large polycystic
kidneys, or other conditions that compromise the integrity of the abdominal wall, abdominal surface, or intra-
abdominal cavity (perform peritoneal dialysis with great care); Aortic graft replacement and severe pulmonary
disease (perform peritoneal dialysis with caution); Protein, amino acids, water-soluble vitamins, and other medicines
may be lost during peritoneal dialysis and may require replacement (carefully monitor patients to avoid over- and
under-hydration); Routine periodic evaluation of blood chemistries, serum electrolyte concentrations, and
hematologic factors, as well as other indicators of patient status, should be performed for stable patients
undergoing maintenance peritoneal dialysis.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Abdominal pain, bleeding, peritonitis, catheter blockage, difficulty in fluid removal, ileus, electrolyte and fluid
imbalances, hypovolemia, hypervolemia, hypertension, hypotension, hyperphosphatemia, muscle cramping, fungal
peritonitis, bacterial peritonitis, catheter-related infection, hypervolemia, fluid retention, hypokalemia,
hyponatremia, dehydration, hypochloremia, dyspnea, sclerosing encapsulating, peritonitis, peritoneal cloudy
effluent, vomiting, diarrhea, nausea, constipation, abdominal distension, abdominal discomfort, Stevens-Johnson
syndrome, urticaria, rash, pruritus, myalgia, musculoskeletal pain, muscle spasms, generalized edema, pyrexia,
malaise, infusion site pain, catheter-related complication.

Drug Interactions

NOTE: Additives may be incompatible. Consult with pharmacist familiar with peritoneal dialysis.

Mechanism of Action

A sterile, nonpyrogenic solution containing electrolytes at a concentration close to the electrolytic composition of
plasma and glucose in varying concentrations or other suitable osmotic agents. It is used for peritoneal dialysis, a
procedure for removing toxic substances and metabolites normally excreted by the kidneys, and for aiding in the
regulation of fluid and electrolyte balance.

TOPIC › 1% lotion, 125 mL bottle (Rx)

For scabies, by topical application, ADULT and CHILD >2 months, apply 5% lotion to entire skin from chin down to
and including toes and under fingernails and toenails, leave on for 8–14 hours, repeat in 1–2 weeks. Pediculosis

after 10 minutes.

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:21:08 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

TOPIC › 1% lotion, 125 mL bottle (Rx)

Treat close contacts. Wash and dry linens to prevent re-infection. Re-apply to hands after handwashing.
Thoroughly massage the product into the skin from the head to the soles of the feet. Scabies rarely infests the
scalp of adults, although the hairline, neck, temple, and forehead may be infested in infants and geriatric patients.
Remove the product by washing (shower or bath) after 8 to 14 hours. Infants should be treated on the scalp,
temple, and forehead. ONE APPLICATION IS GENERALLY CURATIVE. Patients may experience persistent pruritus
after treatment. This is rarely a sign of treatment failure and is NOT an indication for retreatment. Demonstrable
living mites after 14 days indicate that retreatment is necessary. Avoid contact with eyes, mucous membrane, and
broken skin.

Dose Adjustment

No information found

Indications

Treatment of scabies (Sarcoptes scabiei); head pediculosis

Precautions

Dermatological effects (may temporarily exacerbate pruritus, edema, and erythema associated with scabies
infestation).
Children (safety and effectiveness in children <2 months have not been established).
Lactation (excreted in breast milk; withhold drug while mother is nursing)

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Mild and transient burning and stinging, pruritus, erythema, numbness, tingling, rash, skin and scalp
irritation, eczema, localized edema

Drug Interactions

No information found

Mechanism of Action

A topical scabicidal agent. It acts on the nerve cell membrane to disrupt the sodium channel current by which the
polarization of the membrane is regulated, resulting to paralysis.

May be administered by IM, SC, or IV injection. IV push should be administered slowly using a diluted solution. 10
mg/mL has been recommended. Abrupt discontinuation following prolonged use may also lead to withdrawal
symptoms. NOTE: IM administration is preferred when repeated doses are required.

Page 1 of 4



April 25, 2026, 2:21:25 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Avoid use. If used for acute pain, limit to 1–2 doses.

Renal Impairment:
Avoid use.

Hepatic Impairment:
Use with caution in severe impairment. Consider lower initial dose when initiating therapy. An increased opioid
effect may be seen in patients with cirrhosis.

Sickle-cell Disease:
Use with caution and decrease initial dose. NOT recommended for use in this population and should only be used if
vaso-occlusive crisis occurs and only if it is the only effective opioid for the patient.

Indications

Management of moderate-to-severe pain; adjunct to anesthesia and preoperative sedation.

Contraindications

Use with or within 14 days of MAO inhibitors; severe respiratory insufficiency.

Precautions

WARNING: Abuse, misuse, and diversion. Users are exposed to the risks of addiction, abuse, and misuse, potentially
leading to overdose and death. Monitor all patients for development of these behaviors or conditions. Risk for opioid
abuse is increased in patients with a personal or family history of substance abuse, including drug or alcohol abuse
or addiction, or mental illness.

Hypovolemia (Use with caution); Cardiovascular disease; Supraventricular tachycardia; Pre-existing respiratory
compromise, e.g., hypercapnia, hypoxia; COPD or other obstructive airway disease. Adrenal insufficiency; Thyroid
dysfunction; History of drug abuse or acute alcoholism; Morbidly obese; Sickle-cell disease; Biliary tract disorder;
Hepatic or renal impairment (Avoid use); Prostatic hyperplasia. CNS depression (Avoid use); Coma (Avoid use); Head
injury (Use with extreme caution); Intracranial lesions; Elevated intracranial pressure; Toxic psychoses; Elderly and
debilitated patients;

Pregnancy (avoid prolonged use or high doses at term);

Lactation

SKILLED TASKS. May impair ability to perform tasks, which require mental alertness, like driving or operating
machinery.

Page 2 of 4



April 25, 2026, 2:21:25 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Bradycardia, cardiac arrest, circulatory depression, flushing, hypotension, palpitations, syncope,
tachycardia, agitation, confusion, shock, delirium, disorientation, dizziness, drug dependence (physical),
hallucination, headache, habituation, increased intracranial pressure, seizure, involuntary muscle movements
(muscle twitching, myoclonus), mood changes (euphoria, dysphoria), sedation, serotonin syndrome, skin rash,
diaphoresis, pruritus, urticaria, biliary colic, constipation, nausea, spasm of sphincter of Oddi, vomiting, xerostomia,
urinary retention, anaphylaxis, histamine release, injection site reaction (pain, wheal, flare), hypersensitivity reaction,
tremor, weakness, visual disturbance, respiratory arrest, respiratory depression, dyspnea.

Less Common: Hypogonadism

Page 3 of 4



April 25, 2026, 2:21:25 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Pethidine: Amphetamines (analgesic effect); Barbiturates e.g. Phenobarbital,
Brimonidine (Topical); Tetrahydrocannabinol, Doxylamine; Dronabinol, Magnesium sulfate; Minocycline, Nabilone
(CNS depressant effect)
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Antiemetics 5HT3 Antagonists e.g., Ondansetron (serotonin
syndrome); Ethyl alcohol (CNS depressant effect); Pramipexole, Ropinirole (sedative effect)
Increases excretion of Pethidine: Ammonium Chloride; Barbiturates e.g. Phenobarbital
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Pethidine: Anticholinergic Agents e.g., Atropine (constipation and
urinary retention); Antipsychotic Agents, Phenothiazines (hypotensive effect); CNS Depressants; Rufinamide
(sleepiness and dizziness); Succinylcholine (bradycardic effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Antipsychotic Agents (neuroleptic malignant
syndrome); Desmopressin; Diuretics; Metoclopramide (serotonin syndrome; neuroleptic malignant syndrome);
Ramosetron (constipating effect)

Avoid concomitant use with:
Decreases serum concentration of Pethidine: Protease Inhibitors e.g. Lopinavir
Enhances therapeutic effect of Pethidine: Droperidol, Hydroxyzine Methotrimeprazine (CNS depressant effect)
[reduce codeine dose by 50% with concomitant use] (CNS depressant effect)
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Hydrocodone (CNS depressant effect) [consider starting at a 20–
30% lower hydrocodone dose when used concomitantly], Paraldehyde, Sodium oxybate, Suvorexant, Tapentadol
[start tapentadol at one-third to one-half of the normal dose when used concomitantly], Thalidomide, Zolpidem (CNS
depressant effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Pethidine: MAO Inhibitors (serotonin syndrome); Protease Inhibitors e.g.
Lopinavir
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Azelastine(Nasal) (CNS depressant effect);
Dapoxetine; Serotonin Modulators [Except Tedizolid] (serotonin syndrome)
Increases serum concentration of toxic metabolites of Pethidine: Protease Inhibitors e.g. Lopinavir
Reduces therapeutic effect of Pethidine: Mixed Agonist / Antagonist Opioids (analgesic effect); Naltrexone
Reduces therapeutic effect of Clopidogrel (antiplatelet effect)

Mechanism of Action

Meperidine binds to the opioid receptors in the CNS, causing inhibition of ascending pain pathways, altering the
perception of and response to pain and producing generalized CNS depression.

Administration

ORAL › 15 mg tablet (Rx)

For oral administration, may be taken with or without food. For IM administration, inject deep into muscle. Do NOT
exceed 5 mL per injection site due to potential for tissue irritation. For IV administration, administer at 50–100
mg/minute. Avoid rapid IV administration at >60 mg/minute in adults and >30 mg/minute in children. Avoid
extravasation. Do NOT administer by SC or intra-arterial injection. Do NOT discontinue abruptly because of the
possibility of increasing seizure frequency. Withdraw therapy gradually to minimize the potential of increased
seizure frequency, unless safety concerns require a more rapid withdrawal. NOTE: May cause loss in vitamin D due
to malabsorption. Increase intake of food rich in vitamin D. Vitamin D and/or calcium supplementation may be
necessary.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Initiate at the lowest recommended dose.

Renal Impairment:
In CrCl <10 mL/minute, administer ever 12–16 hours.
In patients under hemodialysis, administer dose before dialysis and 50% of the dose after dialysis.
In patients under peritoneal dialysis, administer 50% of normal dose.

Hepatic Impairment:
Reduce doses. Phenobarbital exposure is increased with hepatic impairment. Use with caution.

Indications

Management of generalized tonic-clonic (grand-mal), status epilepticus, and partial seizures.

April 25, 2026, 2:23:24 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Marked hepatic impairment; dyspnea or airway obstruction; porphyria (manifest and latent); intraarterial and SC
administration; history of sedative / hypnotic addiction; nephritic patients (large doses).

triplicate prescription for pharmacists to dispense it. To be prescribed and dispensed through Yellow Rx. Only one (1)
drug preparation shall be prescribed in one single prescription form. Partial filling allowed. STRICTLY NO REFILL.

CNS depression; paradoxical stimulatory response (e.g., agitation and hyperactivity); depression; suicidal
tendencies; Hypotension; Respiratory depression; Hepatic and Renal impairment; hypoadrenalism; Substance abuse
(e.g., drug dependence; tolerance)

Use with caution in:
Debilitated patients; Elderly (at risk of overdose at low doses; increased risk of developing tolerance to sleep
effects; increased risk of physical dependence; may induce paradoxical stimulation, cause or aggravate depression
and confusion); Children (increased risk for paradoxical stimulatory response).

Mechanism of Action

Phenobarbital is a long-acting barbiturate with sedative, hypnotic, and anticonvulsant properties. It depresses the
sensory cortex, decrease motor activity, alter cerebellar function, and produce drowsiness, sedation, and hypnosis.
In high doses, barbiturates exhibit anticonvulsant activity.

Drug Classification

Sensory Organs

THERAPEUTIC

Ophthalmologicals

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

OPHTHALM › 2.5% eye drops solution (as hydrochloride), 5 mL bottle (Rx)

Dosage

OPHTHALM › 2.5% eye drops solution (as hydrochloride), 5 mL bottle (Rx)

Vasoconstriction and pupil dilation, into the eye, ADULT, 1 drop on the upper limbus, may repeat after 1 hour if
needed. Uveitis, into the eye, ADULT, 1 drop to the upper surface of the cornea, may be repeated as necessary, not
to exceed 3 times; treatment may be continued the following day if necessary. Mydriasis, into the eye, ADULT and
CHILD ≥1 year, instill 1 drop, may repeat in 10–60 minutes as needed; CHILD <1 year, instill 1 drop 15–30 minutes prior
to procedure. Ophthalmoscopic examination, into the eye, ADULT, 1 drop in each eye; sufficient mydriasis to permit
examination is produced in 15–30 minutes; dilation lasts for 1–3 hours. Blanching test, into the eye, ADULT, instill 1–
2 drops to the injected eye; examine for perilimbal blanching after 5 minutes [if blanching occurs, congestion is
superficial]. NOTE: May instill a suitable topical anesthetic a few minutes prior to phenylephrine to prevent
stinging and consequent dilution of the solution by lacrimation.

Administration

OPHTHALM › 2.5% eye drops solution (as hydrochloride), 5 mL bottle (Rx)

For topical ophthalmic use only. NOT for intraocular use. Apply finger pressure to the lacrimal sac (inside corner of
the eye) during and for 2–3 minutes after instillation to decrease risk of absorption and systemic effects. Do NOT
touch dropper tip to eyelids or other surfaces when placing drops in eyes to avoid contamination.

Dose Adjustment

No information found

Indications

Vasoconstrictor, decongestant, and mydriatic in a variety of ophthalmic conditions and procedures; pupillary dilation
in uveitis; for refraction without cycloplegia; for funduscopy and other diagnostic procedures.

Contraindications

Narrow angle glaucoma; low birth weight infants; severe arteriosclerotic cardiovascular or cerebrovascular disease;
intraocular operative procedures where corneal epithelial barrier has been disturbed.

Precautions

WARNING: Prolonged exposure to air or strong light may cause oxidation and discoloration. Do NOT use if solution is
brown or contains a precipitate.

Cardiac disease;
Significant hypertension;
Advanced arteriosclerosis;
Elderly (rebound miosis has been reported; may develop transient pigment floaters in the aqueous humor 40–45
minutes following administration);
Children and infants (use with caution in children with known cardiac anomalies).
Pregnancy (use only if clearly needed);
Lactation (use with caution)

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common:
Marked increase in blood pressure, syncope, myocardial infarction, tachycardia, arrhythmia, fatal subarachnoid
hemorrhage.

Dosage

ORAL › 100 mg capsule (as sodium salt) (Rx)

Status epilepticus, by IV injection, ADULT, initially 10 to 15 mg/kg loading dose at a maximum rate of 50 mg/minute,
may give an additional 5–10 mg/kg 10 minutes after the loading dose; or 10–15 mg/kg at a maximum rate of 50
mg/minute; ADOLESCENT, CHILD, and INFANT, initially 15–20 mg/kg loading dose at a maximum rate of 1–3
mg/kg/minute or 50 mg/minute, whichever is slower, may give an additional 5–10 mg/kg 10 minutes after the
loading dose; or 15–20 mg/kg loading dose, followed by maintenance therapy. Anticonvulsant, by IV injection,
ADULT, 20 mg/kg loading dose at a maximum rate of 50 mg/minute, may give an additional dose of 5–10 mg/kg 10
minutes after the loading dose; or 10 to 15 mg/kg at a max. rate of 50 mg/minute; Anticonvulsant, maintenance, by
mouth or by IV injection, ADULT and ADOLESCENT, 100 mg every 6–8 hours; CHILD and INFANT, initially 5 mg/kg
daily in 2–3 divided doses; usual dose range, 4–8 mg/kg daily (maximum daily dose, 300 mg or 8–10 mg/kg daily in
divided doses).

Page 1 of 5



April 25, 2026, 2:26:01 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

ORAL › 100 mg capsule (as sodium salt) (Rx)

For oral administration, preferably take on an empty stomach. Taking with food may decrease phenytoin serum
concentrations. For oral suspension, shake well prior to use. Tube feedings decrease bioavailability. Absorption is
impaired when given concurrently to patients who are receiving continuous nasogastric feedings. Hold tube
feedings 1–2 hours before and 1–2 hours after phenytoin. Divide daily dose and withhold administration of
supplements for 1–2 hours before and after each phenytoin dose to resolve this interaction. For IV administration,
may be administered by direct IV injection. Administer by infusion pump either undiluted or diluted in normal saline
as an IV piggyback (IVPB) to prevent exceeding the maximum infusion rate. The maximum rate of IV administration
is 50 mg/minute in adults and 0.5 to 1 mg/minute in neonates. An inline 0.22 to 0.55-micron filter is recommended
for IVPB solutions due to the potential for precipitation of the solution. Avoid extravasation. Ensure proper needle
or catheter placement prior to and during IV infusion. Following IV administration, inject NS through the same
needle or IV catheter to prevent irritation. If extravasation occurs, stop infusion immediately and disconnect.
Leave needle or cannula in place. Gently aspirate extravasated solution. Do NOT flush the line. Remove needle or
cannula and elevate the extremity

Dose Adjustment

NOTE: Phenytoin base (e.g., oral suspension) contains ~8% more drug than phenytoin sodium (~92 mg base is
equivalent to 100 mg phenytoin sodium). Dosage adjustments and closer serum monitoring may be necessary when
switching dosage forms.

Geriatric:
Clearance is decreased in the elderly. Lower doses or lessen dosing frequency.

Hepatic Impairment:
In cirrhosis, clearance may be substantially reduced and plasma level monitoring with dose adjustment is advisable.
Free phenytoin levels should be monitored closely.

Obesity:
Loading dose = 14 mg/kg IBW + 19 mg/kg weight in excess of IBW

Indications

Management of generalized tonic-clonic (grand mal), complex partial seizures; prevention of seizures following
neurosurgery.

Page 2 of 5



April 25, 2026, 2:26:01 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Concurrent use of delavirdine; sinus bradycardia, sinoatrial block, secondand third-degree heart block, Adams-
Stokes syndrome (IV).

Precautions

WARNING: Cardiovascular risk. The rate of IV administration should not exceed 50 mg/min in adults and 1–3 mg/kg per
min (or 50 mg/min, whichever is slower) in pediatric patients because of the risk of severe hypotension and cardiac
arrhythmias. Careful cardiac monitoring is needed during and after administering IV phenytoin. Reduction in rate of
administration or discontinuation of dosing may be needed if cardiovascular toxicities are observed below the
recommended infusion rate.

Hazardous agent. Use appropriate precautions for handling and disposal.

Blood dyscrasias (may be fatal); porphyria; bone effects (e.g., decreased bone mineral density and bone fractures);
cardiovascular events (e.g., hypotension and severe cardiac arrhythmias with rapid administration); cardiovascular
disease; dermatologic reactions; hypersensitivity; suicidal ideation; hepatic impairment; hypoalbuminemia;
hypothyroidism; Asian ancestry (variant HLA-B*1502 may be at an increased risk of developing SJS and/or TEN);
debilitated patients.

Elderly (higher incidence of adverse effects; avoid use in older adults with a history of falls or fractures as it may
cause syncope, impaired psychomotor function, or ataxia).

Pregnancy (may cause fetal hydantoin syndrome; crosses the placenta; increased risk of congenital malformations
and adverse outcomes may occur following in utero phenytoin exposure); lactation (excreted in breastmilk; use with
caution).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Atrial conduction depression (IV), bradycardia (IV), circulatory shock (IV), cardiac arrhythmia (IV),
hypotension (IV), periarteritis nodosa, ventricular conduction depression (IV), ventricular fibrillation (IV), confusion,
dizziness, ataxia, drowsiness, decreased coordination, nystagmus, headache, slurred speech, mood changes,
insomnia, nervousness, paresthesia, peripheral neuropathy (chronic treatment), twitching, vertigo, bullous
dermatitis, exfoliative dermatitis, hypertrichosis, morbilliform rash, scarlatiniform rash, skin or other tissue necrosis
(IV administration), skin rash, toxic epidermal necrolysis, hyperglycemia, vitamin D deficiency (chronic treatment),
constipation, dysgeusia (metallic taste), gingival hyperplasia, nausea, enlargement of facial features (lips), vomiting,
Peyronie's disease, granulocytopenia, Hodgkin lymphoma, agranulocytosis, immunoglobulin abnormality, leukopenia,
lymphadenopathy, macrocytosis, megaloblastic, anemia, malignant lymphoma, pancytopenia, pseudolymphoma,
purpuric dermatitis, thrombocytopenia, acute hepatic failure, hepatitis, anaphylaxis, DRESS syndrome, Injection site
reaction ("purple glove syndrome"; edema, discoloration, and pain distal to injection site), local inflammation,
irritation, tissue necrosis (IV), localized tenderness (IV), coarsening of facial features, osteomalacia, systemic lupus
erythematosus, tremor, fever, tissue sloughing (IV)

Less Common: Dyskinesia, hepatotoxicity (idiosyncratic)

FOOD INTERACTION
Ethanol. Inhibits metabolism of phenytoin and may also increase CNS depression. Chronic ethanol use stimulates
metabolism of phenytoin.

Folic acid. May decrease mucosal uptake of folic acid. Administer prophylactic doses of folic acid and cyanocobalamin
to avoid folic acid deficiency and megaloblastic anemia. 0.5 mg folic acid daily has been shown to reduce the
incidence of phenytoin-induced gingival overgrowth in children. However, folate supplementation may increase
seizures in some patients (dose dependent).

Calcium. Hypocalcemia has been reported in patients taking prolonged high-dose therapy. administer an additional
4000 units/week of vitamin D to prevent hypocalcemia.

Vitamin D. Phenytoin interferes with vitamin D metabolism and osteomalacia. Consider vitamin D supplementation.

Mechanism of Action

A hydantoin antiepileptic drug that works primarily through the reduction of hyperexcitability on sodium channels. It
stabilizes neuronal membranes and decreases seizure activity.

Asystole, bradycardia, palpitation, hallucinations,
nervousness, restlessness, seizure, defecation,
diarrhea, nausea, salivation, stomach pain, vomiting,
urinary frequency, twitching, lacrimation, miosis,
bronchospasm, dyspnea, pulmonary edema, respiratory
distress, respiratory paralysis, diaphoresis,
hypersensitivity

Drug Interactions

Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Beta Blockers [except Levobunolol, Metipranolol]
(bradycardic effect)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Physostigmine:
Corticosteroids, Systemic (increased muscular
weakness)

Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Anticholinergic Agents, Neuromuscular-blocking Agents,
Nondepolarizing

Avoid concomitant use with:
Increases serum concentration Succinylcholine

Mechanism of Action

A carbamate that inhibits acetylcholinesterase and prolongs
the central and peripheral effects of acetylcholine.

PHYTOMENADIONE (PHYTONADIONE, VITAMIN K1) (Other Plain
Vitamin Preparations)

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

B02BA01

ANATOMICAL

Blood and Blood Forming Organs

THERAPEUTIC

Antihemorrhagics

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Vitamin K and Other Hemostatics

CHEMICAL CLASS

Vitamin K

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IV › ORAL › 2 mg/0.2 mL pediatric ampule (as mixed micelle) (Rx)

IM › IV › SC › 10 mg/mL, 1 mL ampule (as mixed micelle) (Rx)

IM › IV › SC › 10 mg/mL, 1 mL ampule (as aqueous colloidal solution with benzyl alcohol) (Rx)

Dosage

IV / ORAL › 2 mg/0.2 mL pediatric ampule (as mixed micelle) (Rx)

Prophylaxis of hemorrhagic disease of the newborn, by IM injection, NEONATE, 0.5-1 mg as single dose; by mouth,
NEONATE, 2 mg followed by a second dose after 4-7 days and for breastfed babies a third dose after 1 month.
Treatment of hemorrhagic disease of the newborn, by slow IV or IM injection, NEONATE, 1 mg (with further doses if
necessary, at 8-hour intervals).

Administration

IV / ORAL › 2 mg/0.2 mL pediatric ampule (as mixed micelle) (Rx)

IV administration should be slow, over 30 seconds. Rapid infusion can cause dyspnea, chest pain, and back pain.

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:26:54 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Dose should be at the lower end of the recommended range.

Hepatic Impairment:
For mild-to-moderate impairment, use with caution.
For severe impairment, refer patient to a specialist.

Indications

Management of anticoagulant-induced prothrombin deficiency caused by indanedione or coumarin derivatives such
as warfarin; treatment and prophylaxis against hemorrhagic disease of the newborn.

Contraindications

Known hypersensitivity to phytomenadione or any component of the formulation; avoid IM if bleeding; pregnancy (3rd
trimester); it is not effective in hereditary hypoprothrombinemia caused by liver disease.

Precautions

Hepatic impairment; Elderly.
NOTE: Fat-soluble Vitamin. Patients with fat malabsorption, especially in biliary obstruction or hepatic disease may
become Vitamin K deficient.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Erythema, flushing, nausea, pain, tenderness, tiredness, vomiting, warmth sensation
Less Common: Allergic reactions (including anaphylaxis), bradycardia, bronchospasm,hypotension, rebound bleeding
Rare: Anaphylactoid reactions,hyperbilirubinemia, kernicterus dizziness, hemolytic dyspnea, anemia

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:26:54 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Decreases therapeutic effect of Phytomenadione: Anticonvulsants, e.g., Phenobarbital, Phenytoin, Antituberculosis
Drugs, e.g., Isoniazid, Rifampicin (may cause vitamin K deficiency bleeding on the first day of life in newborns)

Avoid concomitant use with:
Decreases therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Anticoagulants, e.g., Warfarin (increases synthesis of blood
clotting factors)

Mechanism of Action

A fat-soluble vitamin that is an essential cofactor in the synthesis of blood coagulation factors: prothrombin (II),
proconvertin (VII), plasma thromboplastin component (IX), Stuart factor (X), and proteins C and S.

Pregnancy & Remarks

PREGNANCY CATEGORY

C; X in 3rd trimester or near term

PILOCARPINE

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

S01EB01

ANATOMICAL

Sensory Organs

THERAPEUTIC

Ophthalmologicals

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Antiglaucoma Preparations and Miotics

CHEMICAL CLASS

Anticholinesterases (Parasympathomimetics)

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

OPHTHALM › 1% eye drops solution (as hydrochloride), 10 mL bottle (Rx)

OPHTHALM › 1% eye drops solution (as hydrochloride), 15 mL bottle (Rx)

OPHTHALM › 2% eye drops solution (as hydrochloride), 10 mL bottle (Rx)

OPHTHALM › 2% eye drops solution (as hydrochloride), 15 mL bottle (Rx)

Dosage

OPHTHALM › 1% eye drops solution (as hydrochloride), 10 mL bottle (Rx)

Reduction of elevated IOP in open-angle glaucoma or ocular hypertension, into the eye, ADULT, 1 drop of 2%
solution in the affected eye(s) up to 4 times daily; CHILD <2 years, 1 drop of 2% solution in the affected eye(s) 3
times daily [NOTE: The frequency of instillation and concentration of pilocarpine are determined by the severity of
the elevated IOP and miotic response of the patient]. Management of acute angle-closure glaucoma, into the eye,
ADULT, initially 1 drop of 2% solution in the affected eye(s) up to 3 times over a 30-minute period [NOTE: Treatment
with secretory suppressants and hyperosmotic agents may be needed to lower IOP below 50 mmHg and relieve iris
ischemia prior to administration. If laser iridoplasty or iridotomy is used to break the attack, instill 1 drop of 4%
solution prior to the procedure. Following laser iridoplasty, instill 1 drop of 2% solution 4 times daily until an
iridotomy can be performed]. Prevention of post-operative elevated IOP associated with laser surgery, into the
eye, ADULT, instill 1 drop of 1% or 2% solution in the affected eye(s) 15–60 minutes prior to surgery, or 2 drops 5
minutes apart. Induction of miosis, into the eye, ADULT, instill 1 drop of 2% solution in the affected eye(s) or 2
drops 5 minutes apart; CHILD <2 years, 1 drop 2% solution in the affected eye(s) 15 to 60 minutes prior to surgery.

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:27:06 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

OPHTHALM › 1% eye drops solution (as hydrochloride), 10 mL bottle (Rx)

For topical ophthalmic use only. NOT for injection Apply finger pressure to the lacrimal sac (inside corner of the
eye) during and for 1–2 minutes after instillation to decrease risk of absorption and systemic effects. Contact
lenses should be removed prior to the administration and may be reinserted 10 minutes after administration. Do
NOT touch dropper tip to eyelids or other surfaces when placing drops in eyes to avoid contamination. NOTE:
Pilocarpine-naïve patients should be started on 1% concentration as higher concentrations are often not
tolerated initially. May be used in combination with beta blockers, carbonic anhydrase inhibitors, hyperosmotic
agents, or sympathomimetics. If more than one topical ophthalmic product is being used, administer at least 5
minutes apart.

Dose Adjustment

No information found

Indications

Reduction of elevated IOP in open-angle glaucoma or ocular hypertension; management of acute angle-closure
glaucoma; prevention of post-operative elevated IOP associated with laser surgery; induction of miosis.

Contraindications

Conditions where constriction is undesirable (e.g., acute iritis).

Precautions

Poor illumination (e.g., accommodation spasm);
Pre-existing retinal disease (e.g., rare cases of retinal
detachment);
Iritis;
Primary congenital glaucoma (e.g., cases of paradoxical
increase in IOP);
Pregnancy (administer only if clearly needed);
Lactation (not known if excreted in breast milk).

SKILLED TASKS. May cause blurred vision. Avoid performing
tasks, which require mental alertness, e.g., operating
machinery or driving.

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:27:06 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common:
Headache, brow ache, accommodative change, blurred vision, eye irritation, visual impairment, eye pain, ciliary
spasm, conjunctival vascular congestion, induced myopia, reduced visual acuity in poor illumination, lens opacity
(prolonged use).

Drug Interactions

Avoid concomitant use with: Enhances therapeutic effect of Pilocarpine:
Parasympathomimetics (additive effect)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
Beta Blockers (conduction disturbances), Miotic drugs (toxic reactions)

Reduces anticholinergic effects of Pilocarpine:Atropine, Ipratropium (Inhalation)

Mechanism of Action

A direct acting cholinergic parasympathomimetic agent that acts through direct stimulation of muscarinic receptors
and smooth muscle, such as the iris and secretory glands. It contracts the ciliary muscle, causing increased tension
on the scleral spur and opening of the trabecular meshwork spaces. This reduces outflow resistance, resulting in a
decrease in intraocular pressure (IOP). Pilocarpine also causes a contraction of the iris sphincter muscle, producing
miosis.

Pregnancy & Remarks

PREGNANCY CATEGORY

Drug Classification

ANATOMICAL

Anti-Infectives for systemic use

THERAPEUTIC

Antibiotics and Chemotherapeutics for Dermatological Use

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Corticosteroids

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IV INFUSION › [WATCH] 2 g piperacillin + 250 mg tazobactam (as sodium salt) per vial (Rx)

IV INFUSION › [WATCH] 4 g piperacillin + 500 mg tazobactam (as sodium salt) per vial (Rx)

April 24, 2026, 11:20:01 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dosage

IV INFUSION › [WATCH] 2 g piperacillin + 250 mg tazobactam (as sodium salt) per vial (Rx)

Blood-borne infection due to intra-abdominal source, by IV infusion, ADULT (second line), 300 mg (piperacillin
component) for 10-14 days or longer depending on established foci of infection; maximum daily dose: 9-16g.
Healthcare -associated shock, by IV infusion, ADULT, 300mg/kg per day every 8 hours (piperacillin component)
(Max: 9-16 per day) for a period of 10-14 days in the absence of a complication. Severe sepsis and aseptic shock, by
IV infusion, CHILD, 300mg/kg per day every 8 hours (piperacillin component) (Max: 9-16 g per day) for a period of 10-
14 days in the absence of a complication. Febrile neutropenia, by IV infusion, ADULT, 4.5g every 6 hours, duration of
treatment should be dictated by organism or site; CHILD, 300mg/kg per day divided every 6 hours. Sepsis, Non-
neutropenic, by IV infusion, ADULT, (first line) 4.5g every 6-8 hours. Sepsis, Intra-abdominal suspect, by IV infusion,
ADULT (first line), 4.5g every 6-8 hours. Sepsis, Urinary tract infection suspect, by IV infusion, ADULT (first line), 4.5g
every 6-8 hours. Sepsis, Illegal drug IV use suspect, by IV infusion, ADULT, 4.5g every 6-8 hours. Osteomyelitis
(hematogenous, long bone), by IV infusion, ADULT, 4.5g every 8 hours for 4-6 weeks. Vertebral, including disk space
infection and other sites, by IV infusion, ADULT, (first and second line) 4.5g every 8 hours, optimal duration of
treatment is unknown, usually 6-12 weeks. Foot bone (calcaneus), following puncture wound, by IV infusion,
ADULT, 4.5g every 8 hours. Long bone, post-internal fixation of fracture, by IV infusion, ADULT, 4.5g every 8 hours.
Spinal implant, by IV infusion, ADULT, 4.5g every 8 hours (within 30 days). Acute bacterial arthritis, monoarticular, by
IV infusion, ADULT, (not at risk for STI) 4.5g every 8 hours for 2-4 weeks. CLABSI, by IV infusion, CHILD, 200-
300mg/kg per day every 8 hours (plus vancomycin and aminoglycoside) 5-7 days or 10-14 days when catheter is
retained or removed. Cardiovascular infection (impaired host, neutropenic), by IV infusion, ADULT, 4.5g every 6-8
hours (plus vancomycin). Primary spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, by IV infusion, ADULT (first line), 4.5g every 6
hours or 4-hour infusion of 4.5g every 8 hours; CHILD, 300mg/kg per day divided 3 doses (piperacillin component).
Secondary peritonitis, by IV infusion, CHILD, 300mg/kg per day divided 3 doses (piperacillin component) generally
given for 5-10 days but the primary basis for duration of antibiotic treatment is the patient's clinical course. Liver
abscess, by IV infusion, ADULT, 4.5g every 4-6 hours (with metronidazole); CHILD, 300mg/kg per day divided in 3
doses (piperacillin component) (plus metronidazole). Gallbladder infection, by IV infusion, CHILD, 300mg/kg per day
divided by 3 doses (piperacillin component). Biliary complicated intra-abdominal infection (community-acquired
acute cholecystitis), by IV infusion, ADULT, (first line) 4.5g every 6 hours. Extra-biliary complicated intra-abdominal
infection (high risk), by IV infusion, ADULT, (first line) 4.5g every 6 hours. Acute Peritonitis, by IV infusion, ADULT,
(first line) 4.5g every 4-6 hours. (Maximum dose for children is 3000 mg/ dose) Orbital Cellulitis, by IV infusion,
ADULT, (if MRSA is not considered) 4.5g/kg every 8 hours; CHILD, (first line) 240-300mg/kg per day in 3-4 doses
(piperacillin component) (max of 16g/day). Jugular vein suppurative phlebitis (Lemierre's syndrome), by IV infusion,
ADULT/CHILD, (first line) 4.5g every 8 hours. Acute sinusitis in hospitalized patients with intubation, by IV infusion,
ADULT/CHILD, (first line) 4.5g every 6-8 hours. Necrotizing otitis externa, by IV infusion, ADULT, (first line) 4.5g
every 6 hours, (second line) 4.5g every 6 hours +/- gentamicin or amikacin per day; CHILD, (second line) 300mg/kg
per day every 8 hours. Acute mastoiditis, by IV infusion, ADULT, (second line) 4.5g every 8 hours (plus vancomycin).
Chronic or recurrent mastoiditis, by IV infusion, CHILD, 300mg/kg per day every 6 hours (plus gentamicin). High-risk
CAP (Risk for P. aeruginosa), by IV infusion, ADULT, 4.5g every 6 hours (plus azithromycin and gentamicin or
levofloxacin or ciprofloxacin). Lung abscess, by IV infusion, ADULT, 4.5g every 8 hours (for mixed infections with
resistant gram-negative aerobes) in combination with clindamycin or ampicillin-sulbactam or ceftriaxone for 4-6
hours. Pneumonia (anaerobic or aspiration with or without lung abscess), by IV infusion, ADULT, (second line) 4.5g
every 8 hours. (HAP) Hospital-acquired pneumonia and Ventilator-associated pneumonia (MDR pathogens), by IV
infusion, ADULT/CHILD, 300mg/kg/day every 6 hours. HAP with or without high risk of mortality and with or without
risk factor for MDR, by IV infusion, ADULT and CHILD, 4.5g every 6 hours. Empiric treatment of Ventilation-acquired
pneumonia, by IV infusion, ADULT and CHILD, 4.5g every 6 hours. Lower respiratory tract infection due to P.
aeruginosa, by IV infusion, ADULT, 4.5g every 6 hours by extended infusion; CHILD (second line), 300mg/kg/day
divided every 6 hours. NOTE: Maximum dose for adults is 18 g daily unless specified

Administration

IV INFUSION › [WATCH] 2 g piperacillin + 250 mg tazobactam (as sodium salt) per vial (Rx)

Compatible diluents for reconstitution are 0.9% Sodium Chloride for Injection, Sterile Water for Injection,
Bacteriostatic Water for Injection, Bacteriostatic Water for Injection with benzyl alcohol, Bacteriostatic water for
injection with parabens. Reconstitute vial with the right diluent. Shake well until dissolved.

Dose Adjustment

Renal Impairment:
ClCr 20 to 40 mL/min:
Nosocomial pneumonia: 3.375 g IV every 6 hours
Other indications: 2.25 g IV every 6 hours

Indications

Blood-borne infections due to urinary source (complicated UTI), blood-borne infections due to intra-abdominal
source, high risk blood-borne infection, health care associated sepsis, severe sepsis and associated shock, febrile
neutropenia in children, non-neutropenic sepsis, sepsis from suspect urinary tract infection, sepsis from suspect
intra-abdominal source, sepsis from suspect illicit drug IV use source, osteomyelitis (hematogenous), vertebral,
including disk space infection and other sites, foot bone (calcaneus) following puncture wound, long bone post-
internal fixation of fracture, spinal implant, acute bacterial arthritis, central line-associated bloodstream infection
(CLABSI) in children, cardiovascular infection (impaired host, burn neutropenic), primary spontaneous bacterial
peritonitis, secondary peritonitis, liver abscess, gallbladder infection, biliary complicated intra-abdominal infection,
extra-biliary complicated intra-abdominal infection, orbital cellulitis, jugular vein suppurative phlebitis (Lemierre’s
syndrome), necrotizing otitis externa, acute mastoiditis, chronic or recurrent mastoiditis, high-risk CAP, lung
abscess, pneumonia (anaerobic or aspiration with or without lung abscess)

Contraindications

History of acute, severe allergic reaction to any other ß-lactam active substances (e.g. cephalosporin, monobactam
or carbapenem)

Precautions

WARNING: Serious and occasional fatal hypersensitivity (anaphylactic) reactions for people with hypersensitivity to
multiple allergens.
Cross sensitivity reactions may happen with other lactam antibiotics such as cephalosporins.
In case of severe, persistent diarrhea, the possibility of antibiotic -induced pseudomembranous colitis must be
taken into consideration.

Patient with cystic fibrosis, history of seizure disorder, renal impairment, children, pregnancy and lactation

Page 3 of 5



April 24, 2026, 11:20:01 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Phlebitis, headache, insomnia, skin rash, pruritus, decreased serum albumin, decreased serum glucose,
decreased serum total protein, electrolyte disturbance, hypokalemia, increased gamma-glutamyl transferase,
hyperglycemia, diarrhea, constipation, nausea, dyspepsia, vomiting, abdominal pain, decreased hematocrit,
decreased hemoglobin, eosinophilia, positive direct Coombs test, leukopenia, neutropenia, prolonged bleeding
time, prolonged partial thromboplastin time, prolonged prothrombin time, thrombocythemia, thrombocytopenia,
increased serum alkaline phosphatase and bilirubin, increased serum ALT and AST, candidiasis, local irritation,
increased BUN, increased serum creatinine, fever
Less Common: Flushing, hypotension, rigors, thrombophlebitis, purpura, pseudomembranous colitis, hypoglycemia,
anaphylaxis, injection site reaction, arthralgia, myalgia, epistaxis

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
BCG Vaccine

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
Vancomycin

Enhances absorption of the following drugs:
Vecuronium

Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Vitamin K Antagonists e.g. warfarin

Avoid concomitant use with:
Decreases serum concentration of the following drugs:
Aminoglycosides e.g. Streptomycin

Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
BCG (Intravesical), Sodium Picosulfate

Increases serum concentration of Piperacillin + Tazobactam:
Probenecid

Reduces therapeutic effect of Piperacillin + Tazobactam:
Tetracycline, Typhoid Vaccine (Only the live attenuated Ty21a strain is affected)

Mechanism of Action

The β-lactamase inhibitors bind to β-lactamases and inactivate them. The β-lactamase inhibitors lack direct
antimicrobial activity but when combined with an antibiotic, they extend the spectrum of activity and increase
stability against β-lactamases.

Indications

Blood-borne infections due to urinary source (complicated UTI), blood-borne infections due to intra-abdominal
source, high risk blood-borne infection, health care associated sepsis, severe sepsis and associated shock, febrile
neutropenia in children, non-neutropenic sepsis, sepsis from suspect urinary tract infection, sepsis from suspect
intra-abdominal source, sepsis from suspect illicit drug IV use source, osteomyelitis (hematogenous), vertebral,
including disk space infection and other sites, foot bone (calcaneus) following puncture wound, long bone post-
internal fixation of fracture, spinal implant, acute bacterial arthritis, central line-associated bloodstream infection
(CLABSI) in children, cardiovascular infection (impaired host, burn neutropenic), primary spontaneous bacterial
peritonitis, secondary peritonitis, liver abscess, gallbladder infection, biliary complicated intra-abdominal infection,
extra-biliary complicated intra-abdominal infection, orbital cellulitis, jugular vein suppurative phlebitis (Lemierre’s
syndrome), necrotizing otitis externa, acute mastoiditis, chronic or recurrent mastoiditis, high-risk CAP, lung
abscess, pneumonia (anaerobic or aspiration with or without lung abscess)

Contraindications

History of acute, severe allergic reaction to any other ß-lactam active substances (e.g. cephalosporin, monobactam
or carbapenem)

Precautions

WARNING: Serious and occasional fatal hypersensitivity (anaphylactic) reactions for people with hypersensitivity to
multiple allergens.
Cross sensitivity reactions may happen with other lactam antibiotics such as cephalosporins.
In case of severe, persistent diarrhea, the possibility of antibiotic -induced pseudomembranous colitis must be
taken into consideration.

Patient with cystic fibrosis, history of seizure disorder, renal impairment, children, pregnancy and lactation

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Phlebitis, headache, insomnia, skin rash, pruritus, decreased serum albumin, decreased serum glucose,
decreased serum total protein, electrolyte disturbance, hypokalemia, increased gamma-glutamyl transferase,
hyperglycemia, diarrhea, constipation, nausea, dyspepsia, vomiting, abdominal pain, decreased hematocrit,
decreased hemoglobin, eosinophilia, positive direct Coombs test, leukopenia, neutropenia, prolonged bleeding
time, prolonged partial thromboplastin time, prolonged prothrombin time, thrombocythemia, thrombocytopenia,
increased serum alkaline phosphatase and bilirubin, increased serum ALT and AST, candidiasis, local irritation,
increased BUN, increased serum creatinine, fever
Less Common: Flushing, hypotension, rigors, thrombophlebitis, purpura, pseudomembranous colitis, hypoglycemia,
anaphylaxis, injection site reaction, arthralgia, myalgia, epistaxis

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
BCG Vaccine

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
Vancomycin

Enhances absorption of the following drugs:
Vecuronium

Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Vitamin K Antagonists e.g. warfarin

Avoid concomitant use with:
Decreases serum concentration of the following drugs:
Aminoglycosides e.g. Streptomycin

Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
BCG (Intravesical), Sodium Picosulfate

Increases serum concentration of Piperacillin + Tazobactam:
Probenecid

Reduces therapeutic effect of Piperacillin + Tazobactam:
Tetracycline, Typhoid Vaccine (Only the live attenuated Ty21a strain is affected)

Mechanism of Action

The β-lactamase inhibitors bind to β-lactamases and inactivate them. The β-lactamase inhibitors lack direct
antimicrobial activity but when combined with an antibiotic, they extend the spectrum of activity and increase
stability against β-lactamases.
