Indications

For adults and pediatric patients (12 years of age and older and weighing at least 40 kg) for the treatment of
coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID 19) requiring hospitalization.

Contraindications

Contraindicated in patients with a history of hypersensitivity reactions to Remdesivir or any components of the
product

Precautions

Hypersensitivity including infusion-related and anaphylactic reactions.

Increased risk of transaminase elevations

Adverse Drug Reactions

Less common: Hypersensitivity reactions, Generalized seisures and Rash.

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:35:05 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

concomitant use with Chloroquine phosphate or Hyroxychloroquine, risk of reduced antiviral activity.

Mechanism of Action

Remdesivir is an antiviral drug with activity against severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2),
Remdesivir is an inhibitor of the SARS-CoV-2 RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which is essential for viral
replication. Remdesivir is an adenosine nucleotide prodrug that distributes into cells where it is metabolized to a
nucleoside monophosphate intermediate by carboxyesterase 1 and/or cathepsin A, depending upon the cell type.
The nucleoside monophosphate is subsequently phosphorylated by cellular kinases to form the pharmacologically
active nucleoside triphosphate metabolite (GS-443902). Remdesivir triphosphate (RDV-TP) acts as an analog of
adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and competes with high selectivity (3.65-fold) over the natural ATP substrate for
incorporation into nascent RNA chains by the SARS-CoV-2 RNA-dependent RNA polymerase, which results in delayed
chain termination (position i+3) during replication of the viral RNA. In a biochemical assay assessing RDV-TP
incorporation by the MERS-CoV RdRp complex, RDV-TP inhibited RNA synthesis with an IC50 value of 0.032 µM. RDV-
TP can also inhibit viral RNA synthesis following its incorporation into the template viral RNA as a result of read-
through by the viral polymerase that may occur at higher nucleotide concentrations. When remdesivir nucleotide is
present in the viral RNA template, the efficiency of incorporation of the complementary natural nucleotide is
compromised, thereby inhibiting viral RNA synthesis. Remdesivir triphosphate is a weak inhibitor of mammalian DNA
and RNA polymerases, including human mitochondrial RNA polymerase.

Prevention of vitamin A deficiency (universal or targeted distribution programs): by mouth, ADULT, 200,000 IU

(woman of childbearing age), 200,000 IU at delivery or within 8 weeks of delivery; CHILD >1 year (preschool),

Indications

For the prevention and treatment of vitamin A deficiency states (e.g., xerophthalmia and night blindness); prevention
of complications of measles.

Contraindications

Hypervitaminosis A; known hypersensitivity to vitamin A, or any component of the formulation; dosages exceeding
the Recommended Energy and Nutrient Intake (RENI); women who are, or may become, pregnant.

Precautions

WARNING: Severe congenital malformations may occur in infants of mothers consuming large amounts of oral
retinoids for acne treatment.

Patients on prolonged daily administration over 25,000 IU should be under close supervision; Chronic intake of
vitamin A at levels 10–20 times the RDA can lead to hypervitaminosis A; Pregnancy (excessive doses during the first
trimester may be teratogenic); Breastfeeding (there is theoretical risk of toxicity in infants of mothers taking large
doses).

NOTE: Dietary reference intakes (DRIs): Tolerable upper intake level (UL) for adults is 3,000 micrograms daily of
preformed vitamin A, based on teratogenicity as the critical adverse effect for women of childbearing age and liver
pathology for all other adults.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Less Common: Diplopia, headache, nausea, symmetric papilledema, vomiting
Rare: Birth defects (e.g., tense and bulging fontanelle in infants), dry hair, enlarged liver, increased intracranial

Indications

Treatment of chronic hepatitis C (HCV)

Contraindications

Women who are or may become pregnant; male partners of pregnant women, autoimmune hepatitis,
hemoglobinopathies; creatinine clearance <50 mL/minute.

Precautions

HCV treatment regimens (Do not use ribavirin alone for treatment of chronic HCV infection); Fetal/neonatal morbidity
and mortality (Teratogenic and/or embroyocidal. Exercise extreme care to avoid pregnancy in female patients and in
female partners of male patients);
Hematologic effects (Hemolytic anemia reported in patients receiving oral ribavirin in conjunction with interferon
alfa; anemia usually occurs 1-2 weeks after initiation of therapy);
Cardiovascular effects (Fatal and nonfatal MI reported in patients with anemia due to oral ribavirin.);
Hepatic failure (Patients with chronic HCV infection and cirrhosis may be at risk of hepatic decompensation and death
during interferon alfa therapy);
Pancreatitis (Temporarily interrupt oral ribavirin in patients with manifestations of pancreatitis.);
Dental and periodontal disorders;
Pregnancy (Take extreme care to avoid pregnancy).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Fatigue, headache, hemolysis, myalgia, nausea, rigors, fever, insomnia, depression, decreased Hgb,
hyperbilirubinemia, arthralgia, alopecia, irritability, musculoskeletal pain, rash, anorexia, dizziness, pruritus, flu-like
syndrome, dyspnea, nasal congestion, dyspepsia, impaired concentration, thrombocytopenia, sinusitis, vomiting,
emotional liability, and decreased WBC.

Less Common: Hemolytic anemia, weakness, chest pain, taste perversion, and nervousness.

Rare: Dehydration, hearing impairment, hearing loss, retinal detachment, pure red cell aplasia, serious skin
reactions, liver and renal graft rejection, homicidal ideation, and growth inhibition in pediatric patients.

Increases toxicity of Abacavir
Decreases effects of Zidovudine

Although not completely understood, Ribarivin appears to interfere with RNA and DNA synthesis and subsequently
inhibit protein synthesis and viral replication.

Precautions

WARNING:
Use with caution in patients with hepatic impairment, renal impairment.
Monitor patients for: Hematologic Toxicity, Hypersensitivity Reactions, Superinfection; Uveitis, potentially significant
interactions may exist, requiring dose or frequency adjustment, additional monitoring, and/or selection of
alternative therapy;
Must not be administered for Mycobacterium Avium Complex prophylaxis to patients with active tuberculosis since
its use may lead to the development of TB resistant to both rifabutin and rifampin. Caution that active TB in the
HIVpositive patient may present atypically (negative PPD or extrapulmonary manifestations); Remove soft contact

neutropenia, epigastric pain, ageusia, fever, vomiting.

Antimycobacterial drugs are used in the treatment of diseases caused by members of the Mycobacterium genus,

April 24, 2026, 11:20:47 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Hepatic Impairment:
Dose reduction is warranted (maximum, 8 mg/kg/day).

Indications

Treatment of tuberculosis in combination with isoniazid, pyrazinamide and ethambutol, treatment of leprosy using
combination therapy

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to rifamycins, or any component of the formulation; jaundice.

Precautions

Alcohol dependency, the elderly, and in prolonged therapy; daily alcohol intake (may increase the risk of druginduced
hepatotoxicity); renal impairment; hepatic impairment (impaired elimination; monitor liver function); porphyria; it
discolors soft lenses.

IMPORTANT:
Advise patient on hormonal contraceptives to use additional means (effectiveness is reduced); alternative family
planning advice shall be offered.

LIVER DISORDERS:
Monitor liver function and blood count in liver disorder. Patients or their caregivers should be told how to recognize
signs of liver disorders and advised to discontinue treatment and seek immediate medical attention if symptoms
such as persistent nausea, vomiting, malaise, or jaundice develop.

Pregnancy (first trimester: very high doses are teratogenic in animal studies; third trimester: risk of neonatal
bleeding may be increased); and breastfeeding (amount too small to be harmful; may cause loose bowel movement in
babies).

NOTE:
Resumption of rifampicin treatment after a long interval may cause serious immunological reactions, resulting in
renal impairment, hemolysis, or thrombocytopenia – discontinue permanently if serious adverse effects occur.

Page 2 of 4



April 24, 2026, 11:20:47 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common:
Anorexia, cramps, diarrhea, epigastric distress, flatulence, increased hepatic enzymes, nausea, orangered coloration
of body fluids (urine, tears, saliva and sputum), pancreatitis, rash, staining of soft contact lenses, vomiting.

Less Common:
Ataxia, behavioral changes, dizziness,edema, fatigue, flu-like syndrome (characterized by fever, chills, myalgia),
flushing, headache, heartburn, menstrual disturbances, numbness.

Rare:
Acute renal failure, cerebral hemorrhage, C. difficileassociated disease, collapse, exfoliative dermatitis, hemolytic
anemia, hepatitis, jaundice, leukopenia, myopathy, pemphigoid reactions, psychosis, respiratory symptoms, shock,
thrombocytopenic purpura, toxic epidermal necrolysis.

Drug Interactions

NOTE:
Enzyme induction: accelerates the metabolism of various substrates, thus possibly decreasing their effects.

Monitor closely with:
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Azole antifungal e.g. ketoconazole Beta blockers e.g., metoprolol, propranolol Cotrimoxazole, Digoxin, Doxycycline,
Theophylline

Increases metabolism of the following drugs:
Corticosteroids e.g., hydrocortisone, prednisone, prednisolone; Thyroid hormones e.g. Thyroxine

Avoid concomitant use with:
Reduces absorption of Rifampicin:
Antacids (e.g., aluminum and magnesium hydroxide)

Reduces therapeutic effect of Oral Contraceptives

Increases metabolism of Praziquantel

Antimycobacterial drugs are used in the treatment of diseases caused by members of the Mycobacterium genus,
including tuberculosis (TB) and leprosy.

April 25, 2026, 2:36:12 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Hepatic Impairment:
Dose reduction is warranted (maximum, 8 mg/kg/day).

Indications

Treatment of tuberculosis in combination with isoniazid, pyrazinamide and ethambutol, treatment of leprosy using
combination therapy

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to rifamycins, or any component of the formulation; jaundice.

Precautions

Alcohol dependency, the elderly, and in prolonged therapy; daily alcohol intake (may increase the risk of druginduced
hepatotoxicity); renal impairment; hepatic impairment (impaired elimination; monitor liver function); porphyria; it
discolors soft lenses.

IMPORTANT:
Advise patient on hormonal contraceptives to use additional means (effectiveness is reduced); alternative family
planning advice shall be offered.

LIVER DISORDERS:
Monitor liver function and blood count in liver disorder. Patients or their caregivers should be told how to recognize
signs of liver disorders and advised to discontinue treatment and seek immediate medical attention if symptoms
such as persistent nausea, vomiting, malaise, or jaundice develop.

Pregnancy (first trimester: very high doses are teratogenic in animal studies; third trimester: risk of neonatal
bleeding may be increased); and breastfeeding (amount too small to be harmful; may cause loose bowel movement in
babies).

NOTE:
Resumption of rifampicin treatment after a long interval may cause serious immunological reactions, resulting in
renal impairment, hemolysis, or thrombocytopenia – discontinue permanently if serious adverse effects occur.

Page 2 of 4



April 25, 2026, 2:36:12 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common:
Anorexia, cramps, diarrhea, epigastric distress, flatulence, increased hepatic enzymes, nausea, orangered coloration
of body fluids (urine, tears, saliva and sputum), pancreatitis, rash, staining of soft contact lenses, vomiting.

Less Common:
Ataxia, behavioral changes, dizziness,edema, fatigue, flu-like syndrome (characterized by fever, chills, myalgia),
flushing, headache, heartburn, menstrual disturbances, numbness.

Rare:
Acute renal failure, cerebral hemorrhage, C. difficileassociated disease, collapse, exfoliative dermatitis, hemolytic
anemia, hepatitis, jaundice, leukopenia, myopathy, pemphigoid reactions, psychosis, respiratory symptoms, shock,
thrombocytopenic purpura, toxic epidermal necrolysis.

Drug Interactions

NOTE:
Enzyme induction: accelerates the metabolism of various substrates, thus possibly decreasing their effects.

Monitor closely with:
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Azole antifungal e.g. ketoconazole Beta blockers e.g., metoprolol, propranolol Cotrimoxazole, Digoxin, Doxycycline,
Theophylline

Increases metabolism of the following drugs:
Corticosteroids e.g., hydrocortisone, prednisone, prednisolone; Thyroid hormones e.g. Thyroxine

Avoid concomitant use with:
Reduces absorption of Rifampicin:
Antacids (e.g., aluminum and magnesium hydroxide)

Reduces therapeutic effect of Oral Contraceptives

Increases metabolism of Praziquantel

Mechanism of Action

Antimycobacterial drugs are used in the treatment of diseases caused by members of the Mycobacterium genus,
including tuberculosis (TB) and leprosy.
Combination chemotherapy is essential to kill heterogeneous populations of bacterial cells located in different
conditions within the host, and to prevent drug resistance.

ORAL › [WATCH] 200 mg tablet (Rx)

Hepatic encephalopathy: by mouth, ADULT, 400 mg every 8 hours for 5-10 days.

Administration

ORAL › [WATCH] 200 mg tablet (Rx)

For oral administration, may be taken with or without food.

Dose Adjustment

Renal and hepatic impairment: No dosage adjustment necessary. Use with caution.

Indications

Prevention and treatment of hepatic encephalopathy.

Page 1 of 2



April 25, 2026, 2:36:22 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to rifaximin, other rifamycin antibiotics, or any component of the formulation.

Precautions

Hypersensitivity (e.g., exfoliative dermatitis, rash, urticarial, flushing, angioneurotic edema, pruritus, and
anaphylaxis);
Superinfection (Prolonged use may result in fungal or bacterial superinfection, i.e. C. difficile-associated diarrhea
(CDAD) and pseudomembranous colitis, CDAD has been observed >2 months post-antibiotic treatment);
Hepatic impairment (Monitor patients with severe hepatic impairment for increased systemic exposure).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Peripheral edema, dizziness, fatigue, ascites, and nausea.
Less Common: Headache, depression, pruritus, skin rash, abdominal pain, pseudomembranous colitis, anemia,
muscle spasm, arthralgia, nasopharyngitis, dyspnea, epistaxis, and fever.
Rare: Anaphylaxis, angioedema, Clostridium difficile- associated diarrhea, exfoliative dermatitis, flushing,
hypersensitivity reaction, and urticarial.

Drug Interactions

Avoid concomitant use with:
BCG, intravesical (live), Cholera vaccine (Do not administer cholera vaccine to patients who have received oral or
parenteral antibiotics within 14 days prior to vaccination).

Mechanism of Action

Rifaximin inhibits bacterial RNA synthesis by binding to bacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase. It lowers
incidence of hepatic encephalopathy due to its broad- spectrum activity against ammonia-producing enteric
bacteria.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Initially 0.5 mg twice daily. Slowly increase by 0.5 mg twice daily and increase dose not more than once a week for
doses above 1.5 mg twice daily.

Renal Impairment:
Initially 0.5 mg by mouth twice daily for the first week. Increase to 1 mg by mouth twice daily on the second week.
Slowly increase doses beyond 1.5 mg by mouth twice daily at intervals of 1 week or longer.
For long-acting injectable formulation, do not administer unless service user/patient has demonstrated tolerability
to at least 2 mg by mouth once daily or in divided doses. An initial dose of 12.5 mg may be more appropriate.
Maintenance doses should be 25 mg every two weeks, with oral risperidone being continued for 3 weeks after the
first injection.

Hepatic Impairment:
Initially 0.5 mg by mouth twice daily for the first week. Increase to 1 mg by mouth twice daily on the second week.
Slowly increase doses beyond 1.5 mg by mouth twice daily at intervals of 1 week or longer.
For long-acting injectable formulation, do not administer unless service user/patient has demonstrated tolerability
to at least 2 mg by mouth once daily or in divided doses. An initial dose of 12.5 mg may be more appropriate.
Maintenance doses should be 25 mg every two weeks, with oral risperidone being continued for 3 weeks after the
first injection.

Indications

Treatment of schizophrenia; delaying relapse in schizophrenia; other psychotic disorders; treatment of acute and
long term mania/mixed mania (monotherapy and adjunct to lithium or valproate); autism-related irritability in
children; bipolar maintenance (monotherapy and adjunct to lithium or valproate)

Administration

IV › 10 mg/mL, 10 mL vial (Rx)

Refer to institutional guideline on administration, if available. For IV infusion only. For initial IV infusion, start at a
rate of 50 mg/hour. If there is no reaction, increase the rate by 50 mg/hour increments every 30 minutes, to a
maximum rate of 400 mg/hour. For subsequent IV infusions, Standard infusion rate: If patient tolerated initial
infusion, start at 100 mg/hour. If there is no reaction, increase the rate by 100 mg/hour increments every 30
minutes, to a maximum rate of 400 mg/hour. Accelerated infusion rate (90 minutes): For patients with previously
untreated follicular NHL and diffuse large B-cell NHL who are receiving a corticosteroid concurrently, with a
circulating lymphocyte count <5000/mm3, or have no significant cardiovascular disease, after tolerance has been
established (no grade 3 or 4 infusion-related event) at the recommended infusion rate in cycle 1, a rapid infusion
rate may be used beginning with cycle 2. Administer dose over 90 minutes, with 20% of the dose administered
over the first 30 minutes and the remaining 80% is given over 60 minutes, after the daily corticosteroid,
acetaminophen, and diphenhydramine. If the 90-minute infusion in cycle 2 is tolerated, the same rate may be used
for the remainder of the treatment regimen (through cycles 6 or 8). Do NOT administer by IV push or bolus. If a
reaction occurs, slow or stop the infusion. Once reaction abates, restart infusion at 50% of the previous rate.
Discontinue infusion in the event of serious or life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias. NOTE: Pretreatment with
acetaminophen and an antihistamine is recommended. Anti-hyperuricemic therapy and aggressive hydration are
recommended for patients with a high tumor burden or lymphocytes >25,000/mm3. In patients with CLL,
Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP) and antiherpetic viral prophylaxis is recommended during treatment (and
for up to 12 months following treatment). In granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA) and microscopic polyangiitis
(MPA), PCP prophylaxis is recommended during and for 6 months after treatment.

Precautions

WARNING: Can result in serious, including fatal, infusion reactions. Deaths within 24 hours of infusion have been
reported. Carefully monitor patients during infusions. Discontinue when severe reactions occur and administer
medical treatment for grade 3 or 4 infusion reactions.
Severe, sometimes fatal, mucocutaneous reactions (e.g., paraneoplastic pemphigus, Stevens-Johnson syndrome,
lichenoid dermatitis, toxic epidermal necrolysis, vesiculobullous dermatitis) have been reported.
Hepatitis B virus (HBV) reactivation can occur, in some cases resulting in fulminant hepatitis, hepatic failure, and
death. Screen all patients for HBV infection by measuring HBsAg and anti-HBc before treatment initiation. Monitor
patients during and after treatment. Discontinue in the event of HBV reactivation.
JC virus infection resulting in progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) and death can occur.

WARNING: Hazardous agent. Use appropriate precautions for handling and disposal.

Bowel obstruction or perforation (e.g. abdominal pain, bowel obstruction, perforation; complaints of abdominal pain
or repeated vomiting should be evaluated, especially if early in the treatment course)

Cardiovascular disease (use with caution; discontinue if serious cardiac arrhythmia occurs); respiratory disease (use
with caution in patients)

Hepatitis B virus reactivation (monitor patients for clinical and laboratory signs of hepatitis or HBV during and several
months after treatment; reactivation may occur even in those with previously resolved HBV infection, and up to 24
months after therapy discontinuation; use with caution in those with evidence of prior HBV infection)

Infections (serious and potentially fatal bacterial, fungal, and either new or reactivated viral infections may occur
during treatment and after; monitor immunoglobulin levels as necessary; discontinue in patients who develop other
serious infections and initiate appropriateantiinfectivetreatment; use is not recommended in active infection)

Infusion reactions (e.g. hypotension, angioedema, bronchospasm, hypoxia, urticaria, pulmonary infiltrates, ARDS,
myocardial infarction, ventricular fibrillation, cardiogenic shock, or anaphylaxis; closely monitor patients with history
of prior cardiopulmonary reactions, with preexisting cardiac or pulmonary conditions, or with high numbers of
circulating malignant cells, i.e., >25,000/mm3; perform cardiacmonitoring during and after the infusion in patients
who develop clinically significant arrhythmias or who have a history of arrhythmia or angina; mild-to-moderate
infusion-related reactions, e.g., chills, fever, or rigors, occur frequently and are typically managed through slowing or
interrupting the infusion; due to the potential for hypotension, consider withholding antihypertensives 12 hours

Mucocutaneous reactions (discontinue in severe mucocutaneous skin reactions); progressive multifocal

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Use with caution. Patients =65 years exhibited a slightly prolonged median clinical duration.

Renal Impairment and biliary tract disease:
Maintenance dose: 75–100 micrograms/kg per hour by injection or 300–400 micrograms/kg per hour by infusion.

Hepatic Impairment:

Obesity:
Reduce dose based on the patient’s ideal body weight.

Adjunct to general anesthesia to facilitate both rapid sequence and routine tracheal intubation; to provide skeletal
muscle relaxation during surgery or mechanical ventilation

Known hypersensitivity to rocuronium or other neuromuscular blocking agents

Precautions

WARNING: This drug should only be administered by experienced clinicians or trained individuals supervised by an
experienced clinician familiar with the use, actions, characteristics, and complications of neuromuscular blocking
agents. Should not be administered unless facilities for intubation, mechanical ventilation, oxygen therapy, and an
antagonist are immediately available

anesthesia to minimize potentiation);

Anaphylaxis (may be life-threatening and fatal);

Cross-reactivity between neuromuscular blocking agents, both depolarizing and nondepolarizing, has been

Residual paralysis (extubate patient only after sufficient recovery from neuromuscular block);

Malignant hyperthermia (use with caution);

Prolonged circulation time;

QT interval prolongation;

Pulmonary vascular resistance (use with caution);

Extravasation (may be associated with signs and symptoms of local irritation);

Cardiovascular disease;

Respiratory disease;

Myasthenia gravis or myasthenic syndrome, e.g. Lambert-Eaton;

Biliary tract disease;

Neuromuscular disease;

Previous poliomyelitis;

Burn injury;

Severe electrolyte disturbances;

Renal and hepatic impairment.

vascular resistance; anaphylaxis (potentially fatal)

Less Common: Arrhythmia, abnormal electrocardiogram, tachycardia, nausea, vomiting, asthma, hiccup, rash,

Drug Interactions

Enhances therapeutic effect of Rocuronium: Antibiotics, Beta Blockers e.g., Propranolol, Calcium Channel Blockers

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Rocuronium: Antibiotics (prolongation of neuromuscular block)

Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Anticonvulsants, Calcium Chloride, Carbamazepine, Phenytoin
[chronic administration], Potassium Chloride, Protease Inhibitors e.g., Ritonavir

Mechanism of Action

A non-depolarizing, neuromuscular-blocking agent with a rapid to intermediate onset depending on the dose and
intermediate duration. It acts by competing for cholinergic receptors at the motor end-plate.

Administration

IM / IV › 10 mg/mL, 10 mL ampule (as hydrochloride) (Rx)

Administered by local infiltration, epidural block and epidural infusion, or intermittent bolus. Do NOT administer
large volume of anesthetic rapidly, including emergency situations where rapid administration is necessary. Use
an infusion pump to slowly administer local anesthetics to or around surgical wound sites and/or near peripheral
nerves for postoperative analgesia. Avoid IV injections. Perform aspiration prior to administration. Re-position the
needle until no return of blood can be elicited by aspiration. However, absence of blood in the syringe does NOT
guarantee that IV injection has been avoided. IV regional anesthesia (Bier block) is not recommended. NOTE: A test
dose of short-acting local anesthetic containing epinephrine (e.g., 3–5 mL) is recommended prior administration.
Incremental ropivacaine dosing is recommended.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Use with caution. Initial dose reductions may be necessary.

Indications

Surgical anesthesia, labor pain management, postoperative pain management.

Contraindications

IV regional anesthesia (Bier block), obstetric paracervical block anesthesia.

Precautions

WARNING: Should be administered by clinicians well-trained in the diagnosis and management of emergencies that
may arise from the use of these agents. Resuscitative equipment, oxygen, and other resuscitative drugs should be
available for immediate use.

CNS toxicity (restlessness, anxiety, tinnitus, dizziness, blurred vision, tremors, depression, or drowsiness may be
early warning signs of CNS toxicity); Seizures (convulsions due to systemic toxicity leading to cardiac arrest have
been reported); Neurological and psychiatric disorders (may be at greater risk for toxicity). Chondrolysis (primarily of
the shoulder joint, has occurred following continuous intra-articular infusion; destruction of articular cartilage has
occurred when infused directly into the shoulder); Respiratory arrest (have been associated with rare occurrences
of sudden respiratory arrest). Cardiovascular disease (may be at greater risk for toxicity); Porphyria; Hepatic
impairment (undergoes hepatic metabolism; at an increased risk for developing toxic drug levels); Renal impairment
(renally excreted; may be at greater risk for toxicity); Acutely ill and debilitated patients (may be at greater risk for
toxicity).

Elderly (may be at greater risk for toxicity; cardiovascular adverse events may be age-related; more common in
patients >61 years of age); Children (use extra precaution to ensure accuracy and minimize potential toxicity).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common and Less Common: Bradycardia, chest pain, hypertension, hypotension (dose-related; age-related),
tachycardia, anxiety, dizziness, headache, hypoesthesia, paresthesia, rigors, chills, shivering, pruritus, hypokalemia,
nausea, oral paresthesia, vomiting, oliguria, urinary retention, urinary tract infection, anemia, back pain, dyspnea,
fever.

Rare: Angioedema, apnea (associated with epidural block in head/neck region), bronchospasm, cardiac insufficiency,
chondrolysis (continuous intra-articular administration), circulatory shock, dyskinesia, hypersensitivity reaction,
seizure, hallucination, hyperthermia, laryngeal edema, myocardial infarction, skin rash, syncope, tinnitus, urticaria,
ventricular arrhythmia.

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Reduces diagnostic effect of Technetium Tc 99m Tilmanocept

Avoid concomitant use with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Blood Pressure-lowering Agents (antihypertensive effect);
Hypotension-associated Agents (hypotensive effect); Neuromuscular-blocking Agents e.g., Succinylcholine
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Ropivacaine: Hyaluronidase
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Bupivacaine (Liposomal)

Mechanism of Action

Ropivacaine is a local anesthetic of the amide type with analgesic activity. It binds to voltage-gated sodium ion
channels in the neuronal membrane resulting in a stabilization of the membrane and inhibition of depolarization. This
blocks nerve impulse generation and propagation, resulting in a reversible loss of sensation.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Start at low dose. Initiate dose at 5 mg once daily.

Renal Impairment:
For mild-to-moderate impairment, initiate dose at 5 mg once daily with a maximum dose of 10 mg once daily.

Asian Ancestry:
Patients may build up higher drug levels and be at higher risk to develop myopathy. Initiate dose at 5 mg. Do not
administer 40 mg dose.

Patients at Risk for Myopathy:
Consider lower initial dose, e.g., 5 mg daily in adults.

Indications

Prevention of cardiovascular events in patients at high risk of a first cardiovascular event; mixed dyslipidemia, or
homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia in patients who have not responded adequately to diet and other
appropriate measures; (adults) treatment of primary hypercholesterolemia, e.g., heterozygous familial and non-
familial hypercholesterolemia; (children and adolescents) treatment of heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia

Contraindications

Pregnancy; breastfeeding; women who are planning to conceive or those using inadequate contraception; severe
renal impairment; active liver disease; unexplained persistent elevation in serum transaminases

Precautions

Renal impairment (may reduce elimination of rosuvastatin; increases risk of myopathy); Hepatic effects (hepatic
impairment may impair elimination of rosuvastatin).
Myopathy (may occur with lipid lowering agent; monitor creatine kinase (CK) levels and discontinue if these are
markedly elevated; increased risk for rhabdomyolysis); immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy (have been rarely
reported)
Severe intercurrent illness, e.g., infection, trauma, metabolic disorder, endocrine and electrolyte disturbances,
uncontrolled seizures (increases risk of myopathy, rhabdomyolysis, and renal failure).
Diabetes mellitus (small increases in HbA1c and fasting blood glucose have been reported); hematuria and
proteinuria; hypothyroidism (should be managed adequately before starting treatment with a statin)
Treatment with systemic Sodium Fusidate (may increase the risk of rhabdomyolysis)
Elderly (risk of myopathy is higher); children (safety and efficacy have not been established in children <10 years;
establish a healthy lifestyle to reduce cardiovascular risk)
Pregnancy (use during first trimester has been associated with fetal malformation; decreased synthesis of
cholesterol may possibly affect fetal development); lactation

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:38:40 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Abdominal pain, arthralgia, constipation, diabetes mellitus, dizziness, elevated creatine kinase or
aminotransferase concentrations, headache, flu-like illness, insomnia, mild GI symptoms, myalgia, nausea, UTI,
weakness
Less Common: Pruritus, rash, urticaria
Rare: Myopathy (including myositis), angioedema, alopecia, amnesia, anaphylaxis, gynecomastia, hematuria, hepatitis,
hypersensitivity (rash, pruritus, urticaria), impotence, interstitial lung disease, jaundice, liver failure, pancreatitis,
paresthesia, peripheral neuropathy, proteinuria, rhabdomyolysis, renal failure, toxic epidermal necrolysis

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Rosuvastatin:
Fibrates e.g. Fenofibrate (myopathy; rhabdomyolysis)

Avoid concomitant use with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs:
Warfarin (increases INR)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Rosuvastatin:
Niacin (rhabdomyolysis; increased toxicity due to pharmacodynamic synergism)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
Warfarin (bleeding)

Reduces absorption of Rosuvastatin:
Bile Acid Binding Resins [administer statin at least 1 hour before, or 4 hours after, the resin]

Mechanism of Action

An active HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor, which is effective in reducing LDL-cholesterol concentration and has been
efficacious in severe hypercholesterolemia

SACUBITRIL + VALSARTAN

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

C09DX04

ANATOMICAL

Cardiovascular System

THERAPEUTIC

Agents Acting on the Renin-Angiotensin System

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Angiotensin II Antagonists, Combinations

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › 24.3 mg + 25.7 mg Tablet (Rx)

ORAL › 48.6 mg + 51.4 mg Tablet (Rx)

ORAL › 97.2 mg + 102.8 mg Tablet (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › 24.3 mg + 25.7 mg Tablet (Rx)

Heart failure, by mouth, ADULT, 100 mg tab twice daily initially, double the dose after 2-4 weeks until target dose of
200 mg twice daily as tolerated.

Administration

ORAL › 24.3 mg + 25.7 mg Tablet (Rx)

Administer with or without food

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:39:51 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Renal impairment:
Mild to moderate- no dosage adjustment necessary.
Severe- reduce starting dose to 50 mg tab twice daily; double the dose every 2-4 weeks to target maintenance dose
of 200 mg twice daily as tolerated.

Hepatic impairment:
Mild- no dosage adjustment necessary.
Moderate- reduce starting dose to 50 mg tab twice daily; double the dose every 2-4 weeks to target maintenance
dose of 200 mg twice daily as tolerated.
Severe- use not recommended.

Indications

Reduce the risk of cardiovascular death and hospitalization for heart failure in patients with chronic heart failure
(NYHA Class II-IV) and reduced ejection fraction; usually administered in conjunction with other heart failure
therapies, in place of an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor or other angiotensin II receptor blocker
(ARB).

Contraindications

Hypersensitivity to sacubitril, valsartan, or any component of the formulation; history of angioedema related to
previous ACE inhibitor or ARB therapy; concomitant use or use within 36 hours of ACE inhibitors; concomitant use of
aliskiren in patients with diabetes.

Precautions

May cause angioedema, hyperkalemia, hypotension, renal function deterioration; use in caution in patients with
aortic or mitral stenosis, heart failure, hepatic impairment, renal artery stenosis, and renal impairment

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Hypotension, hyperkalemia, and increased serum creatinine.
Less Common: Cough, dizziness, orthostatic hypotension, falls, decreased hematocrit and hemoglobin, angioedema,
and renal failure.
Rare: Anaphylaxis, hypersensitivity, pruritus, and skin rash.

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:39:51 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Both drugs increase serum potassium:
Aspirin, Atenolol, Betaxolol, Bisoprolol, Candesartan, Carvedilol, Celecoxib, Diclofenac, Digoxin, Esmolol, Ibuprofen,
Ketorolac, Losartan, Mefenamic Acid, Naproxen, Potassium Chloride, Potassium Citrate, Propranolol, Spironolactone,
Succinylcholine, Telmisartan

Decreases effects of sacubitril/valsartan:
Aspirin, Celecoxib, Diclofenac, Ibuprofen, Ketoprofen, Ketorolac, Mefenamic Acid, Naproxen.

Either drug increases the toxicity of the other:
Aspirin, Celecoxib, Diclofenac, Ibuprofen, Ketorolac, Mefenamic Acid, Naproxen

Increases effects of sacubitril/valsartan
Atorvastatin, Carbamazepine, Carbidopa, Clarithromycin, Daclatasvir, Digoxin, Erythromycin, Levodopa, Paclitaxel,
Rifampin, Simvastatin, Tacrolimus

Increases toxicity of sacubitril/valsartan
Enoxaparin, Heparin

Increases the toxicity of the following:
Atorvastatin

Avoid concomitant use with:
Either increases the toxicity of the other:
Captopril, Enalapril, Lithium

Mechanism of Action

Sacubitril is a prodrug that inhibits neprilysin leading to increased natriuretic peptides, which promotes excretion of
sodium by the kidneys; valsartan produces direct antagonism of the angiotensin II receptors preventing
vasoconstriction, aldosterone release, arginine vasopressin release, water intake, and hypertrophic responses.

Respiratory System

RESPIR › DPI: 200 micrograms per dose (as sulfate) with appropriate accompanying dispenser (Rx)

RESPIR › MDI: 100 micrograms per dose (as sulfate) x 200 doses (Rx)

RESPIR › Respiratory solution for nebulization: 1 mg/mL (as sulfate), 2.5 mL (unit dose) (Rx)

RESPIR › Respiratory solution for nebulization: 2 mg/mL (as sulfate), 2.5 mL (unit dose) (Rx)

Dosage

RESPIR › DPI: 200 micrograms per dose (as sulfate) with appropriate accompanying dispenser (Rx)

Chronic asthma (as adjunct in stepped treatment), by aerosol inhalation, ADULT, 100–200 micrograms (1–2 puffs) up
to 3–4 times daily; CHILD, 100 micrograms (1 puff) 3–4 times daily, increased to 200 micrograms (2 puffs) 3–4 times
daily if necessary. Prophylaxis of exercise-induced bronchospasm, by aerosol inhalation, ADULT, 200 micrograms
(2 puffs); CHILD, 100 micrograms (1 puff) increased to 200 micrograms (2 puffs) if required. Prophylaxis of exercise-
induced bronchospasm, by aerosol inhalation, ADULT, 200 micrograms (2 puffs); CHILD, 100 micrograms (1 puff)
increased to 200 micrograms (2 puffs) if required. Severe acute asthma, chronic bronchospasm (unresponsive to
conventional treatment), by inhalation of nebulized solution, ADULT and CHILD >18 months, 2.5 mg repeated up to
4 times daily; may be increased to 5 mg if necessary (consider medical assessment since alternative therapy may
be indicated); CHILD <18 months, clinical efficacy uncertain (transient hypoxemia may occur – consider oxygen
supplementation).

Page 1 of 3



April 24, 2026, 11:20:58 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

RESPIR › DPI: 200 micrograms per dose (as sulfate) with appropriate accompanying dispenser (Rx)

Multi-dose solution for nebulization may need dilution with 0.9% sodium chloride to obtain final volume for the
nebulizer

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric: Start with a lower dose than the usual adult dose. Gradually increase if needed.

Indications

Prophylaxis and treatment of asthma; bronchospasm in patients with reversible obstructive airway disease;
prevention of exercise-induced bronchospasm

Contraindications

Severe pre-eclampsia and eclampsia; intrauterine infection; intrauterine fetal death; ante partum hemorrhage,
placenta previa; cord compression; threatened miscarriage; cardiac disease

Precautions

WARNING: Excessive use and reliance on beta2- agonists for the treatment of asthma without an
anti_x0002_inflammatory agent have been associated with an increased incidence of sudden deaths. Beta2-
agonists inhalation can occasionally cause paradoxical bronchospasm and hypoxemia.

Paradoxical bronchospasm (should be treated immediately
with alternative therapy); discontinue treatment if the
patient develops signs of pulmonary edema; Myocardial
insufficiency, arrhythmias, susceptibility to QT-interval
prolongation, hypertension; Diabetes mellitus, especially
IV administration (monitor blood glucose);
hyperthyroidism; renal and hepatic impairment (use with
caution in high-dose treatments); Pregnancy (high doses
should be given through inhalation only); lactation
(monitor breastfeeding infant).

Page 2 of 3



April 24, 2026, 11:20:58 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Cough, headache, musculoskeletal pain, palpitations, throat irritation, tremor, upper respiratory
inflammation, viral respiratory infections

Less Common: Agitation, hyperactivity in children, hyperglycemia (high doses), insomnia, tachycardia

Rare: Allergic reactions, hypokalemia, lactic acidosis, paradoxical bronchospasm

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Methyldopa (acute hypotension)

Avoid concomitant use with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Salbutamol:
Corticosteroids (serious hypokalemia), Diuretics (serious hypokalemia), Drugs that increase blood glucose
concentration e.g., Glucocorticoids, Antipsychotics, Calcineurin Inhibitors, high-dose Thiazide Diuretics, Somatropin
(hyperglycemia), Drugs that reduce
potassium concentration e.g., Amphotericin B, Diuretics (hypokalemia), Theophylline (serious hypokalemia)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
Sympathomimetic Amines e.g., Ephedrine, Phenylephrine, Pseudoephedrine (sympathetic adverse effects e.g.,
tremor, tachycardia, headache)

Reduces therapeutic effect of Beta Blockers e.g., Propranolol (may precipitate asthma)

Mechanism of Action

A short-acting, beta2-adrenergic agonist used in the treatment or prevention of bronchospasm.

April 25, 2026, 2:40:23 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Renal or hepatic impairment (monitor for signs of salicylate toxicity, i.e., nausea, vomiting, dizziness, loss of hearing,
tinnitus, lethargy, hyperpnea, diarrhea, psychic disturbances); Impaired peripheral circulation; Diabetes; Significant
peripheral neuropathy; Children (monitor for signs of salicylate toxicity, listed above; avoid use in children with
varicella or influenza due to potential risk of developing Reye’s Syndrome); Lactation.

Indications

Keratolytic; management of keratosis in arsenic poisoning; removal of excessive keratin in hyperkeratotic skin
disorders; acne

Contraindications

Prolonged use or application to large areas; impaired circulation; warts with hair growth or on face; birthmarks;
moles

Precautions

Renal or hepatic impairment (monitor for signs of salicylate toxicity, i.e., nausea, vomiting, dizziness, loss of hearing,
tinnitus, lethargy, hyperpnea, diarrhea, psychic disturbances); Impaired peripheral circulation; Diabetes; Significant
peripheral neuropathy;

Children (monitor for signs of salicylate toxicity, listed above; avoid use in children with varicella or influenza due to
potential risk of developing Reye’s Syndrome); Lactation.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Dermatitis, skin peeling, discomfort, irritation, dryness, ulceration, erosion, salicylate toxicity (systemic),
toxic inner ear damage, excessive erythema and scaling (when used on open skin lesions)
Rare: Hypersensitivity reactions (potentially fatal, e.g., anaphylactic reaction)

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:40:23 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs:
Oral Anticoagulants (bleeding); Pyrazinamide (pyrazinamide-induced uricemia); Sulfonylureas (hypoglycemia)

Increases serum concentration of Salicylic Acid:
Aspirin, Other salicylate-containing Medications
Reduces therapeutic effect of Heparin (interferes with heparin hemostasis due to decreased platelet adhesiveness)

Administration: For external use only. Avoid contact with eyes or mucous membrane.

NOTE. Excessive application of the product other than is needed to cover the affected area will not result in more
therapeutic benefit.

Mechanism of Action

2-hydroxy derivative of benzoic acid that produces desquamation of the horny layer of skin while not effecting
qualitative or quantitative changes in the structure of the viable epidermis.

SELEGILINE

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

N04BD01

ANATOMICAL

Nervous System

THERAPEUTIC

Anti-Parkinson Drugs

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Dopaminergic Agents

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › 5 mg tablet (as hydrochloride) (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › 5 mg tablet (as hydrochloride) (Rx)

Parkinson’s disease, by mouth, ADULT, initially 2.5 mg twice daily; slowly increase to 5 mg twice daily and reduce
the dose of levodopa after 2–3 days; avoid doses greater than 10 mg daily.

Administration

ORAL › 5 mg tablet (as hydrochloride) (Rx)

Should be taken with food. Do NOT discontinue abruptly. Gradually taper dose. When discontinuing selegiline, wait
2 weeks (wash-out period) before starting another antidepressant to allow enough time for the body to
regenerate MAO enzyme. NOTE: Abrupt discontinuation or interruption of antidepressant therapy has been
associated with a discontinuation syndrome, which may resemble neuroleptic malignant syndrome; symptoms
may include elevated temperature, muscular rigidity, altered consciousness and autonomic instability.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Increase doses with caution.

Renal Impairment:
Use with caution. May require a lower dose to prevent accumulation in the plasma.

Hepatic Impairment:
May require a lower dose.

Indications

Adjunctive therapy for the symptomatic treatment of parkinsonian syndrome.

Contraindications

Pheochromocytoma; elective surgery requiring general anesthesia; active duodenal ulceration, active gastric
ulceration, avoid or use with great caution in postural hypotension (when used in combination with levodopa).

Precautions

WARNING: Antidepressants increase the risk of suicidal thoughts and behaviors in pediatric and young adult
patients. Closely monitor for clinical worsening and for emergence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors.

Blood pressure effects (e.g., hypertension and orthostatic hypotension); CNS depression; dyskinesia; impulse
control disorders.

Melanoma; psychosis; serotonin syndrome; hepatic and renal impairment; hypoglycemia; surgery patients.

Elderly (orthostatic hypotension when titrating dose); children (monitor for activation of suicidal ideation).

Pregnancy (use is not recommended, especially during the first trimester); lactation.

SKILLED TASKS. May impair ability to perform tasks, which require mental alertness, like driving or operating
machinery.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: hypertension, hypotension, chest pain, peripheral edema, body pain, confusion, hallucination, vivid dream,
lethargy, abnormality in thinking, agitation, amnesia, paresthesia, headache, dizziness, insomnia, skin rash, acne
vulgaris, diaphoresis, pruritus, weight loss, diarrhea, xerostomia, abdominal pain, nausea, dyspepsia, constipation,
vomiting, dysgeusia, flatulence, anorexia, gastroenteritis, urinary retention, dysmenorrhea, sexual disorder, UTI,
urinary frequency, uterine hemorrhage, bruise, leg cramps, myalgia, tinnitus, pharyngitis, sinusitis, bronchitis, cough.

Less Common: Agitation, ankle edema, arrthymias, anxiety, dyspnea, angina, blurred vision, loss of appetite,
palpitation, micturition difficulties, leukocytopenia, postural hypotension, skin reactions, thrombocytopenia,
supraventricular tachycardia.

Rare: Activation of suicidal ideation and behavior.

Mechanism of Action

Selegeline is a phenethylamine selective MAO-B inhibitor used as an adjunct to levodopa in managing Parkinson’s
disease. It increases dopaminergic activity by intervening w/ the reuptake of dopamine at the synapse. It also
irreversibly inhibits monoamine oxidase (MAO)-B which is involved in the metabolism of dopamine in the brain.

Indications

Induction and maintenance of general anesthesia.

Contraindications

Known or suspected susceptibility to malignant hyperthermia; history of moderate to severe hepatic impairment
associated with halogenated
anesthetics.

Precautions

Increased intracranial pressure; Neuromuscular disease e.g. Duchenne muscular dystrophy; mitochondrial disorder;
Hypovolemia; Hypotension; Hemodynamically compromised patients; Patients at risk of QT prolongation; Hepatic and
renal impairment

Children; Pregnancy and lactation

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Hypotension, tachycardia, bradycardia, hypertension, agitation, drowsiness, shivering, dizziness,
headache, hypothermia, myoclonus, delirium (emergence), sialorrhea, cough, airway obstruction, laryngospasm,
breath-holding, apnea, fever

Less Common: Acidosis, albuminuria, amblyopia, anaphylactoid reaction, anaphylaxis, atrial arrhythmia, bigeminy,
bronchospasm, atrial fibrillation, cardiac arrhythmia, chest discomfort, complete AV block, confusion, conjunctivitis,
crying, dysgeusia, ST-segment depression on ECG, dyspnea, extrasystoles (supraventricular), facial edema,
glycosuria, hemorrhage, hepatic failure, hepatic insufficiency, hepatic necrosis, hepatitis, hepatotoxicity
(idiosyncratic), hiccups, hypertonia, hyperbilirubinemia, hyperglycemia, hypoxia, hypersensitivity reaction,
hyperkalemia (pediatric; postoperative), hyperventilation, hypophosphatemia, hypoventilation, insomnia, jaundice,
leukocytosis (transient), malignant hyperthermia, myoglobinuria, nervousness, oliguria, pain, pharyngitis, pruritus,
second degree atrioventricular block, skin rash, stridor, syncope, thrombocytopenia, tooth enamel damage
(fluorosis), urinary retention, urticaria, ventricular premature contractions, weakness, wheezing, xerostomia

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:41:22 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Avoid concomitant use with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Blood pressure-lowering agents and Hypotensive agents
(hypotensive effect); Dopamine; Epinephrine nasal, oral inhalation and systemic; Norepinephrine (arrhythmogenic
effect); Metyrosine, Pramipexole, Ropinirole, Rotigotine (sedative effect); Neuromuscular-blocking Agents;
Nondepolarizing (neuromuscular-blocking effect); QTcprolonging Agents (QTc-prolonging effect)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Sevoflurane: Bambuterol; Epinephrine nasal, oral inhalation and
systemic (arrhythmogenic effect); Methylphenidate (hypertensive effect); Rufinamide (sleepiness and dizziness)

Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following drugs: Fenoterol; Formoterol; Isoproterenol; Metaraminol
(arrhythmogenic effect); Other CNS depressants; SSRIs (psychomotor impairment)

Mechanism of Action

Sevoflurane is a fluorinated isopropyl esther. It may act by interfering with the release and re-uptake of
neurotransmitters at post-synaptic terminals and/or alter ionic conduction following receptor activation. It also
activates GABA receptors that produces general anesthetic effect, a decrease in myocardial contractility and mean
arterial pressure and increases respiratory rate.

ORAL › 40 mg tablet (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › 20 mg tablet (Rx)

Homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia, by mouth, ADULT, initially 40 mg daily at night, adjusted at intervals of
at least 4 weeks (see restricted dosing with 80 mg once daily at night). Prevention of cardiovascular events, by
mouth, ADULT, initially 10–40 mg once daily at night, adjusted at intervals of at least 4 weeks with recommended

protease inhibitors, and gemfibrozil.

ORAL › 20 mg tablet (Rx)

May be taken with or without food. Avoid excessive consumption, i.e., >1 L daily of grapefruit juice.

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:41:51 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Use with caution. Start at low dose.

Renal Impairment:
For mild-to-moderate renal impairment, dose reduction is warranted.
For severe impairment, refer patient to a specialist.

Hepatic Impairment:
Use with caution. Start at low dose. Contraindicated in active liver disease or persistent abnormal liver function
tests.

Restricted Dosing for 80 mg:
Restrict use of 80-mg dose to patients who have been taking Simvastatin 80 mg chronically, e.g., for 12 months or
more, without evidence of muscle toxicity, to minimize the risk of myopathy, including rhabdomyolysis, particularly
during the first year of treatment.

Indications

For prevention of cardiovascular events in patients with high cardiovascular risk due to atherosclerotic
cardiovascular disease or DM; primary hypercholesterolemia; homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia, or
combined hyperlipidemia in patients who have not yet responded adequately to diet or other appropriate measures;
(children and adolescents) heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia

Contraindications

Known hypersensitivity to simvastatin or any component of the formulation; active liver disease, or persistently
abnormal liver function tests; porphyria; pregnancy (congenital anomalies reported; decreased synthesis of
cholesterol possibly affects fetal development); breastfeeding

Precautions

Muscle effects (do not start simvastatin if creatine kinase [CK] is elevated in patients at high risk of muscle effects;
risk of myopathy is increased if simvastatin is given at high doses or with a fibrate, with lipid-lowering doses of
nicotinic acid, or with immunosuppressants; monitor liver function and creatine kinase). Rhabdomyolysis (rarely
occurs; risk may be increased in renal impairment and hypothyroidism).
Severe intercurrent illness, e.g., infection, trauma, or metabolic disorder (increased risk of myopathy,
rhabdomyolysis, and renal failure;); hypothyroidism (should be managed before starting treatment with a statin);
surgery (increased risk of acute renal impairment, which increases the likelihood of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis).
History of liver disease, or high alcohol intake (monitor liver function at initiation of treatment, after 4 to 12 weeks
and periodically thereafter, e.g., at 6-month intervals or when clinically indicated; discontinue if serum transaminase
concentration rises to, and persists at, 3 times the upper limit of the reference range); renal impairment).
Elderly (increased risk of myopathy); Children (safety and efficacy have not been established in children <10 years).

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:41:51 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Abdominal pain, atrial fibrillation, constipation, diabetes mellitus, dizziness, eczema, edema, elevated CK
and serum transaminase level, flatulence, gastritis, headache, insomnia, myalgia, nausea, upper respiratory
infection, urinary tract infection, vertigo, vomiting
Less Common: Mild GI symptoms
Rare: Anaphylaxis, anemia, angioedema, gynecomastia, hepatitis, hypersensitivity, impotence, interstitial lung
disease, jaundice, liver failure, myopathy, pancreatitis, peripheral neuropathy, pruritus, rash, renal failure,
rhabdomyolysis, toxic epidermal necrolysis

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Reduces therapeutic effect of Simvastatin:
Carbamazepine

Avoid concomitant use with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of simvastatin:
Clotrimazole (myopathy), CYP3A4 Inhibitors, e.g., Clarithromycin, Erythromycin, Ketoconazole, Diltiazem (myopathy;
rhabdomyolysis)

Increases serum concentration of the following drugs:
CYP3A4 Inhibitors, e.g., Clarithromycin, Erythromycin, Ketoconazole, Diltiazem

Reduces absorption of simvastatin:
Bile Acid Binding Resins [administer statin at least 1 hour before, or 4 hours after, the resin]

Mechanism of Action

An HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor, which is effective in reducing LDL cholesterol concentration, and has been
reported to reduce the incidence of fatal and non-fatal MI, stroke and mortality, and the need for coronary and non-
coronary revascularization procedures.

Drug Classification

Immunosuppressants

Selective Immunosuppressants

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › 1 mg tablet (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › 0.5 mg tablet (Rx)

Low-to-moderate immunologic risk renal transplant, by mouth, ADULT and CHILD =13 years or<40 kg, in
combination with cyclosporine and corticosteroids, initially, 3 mg/m2 on day 1 as loading dose, followed by 1 mg/m2
once daily as maintenance; ADULT and CHILD =13 years or =40 kg, 6 mg on day 1 as loading dose, followed by 2 mg
once daily as maintenance [NOTE: May be withdrawn after 2–4 months, in conjunction with an increase in sirolimus
dosage]. High immunologic risk renal transplant patients, by mouth, ADULT, in combination with cyclosporine and
corticosteroids for the first year, 15 mg on day 1 as loading dose, followed by 5 mg daily as maintenance; obtain
trough concentration between days 5–7 and adjust accordingly; continue concurrent cyclosporine + sirolimus +
corticosteroid therapy for 1 year following transplantation; further adjustment of the regimen must be based on
clinical status [NOTE: Adjustment of immunosuppressive therapy beyond 12 months should be considered based
on clinical judgment]. Lymphangioleiomyomatosis, by mouth, ADULT, initially 2 mg once daily; obtain trough
concentration in 10–20 days and adjust dose to maintain a target concentration of 5– 15 nanograms/mL. Note:
Maximum dose for all approved indications (Adults, Geriatric, Adolescents) is 40 mg/day.

Administration

ORAL › 0.5 mg tablet (Rx)

Administer consistently with regards to meals. Ingestion with high-fat meals decreases peak concentrations but
increases AUC by 23–35%. Administer initial dose as soon as possible after transplant. To be taken 4 hours after
oral cyclosporine. Do NOT crush, split, or chew the tablet. NOTE: Limit exposure to sunlight and ultraviolet light by
wearing protective clothing and using effective sunscreen to minimize the risk for malignancy.

Contraindications

No information found

Precautions

WARNING: Should only be used by physicians experienced in immunosuppressive therapy and management of
transplant patients. Adequate laboratory and supportive medical resources must be readily available.

Therapeutic drug monitoring is recommended, however, variations in methods to determine sirolimus whole blood
concentrations, as well as interlaboratory variations, may result in improper dosage adjustments, which may lead to
subtherapeutic or toxic levels. Determine the assay method used to assure consistency, and be aware of alterations
to assay method or reference range and that values from different assays may not be interchangeable.

May cause increased susceptibility to infection and the development of lymphoma and other malignancies. NOT
recommended for use in liver transplantation.

Associated with an increased risk of hepatic artery thrombosis (HAT), graft failure, and increased mortality with
evidence of infection.

NOT recommended for use in lung transplantation. Bronchial anastomotic dehiscence cases have been reported,
mostly fatal.

Anaphylactic or hypersensitivity reactions (e.g. anaphylactic or anaphylactoid reactions, angioedema, exfoliative
dermatitis, and hypersensitivity vasculitis); angioedema

Infections (opportunistic infections including activation of latent viral infections, fatal infections, and sepsis may
occur; progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML), an opportunistic CNS infection caused by reactivation of
the JC virus, has been reported)

Interstitial lung disease (e.g. pneumonitis, bronchiolitis obliterans organizing pneumonia [BOOP], pulmonary fibrosis;
may be fatal; may be associated with pulmonary hypertension).

Hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS); Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura, or thrombotic microangiopathy;

Hyperlipidemia

Lymphocele or fluid accumulation (e.g. peripheral edema, lymphedema, ascites, and pleural and pericardial effusions)

Malignancy (increased risk of other malignancies such as skin cancer)

Proteinuria (may increase urinary protein excretion; new onset nephrotic syndrome has been reported)

Renal effects (may increase serum creatinine and decrease GFR; may delay recovery of renal function in patients
with delayed allograft function)

Wound dehiscence or healing (may cause wound dehiscence and impaired healing)

nasopharyngitis, nausea, nephrotic syndrome, neutropenia, osteonecrosis, ovarian cyst, pancreatitis, pancytopenia,
pericardial effusion, pleural effusion, pneumonia due to Pneumocystis jiroveci (P. carinii), peripheral edema,
pneumonia, progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy, proteinuria, pseudomembranous colitis, pulmonary
alveolitis, pulmonary hemorrhage, pulmonary embolism, pyelonephritis, renal disease, sepsis, skin carcinoma,
reversible posterior leukoencephalopathy syndrome, skin rash, stomatitis, tachycardia, thrombocytopenia,
thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura, upper respiratory tract infection, tuberculosis, urinary tract infection, weight
loss, wound dehiscence, wound healing impairment

Page 4 of 5



April 25, 2026, 2:42:02 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Enhances therapeutic effect of Sirolimus: Trastuzumab (neutropenic effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Sirolimus: Denosumab (serious infections)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of ACE Inhibitors, e.g., Captopril, Enalapril
Reduces diagnostic effect of Coccidioides immitis Skin Test
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Antidiabetic Agents, Sipuleucel-T

Avoid concomitant use with:
Decreases serum concentration of Sirolimus: Dabrafenib, Efavirenz, Enzalutamide, Fosphenytoin, Mitotane,
Phenytoin, Rifampin
Decreases serum concentration of Tacrolimus (Systemic)
Enhances therapeutic effect of Sirolimus: Roflumilast (immunosuppressive effect)
Enhances therapeutic effect of Tofacitinib (immunosuppressive effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Sirolimus: Pimecrolimus, Tacrolimus
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of the following
drugs: Clozapine (agranulocytosis), Cyclosporine (Systemic) (calcineurin inhibitor-induced hemolytic uremic
syndrome, thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura, or thrombotic microangiopathy), Dipyrone (agranulocytosis;
pancytopenia), Leflunomide (hematologic toxicity such as pancytopenia, agranulocytosis, and/or thrombocytopenia),
Natalizumab (concurrent infection), Tacrolimus
Increases serum concentration of Sirolimus: Boceprevir, Conivaptan, Crizotinib, Cyclosporine (Systemic), Dasabuvir,
Fluconazole, Fusidic Acid (Systemic), Itraconazole, Ketoconazole (Systemic), Macrolide Antibiotics [except
Azithromycin, Fidaxomicin, Spiramycin], Mifepristone, Ritonavir, Telaprevir; Vaccines, Live (infection), Voriconazole
Reduces therapeutic effect of the following drugs: BCG (Intravesical), Vaccines (immune response)

FOOD INTERACTION. Grapefruit Juice may decrease the clearance of Sirolimus.

Mechanism of Action

Inhibits the regulatory kinase, mTOR (mechanistic target of rapamycin). This inhibition suppresses cytokine mediated
T-cell proliferation and the proliferation of lymphangioleiomyomatosis cells, thus preventing acute rejection of
allografts and prolongs graft survival.

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

A02AH

ANATOMICAL

Alimentary Tract and Metabolism

THERAPEUTIC

Drugs for Acid Related Disorder

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Antacids

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › 325 mg tablet (OTC)

ORAL › 650 mg tablet (OTC)

IV › 1 mEq/mL, 10 mL vial (pediatric) (Rx)

IV › 1 mEq/mL, 20 mL ampul/vial (Rx)

IV › 1 mEq/mL, 50 mL ampul/vial (Rx)

IV › 1 mEq/mL, 100 mL ampul/vial (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › 325 mg tablet (OTC)

ORAL Antacid: by mouth, ADULT, 2–8 tablets every 4 hours (maximum, 48 tablets in 24 hours); ADULT ≥60 years, 2–
4 tablets every 4 hours (maximum, 24 tablets in 24 hours. Urine alkalinizer: by mouth, ADULT, initially 3.94 g, then
0.97–1.95 g every 4 hours; CHILD, 84–840 mg/kg daily, in divided doses

Administration

ORAL › 325 mg tablet (OTC)

Should be taken on an empty stomach. Tablets may be swallowed whole or dissolved in water prior to use. DO NOT
add oral preparation to calcium-containing solutions.

Page 1 of 3



April 24, 2026, 11:21:08 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric, Renal and Hepatic Impairment: dose adjustment may be required

Indications

Symptomatic relief of hyperacidity (belching, heartburn, indigestion, gas pains), gastritis, and peptic ulcer; urine
alkalinizer.

Contraindications

Diuretics known to produce hypochloremic alkalosis; edema; hypertension; hypocalcemia; hypochloremia;
hypernatremia; impaired renal function; metabolic alkalosis; respiratory alkalosis or acidosis; any situation where
administration of sodium could be clinically detrimental.

Precautions

WARNING: Ask attending physician before use if on a sodium restricted diet. Do not use maximum dose for more than
2 weeks.

Cardiac, liver, or renal disease; fluid or solute overload; postoperative patients with cardiovascular or renal
insufficiency (e.g., sodium or water retention and edema which may result in serious pulmonary edema);

Arrested patients with preexisting metabolic acidosis, hyperkalemia, or tricyclic or barbiturate overdose;
Elderly; Pregnancy (restrict intake in hypertension and toxemia).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Belching, gastric distention, flatulence, metabolic alkalosis, electrolyte imbalance, (sodium overload,
hypocalcemia, hypokalemia, milk-alkali syndrome, dehydration), reduction in CSF pressure, intracranial hemorrhage,
severe tissue damage following extravasation of IV solution, renal calculi or crystals, impaired kidney function

Page 2 of 3



April 24, 2026, 11:21:08 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Decreases the absorption of Ketoconazole
Decreases therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Chlorpropamide, Lithium Carbonate, Salicylates, Tetracyclines
Increases therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Appetite Suppressants (e.g., Amphetamines), Flecainide,
Mecamylamine, Quinidine, Sympathomimetics (e.g., Ephedrine, Dopamine)

NOTE: Sodium bicarbonate raises intra-gastric pH, which may affect the absorption of certain drugs.

Mechanism of Action

A short-acting, potent systemic antacid that rapidly neutralizes gastric acid to form sodium chloride, carbon dioxide,
and water. After absorption of sodium bicarbonate, plasma alkali reserve is increased, and excess sodium and
bicarbonate ions are excreted in urine, rendering urine less acid.

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

B05XA02

ANATOMICAL

Blood and Blood Forming Organs

THERAPEUTIC

Blood Substitutes and Perfusion Solutions

PHARMACOLOGICAL

I.V. Solutions Additives

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

ORAL › 325 mg tablet (Rx)

ORAL › 650 mg tablet (Rx)

IV › 1 mEq/mL, 10 mL vial (pediatric) (Rx)

IV INFUSION › 1 mEq/mL, 50 mL ampule / vial (adult) (Rx)

IV INFUSION › 1 mEq/mL, 100 mL ampule / vial (adult) (Rx)

Dosage

ORAL › 325 mg tablet (Rx)

NOTE: Doses are adjusted according to pH, PaCO2, calculated base deficit, clinical response, and fluid limitations
of the patient. Cardiac arrest: by IV injection, ADULT, initially 1 mEq/kg of 7.5% or 8.4% solution initially, then 0.5
mEq/kg every 10 minutes, depending on arterial blood gas determinations (8.4% solutions contain 50 mEq/50 mL),
given over 1–2 minutes; CHILD, 0.5–1 mEq/kg of 4.2% solution every 10 minutes, depending on arterial blood gas
determinations, given over 1–2 minutes. Metabolic acidosis: by IV infusion, ADULT, 2–5 mEq/kg over 4–8 hours;
CHILD and INFANT, 2 to 3 mEq/kg daily of 4.2% solution over 4–8 hours. Amount (mEq NaHCO3) = (base deficit)(0.3)
(BW in kg) Amount (mEq NaHCO3)=(27 mEq HCO3/L)(0.3)(BW in kg)

Page 1 of 2



April 24, 2026, 11:21:14 PM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

ORAL › 325 mg tablet (Rx)

Give 4.2% (0.5 mEq/mL) and 5% (0.595 mEq/mL) NaHCO3 solutions undiluted. Dilute 7.5% (0.892 mEq/mL) and 8.4%
(1 mEq/mL) solutions with compatible IV solutions. Dilute to at least 4.2% for infants and children. Give a bolus
dose only in emergency situations. The usual rate is 2–5 mEq/kg over 4–8 hours. Do not exceed 50 mEq/hour. Stop
infusion immediately if extravasation occurs. Flush IV line thoroughly before and after administration. WARNING:
Rapid or excessive administration may produce alkalosis, hypokalemia, and hypocalcemia. Cardiac arrhythmias may
result from an intracellular shift of potassium. Many other complications may arise from electrolyte imbalance. See
Sodium Bicarbonate under Antacids in Chapter 1: Alimentary Tract and Metabolism for other information

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric, Renal and Hepatic Impairment:
Dose adjustment may be required

Indications

Systemic alkalinizer to correct metabolic acidosis; buffer for acidic parenteral solutions to prevent acidosis; for
parenteral nutrition.

Contraindications

Diuretics known to produce hypochloremic alkalosis; edema; hypertension; hypocalcemia; hypochloremia;
hypernatremia; impaired renal function; metabolic alkalosis; respiratory alkalosis or acidosis; any situation where
administration of sodium could be clinically detrimental

Mechanism of Action

A sterile preparation that contains sodium bicarbonate, utilized as an alkalizing agent.

SODIUM CALCIUM EDETATE

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

V03AB03

ANATOMICAL

Various

THERAPEUTIC

All Other Therapeutic Products

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Specific Antidotes

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IM › 200 mg/mL, 5 mL ampule (Rx)

Dosage

IM › 200 mg/mL, 5 mL ampule (Rx)

Lead poisoning, asymptomatic with blood lead levels >70 micrograms/dL or symptomatic without encephalopathy,
by deep IM injection, ADULT, 167 mg/m2 every 4 hours for 3–5 days, may treat with dimercaprol until blood lead level
is <50 micrograms/dL. Lead poisoning, blood lead levels >70 micrograms/dL and/or symptoms of encephalopathy,
by deep IM injection, ADULT, 250 mg/m2 every 4 hours for 5 days with dimercaprol, administer first dose at least 4
hours after first dose of dimercaprol, may be repeated after at least 2 treatment-free days if needed [NOTE: Both
drugs may be given simultaneously at separate sites]. NOTE: Total daily dose (1000 mg/m2) should be divided into
equal doses spaced 8–12 hours apart.

Administration

IM › 200 mg/mL, 5 mL ampule (Rx)

For IM use only. Administer IV fluid if possible prior to first dose to ensure adequate diuresis and reduce incidence
of treatment induced nephrotoxicity NOTE: Add lidocaine or procaine to minimize pain at injection site. Final
lidocaine or procaine concentration should be 5 mg/mL (0.5%).

Dose Adjustment

Renal Impairment:
May require dose reduction.

April 25, 2026, 2:42:36 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Indications

For the reduction of blood levels and depot
stores of lead in lead poisoning (acute and chronic) and
lead encephalopathy

Contraindications

Anuria; active renal disease; hepatitis

Precautions

WARNING: Do NOT confuse with sodium edetate.

Capable of producing toxic effects, which can be fatal.

Inform physician immediately if urine output stops for
a period of 12 hours. Stop therapy if anuria or
severe oliguria develops.

Renal damage (eg. proteinuria and microscopic hematuria;
dose-dependent);
Lactation.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common:
Pain at injection site, fever, chills, malaise,
fatigue, myalgia, arthralgia, hypotension, cardiac rhythm
irregularities, acute necrosis of proximal tubules,
infrequent changes in distal tubules and glomeruli,glycosuria, proteinuria, microscopic hematuria, large
epithelial cells in urinary sediment, tremors, headache,
numbness, tingling, cheilosis, nausea, vomiting,
anorexia, excessive thirst, milk increases in SGOT and
SGPT, histamine-like reactions, rash, bone marrow
depression (transient), anemia, zinc deficiency,
hypercalcemia

Less Common:
Nephrotoxicity (potentially fatal)

April 25, 2026, 2:42:36 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Incompatible with the following products:
Zinc Insulin Preparations (chelates with zinc)

Mechanism of Action

The calcium chelate of edetate disodium. It chelates divalent and trivalent metals, forming stable chelates that
displace calcium from the molecule, forming a water-soluble complex excreted by the kidneys.

PAREN › 2.5 mEq/mL, 50 mL vial (Rx)

Dosage

PAREN › 2.5 mEq/mL, 20 mL vial (Rx)

Dose is dependent on degree of sodium depletion (i.e., moderately severe hyponatremia where serum sodium is
between 125–129 mmol/L or profound hyponatremia where serum sodium is <125 mmol/L); on severity of
symptoms (i.e., moderately or severely symptomatic); and on time of development of hyponatremia (acute, where
hyponatremia is documented to exist <48hrs). IMPORTANT NOTE: Sodium deficit must be corrected gradually in
stages. Avoid overtly rapid correction to prevent development of Osmotic Demyelination Syndrome (previously
named Central Pontine Myelinosis). The rate of correction of hyponatremia should not be more than 10 mmol/L (or
10 mEq/L) in 24 hours, or 18 mmol/L (or 10 mEq/L) in 48 hours.

Administration

PAREN › 2.5 mEq/mL, 20 mL vial (Rx)

Administer based on calculated dose requirements for each patient. Must be diluted before infusion to avoid a
sudden increase in the plasma-sodium level. Too rapid administration should be avoided [see Important Note
under Dose]. Inspect visually for particulate matter and discoloration prior to administration. Do NOT use unless
the solution is clear, and seal is intact. Discard unused portion

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:42:42 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

Renal Impairment:
Select doses with caution due to potential to
result in sodium retention and increased risk
of adverse effects.

Indications

Hyponatremia with overt manifestations

Contraindications

Where the administration of
sodium or chloride could be clinically detrimental (e.g., hypernatremia); fluid retention; hypertension; CHF

Precautions

WARNING: Contains aluminum, which may be toxic. May reach toxic aluminum levels with prolonged use, especially if
kidney function is impaired.

Monitor fluid balance, electrolyte concentration, and acid-base balance; observe for hypernatremia, water retention
and pulmonary edema; restrict in patients with renal insufficiency or failure, cardiac failure, hypertension, peripheral
and pulmonary edema, or toxemia of pregnancy; Seizures or neurologic deficit (requires aggressive therapy); IV
administration can cause fluid and/ or solute overloading resulting in dilution of other electrolyte concentration,
overhydration, congested states or pulmonary edema; excessive administration of potassium-free solutions may
result in significant hypokalemia; Premature neonates.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Air embolization, febrile response, local tenderness, tissue necrosis or infection at the site of injection, venous
thrombosis or phlebitis extending from the site of injection, extravasation [NOTE: May occur because of the
solution, added drugs or the technique of reconstitution or administration].

Rare: Edema, sodium accumulation

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 2:42:42 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Monitor closely with:
Increases risk of hypertension and edema of the following drugs due to sodium and water retention: Corticosteroids
e.g. Prednisone

Mechanism of Action

A sterile, non-pyrogenic, concentrated solution of sodium chloride administered to replenish electrolytes.

or peeling skin with or without fever, wheezing, tightness
in the chest or throat, trouble breathing, swallowing, or
talking, unusual hoarseness, swelling of the mouth, face,
lips, tongue, or throat, post-operative inflammation,
corneal edema, corneal decompensation.

Drug Interactions

No known significant interactions

Mechanism of Action

A polysaccharide which functions as a tissue lubricant in ophthalmic surgery because it forms a viscoelastic solution
in water. It is a suitable substitute for aqueous and vitreous humor.

Contraindications

Preexisting vomiting and diarrhea (treatment); concurrent antithyroid medication (discontinue 3–4 days prior to
administration) ; women who are or may become pregnant; breastfeeding; treatment of thyroid malignancies shown
to have no iodide uptake (including majority of medullary and anaplastic carcinomas)

Precautions

WARNING:
Adhere to radiation safety precautions regarding radioactive iodine treatment. Use appropriate precautions for
handling and disposal.
Wear waterproof gloves during preparation, handling, and administration.

Hypersensitivity (eg. rash and hives);
Renal impairment;
Infertility (eg. transient, dose-related testicular function impairment);
Radiation-induced thyroiditis (may occur with gland enlargement and thyroid release);
Radiation toxicities (dose-dependent fatalities);
Thyroid enlargement (may occur with discontinuation; may cause thyroid enlargement and obstructive complications
of the trachea, esophagus, or blood vessels in the neck);
Elderly;
Patients <30 years (use is not recommended);
Children;
Pregnancy (may cause severe and irreversible hypothyroidism in neonates);
Lactation (excreted in breastmilk; discontinue breastfeeding at least 4 weeks prior to therapy).

Chest pain, tachycardia, metallic taste, alopecia, pruritus, skin lesion (iododerma), skin rash, urticaria,
hypoparathyroidism, thyroid storm, hypothyroidism, thyroiditis, gastritis, hyperthyroidism, nausea (high dose),
salivary gland disease, odynophagia, sialadenitis, sore throat, unpleasant taste, vomiting, infertility (transient;
permanent in repeated or high dose), acute leukemia, bone marrow depression (high dose), hematologic abnormality,
leukopenia, solid tumor, thrombocytopenia, anaphylaxis, hypersensitivity reaction, immunosuppression, neck
discomfort (tenderness or swelling), lacrimation, bronchospasm,anemia, cough, chromosomal abnormality, local
swelling, radiation injury

Less Common:
Conjunctivitis, epiphora, radiation pneumonitis, xerophthalmia, xerostomia

Rare:
Neoplasm

Thyroid Products

Mechanism of Action

A radiopharmaceutical that is rapidly absorbed and distributed within the extracellular fluid of the body. Iodide is
concentrated in the thyroid via the sodium/iodide symporter, and subsequently oxidized to iodine. Beta emission of
sodium iodide I131 destroys thyroid tissue.
