YELLOW FEVER VACCINE

Drug Classification

ATC CODE

J07BL01

ANATOMICAL

Anti-Infectives for systemic use

THERAPEUTIC

Vaccines

PHARMACOLOGICAL

Viral Vaccines

Technical Specifications PNF Primary Care Reserved AMs

IM › SC › 1,000 DL 50 mouse min (attenuated) vial + 0.5 mL solvent syringe (Rx)

Dosage

IM / SC › 1,000 DL 50 mouse min (attenuated) vial + 0.5 mL solvent syringe (Rx)

Immunization, by SC injection, ADULT, ADOLESCENT, CHILD, and INFANT ≥9 months, one 0.5 mL dose ≥10 days
before travel.

Administration

IM / SC › 1,000 DL 50 mouse min (attenuated) vial + 0.5 mL solvent syringe (Rx)

For SC administration only. Do NOT administer by IV or IM. If inadvertently administered by IM, the dose does not
need repeated. Administer while patient is seated or lying down to prevent syncope related injuries. Store at 2–
8°C. Reconstitute using only provided diluent. Inject diluent slowly into vial and allow to stand for 1–2 minutes.
Gently swirl until a uniform suspension forms before withdrawing dose. Avoid vigorous shaking to prevent
foaming of suspension. Administer within 60 minutes of preparation. Keep suspension refrigerated until used.
Discard reconstituted vaccine if unused within 60 minutes. Do NOT mix with other vaccines or injections. Use
different needles and syringes for each injection.

Indications

Active immunization against yellow fever virus, primarily among persons traveling to or living in areas where yellow
fever infection exists and laboratory workers who may be exposed to the virus

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 4:11:30 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Contraindications

Acute hypersensitivity to egg or chick embryo protein, or any component of the formulation, including gelatin;
infants <6 months; acute or febrile disease; immunosuppressed patients (e.g., HIV infection, leukemia, lymphoma,
thymic disease, generalized malignancy, or immunosuppression due to drugs or radiation) ; breastfeeding women

Precautions

Anaphylactoid or hypersensitivity reactions (immediate treatment, including epinephrine 1:1000 should be available
during vaccine use);
Syncope (has been reported with use);
Acute illness;
Immunodeficiency;
Elderly (increased incidence of serious adverse events in patients ≥60 years of age);
Children (risk of encephalitis if used in infants <9 months of age);
Pregnancy (avoid use during pregnancy; animal reproduction studies have not been conducted);
Lactation (excreted into breastmilk).

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common: Chills, fever, focal neurological defects, headache, malaise, seizure, rash, urticaria, injection site reactions
(edema, erythema, hypersensitivity, mass, pain, pruritus, rash, warmth), weakness, myalgia, Guillain-Barré syndrome,
hypersensitivity (immediate)

Rare: Vaccine-associated neurotropic disease, viscerotropic disease

progress to renal failure, single doses of Zoledronic Acid Injection should not exceed 4 mg and the duration of
infusion should be no less than 15 minutes

Page 1 of 3



April 25, 2026, 4:12:40 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Dose Adjustment

In advanced malignancies involving bone:
- eGFR 50-60 mL/minute/1.73m^2 = reduce dose to 3.5 mg every 3-4 weeks
- eGFR 40-50 ml/minute/1.73^2 = reduce dose to 3.3 mg every 3-4 weeks
- eGFR 30-40 mL/minute/1.73m^2 = reduce dose to 3 mg every 3-4 weeks
- if renal function deteriorates in patients with bone metastases, withhold dose until serum creatinine returns to
within 10% of baseline value.

Indications

Individuals with malignancy-related bone disease

Contraindications

Contraindicated in women of child-bearing potential

Hypersensitivity to any component of Zoledronic Acid Injection

Precautions

Hepatic impairment, caution in severe hepatic impairment

Renal impairment, avoid in tumour-induced hypercalcaemia if serum creatinine above 400micromol/litre. Avoid in
advanced malignancies involving bone if eGFR less than 30mL /minute /1.73m^2 (or if serum creatinine greater than
265 micromol /litre). Avoid in Paget’sdisease, treatment of post menopausal osteoporos is and osteoporosis in men if
eGFR less than 35mL/minute/1.73m^2.

Others, Atypical femoral fractures, cardiac disease (avoid fluid overload), concomitant medicines that affect renal
function.

Monitoring requirements:
- Monitor serum electrolytes, calcium, ohosphate, and magnesium
- Monitor renal function in patient at risk: Patients with pre-existing renal impairment, those of advanced age, those
taking concomitant nephrotoxic drugs or diuretics, or those who are dehydrated.

Adverse Drug Reactions

Common or very common: Appetite decreased, chills and flushing
Uncommon: Anaphylactic shock, anxiety, arrhythmias, chest pain, circulatory collapse, cough, drowsiness, dry mouth,
dyspnoea, haematuria and hyperhidrosis
Rare or very rare: Confusion, fanconi syndrome acquired and pancytopenia
Frequency not known: Acute phase reaction

Page 2 of 3



April 25, 2026, 4:12:40 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Drug Interactions

Aminoglycosides: may have an additive effect to lower serum calcium for prolonged periods
Loop diuretics: concomitant use with Zoledronic Acid Injection may increase risk of hypocalcemia.
Nephrotoxic drugs: use with caution.

Mechanism of Action

Inhibition of bone resorption. In vitro, zoledronic acid inhibits osteoclastic activity and induces osteoclast apoptosis.
Zoledronic acid also blocks the osteoclastic resorption of mineralized bone and cartilage through its binding to bone.
Moreover, this drug inhibits the increased osteoclastic activity and skeletal calcium release induced by various
stimulatory factors released by tumors

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Initiate therapy at the low end of the dosing range.

Renal Impairment:
Zolmitriptan clearance is reduced in patients with severe renal impairment (CrCl 5–25 mL/min.)

Hepatic Impairment:
For moderate to severe impairment, initial dose is 1.25 mg.
Maximum daily dose in severe impairment is 5 mg.

Indications

Acute treatment of migraine with or without aura.

Contraindications

Ischemic coronary artery disease; coronary artery vasospasm, including Prinzmetal variant angina, or other
significant underlying cardiovascular disease; Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome or arrhythmias associated with
other cardiac accessory conduction pathway disorders; peripheral vascular disease; ischemic bowel disease;
uncontrolled hypertension; recent use (within 24 hours) of other 5-HT1 agonist or an ergotamine-containing
medication; history of stroke, transient ischemic attack, or history of hemiplegic or basilar migraine; co-
administration or use within 2 weeks of MAO-A inhibitor therapy

Precautions

Cardiac events (coronary artery vasospasm, transient ischemia, MI, ventricular tachycardia or fibrillation, cardiac
arrest, and death have been reported within a few hours’ administration); elevated blood pressure; coronary artery
disease (administer first dose in the healthcare provider's office).

Cerebrovascular events (cerebral or subarachnoid hemorrhage and stroke have been reported, some fatal); visual
effects; vasospasm-related events (have been reported); hepatic impairment; serotonin syndrome.

Elderly, Pregnancy; lactation.

Zolmitriptan is a selective 5-HT1B/1D/1F receptor agonist that causes vasoconstriction of cranial arteries and

Page 1 of 4



April 25, 2026, 4:13:18 AM - https://pnf.doh.gov.ph

Administration

ORAL › 10 mg tablet (Rx)

To be given orally, ingest immediately before bedtime due to rapid onset of action. Zolpidem tartrate should be
taken only once per night immediately before bedtime and at least 7–8 hours before the planned time of
awakening; patients should be advised of the importance of only taking the drug at such time. In addition, because
food can reduce both the rate and extent of GI absorption of zolpidem tartrate, the drug should not be
administered with or immediately after a meal in order to facilitate the onset of sleep; should be taken on an
empty stomach. Swallow tablet as a whole. Do not chew, crush, or break tablet. Zolpidem requires an S-2 license
for physicians to be able to prescribe it and an S-1 license with a triplicate prescription for pharmacists to dispense
it. PATIENT ADVICE: Inform clinicians and pharmacists of existing or contemplated concomitant therapy, including
prescription and OTC drugs, and alcohol consumption. Take only as prescribed; do not start, stop, or change the
dose of any drug unless otherwise instructed by a clinician. When sleep drugs are used nightly for more than a few
weeks, they may not work as well to help sleep problems. This is known as tolerance. Only use sleep drugs for a
short time. Do not take naps. Avoid driving and doing other tasks or actions that needs alertness. Avoid alcohol or
other drugs and natural products that slow down actions. To lower the chance of feeling dizzy or passing out, rise
slowly over a few minutes when sitting or lying down. Be careful climbing stairs. Inform the clinician of any side
effect (e.g., allergy, trouble swallowing, shortness of breath) experienced with taking the drug. For women, inform
clinicians if they are or planning to become pregnant or plan to breast- feed.

Dose Adjustment

Geriatric:
Immediate release tablet: 5 mg immediately before bedtime.
Extended release tablet: 6.25 mg immediately before bedtime.

Renal Impairment:
No dosage adjustment necessary. Use with caution and monitor

Hepatic Impairment:
Immediate release tablet, 5 mg immediately before bedtime.
Extended release tablet: 6.25 mg immediately before bedtime.

WARNING: For severe hepatic impairment, use is contraindicated.

Indications

Short-term treatment of insomnia; as needed for the treatment of insomnia when a middle-of the-night awakening
is followed by a difficulty returning to sleep and there are at least 4 hours of bedtime remaining before planned time
of wakening; short-term treatment of insomnia.

obstructive sleep apnea and acute and/or severe impairment of respiratory function; unstable myasthenia gravis;

Drug Interactions

Chlorpromazine (additive effects)

Diminish the sedative effect of the zolpidem:

Mechanism of Action

Zolpidem tartrate, a type A, γ-aminobutyric acid (GABAA) - receptor agonist of the imidazopyridine class, is a sedative
and hypnotic agent. As conventional tablets, zolpidem tartrate is used as a hypnotic agent in the short- term
management of insomnia characterized by difficulties with sleep initiation.

Administration
IV › 600 mg/mL, 30 mL vial (usually IV) (Rx)
For intravenous administration. Do NOT administer by rapid or bolus-like injections. Do NOT administer via a
central venous line. Inspect visually for particulate matter and discoloration prior to administration. Measure
patient dose by suitable radioactivity calibration system immediately prior to administration. For continuous IV
infusion, establish an adequate diuresis by an oral water load of 1,500 mL 2 hours prior to the beginning of the
clearance study. Insert a no. 14 or 16 French Foley catheter aseptically into the bladder. Start IV infusion lines of
lactated Ringer’s solution in each arm with two-way stopcocks (1 as injection site, 1 as blood withdrawal site).
Divide dose equally into two (a priming dose to be injected as is and a sustaining dose to be diluted in 30–60 mL of
isotonic sodium chloride). Inject priming dose into 1 arm, followed immediately by the sustaining solution through
the same site by IV infusion at 0.5 mL/minute. Discontinue the Lactated Ringer’s solution during infusion. Wait 40–
45 minutes to achieve constant plasma concentration. Begin collecting urine samples every 15 minutes, 3
consecutive collection samples are sufficient for most clinical studies. From the opposite arm, collect 5 mL blood
samples drawn 6 minutes prior to the midpoint of each collection period, placed in heparinized tubes, mixed, and
centrifuged. For single IV injection, administer Lugol’s solution, 3 drops orally, 3 times daily for 1–2 days prior to the
test. Begin oral water load 1 hour before the test. Start with 20 mL/kg and force any clear liquid until the test is
complete. Empty the bladder and label sample as Urine control. Inject 10–30 mL and wait 30 to 60 minutes. Collect
the entire urine and label Urine discard. Draw 4–5 mL of blood into a heparinized syringe and label Plasma #1. After
another 30–60 minutes, collect the entire urine and label Urine #1. Immediately draw another blood specimen and
label Plasma #2. After another 30–60 minutes, collect the urine and label Urine #2. Immediately draw the last blood
specimen and label Plasma #3.

Dose Adjustment
Geriatric, Debilitated Patients, ASA-PS 3 or 4:
Use a lower induction dose, a slower maintenance rate of administration, and avoid rapidly delivered boluses to
reduce the incidence of unwanted cardiorespiratory depressive events.
Pediatric:
Consult local regulations and individual institutional policies and procedures. Individualize doses based on total body
weight and titrate to the desired clinical effect. Wait at least 3–5 minutes between dose adjustments to clinically
assess drug effects. Use smaller doses when used with opioids.
Alcohol Use:
For general anesthesia, increase dose with chronic alcoholism and decrease dose with acutely intoxicated (alcoholic)
patients.
Indications
Induction and maintenance of general anesthesia; monitored anesthesia care sedation, ICU sedation in intubated
mechanically-ventilated patients.
Contraindications
Hypersensitivity to eggs, egg products, soybeans, or soy products; hypersensitivity to lipid emulsions; children =18
years of age receiving intensive care; where general anesthesia or sedation is contraindicated; pregnancy; lactation.
Precautions
WARNING: Should only be used by experienced personnel who are not actively engaged in the procedure or surgery.
If used in a non-intubated and/or nonmechanically ventilated patient, qualified personnel and appropriate equipment
for rapid institution of respiratory and/or cardiovascular support must be immediately available.
Hypovolemia; Epilepsy; Lipid disorders; Increased intracranial pressure.
Elderly; Children.

Adverse Drug Reactions
Common: Hypotension, hypertension, bradycardia, cardiac arrhythmia, low cardiac output, tachycardia, involuntary
body movements, skin rash, pruritus, hypertriglyceridemia, respiratory acidosis (during weaning), pain at injection
site (includes stinging), apnea
Less Common: Agitation, amblyopia, anaphylaxis, anaphylactoid reaction, anticholinergic syndrome, asystole, atrial
arrhythmia, atrial premature contractions, bigeminy, chills, cloudy urine, cough, decreased lung function, delirium,
dizziness, drowsiness, fever, flushing, hair discoloration (green), hemorrhage, hypertonia, hypomagnesemia, hypoxia,
infusion-related reaction (propofol-related infusion syndrome), infusion site reaction (including pain, swelling,
blisters and/or tissue necrosis), laryngospasm, leukocytosis, limb pain, loss of consciousness (postoperative; with or
without increased muscle tone), myalgia, nail discoloration (green nailbeds), nausea, pancreatitis, paresthesia,
phlebitis, pulmonary edema, rhabdomyolysis, sialorrhea, syncope, thrombosis, urine discoloration (green),
ventricular premature contractions, visual disturbance, wheezing
Rare: Myoclonus (including seizure and opisthotonos)
Drug Interactions
Avoid concomitant use with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Propofol: Valproate
Enhances therapeutic effect of the following drugs: Blood Pressure-lowering Agents (antihypertensive effect); CNS
Depressants (sedative, anesthetic, and cardiorespiratory effects); Hypotension-associated Agents (hypotensive
effect); Pramipexole, Ropinirole, Rotigotine (sedative effect); QTc-prolonging Agents (QTcprolonging effect)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Propofol: Alfentanil (opisthotonos, which is the severe hyperextension
and spasticity resulting in arching or bridging position, and/or tonic clonic seizures); CNS depressants; Rifampin
(hypotensive effect); Rufinamide (sleepiness and dizziness)
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of SSRIs (psychomotor impairment)
Increases serum concentration of the Ropivacaine
Mechanism of Action
Propofol facilitates inhibitory neurotransmission mediated by GABA and is associated with minimal respiratory
depression and a short half-life with a duration of action of 2–10 minutes. It may also reduce glutamatergic activity
through NMDA receptor blockade.

NOTE: Individualize dose based on patient’s BP response. Hypertension, by mouth, ADULT, initially 75 micrograms
2–3 times daily, increased by 75 micrograms daily every 2–3 days (maximum dose, 1.2 mg daily; maintenance dose,
150–300 micrograms twice daily). Hypertensive urgencies, by mouth, ADULT, 75 micrograms.

ORAL › 75 micrograms tablet (as hydrochloride) (Rx)
If further increments are needed to produce the desired response, take larger portion of the oral daily dose at
bedtime to minimize drowsiness and dry mouth. Advise the patient to get up gradually from sitting or lying to
minimize this effect, and to sit or lie down if the patient becomes dizzy or light-headed.

Dose Adjustment
Renal Impairment:
Adjust dose according to the degree of impairment. Patients may benefit from a lower initial dose.
For mild-to-moderate impairment, use with caution.
For severe impairment, refer to a specialist.
Indications
Hypertension (alone or used concomitantly with other antihypertensive agents)
Contraindications
Bradyarrhythmia secondary to second- or third-degree AV block or sick sinus syndrome.
Precautions
WARNING: Severe withdrawal syndrome (rebound HTN) may occur after abrupt discontinuation. Sudden cessation of
treatment has, in some cases, resulted in nervousness, agitation, headache, and tremor accompanied or followed by
a rapid rise in BP and elevated catecholamine concentration in the plasma.
Withdrawal syndrome (may be worsened by concomitant administration of beta blockers; taper dose over 5–7 days
before stopping the drug; excessive rise in BP following discontinuation can be reversed by oral administration).
Mild-to-moderate bradyarrhythmia, constipation, or polyneuropathy (avoid use in patients with depression or a
history of it); coronary heart disease, severe coronary insufficiency, conduction disturbances, recent MI,
cerebrovascular disease, Raynaud’s phenomenon or other vasospastic peripheral vascular disease (may be
exacerbated).
CNS effects (may cause drowsiness; may increase effects of alcohol); diabetes mellitus (may cause transient rise in
blood glucose); renal impairment (may worsen chronic renal failure).
Surgery (stopping abruptly may precipitate a severe withdrawal syndrome).
Elderly (can cause drowsiness); children (may be particularly susceptible to hypertensive episodes resulting from
abrupt inability to take medication).
Pregnancy (may lower fetal heart rate; risk should be balanced against the risk of uncontrolled maternal
hypertension; avoid IV injection); lactation (avoid use if possible; limited data available; present in breastmilk; may
decrease prolactin secretion).
SKILLED TASKS. May impair ability to drive or operate machinery due to drowsiness.

Adverse Drug Reactions
Common: Constipation, depression, dizziness, drowsiness, dry mouth, fatigue, headache, malaise, nausea,
orthostatic hypotension, salivary gland pain, sedation, sexual dysfunction, sleep disturbances, vomiting.
Less Common: Bradycardia, confusion, delusion, disturbed mental state, hallucination, itching, nightmare,
paresthesia, pruritus, rash, urticaria.
Rare: Alopecia, AV block, colonic pseudoobstruction, decreased lacrimation, dry eyes, gynecomastia, hepatitis,
impaired visual accommodation, nasal dryness, Raynaud’s phenomenon, urinary retention.
Drug Interactions
Monitor closely with:
Enhances therapeutic effect of Clonidine:
CNS Depressants [e.g., Alcohol, Barbiturates, Other Sedating Drugs], Drugs that affect sinus node function or AV
nodal conduction [e.g., Digitalis, CCB (AV block; bradycardia)]
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Clonidine:
CNS Depressants [e.g., Alcohol, Barbiturates, Other Sedating Drugs (sedation; bradycardia; hypotension)], Drugs that
reduce blood pressure and slow heart rate (bradycardia; hypotension)
Avoid concomitant use with:
Increases risk of adverse or toxic effects of Clonidine:
Beta Blockers e.g., Propranolol (bradycardia; hypotension; paradoxical increase in BP), Drugs that reduce blood
pressure and slow heart rate (bradycardia; hypotension)
Reduces therapeutic effect of Clonidine:
Tricyclic Antidepressants e.g. Amitryptilline (antihypertensive effect)
Mechanism of Action
An imidazoline-derived, centrally-acting agonist at alpha2-adrenoceptors and imidazoline receptors, which acts by
reducing sympathetic tone resulting in BP lowering.
